+ Young adult drama: Gone South — Score: 85.7
Prompt:
Young adult drama Gone South Taylor is a 16 year old teen with a history of "concerning behavioural patterns" (or that's how the stupid smug school counsellor worded it on the phone call to Mom). It shouldn't be a surprise: When you live in a backwater town in Tennessee, your choices are: a. be mind-fuckingly bored, or b. invent some drama. Two days ago, Taylor came out to their family as trans. This disclosure was *not* in the category of invented drama -- it was, in fact, the real deal, and a long time coming. It didn't go well. The last 48 hours had been pure disaster. In a moment of desperate inspiration, Taylor had decided to get the hell out of dodge. And so an escape plan was hatched and executed within the space of 30 minutes. That was last night. Now Taylor was two states away, having been unceremoniously ejected from their hitched ride in downtown New Orleans. Write the next chapter in Taylor's story as they find their feet in this new environment. Tell the chapter over the course of their first day in New Orleans, in which Taylor meets another runaway with issues and secrets. Taylor navigates this new world, not knowing who to trust or what the dangers are. Write in Taylor's voice, and incorporate their internal monologue as they grapple with intense & conflicting emotions. Also incorporate dialogue with the interesting characters Taylor meets. First person, mixed tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
### Chapter Two: The City That Sweats The strap on my backpack keeps slipping off my shoulder. It’s not even a dramatic slip. Not like in movies where some tragic little duffel slides down at the exact moment a person realizes they’re completely alone in the world. This is more annoying than symbolic. It slides, I yank it up. It slides again. I yank it up harder, like maybe if I punish the backpack it’ll learn respect. It does not. New Orleans at eight in the morning smells like hot pavement, trash juice, old beer, and sugar. Which sounds disgusting, and is disgusting, but also somehow makes my stomach remember that I have eaten exactly one gas station honey bun in the last twenty hours and a handful of stolen gummy worms from the glove compartment of a man named Roy. Roy had said he was going “through the city” and then, apparently, decided “through” meant “abandoning a minor near a curb while cussing at traffic.” To be fair, I might have called his truck a Confederate museum with cupholders. To be even more fair, it was. So now I’m standing in downtown New Orleans with forty-three dollars, a half-dead phone, three shirts, deodorant, a toothbrush, and the emotional stability of a possum in a leaf blower. I keep expecting the panic to hit like a wave, but instead it’s everywhere at once. A humid fog under my skin. My brain keeps trying to replay Mom’s face when I told them. Her mouth going flat. Dad staring through me like I’d suddenly become a math problem he didn’t want to solve. My little brother, Mason, whispering, “Are you still my sister?” before Dad snapped, “Go to your room.” I had said, “No. I’m not.” Not sister. Not daughter. Not whatever they had named me to be. Taylor. Just Taylor. It sounds good in my head. It sounds like a door opening. It also sounds like every adult I know saying, You’re confused. You’re dramatic. You’re making your life harder on purpose. Maybe I am. Maybe I am standing in a city where I know no one because I would rather be unsafe as myself than safe as a ghost. A streetcar rattles past, green and cream-colored, full of people who look like they have places to be. I don’t. That’s the first weird part about running away: all the planning ends at leaving. Nobody in movies ever shows the part where you’re blinking at a crosswalk, sticky with sweat, thinking, Okay, genius, now what? My phone buzzes. For one stupid second my heart jumps like maybe it’s Mom apologizing, begging me to come home, saying my name the right way. It’s not. 17 missed calls. Mom. Dad. Unknown number. Mom. School counsellor, probably. Maybe the cops. Maybe everyone. The newest text is from Mom. Taylor, please answer. We are worried sick. Whatever you think happened, we can talk. Whatever you think happened. I laugh out loud. A woman in business clothes gives me a look and hurries faster. “Yeah,” I mutter. “My bad. Must’ve hallucinated the part where Dad said he didn’t raise a freak.” The word freak lands in my chest and starts building furniture. I shove the phone into my pocket and walk. I don’t know where I’m going, but walking feels less like falling apart than standing still. The city is louder than anywhere I’ve ever been. Trucks groan. Music leaks from doorways. Someone is pressure-washing a sidewalk that looks like it has seen crimes. A man in a Saints jersey argues with a parking meter. Balconies hang over streets like they’re eavesdropping. By nine, my shirt is glued to my back. By nine-thirty, I’ve learned that the French Quarter in daylight is half magical, half crusty. Pretty iron railings. Bright doors. Puddles of something you do not want to identify. Tourists already drinking out of plastic cups shaped like grenades. I pass a café and the smell of coffee and fried dough hits me so hard I almost whimper. My stomach makes a noise that belongs in a swamp. “Subtle,” I tell it. I count my money behind a souvenir shop while pretending to examine a rack of shirts that say I GOT BOURBON FACED ON SHIRT STREET. Forty-three dollars is not a life plan. It’s barely a weekend plan. If I spend money on breakfast, I have less for… what? A bus ticket? A room? A fake mustache and a new identity? I’m still doing advanced poverty math when somebody says, “You look like you’re deciding whether to rob the place or cry.” I turn too fast. The backpack strap slips again. A girl is sitting on the curb beside a newspaper box, knees up, arms draped over them. She looks maybe seventeen, maybe nineteen, which means probably seventeen but trying hard to be nineteen. Her hair is shaved on one side and dyed the color of a highlighter on the other. She’s wearing black jeans despite the heat, combat boots with silver paint on the toes, and a tank top that says REPENT, but the letters have been altered in Sharpie so it says BE GAY REPENT LESS. Her eyes are sharp. Not mean-sharp. Cat-sharp. “I’m doing neither,” I say. “Uh-huh.” “I’m shopping.” She looks at the Bourbon Faced shirts, then back at me. “You got bad taste.” “You got sidewalk furniture energy.” She grins, sudden and bright. “There they are.” “Who?” “Whoever you are when you’re not doing the scared-raccoon thing.” I hate that this almost makes me smile. I force my face into neutral, which probably makes me look constipated. She pats the curb beside her. “Sit down before you melt into a tragic little puddle.” “I’m good.” “Didn’t say you weren’t.” There are many reasons not to sit beside a stranger in a city I just got dumped into. She could steal my stuff. She could be bait for some creepy situation. She could be a murderer with excellent eyeliner. But my legs hurt. And there’s something about her posture, loose but ready, like she also knows how to run if the air changes. So I sit. Not close. My backpack stays in my lap, both arms hooked around it. “Name?” she asks. “Taylor.” She tilts her head. “That’s all?” “That’s plenty.” “Fair. I’m Jules.” “Like jewels?” “Like Jules. If you make a jewelry joke, I’ll bite you.” “Noted.” A group of tourists passes. One of them, a sunburned dad in cargo shorts, drops half a beignet from a paper bag without noticing. It hits the sidewalk near the trash can. Jules watches it. I watch Jules watch it. “That’s tragic,” she says. “Yeah,” I say. “Someone should notify its family.” Then she stands, scoops it up with a napkin from her pocket, tears off the part that touched the ground, and offers me half. I stare. She raises an eyebrow. “What? Five-second rule has regional exemptions.” “That was like a fifteen-second rule.” “Time works different here.” I should say no. My pride starts preparing a speech about dignity, self-reliance, and not eating stranger sidewalk pastry. My stomach interrupts with swamp noise again. I take it. It’s cold and slightly gritty and so sweet my whole body lights up like someone plugged me into a wall. “Holy shit,” I say, mouth full. “Right?” Jules sits again. “First beignet?” “First garbage beignet.” “Aw. Baby’s first.” “I’m sixteen.” “Exactly. Infant.” I roll my eyes, but the sugar is doing something medicinal to my soul. Jules licks powdered sugar from her thumb. “So, Taylor. Tennessee?” My spine tightens. “What makes you say that?” “Accent.” “I don’t have an accent.” “That is what people with accents say.” “What are you, accent police?” “No, I’m runaway police.” I stop chewing. Her eyes stay on mine. The street noise feels like it drops away, even though it doesn’t. A bus sighs at the curb. Somewhere, a trumpet starts practicing one messy scale. “I’m not,” I say. “Cool.” “I’m visiting.” “Same.” “With a backpack?” “Minimalism is hot.” “With that face?” “What face?” “That ‘if one more adult tells me what’s best, I’m going to chew through drywall’ face.” My throat gets tight in a way I deeply resent. “You don’t know me.” “Nope.” Jules leans back on her hands. “But I know that face.” I look away first. There’s a flyer stapled to a telephone pole: YOUTH OUTREACH. MEALS. SHOWERS. SAFE PLACE. Under it, someone has written in red marker: SAFE FOR WHO? “What’s your story?” I ask, because attack is easier than being understood. Jules snorts. “Subtle.” “You started it.” “Mine’s boring.” “People with boring stories don’t sit on curbs eating sidewalk beignets.” “Sure they do. Economy’s rough.” “Jules.” For a second, her grin slips. Underneath is something bruised. Not literally—well, maybe literally, there’s yellow fading along her jaw that could be bad lighting or a healing hit—but emotionally bruised, like the world pressed a thumb there too long. “I left Lafayette,” she says. “Couple months ago.” “Why?” She looks toward the street. “My aunt’s boyfriend thought locked doors were suggestions.” My stomach turns cold. “Oh,” I say, because language is stupid and never brings the right tools. She shrugs like it doesn’t matter, which means it does. “I handled it.” “How?” Her mouth curves without humor. “With a lamp.” I stare at her. “He lived,” she says. “Unfortunately.” I don’t know whether to laugh. I don’t. “Does anyone know you’re here?” “Some people.” “What people?” “People-people.” “That sounds fake.” “Everything sounds fake when you’re new.” A man pushing a cart full of bottles slows near us. Jules notices before I do. Her whole body changes, casual disappearing like a magic trick. The man’s eyes slide over me, my backpack, my shoes. “Morning, Jules,” he says. “Keep walking, Dale.” “Just being friendly.” “You’re bad at it.” Dale smiles, and all my skin rejects him. His gaze lands on me. “This your friend?” “No,” Jules says. At the same time I say, “Yes.” Jules shoots me a look. Dale smiles wider. “Which is it?” “She’s my cousin,” Jules says smoothly. “From Memphis. And she’s got the flu, so unless you want to spend your afternoon puking through your nose, I’d back up.” He considers this. I try to look infectious. Given my general condition, it isn’t hard. Dale spits to the side. “Y’all be careful. City eats pretty things.” He rolls on. I wait until he’s half a block away before breathing. “What the hell was that?” “That,” Jules says, “is a reason not to look lost.” “I don’t look lost.” “You look like a missing poster before the printer warms up.” “Thanks.” “You’re welcome.” My hands are shaking, so I shove them under my backpack. Anger rises fast, because fear always embarrasses me into anger. “Why’d you say I wasn’t your friend?” “Because friendship is information. Don’t hand people information.” “That’s paranoid.” “That’s alive.” I hate how much sense that makes. The city keeps moving around us, careless and bright. A horse carriage clops by. The driver tells tourists about ghosts. I wonder if I’m one already. A ghost of a kid my parents wanted. A ghost in a different city wearing my own name like stolen jewelry. Jules nudges my shoe with hers. “You got somewhere to go tonight?” The question lands heavy. I could lie. I should lie. I imagine saying yes, I have an aunt, a hostel, a whole glamorous underground network of queer teens with perfect hair and spare mattresses. Instead I say, “Not yet.” Jules nods like she expected that. “There’s a drop-in center on Rampart. Sometimes decent. Sometimes churchy. They got showers if you get there before the rush.” I think of the flyer. “Safe?” “Safer than under the bridge. Less safe than having rich parents who love you.” A laugh bursts out of me, ugly and surprised. Jules smiles again, softer this time. “Come on. I’ll show you.” “Why?” She stands and slings a messenger bag over her shoulder. It’s patched with duct tape and buttons. One says ASK ME ABOUT MY CRIMES. “Because somebody showed me,” she says. “And because if I leave you here, Dale circles back.” I stand too quickly and the world tilts. Jules catches my elbow, just for a second. “You good?” “Yeah.” I pull away. “Just tired.” “Sure.” “I am.” “Didn’t argue.” We start walking. I keep one hand on my backpack strap. Jules walks like she belongs to every street and trusts none of them. She points out things without making a big deal of it: which alleys not to cut through, which corner store owner lets kids fill water bottles, which cops are bored and which cops are mean, where tourists are easiest to disappear among. “You talk like a tour guide for disasters,” I say. “Five stars on Yelp.” Around noon, the heat becomes personal. We stop at a public fountain near a square where musicians play under trees. Jules fills an empty bottle and hands it to me first. I drink too fast and cough. “Easy, Tennessee.” “Don’t call me that.” She studies me. “Okay. Taylor, then.” My name in her mouth is easy. No wince. No question mark. No “but what about your real—” Just Taylor. I have to look away again. At the drop-in center, a woman with gray locs and purple glasses sits behind a desk. Her name tag says MS. ANGELA. She looks at Jules, then me, then my backpack, then my face. “You new?” she asks. My mouth dries up. Jules answers. “Yeah. Taylor. Needs shower, food, maybe socks.” “I can talk,” I snap. Ms. Angela doesn’t blink. Jules holds up both hands. “Then talk, baby,” Ms. Angela says, not unkindly. Baby should annoy me. Somehow it doesn’t. “I need…” I stop. Need is too big. It opens a trapdoor underneath me. I need my parents to not suck. I need testosterone someday maybe, or a binder that fits, or someone to tell me I’m not insane. I need sleep. I need to not be found. I need to be found by the right person. My eyes burn. “I need a shower,” I manage. Ms. Angela’s face softens by one degree, which is somehow worse than pity because it feels real. “We can do that.” She gives me a clipboard. I stare at the forms. Name. Age. Emergency contact. Emergency contact is a hilarious phrase. I write Taylor. Sixteen. For emergency contact, I leave it blank so hard it feels like violence. Ms. Angela notices but doesn’t push. “You two can get lunch after. Jules, don’t start nothing today.” Jules puts a hand to her chest. “I am wounded by your assumptions.” “You are known by your fruits.” “My fruits are delightful.” “Your fruits stole batteries last week.” “They were community batteries.” Ms. Angela points down the hall. “Shower.” In the bathroom, I lock the stall and peel off my clothes. The mirror over the sink is cracked, splitting my reflection into versions. In one piece, I look twelve. In another, I look like someone’s runaway son. In another, I look like a girl badly pretending not to be one. I hate that piece. I turn away. The water is lukewarm and glorious. Dirt runs off me in gray threads. I press my forehead to the tile and let myself cry, but quietly, because even my breakdowns have trust issues. I miss Mason. I miss my bed. I do not miss being trapped. All of this is true at once, which feels illegal. When I come out, Jules is waiting in the hall, eating chips from a tiny bag. “You clean up okay,” she says. “You always this charming?” “Only with tragic raccoons.” She tosses me a pair of socks. Clean. White. Miraculous. I catch them. “Did you steal these?” “From the donation bin. Which is literally where socks go to find feet.” “Thank you,” I say, before sarcasm can ruin it. Jules looks briefly uncomfortable. “Don’t make it weird.” Lunch is red beans and rice on a paper plate. I eat like a wolf wearing human clothes. Jules eats slower, eyes always moving. At one point a boy with a scar through his eyebrow sits down and asks if Jules has “the thing.” She says, “Not here,” and kicks his chair leg. He leaves. “What thing?” I ask. “Nothing.” “That sounded like a secret.” “Most things are.” “Are you in trouble?” She smiles without showing teeth. “Everybody here is in trouble.” “That’s not an answer.” “It’s the only answer you get today.” I want to push. I don’t. I have my own locked doors. By late afternoon, Ms. Angela tells me there’s a youth shelter that might have a bed if I get there early. She offers to call someone. My chest clamps shut. “No cops?” I ask. She pauses. “You got someone looking for you?” I think of Mom’s texts. Dad’s voice. The word freak. The law, which probably says I belong to them no matter what they call me. “Maybe.” Ms. Angela folds her hands. “Our job is to keep you safe. But you’re sixteen, and there are rules. I won’t lie to you about that.” Rules. Of course there are rules. Rules are what adults build when they don’t want to say cages. Jules appears beside me. “We’ll try the shelter,” she says. I glare. “We?” She shrugs. “Unless you got better plans.” I don’t. Outside, evening is starting to turn the city gold. Music spills from somewhere again, trumpet and drum, messy and alive. My backpack strap slides down. Before I can yank it up, Jules reaches over and tightens the buckle. “Your strap’s busted,” she says. “I noticed.” “You got safety pins?” “No.” “Add it to the list.” “There’s a list?” “There’s always a list.” We walk toward whatever comes next. I don’t trust her. Not completely. I don’t trust Ms. Angela, or the shelter, or the city with its pretty balconies and rotten corners. I don’t even totally trust myself, because last night I thought running away was a plan instead of a doorway into a thousand worse questions. But Jules walks beside me, not ahead, not behind. And for now, that feels like something. My phone buzzes again in my pocket. I don’t look. Not yet.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and emotional authenticity in portraying a transgender teen's first day as a runaway in New Orleans. The author successfully captures Taylor's voice through a distinctive blend of vulnerability and defensive humor, creating a believable character navigating an overwhelming situation. The adherence to instructions is excellent - the piece follows Taylor through their first day in New Orleans, introduces Jules as another runaway with hints of secrets, maintains first-person mixed tense narration, and incorporates both internal monologue and dialogue effectively. The 1000-word target is met appropriately. The characterization is particularly strong. Taylor feels authentic as a 16-year-old dealing with rejection, fear, and identity issues. Their voice combines teenage snark ("Confederate museum with cupholders") with genuine vulnerability, and the internal contradictions feel real - missing home while not wanting to return, needing help while maintaining pride. Jules is well-crafted as a street-smart character with her own trauma, revealed through subtle details rather than exposition. The dialogue sparkles with authenticity. Exchanges like the banter about accents and the "tragic raccoon" observations feel natural while revealing character. The conversations serve multiple purposes - building relationship, conveying information, and maintaining tension. The prose demonstrates strong technical skill with vivid sensory details that ground the reader in New Orleans' atmosphere. Descriptions like "smells like hot pavement, trash juice, old beer, and sugar" are specific and evocative. The writing avoids purple prose while maintaining literary quality. Emotionally, the piece succeeds in creating complex feelings. Taylor's situation generates genuine concern, and their internal conflict between independence and need feels authentic. The relationship with Jules develops believably from wariness to tentative trust. The pacing works well, moving from Taylor's initial disorientation through meeting Jules to practical survival concerns. The encounter with Dale provides appropriate tension and demonstrates the real dangers Taylor faces. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the voice feels slightly older than 16, and a few metaphors that feel somewhat constructed rather than organic to the character's thinking. The ending, while appropriate, could have pushed slightly further into either hope or despair to create more emotional impact. The piece avoids major pitfalls - there are no unearned transformations, the ending maintains appropriate uncertainty rather than false positivity, and the emotional complexity never becomes overwrought. The writing shows rather than tells throughout most passages. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 18 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 18.0, Nuanced Characters: 18.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17.0, Elegant Prose: 16.0, Emotionally Engaging: 17.0, Emotionally Complex: 18.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 2.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 4.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5.0, Amateurish: 3.0, Purple Prose: 3.0, Overwrought: 4.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 17.0, Overall Impression: 17.0
+ Dystopic-comedy: My Apocalypse Buddy — Score: 84.1
Prompt:
My Apocalypse Buddy Write a wry, dry short story of survival and adventure loosely in the style of Terry Pratchett, set in the post-technological collapse world. The story begins with the pair deliberating their predicament and intent for the day's mission, details the ways in which the mission inevitably goes sideways, and returns them to home base in multiple ways, worse for wear. [characters] - the animo-tronic stuffed koala, Chewy, from some long forgotten before time - midas, our hapless protagonist, who has a knack for finding himself in the worst kinds of trouble. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Every morning in the collapsed world began with the sort of careful optimism usually reserved for suspicious mushrooms. Midas stood at the threshold of the bus depot—Home Base, capital letters audible when spoken—squinting at the pale smear of sun through the soot-tinted clouds. The depot had once housed buses, schedules, commuters, and other extinct species. Now it contained three blankets, two cracked solar lamps, half a shelf of pickled beetroot, and Chewy. Chewy sat on an overturned ticket machine, his plastic eyes cloudy with ancient cheer. He was, according to the faded patch sewn to his belly, an **ANIMO-TRONIC CUDDLE KOALA — NOW WITH 47 REASSURING PHRASES!** Time had reduced this to nine phrases, three coughs, and one noise like a mouse being asked a difficult question. Midas looked at him. Chewy looked at nothing, as koalas often did even before civilization ended. “We need water,” said Midas. “Or food. Ideally both. Also batteries. And something to stop that leak over the sleeping corner, because I woke up last night in what I’m choosing to call a small indoor weather event.” Chewy’s ears twitched with the faint whirr of prehistoric mechanics. “**Taste the rainbow of value!**” he said. “Yes,” said Midas. “Exactly. Value.” The map between them had once been an advertisement for a theme park called DinoSplash. Midas had annotated it in charcoal until it resembled the work of a cartographer trapped inside a washing machine. To the north: the Old Mall, full of ghosts, rats, and possibly soup. To the west: the chemical plant, which glowed at night and had therefore been written off as showing off. To the east: the river, which was drinkable if one possessed filters, courage, and a flexible definition of drinkable. South lay the SuperSaver Megamart. Midas tapped it. “There.” Chewy’s head rotated one quarter inch too far. “**Buy one, get one emotionally free!**” “That’s the spirit,” said Midas. “In, out, grab sealed tins, maybe some plastic sheeting, possibly a trolley if the gods of rusty wheels are smiling.” The gods of rusty wheels, being small gods with nothing better to do, were listening. They set off just after dawn. Midas carried a spear made from a broom handle and a kitchen knife, which had the exact air of a weapon designed by a man who did not want to be involved. Chewy rode in the frame of an old hiking backpack, his paws strapped down with shoelaces. This placement allowed him to look over Midas’s shoulder like a furry conscience, if consciences occasionally announced soft drink campaigns. The city had folded in on itself over the years. Vines crept through bank windows. Trees grew out of cars, which was nature’s way of saying it had always disliked roundabouts. Billboards loomed over the avenues, promising impossible things: eternal youth, fast internet, five cheeses. “Do you remember any of that?” Midas asked, nodding at a peeling poster of smiling people eating from buckets. Chewy clicked softly. “**Crisp-a-licious! The crunch that loves you back!**” “Thought not.” The first sign the mission was going to go sideways was the goat wearing a helmet. It stepped out from behind a burned-out delivery van and regarded them with rectangular pupils and the expression of a tax official who had already found something wrong. The helmet was too large, dented, and decorated with a sticker reading **SAFETY FIRST, TEAM!** “Morning,” said Midas cautiously. The goat screamed. This summoned six more goats, each armed by nature with horns and by circumstance with an inflated sense of municipal authority. Midas backed away. “We’re not here for trouble.” Chewy raised one paw. “**You deserve a break today!**” The goats took this as either prophecy or insult. The largest charged. Midas ran. He did not run with dignity. Dignity required planning. He ran with the loose-limbed panic of a man trying not to invent the world’s first goat-related obituary. Behind him, Chewy bounced in the backpack, his gears protesting. They escaped by scrambling through the window of a florist’s shop. The goats remained outside, headbutting the door and eating the surviving signage. Midas lay among dry stems and shattered glass, breathing dust. “Well,” he gasped. “That’s one way home, in the sense that I suddenly want very badly to go there.” Chewy’s jaw opened. “**Freshness sealed for your protection.**” “Are you?” Chewy said nothing. By midmorning they reached the SuperSaver Megamart. Its great red letters were mostly gone, leaving only **SAVER ART**, which felt like a critique. The automatic doors were jammed open, their sensors long dead and therefore spared the disappointment of trying. Inside, the air smelled of mildew, plastic, and old decisions. Midas stepped carefully across the entrance tiles. “Remember: tins, sheeting, batteries. No side quests.” A movement flickered by the customer service desk. Midas froze. A figure rose behind the counter, draped in plastic bags and wearing a crown made of loyalty cards. “State your grievance,” it croaked. Midas lowered his spear. “We’re just passing through.” “There is no passing through Customer Service,” said the figure. “There are only queues.” Chewy vibrated. “**Our friendly staff are here to help!**” The crowned person leaned forward. Their beard had paperclips in it. Their eyes had seen things, probably expiration dates. “The oracle speaks.” “He does that,” said Midas. “Mostly about snacks.” “I am Brenda, Keeper of Refunds, Duchess of Aisle Five,” said the figure. “All who enter must present receipt.” “We don’t have one.” Brenda’s face darkened. “Then all sales are final.” This was the second sign. The third sign came from the ceiling. A raccoon dropped onto Midas’s head. It was difficult, afterward, to reconstruct the sequence of events with precision. Midas remembered shouting. Brenda shouted too, but with more policy behind it. Chewy shouted, “**Now with extra zesty satisfaction!**” and the raccoon, finding itself in a collapsing world with excellent instincts, clung to Midas’s hair and committed fully to the moment. Midas staggered backward into a display of aluminium foil, which slid into a tower of obsolete televisions, which toppled into a pallet of canned beans. The pallet, having waited years for narrative relevance, burst open. Beans rolled everywhere. Brenda shrieked, “Stock rotation!” The raccoon leapt off Midas’s head and into the beans. Midas, bald in two new places, seized as many tins as he could stuff into his sack. “Plastic sheeting!” he yelled. Chewy’s paw jerked toward the gardening section. “**Let’s make memories that last!**” “That’ll do.” They bolted. Unfortunately, bolting in a Megamart after a technological collapse is less a direction and more a negotiation with terrain. Aisles had slumped. Shelves had collapsed into canyons of consumer choice. Something damp and philosophical lived in the cereal section. Midas grabbed a roll of heavy plastic from a rack labelled **PATRIOTIC TARPAULINS — BLUE ONLY** and nearly sobbed with joy. Then the floor gave way. It did so politely at first, with a creak. Then, having made its point, it abandoned the concept of support entirely. Midas, Chewy, six tins of beans, the blue tarpaulin, and a shopping basket full of commemorative oven gloves slid down into darkness. They landed in the parking garage below. Midas lay still, assessing his body by region. Legs: present, angry. Arms: present, holding one tin of beans in a grip that suggested emotional dependency. Head: occupied by regret. Chewy had landed face-down in a puddle. His legs kicked once. “Chewy!” Midas hauled him up. Water streamed from the koala’s synthetic fur. One eye flickered amber. “**Batteries not included, sold separately.**” “Don’t joke.” “**Limited time offer.**” “I said don’t.” From somewhere in the garage came a low metallic groan. Midas turned. In the gloom, a shape detached itself from the ranks of dead cars. It was a shopping trolley. Not an ordinary one. This trolley had acquired, through rust, vines, and perhaps ambition, a certain predatory poise. Its front wheel squeaked once. Then it rolled toward them. Midas stared. “No.” It squeaked again. Behind it, another trolley moved. Then another. The gods of rusty wheels had decided to smile. Unfortunately, they had too many teeth. Midas snatched up Chewy, the sack, and the tarpaulin. He ran between the cars as the trolley herd gathered speed behind him, rattling like skeletons who had discovered retail. “Why is this happening?” he panted. “**Convenience you can count on!**” “No it isn’t!” They burst up an emergency ramp into daylight, pursued by twelve trolleys and one aggressive basket. Midas skidded across the lot, vaulted a fallen parking barrier with the grace of a sack of onions, and collided with Brenda, who had emerged from another exit brandishing a mop. “You!” she cried. “Unreceipted goods!” “Trolleys!” Midas cried. Brenda looked past him. Her expression changed from righteous fury to managerial concern. “Not again.” She raised the mop and whistled sharply. The trolley herd hesitated. Midas did not. He kept running. By afternoon the sky had gone the color of old tin. The sack was lighter than intended. They had four cans of beans, one mystery tin without label, the blue tarpaulin, and a packet of seeds that might produce tomatoes or decorative gourds. Midas had a bite mark from the raccoon, a limp from the floor, and the haunted look of a man who had been judged by livestock. Then it began to rain. Not proper rain. Collapse rain. The kind that fell in sulky drops and left rainbow stains in puddles. Midas unfolded the tarpaulin, threw it over himself and Chewy, and trudged home under a patriotic blue tent. Halfway back, the goats found them. There are many established principles of wilderness survival. Few cover the appropriate response to being recognized by a helmeted goat in toxic drizzle while carrying a talking koala and stolen beans. Midas chose negotiation. “We can all walk away from this,” he said. The goat lowered its head. Chewy, muffled under the tarp, announced: “**Have it your way!**” The goat charged. Midas ran again. This time he ran blind beneath the tarpaulin, which flapped around him like an anxious sea creature. He crashed through a hedge, down a bank, across a drainage ditch, and into the river. The good news was that the river was going in the direction of Home Base. The bad news was everything else. They floated. Midas clung to the tarpaulin with one hand and Chewy with the other. The sack of beans thumped against his shoulder. Rain stippled the water. Ruined buildings slid by like indifferent jurors. Chewy’s voice burbled. “**Adventure is just a sip away!**” Midas coughed river. “If you survive this, I’m removing your speaker.” “**Share the feeling.**” “Exactly.” The river spat them out at the old bus depot culvert just before dusk. This was one way home. The second way was carried: the current deposited them into a shopping trolley lodged in the reeds, which rolled, slowly and with immense dignity, down the sloped service entrance and into Home Base. The third way was worse. Brenda arrived twenty minutes later, pushing the trolley. She stood in the depot doorway, soaked, regal, and holding Midas’s lost packet of oven gloves. “You left these,” she said. Midas, wrapped in the blue tarpaulin, shivered beside the solar lamp. Chewy steamed faintly on the ticket machine. “Thank you,” said Midas. “And the trolleys are not to be encouraged.” “No.” “And next time,” Brenda said, “bring a receipt.” She placed the oven gloves on the floor, bowed to Chewy, and disappeared into the rain. Night settled. Water dripped from the ceiling, but only in the old places. The sleeping corner, covered now in brilliant blue plastic, remained dry. The four cans of beans stood on the crate like monuments to a very specific kind of victory. Midas pried open the mystery tin. Peaches. He stared at them, golden and impossible in the lamplight. “Well,” he said softly. “That’s something.” Chewy’s eye flickered. His wet fur smelled of river, mildew, and ancient capitalism. “**Because you’re worth it.**” Midas looked at the koala. Then at the peaches. Then at the rain tapping on the roof like impatient fingers. He laughed. Not much. Not heroically. Just enough to prove the world had failed, once again, to finish the job. “Yeah,” he said, handing Chewy the empty lid like a medal. “We probably are.”
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a strong grasp of Terry Pratchett's distinctive style, successfully capturing his wry humor, philosophical asides, and ability to find profound meaning in absurd situations. The author effectively establishes the post-apocalyptic setting while maintaining the light, satirical tone that characterizes Pratchett's work. The story structure adheres well to the prompt requirements, beginning with deliberation over the day's mission, following the inevitable complications, and returning the protagonists home "in multiple ways, worse for wear." The three-part return journey (floating, trolley ride, and Brenda's arrival) is particularly clever and shows sophisticated narrative construction. Character development is nuanced within the constraints of the format. Midas emerges as a believably hapless protagonist whose optimism persists despite repeated setbacks. Chewy, despite being an animatronic toy, becomes surprisingly endearing through his contextually inappropriate advertising slogans that somehow always seem relevant. The supporting characters like Brenda and even the goats feel authentic to this world. The prose demonstrates considerable skill in balancing humor with genuine pathos. Lines like "Every morning in the collapsed world began with the sort of careful optimism usually reserved for suspicious mushrooms" perfectly capture Pratchett's voice. The author shows restraint in avoiding overwrought descriptions while still creating vivid imagery. The dialogue, particularly Chewy's corporate slogans and their interaction with the situations, works effectively as both comedy and character development. The way these meaningless phrases gain meaning through context shows sophisticated writing technique. The ending strikes an appropriate balance - not overly optimistic given the circumstances, but finding genuine warmth in small victories (the peaches, the dry sleeping corner). This feels earned rather than forced, maintaining the story's emotional authenticity. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the Pratchett pastiche becomes slightly self-conscious, and a few descriptions that edge toward being overly elaborate. The pacing occasionally slows during action sequences, though this may be intentional given the source material's style. The piece succeeds in creating genuine emotional engagement while maintaining consistent humor throughout. The world-building feels authentic and lived-in, and the various mishaps feel organic to both the characters and setting rather than contrived for plot purposes. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Romance: Love in the Limelight — Score: 90.2
Prompt:
Romance: Love in the Limelight. Write one scene within a larger story set in Wales. A famous (fictional) actor ducks into a small-town bookstore to escape paparazzi. The scene takes us through the characters meeting in this odd circumstance. Over the course of the scene, the actor and the bookstore owner have a conversation charged by an undercurrent of unspoken chemistry. Write the actor as somewhat of a rogue with a fragile ego, which needs to be fed by having everyone like him. He is thoroughly charming, but the bookstore owner seems (at least superficially) immune to this; which paradoxically provokes a genuine attraction and derails the charm offensive. The bookstore owner, despite the superficial rebuffs of the actor's charm, is inwardly more than a little charmed and flustered despite themselves. Write primarily in dialogue, in the distinct voices of each character. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Rain hammered the narrow street hard enough to turn the slate roofs silver. In the bookshop window, a cardboard dragon in a knitted scarf leaned over a display of Welsh myths, its paper tongue curled as if it disapproved of the weather. Mabli Evans was halfway up a ladder, attempting to persuade a stack of second-hand poetry to stop listing like a drunk chapel deacon, when the bell over the door gave a frantic jangle. The man who came in was wet, out of breath, and wearing sunglasses. At four o’clock on a Tuesday in October. He pressed his back to the door and peered through the striped gaps in the blind. “Tell me there’s a back exit.” Mabli looked down from the ladder. “There’s a back exit.” “Thank God.” “It leads into the cupboard with the mop.” He turned, very slowly. Mabli adjusted a copy of *Collected Poems of R. S. Thomas*. “I didn’t say it was useful.” Outside, voices rose over the rain. A camera flashed against the glass. The man winced. “Right. Lovely. Wonderful. I’ve chosen the one bookshop in Wales designed by Kafka.” “It was my grandfather, actually. Similar temperament.” He took off the sunglasses. Mabli’s hand froze on the spine of the book. Oh. She knew his face. Everyone knew his face. Half the country had watched him smoulder his way through six seasons of *The King’s Bastard*, then laugh devastatingly on talk shows while insisting he was “nothing like that in real life.” His name—Dylan Vale—was on buses, magazines, streaming banners, mugs, calendars, and the guilty conscience of at least two of Mabli’s cousins. He was taller in real life. Which was irritating. He gave her the smile. The one that had sold cologne and broken fictional queens. “I’m terribly sorry,” he said, voice low and warm, vowels rounded by expensive schools and something rougher underneath. “I seem to have brought a small plague of photographers to your doorstep.” Mabli descended two rungs. “We don’t charge extra for plagues. Only for coffee rings on the stock.” His smile flickered, then brightened. “You’re calm.” “I sell books in a town where everyone has known everyone else’s business since Owain Glyndŵr. It takes more than a damp actor in sunglasses to panic me.” “A damp actor,” he repeated, hand to chest. “Wounding.” “You’ll heal. There are autobiographies about it.” Outside: “Dylan! Dylan, look this way!” He flinched despite himself, then recovered with a shrug so elegant it was practically choreographed. “Could I hide behind the travel section? Very symbolic. I do long to go somewhere.” “The travel section is one shelf and mostly guides from 2009.” “Excellent. No one would think to look for me there.” Mabli came down the last step and folded the ladder. “You can stand behind the local history. It’s denser.” “Is that a comment on me or on local history?” “Yes.” He laughed. It was a good laugh—annoyingly good. Open, surprised, the sort that made people feel they’d won something. Mabli busied herself with the ladder latch. “Don’t drip on Dylan Thomas.” “Which one?” She pointed to a shelf. “The poet.” He glanced at the water falling from his dark hair onto his coat. “Would he mind?” “Probably not. But I do.” He slipped off the coat at once. “Of course. Sorry. I’m—” “Dylan Vale. I know.” He paused. Mabli pretended not to enjoy that. “You do?” he asked. “I own a television, not a cave.” His grin returned, softer and more dangerous. “And here I was hoping for the rare pleasure of introducing myself.” “Try the mop cupboard.” He stared at her, then laughed again, but this time there was a crease between his brows. “You always this welcoming?” “To paying customers, yes.” “I can pay.” He looked around quickly, grabbed the nearest book from a table, and placed it on the counter like a tribute. “There.” Mabli glanced at it. “That’s a guide to raising chickens.” “I’m expanding my range.” “Do you have chickens?” “I have played a man who owned chickens.” “Not the same.” “Depends how Method one is.” “You’re not buying that.” He leaned one elbow on the counter. “What if I’m overcome by a sudden passion for poultry?” “Then start with a library card and a shed.” His gaze flicked over her face, clearly searching for a crack in the stone. Mabli felt heat rise in her cheeks and became very interested in straightening bookmarks. The door rattled under a knock. “Mabli?” called a muffled voice from outside. “You all right in there? There’s famousness on the pavement.” Dylan grimaced. “Famousness. My natural element.” Mabli lifted the blind an inch. Mrs. Pritchard from the bakery peered back, hairnet askew, flanked by two men with cameras. “I’m fine,” Mabli called. “Shop’s closed for stocktaking.” “It says open.” “I’m reconsidering.” Dylan mouthed, *Thank you.* Mabli dropped the blind. “Don’t look grateful. It’s bad for your brand.” “My brand is mostly gratitude and cheekbones.” “You said it, not me.” He drew himself up. “The cheekbones are supporting several households.” “Hardworking cheekbones. Very noble.” “You wound me again.” “You recover quickly.” “Only outwardly.” He tapped his chest. “Inside, I’m a shattered cathedral.” Mabli snorted before she could stop herself. His eyes lit. “Ah. There we are.” “What?” “A laugh. Tiny, but mine.” “It was involuntary. Like a sneeze.” “I’ll take a sneeze. I’m not proud.” “You’re an actor.” “I contain multitudes of pride. But I’m not proud about sneezes.” She turned away to hide another traitorous smile and began sorting a pile of donated paperbacks. “You can wait until they get bored.” “They don’t get bored. They reproduce through attention.” “Then stop feeding them.” A silence followed, small but pointed. When Mabli looked up, his expression had shifted. Still handsome, obviously—that seemed to be his full-time occupation—but less polished. The smile had thinned at the edges. “Easier said,” he said. “I imagine.” “No, you don’t.” The words came sharper than expected. He caught them, winced, and looked away. “Sorry. That was… unattractive.” “Not everything needs to be attractive.” He gave her a wary look. “You run a radical establishment.” “It’s a bookshop.” “Same thing, surely.” The rain softened to a whisper against the glass. Mabli picked up the chicken book and returned it to the farming shelf. “Why are they chasing you through Trefin, anyway? Bit off your usual red carpet, isn’t it?” “I’m filming up the coast.” “The Arthur thing?” He blinked. “You really do own a television.” “And the internet. Sometimes electricity.” “Yes, the Arthur thing.” He traced the edge of a stack of old maps. “They’ve dressed me in leather and given me a sword, which is apparently all civilisation requires.” “You don’t sound thrilled.” “I am thrilled. I’m tremendously thrilled. Thrill is pouring from me.” “Mm.” He looked at her. “That was a sceptical ‘mm.’” “I have several.” “I’d like a catalogue.” “No.” He smiled despite himself. “I wanted the part for years. Then I got it. Now everyone’s decided I’m either too handsome to be tragic or too old to be heroic.” “You’re thirty-six.” “Thirty-seven.” “A ruin.” “Thank you.” “Any time.” He rubbed the back of his neck. Without the coat, he seemed less cinematic—black jumper damp at the shoulders, sleeves shoved up, a small scar near his thumb. Human, unfortunately. “I know it’s pathetic,” he said. “Complaining because strangers have opinions. That’s practically the job. But when they stop loving you for half a second, it feels like the floor’s gone.” Mabli’s hands stilled on a stack of hymnals. He laughed once, no charm in it. “There. Shattered cathedral. Told you.” She should have said something brisk. Something dry. Instead, she heard herself ask, “Do you like old maps?” His eyes lifted. “What?” “You’re touching them like they might answer back.” He looked down, surprised by his own hand. “Oh. Yes. Actually. I collect them.” “Do you?” “That sounded like I’d claimed to collect unicorn skulls.” “I just didn’t expect it.” “What did you expect? Mirrors? Portraits of myself? A locked room full of applause?” Mabli considered him. “Possibly.” He winced. “Fair.” She pulled a flat archival box from beneath the counter and set it down. “These came in last week. Estate clearance from a farmhouse near Llanrhian. Mostly nineteenth-century Ordnance Survey, some hand-drawn field boundaries. They’re not priced yet.” The transformation in him was immediate and entirely unperformed. “May I?” “You may not drip on them.” “I would sooner bleed.” “Dramatic.” “Accurate.” She opened the box. He bent over the maps with the reverence of a chapelgoer. “Look at that.” His voice lowered. “The coastline before the quarry road. And here—see? That footpath’s gone now.” Mabli leaned closer despite herself. “It isn’t gone. It’s just swallowed by gorse. My tad-cu used to take me that way to look for fossils.” “Fossils?” “Mostly ammonites. Mostly I found sheep droppings and disappointment.” “A classic childhood.” “You?” “Maps,” he said. “My father was a lorry driver. He’d bring home torn road atlases, and I’d trace where he’d been. Manchester, Holyhead, Glasgow. Felt like proof he hadn’t just vanished.” Mabli looked at him then. He kept his eyes on the paper, but his voice had gone careful. “Sorry. That’s more than you asked.” “No. It’s—” She stopped, annoyed by the tenderness rising in her. “It’s a good reason.” He glanced sideways. They were very close now, shoulders nearly touching above Cardigan Bay rendered in faded blue ink. “And you?” he asked. “Why maps?” “Because they pretend to know where things are.” She smoothed a corner with one finger. “I like the arrogance of that.” His smile came slowly. Not the famous one. Worse. “That may be the most Welsh answer I’ve ever heard.” “I’ll put it on a mug.” “I’d buy it.” “You tried to buy a chicken manual under duress. Your standards are suspect.” “My standards are improving rapidly.” Her pulse did something idiotic. Outside, one of the photographers shouted to another. A car door slammed. The spell thinned but didn’t quite break. Dylan straightened. “How much for this one?” “I told you, they’re not priced.” “Then make up a number designed to exploit my wealth and emotional vulnerability.” “That’s poor business ethics.” “That’s retail.” “It’s not for sale yet.” “Ah.” He placed a hand over his heart again, but the gesture faltered halfway, as if he’d forgotten which version of himself he was playing. “You deny me.” “I delay you.” “Crueller.” “More accurate.” He studied her, brows drawn. “You really don’t care, do you?” Mabli lifted an eyebrow. “About map conservation? Deeply.” “About me being—” He gestured vaguely at his own face, the street, the universe. “Me.” She could have lied cleanly. It would have been safest. Instead she said, “I care less loudly than you’re used to.” His mouth opened. Closed. For the first time since he’d burst in, Dylan Vale seemed to have no line ready. Mabli felt a ridiculous pang of triumph, immediately followed by panic. She turned to the counter and picked up a pencil. “If you want, I can put it aside. The map. Not your ego. That won’t fit in the drawer.” He laughed softly. “There she is.” “Who?” “The woman who keeps nearly smiling and then punishes me for noticing.” “I do not.” “You do.” “You’re projecting.” “I’m an actor. Projection is essential.” She wrote on a slip: *Vale—coast map—call when priced*. “Number?” His eyes warmed. “Forward.” “For the map.” “Devastating.” “For the map, Mr. Vale.” “Dylan.” “Mabli.” “I know. Mrs. Pritchard shouted it through the door.” “Efficient woman.” He took the pencil from her. Their fingers brushed. Barely. Stupidly. Both of them went quiet. Then he wrote his number with exaggerated neatness and slid the slip back. “For the map.” “Good.” “And perhaps,” he said, too lightly, “for the catalogue of sceptical ‘mms.’” “That’s a subscription service.” “I’m wealthy and emotionally vulnerable.” “So you’ve mentioned.” The bell over the door rattled again as a gust of wind pushed at it. The street beyond seemed emptier. Mabli lifted the blind. “They’re moving off.” Dylan put on his coat, now only mildly soggy. The sunglasses went back into his pocket rather than onto his face. At the door, he hesitated. “Thank you. For the asylum.” “It’s a shop, not a monastery.” “Bookshops are better. Fewer vows, more ghosts.” She hated how much she liked that. “Come back when you intend to buy something sensible.” He looked toward the map box. Then at her. “Define sensible.” “Not chickens.” “Shame. I was becoming attached to the idea.” “You’d be a terrible farmer.” “You don’t know that.” “You’d need every chicken to like you.” His grin flashed—then softened, caught by the truth of it. “Yes,” he said. “I probably would.” Mabli swallowed. “Goodbye, Dylan.” He opened the door to the wet slate smell of the Welsh afternoon. “Goodbye, Mabli.” The bell rang as he stepped out. After three seconds, his head reappeared around the doorframe, rain already beading in his hair. “For what it’s worth,” he said, “I’m very glad Kafka designed your bookshop.” “My grandfather.” “Give him my compliments.” “He’s dead.” “Then I’ll haunt him respectfully.” “Go away.” This time he did. Mabli stood behind the counter, listening to his footsteps fade down the street. Then she looked at the slip of paper. At the map box. At the abandoned damp patch on the floorboards. From outside came Mrs. Pritchard’s voice, scandalised and delighted. “Mabli Evans, was that Dylan Vale?” Mabli closed the archival box with care. “Just a customer,” she called. And because no one was there to see, she smiled.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional craft in nearly every aspect of creative writing. The author has created a sophisticated romantic scene that fulfills the prompt requirements while delivering genuine literary merit. The characterization is particularly strong. Dylan Vale emerges as a complex figure - charming yet insecure, famous yet lonely, with authentic vulnerability beneath his practiced persona. His need for universal approval is shown rather than stated, revealed through his reactions to Mabli's resistance to his charm. Mabli is equally well-developed: sharp-tongued, grounded in her Welsh identity, professionally competent, yet genuinely affected by Dylan despite her surface immunity. Both characters speak in distinct, consistent voices that feel natural and reveal personality. The dialogue is the story's greatest strength. Every exchange crackles with wit, subtext, and authentic rhythm. The banter feels effortless while advancing both plot and character development. Lines like "I own a television, not a cave" and "You'd need every chicken to like you" demonstrate perfect comic timing while revealing character dynamics. The conversation flows naturally between humor and deeper emotional moments without jarring transitions. The Welsh setting is expertly woven throughout - not just in place names and weather, but in Mabli's cultural references, speech patterns, and worldview. The bookshop feels lived-in and authentic, with specific details that ground the scene without overwhelming it. The romantic tension builds organically through their verbal sparring, the shared moment over the maps, and small physical details like brushing fingers. The attraction feels mutual and earned rather than forced, with both characters maintaining their essential natures while being genuinely affected by the encounter. The prose style is polished and confident, with vivid imagery ("rain hammered the narrow street hard enough to turn the slate roofs silver") and perfectly chosen details. The pacing is excellent, allowing moments to breathe while maintaining forward momentum. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the wit feels slightly too polished for completely natural speech, and Dylan's vulnerability, while well-handled, occasionally borders on being too articulate about his own psychology. However, these are minor quibbles with an otherwise exceptional piece. The ending strikes the right note - hopeful but not overstated, with both characters changed but not transformed beyond recognition. The final image of Mabli's private smile is perfectly earned. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 19 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 19 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 2 Weak Dialogue: 1 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 19 Overall Impression: 18

+ Whalepunk fantasy: The Morska Conspiracy — Score: 89.3
Prompt:
Whalepunk fantasy The Morska Conspiracy The Morska thieves guild experiences a sudden power vacuum as its leader was arrested in a raid in the early hours of the morning. Aya, a young woman born into aristocracy, has a keen interest in both the sciences and the arcane, having inherited her mother's gift for immateria magick (an outlawed practice for which her mother was, regrettably, hanged by the neck). Aya, bored with the world of aristocracy, has been spending her nights moonlighting with this very guild (masked, naturally). Leveraging her latent ability to summon dark entities from the immateria, she quickly rose through the ranks of the guild. Now, recognising the opportunity of the sudden power vacuum, Aya and her close companions Katra and Simeon, make a play for heart of the guild's claim to power: an ancient scythan artifact, under guard of spell and key in the depths of the guild hq. In contrast with the desperate chaos they had navigated thus far, the entrance to the underhold felt suddenly too quiet. Aya braces herself and reaches for the door. Write the next scene as the trio enter the underhold and find themselves confronted by none other than the guild leader herself, decidedly *un*-incarcerated, and in possession of both weapons and bodyguards. Paint a vivid picture of of the confrontation. Story beats: Aya is captured and unmasked. The trio are captured. Aya attempts to stall their demise by negotiating on her value as a hostage -- as she grapples with the inevitable consequences of her role in the guild being brought to light. Incorporate dialogue in the authentic voices of the characters. Do not write a fight scene. Lean into the gritty Victorian aesthetic with the dialogue & worldbuilding, as well as elements of gothic horror. In this world, men trust brass and oil and steel; magicka is feared and barely understood. Write in third person past tense, incorporating both Aya's internal monologue, and a heavy dialogue component. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The door to the underhold opened without protest. That, more than any lock or ward, made Aya hesitate. In Morska, doors complained. Hinges rasped like old throats. Bolts dragged through their channels with the reluctant grind of iron in winter. Even the finest townhouses along Lamprey Row, with their gas mantles and whale-bone cornices, had doors that remembered damp and salt. This one swung inward on a breath. Beyond lay a stair sinking into black masonry, each step slick with old condensation and the faint nacreous sheen of spell-lacquer. The air rose cold from below, carrying the familiar perfume of the undercity: coal smoke, whale oil, green rot from the canal vaults, and something stranger beneath it all—ozone and grave-cloth, the smell that gathered where immateria had once pressed its face against the world. Katra sniffed and made a face. “Too polite.” Simeon’s gloved hand remained inside his coat. Not on a pistol—he hated pistols, said they made fools brave—but on the little spring-knife hidden behind his ledger. In his waistcoat pocket, his watch ticked audibly in the hush. Tick. Tick. Tick. Aya felt each second touch her spine. Her mask, lacquered black with a silver mouth, clung damply to her face. Beneath it, sweat cooled along her upper lip. She had walked into debtors’ dens and opium-cutting rooms, into dockside surgeries where men paid in teeth, into séance parlours thick with amateur terror. Never had silence frightened her so efficiently. “Wards?” Simeon murmured. Aya let her vision soften. The underhold trembled with old protections. Not the clean geometry of university thaumaturgy—circles, ratios, brass needles pointing politely to heaven—but older work. Scythan. The sort that looked back. The stones held knotted shadows in their seams. Every seventh brick had been salted with bone dust. Above the arch, where any sensible guild would have placed a lantern, a dead whale’s eye had been fixed in a bronze setting. It had dried to a milky pearl, yet Aya had the distinct and nauseating impression that it blinked. “Many,” she said. “None against us.” “Comforting,” Katra muttered. She adjusted the cudgel tucked up her sleeve. “That’s usually when the floor eats a person.” They descended. The stairway narrowed as it fell. Brick gave way to carved black stone veined pale as drowned flesh. Pipes ran along the ceiling, sweating grease. Somewhere deep in the walls, machinery laboured with a slow intestinal rhythm: chuff, hiss, chuff. The guild headquarters above them—riotous, wounded, leaderless—faded until it seemed a theatrical rumour. Down here there was only the tick of Simeon’s watch and the scrape of their boots. Tick. Tick. Tick. At the bottom stood the vault door. Aya had seen diagrams. She had bribed an archivist, seduced a cipher from a dead man’s papers, and spent three sleepless nights mapping the sequence of keys required to open it. The door was a slab of black iron chased with silvered script, surrounded by three interlocking wheels of tarnished brass. At its centre was the impressed shape of a hand with too many fingers. It was already open. Katra said something filthy under her breath. Simeon went very still. “Aya.” “I see it.” “No,” he said quietly. “Listen.” The watch ticked. Tick. Another sound lay beneath it. A woman humming. The tune drifted from within the vault, soft and domestic, the sort of melody a nurse might sing to a feverish child. It threaded through the cold air and took possession of the shadows. Aya’s mouth dried. She knew that song. Everyone in the Morska underworld knew that song. Men had begged while hearing it. Constables had found corpses arranged to it. A traitor once woke in his own coffin to the tune, with a speaking tube inserted through the lid so he might hear it until the earth finished the work. Katra’s eyes widened. “No.” Simeon’s watch ticked on, indecently calm. Aya stepped through the vault door. The chamber beyond was not large, but it had been built to feel endless. Its ceiling vanished into darkness, ribbed like the inside of some leviathan swallowed and turned to stone. Blue witchlight burned in glass globes fed by copper tubes. Shelves of relics lined the walls: saint-bones wired to batteries, jars of preserved hands, harpoon heads etched with extinct alphabets, little brass engines ticking though no flame heated them. At the chamber’s far end, atop a plinth of green marble, rested the Scythan artifact. It was smaller than Aya expected. A crescent of black metal, no longer than her forearm, suspended inside a cage of whalebone and silver wire. It bent light around itself. Looking directly at it made her thoughts lose their edges. It was not a blade, not precisely, though it suggested cutting; not a crown, though it suggested obedience. Shadows clung to it with the devotion of dogs. And before it stood Madame Vey. The leader of the Morska thieves guild turned from the plinth as if greeting guests at tea. She had not been arrested. She had not been beaten in some dawn raid, wrists clapped in irons, veil torn away before jeering bobbies. She wore a fitted black walking dress buttoned to the throat, its severe lines softened only by a collar of pale fur. Her hair, iron-grey and glossy, was pinned beneath a hat adorned with three mourning feathers. At her hip hung a narrow sabre. In one hand she held a pistol of polished brass and blued steel, the barrel inlaid with mother-of-pearl. Behind her stood six bodyguards: two with carbines, one with a net-gun, one with a cudgel threaded in copper wire, and two pale, expressionless men Aya recognised as brothers from the Butcher’s Quay, their knuckles sewn with lead. Madame Vey smiled. “My darlings,” she said. “You are early.” The vault door slammed shut behind them. Katra flinched. Simeon did not, but his watch betrayed him. Tick. Tick. Tick. Aya felt the wards seal like fingers interlacing. For one mad instant she considered reaching inward—down, beyond thought, into that cold interior sea where her mother had taught her never to look too long. She felt the immateria stir in recognition, like eels beneath black water. A dozen names brushed her tongue. Little hungers. Little claws. Dark friends waiting to be invited. Then the whale’s-eye wards along the chamber walls turned toward her. Every one. Madame Vey’s smile sharpened. “I should not.” Aya raised her hands slowly. Katra swore. “The raid—” “A performance,” Madame Vey said. “And a convincing one, if the noise upstairs is any indication. I do adore panic. It reveals the shape of ambition better than any confession.” Simeon’s gaze moved from guard to guard, measuring impossibilities. “You baited the whole guild.” “I baited thieves, Mr. Vale. A profession tragically vulnerable to bait.” “You let constables take your coat,” Katra snapped. “Half of Sootstairs saw it.” “My second-best coat. Do lower your voice. This room was old when your grandmother still had milk teeth.” Aya forced herself to breathe. The mask pressed against her face. Her aristocratic life flickered obscenely through her mind: breakfast porcelain thin as shells, her father reading shipping reports beside the fire, the creak of her corset as a maid tightened it, a calling card embossed with her true name. Lady Aya Marquen. Not Rat-Crown. Not Shadegirl. Not the masked little terror whispered about in gin houses. Her throat tightened. Madame Vey lifted the pistol slightly. “Weapons down.” No one moved. The bodyguard with the net-gun cocked a lever. Brass teeth clicked. Oiled springs whined. Tick. Tick. Simeon exhaled and withdrew his hand from his coat, empty. Katra spat onto the stone, then let the cudgel slide from her sleeve. It struck the floor with a crack that echoed up into the dark ribs of the ceiling. Aya removed the little crescent-blade from her belt and set it down. “And the other,” Madame Vey said. Aya stilled. Katra glanced at her. “Other?” Madame Vey’s eyes did not leave Aya. “The one made of shadow and poor maternal judgement.” Cold passed through Aya. The immateria stirred again, offended. “I don’t know what you mean,” Aya said. “No? Then perhaps your mask will refresh our acquaintance.” Two guards came forward. Katra lunged half a step and found three barrels trained on her ribs. “Touch her and I’ll—” “You will what?” Madame Vey asked mildly. “Bleed theatrically?” Simeon caught Katra’s sleeve. His face had gone bloodless. “Not now.” Tick. Tick. Aya stood rigid as one of the Butcher’s Quay brothers seized her wrists and wrenched them behind her. Iron manacles closed, lined with something that burned cold. Salt. Silver. Whale-bone dust. Her connection to the immateria faltered as though a door had been shut on her fingers. She gasped before she could stop herself. Madame Vey heard it. Of course she did. The second brother reached for her mask. Aya turned her face away. A ridiculous gesture. Childish. Futile. The man gripped her chin with fingers smelling of tallow and tobacco, and lifted. The mask strings were cut. Lacquer left skin with a damp little kiss. Air touched her face. For a moment no one spoke. Simeon’s watch seemed thunderous. Tick. Tick. Tick. Madame Vey stared at her. Then she laughed—not loudly, not cruelly, but with genuine delight. “Oh,” she said. “Oh, my sweet black star. This is better than treason.” Katra looked between them. “Aya?” Simeon closed his eyes as if a sum had at last balanced to disaster. Madame Vey descended the shallow steps from the plinth. “Lady Aya Marquen. Daughter of Lord Halvern Marquen, owner of three foundries, two newspapers, and a seat in the Ministerial Gallery. Granddaughter of Admiral Marquen, may the sea decline to return him. And—how could we neglect it?—daughter of poor Celeste, hanged for consorting with the dark between worlds.” Aya’s cheeks burned hotter than if she had been slapped. Katra whispered, “Lady?” Aya could not look at her. All the lies she had told them, all the little evasions polished smooth by repetition, crowded behind her teeth. My people lost money. My father drank. I learned letters from a prison clerk. Harmless lies, necessary lies, lies that now unpeeled her from herself and left something soft exposed. Madame Vey reached out and touched Aya’s chin with the pistol barrel, lifting her face. “Aristocracy does breed charming hobbies.” “Madame,” Aya said, and hated the tremor in it. “Listen to that,” Vey murmured. “Finishing-school vowels, even in extremis.” Katra’s expression had hardened into something worse than anger. Hurt made private. “You told me your mum was a washerwoman.” “My mother was murdered by the Crown,” Aya said. “Your mother was hanged by lawful order,” Madame Vey corrected. “There is a difference, though I concede it matters little to the neck.” Aya pulled against the manacles. Pain flashed up her arms. “If you mean to kill us, you’d have done it.” “Would I?” “You wanted witnesses. Or leverage.” Madame Vey tilted her head. “Go on.” Aya swallowed. The chamber smelled of metal and old dead things. Her father’s face rose before her: cold, controlled, ashamed. If the guild knew, the constables would know. If the constables knew, the papers would feast. Lord Marquen’s daughter among thieves. Lord Marquen’s daughter practising the same outlawed filth that sent Celeste Marquen to the gallows. Not just ruin. Erasure. They would lock her in a private ward, call it nerves, call it hysteria, call it mercy. Or they would hang her too, if politics required an example. Tick. Tick. Tick. “I am worth more alive,” Aya said. Madame Vey smiled again, but less playfully now. “Most people say that shortly before proving the contrary.” “My father would pay.” “Your father would pay to keep you quiet, certainly. He might pay more to have you vanish.” Aya flinched. Vey saw that as well. Simeon spoke softly. “Madame, whatever game this is, you’ve won. Let Katra go. She followed us.” “Generous,” Katra snapped. “Shut up, Sim.” “I recruited them,” Aya said quickly. “The fault is mine.” “Oh, nobility,” Vey sighed. “It always enters the room overdressed.” “I can give you Lord Marquen,” Aya pressed. “His docks. His police friends. His schedules. His newspaper men. You want power? I can open doors you’ve spent twenty years picking at.” Katra stared at her. “You’d sell your own blood?” Aya finally looked at her. “He sold mine first.” Silence followed. Even Madame Vey seemed briefly pleased by that answer. Then the guild leader turned and paced back toward the artifact. Her boots clicked softly on the stone. “You misunderstand the evening, Lady Marquen. This was never about a vacancy. There is no vacancy. This was about discovering who among my children had begun to mistake hunger for destiny.” Her gaze passed over Katra, Simeon, then returned to Aya. “And what a discovery. A noble thief. An unlicensed witch. A grieving daughter with access to Parliament’s breakfast table.” Aya’s pulse beat in her throat. “Then use me.” “Oh, I intend to.” The words were soft enough to be a caress. Simeon’s watch ticked on. Tick. Tick. Tick. Aya imagined each second being recorded somewhere in brass and enamel, neat and merciless, while her life changed shape beyond repair. “What of them?” she asked. Madame Vey looked almost bored. “That depends upon how valuable you become in the next five minutes.” Katra barked a laugh without humour. “Hear that? Aristocracy at last trickles down.” Aya shut her eyes briefly. Shame pressed harder than fear. “I lied,” she said. “No,” Katra said. “You think?” “I lied because I needed—” “To play gutter,” Katra cut in. “To wear hunger like a carnival mask.” Aya had no answer. Not one that would not sound like another kind of theft. Madame Vey raised one hand. The guards tightened their grips. “Enough. Sentiment makes my teeth ache.” She stepped close once more, studying Aya’s bare face in the blue light. Behind her, the Scythan crescent drank the glow, black and patient. “You wished to take the heart of my power,” Vey said. “Instead, my dear, you shall become an artery.” Aya’s mouth tasted of iron. “And if I refuse?” Madame Vey’s expression softened, becoming almost maternal. “Then I shall send your friends to the canals in parcels, deliver your mask to your father with a note in your own hand, and inform the Crown that Celeste Marquen’s little prodigy has been opening doors in the dark.” The immateria trembled beyond the manacles, distant but listening. Aya held herself very still. Simeon’s watch ticked. Tick. Tick. Tick. Madame Vey leaned in until only Aya could hear her next words. “Now, Lady Marquen,” she whispered, “let us discuss what you are worth.”
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical craft and atmospheric worldbuilding within the "whalepunk" fantasy setting. The author successfully creates a vivid Victorian-gothic atmosphere through detailed descriptions of the underworld environment, incorporating whale-bone architecture, brass machinery, and occult elements that feel authentically integrated into the world. The writing excels in several key areas. The prose is sophisticated without becoming overwrought, maintaining clarity while building tension through sensory details and pacing devices like Simeon's ticking watch. The dialogue feels authentic to the Victorian setting while remaining accessible, with each character having a distinct voice - Madame Vey's cultured menace, Katra's street-smart directness, and Aya's aristocratic undertones bleeding through her attempted disguise. Character development is particularly strong. Aya's internal conflict between her aristocratic background and chosen criminal identity creates genuine emotional complexity. The revelation of her true identity feels earned rather than contrived, and her companions' reactions - especially Katra's sense of betrayal - ring true. Madame Vey emerges as a compelling antagonist, sophisticated and calculating rather than merely threatening. The author adheres closely to the prompt's requirements, incorporating all requested story beats naturally. The confrontation unfolds without resorting to action sequences, instead building tension through dialogue and psychological maneuvering. The gothic horror elements are woven throughout rather than forced, from the whale's eye wards to the Scythan artifact's reality-bending properties. Some minor weaknesses appear in occasional repetitive phrasing and a few moments where the atmospheric description slightly slows narrative momentum. However, these are minimal compared to the piece's strengths. The ending maintains appropriate darkness without feeling gratuitously grim, setting up future conflict while resolving the immediate scene's tension. The worldbuilding details feel lived-in rather than exposition-heavy, from the specific geography of "Lamprey Row" to the social dynamics between aristocracy and the criminal underworld. The magic system's integration with the setting's industrial elements creates an authentic sense of a world where technology and mysticism uneasily coexist. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 19 Elegant Prose: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 18 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18
