+ Historical fiction: Gladiator — Score: 88.8
Prompt:
Historical Fiction: Write a scene from a story set during the height of the Roman Empire, focusing on a slice of a day in the life of a gladiator. Do not write a combat scene. Use sensory details to capture the sights, sounds, and smells of ancient Rome. Explore the gladiator's thoughts and emotions. The story should also touch on the larger political and social issues of the time period. The piece should feel like a slice of a larger story. First person, past tense, 1000 words.
Model Output:
By the time the first light touched the upper seats of the amphitheater, the city had already begun to sweat. Rome never truly slept, not in those days, not while the emperors still fed it with grain from Africa and gold from everywhere else. Even before dawn, I could hear the wheels of carts grinding over the paving stones beyond the walls of the ludus, the curses of drivers, the bray of a mule, the thin cry of some woman selling hot chickpeas in a street I could not see. The air that came through the high barred window of our cell carried the stink of the Subura—smoke, urine, frying oil, damp wool, old fish, and beneath it all the sour human smell of too many people pressed into too little space. I lay awake on my pallet, staring at a crack in the plaster above me. A fly crawled along it. Slow, patient, free. Beside me, Dama snored with his mouth open, one arm thrown across his chest as if he still held a shield in sleep. He was from Thrace, though the lanista had given him a Latin name because Roman crowds liked names they could shout without twisting their tongues. On the other side of the cell, little Felix muttered in a dream. He had been a baker’s son before debts swallowed his family. He had soft hands when he arrived. They were not soft now. A bell rang in the yard. Dama’s eyes opened at once. No one had to tell us what day it was. The whole ludus knew. You felt it in the way the guards walked, in the way the trainers sharpened their voices, in the way the kitchen slaves avoided meeting our eyes when they brought the morning mash. There were games at the Flavian Amphitheater, great games in honor of the emperor’s return from the north. He had not conquered anything anyone could find on a map, but the city had been told he had brought peace to the frontier, and Rome loved peace best when it arrived wearing a helmet. I sat up, and my back answered with a line of old pain. “You slept?” Dama asked. “A little.” “Liar.” He grinned, showing the gap where a tooth had been knocked loose in training. It had healed badly. Most things did. The door opened. A slave came in carrying a wooden bowl of barley porridge and beans, thick as mortar and just as grey. The food of gladiators, they called it—hordearii, barley men. It made us heavy, made a cushion of flesh against cuts. The nobles who sat on marble benches and perfumed their hands liked to say we ate like animals. I had seen animals fed better. Still, I ate. I ate every spoonful. The porridge stuck to my teeth and sat warm in my belly. There was garlic in it, too much salt, and a film of olive oil glistening on top. My hands trembled only once, when I raised the bowl. I hid it by scratching my beard. In the yard, the others were gathering. The morning sun spilled over the training posts, turning the sand pale gold. The palus stood in rows, scarred from years of wooden swords. Beyond them rose the walls, high and whitewashed, topped by men with spears who watched us as if we were prized dogs likely to bite. Perhaps we were. Our doctore, old Afer, limped across the sand with his vine staff in hand. He had bought his freedom with blood twenty years before and wore it like a badly mended cloak. His face was a map of cuts, one eye clouded, his nose bent sideways. He missed nothing. “Stretch,” he barked. “Loose shoulders. Loose wrists. You go stiff into the arena, they carry you out stiff.” A few men laughed. No one laughed loudly. We moved through the exercises. Bend, twist, lift, breathe. The wood of the practice sword was smooth against my palm, darkened with sweat from hundreds of mornings. My muscles warmed, and for a while fear became something I could order about. Raise the arm. Turn the hip. Step. Breathe. The body obeyed even when the mind wandered. Mine wandered to the city outside. Rome had been restless for weeks. Grain ships had been late in Ostia, delayed by storms or by officials with greedy hands—there was always a reason, and always some man in a clean toga to explain why children went hungry. In the taverns, people muttered about the price of bread. In the alleys, they cursed the emperor softly, then cheered him loudly when soldiers passed. The games were meant to sweeten them. Meat thrown to wolves. Coins thrown to crowds. Men thrown to men. I had once believed Rome was a city. Later I understood it was a mouth. After drills, we were taken to the baths attached to the ludus, not the grand public baths where citizens gossiped under painted ceilings, but a small steamy chamber with cracked tiles and drains that stank of old water. I scraped my skin with a bronze strigil while a boy poured oil over my shoulders. The oil smelled of olives and bitter herbs. It caught in the ridges of scars across my chest. The boy was new. His hands moved carefully, afraid of hurting me or afraid I might hurt him. “What is your name?” I asked. He startled. “Lucius.” “A good Roman name.” He glanced up. He was dark-eyed, eastern perhaps, Syrian or Judean. “It was given to me.” “Many names are.” He lowered his gaze and continued working the oil into my arm. On my left forearm, just below the elbow, I had a mark not made by any blade: three little dots of blue ink. I touched them before every fight. My mother had made them with a fish bone and soot when I was seven, before the soldiers came to our village near the Danube. Three dots for the three household spirits, she had said, though we were too poor for a proper shrine. Touch them, spit once, and death will pass by your door. Death had found her door easily enough. Still, before every fight, I touched the dots. Then I spat into the sand behind my left heel. Not before the trainers, if I could help it. Romans laughed at barbarian charms, while wearing amulets shaped like fists and little bronze phalluses around their own necks. A man mocked another man’s gods only when he had forgotten how frightened he was of his own. At midday, we were given wine watered thin and bread with cheese. From the holding room beneath the amphitheater, the roar of the crowd reached us as a living thing. It came through the stone in waves. First the hunts, then the executions. We could tell by the sound. A cheer had many shapes. There was the eager cheer, the disgusted cheer, the disappointed groan when an animal refused to perform as expected. Sometimes, between the boards overhead, dust drifted down with each stamping of thousands of feet. I sat with my back against the wall and held the piece of bread without eating it. Above us, senators reclined in their white-bordered togas, pretending not to watch too closely. Merchants wiped grease from their fingers and wagered on our blood. Women leaned forward with painted lips. The poor crammed into the upper tiers, baked by the sun, louder than all the rest because noise cost nothing. Slaves stood behind their masters and saw men in chains below them become famous for an afternoon. That was Rome’s genius. It taught every man to look down on someone. Dama sat beside me, rolling a knucklebone between his fingers. “If I win today,” he said, “my patron says he will petition for my rudis.” “You believe him?” “No.” We both smiled. Freedom was a wooden sword, a ceremony, a word spoken by a man who owned you. Some received it. Enough to keep the rest of us dreaming. I had seen free men return to the arena because freedom did not buy bread, because applause was a drug, because outside the ludus a scarred foreigner with no trade was only another hungry mouth in an empire full of them. “What would you do?” Dama asked. “If I were free?” “Yes.” I looked toward the passage where daylight made a blade on the floor. “I would go to the river,” I said. “Which river?” I did not answer. The Danube of my childhood had become less a place than a smell—wet reeds, mud, smoke from pinewood fires. I remembered my sister laughing with her feet in the water. I remembered horses. I remembered snow. But memory was a poor map. Rome had taken me so far from home that even longing had lost its road. A horn sounded above. The room changed. Men stood. Jokes died. The attendants came with armor: helmets with crests, greaves, arm guards, belts, shields. Bronze and iron clattered in the dimness. Sweat thickened the air. Someone vomited into a corner. Someone else prayed to Nemesis, whispering fiercely into his clasped hands. The goddess had a little shrine near the gate, blackened by lamp smoke and crowded with offerings—coins, bits of ribbon, a broken comb, a child’s carved horse. Afer came to me himself. He checked the straps on my manica, tugging hard enough to make me grunt. “You are thinking too much,” he said. “I was saving my stupidity for the sand.” His ruined mouth twitched. “Good. Give them a show, but not your soul.” “I sold that already.” “No,” he said quietly, and for a moment the noise of the amphitheater seemed farther away. “They only rent it.” He moved on before I could answer. When they called my name—Marcus Varro, though that was not the name my mother had spoken over my cradle—I rose. The helmet waited under my arm, smelling of metal, leather, and the ghost-sweat of every man who had worn it before. My mouth had gone dry. My heart beat not fast but deep, like a fist on a door. At the foot of the ramp, I paused beside the small shrine to Nemesis. The lamp flame shook in the draft from the arena entrance. I touched the three blue dots on my arm. Once. Twice. Three times. Then I bent my head as if adjusting my sandal and spat behind my left heel. Death will pass by your door. The roar above swelled as another pair left the sand, one walking, one carried. I did not look. Looking made men imagine. Imagination was sharper than any blade. A guard lifted the gate bolt. Light struck the tunnel, hot and white, full of dust. I smelled sun-baked sand, blood washed poorly away, crushed flowers thrown from the stands, cheap wine, roasted sausages, perfume, dung, fear. Rome’s breath. For a heartbeat, I stood between the dark and the brightness, neither slave nor hero, neither dead nor alive. Somewhere above, the emperor sat beneath an awning of purple, feeding the city spectacle while petitioners waited outside his palace and soldiers guarded granaries by the Tiber. Somewhere beyond the walls, provinces bent under taxes, veterans begged for land, and mothers taught children the names of gods who might not save them. Then the herald shouted, and the crowd took up the name that was not mine. “Varro! Varro! Varro!” I put on my helmet. The world narrowed to bronze and breath. And I stepped forward.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This is a sophisticated piece of historical fiction that demonstrates strong craft and deep understanding of both the Roman period and effective storytelling techniques. The author successfully creates an immersive slice-of-life narrative that avoids the obvious gladiator combat scene while still building tension toward the arena. The first-person narration is consistently maintained with an authentic voice that feels appropriately weighted with experience and resignation. The protagonist emerges as a fully realized character - a former barbarian from the Danube region who has been shaped by years of captivity and forced combat. His observations reveal intelligence, cultural awareness, and a complex relationship with his circumstances that goes beyond simple victimhood or heroism. The sensory details are particularly strong, creating a vivid sense of place through smell, sound, and texture. The description of Rome's morning sounds, the stench of the Subura, the feel of practice weapons, and the atmosphere of the ludus all contribute to authentic world-building. The author demonstrates solid research into Roman social structures, gladiatorial life, and period details without letting the research overwhelm the narrative. The prose style is elegant and controlled, with varied sentence structures that create good rhythm. Metaphorical language is used effectively - "Rome was a mouth" and "imagination was sharper than any blade" - without becoming overwrought. The writing maintains literary quality while remaining accessible. The piece successfully incorporates larger political and social themes through the protagonist's observations about grain shortages, social hierarchies, and the function of games as political control. The line "That was Rome's genius. It taught every man to look down on someone" encapsulates the social commentary effectively. Character interactions feel natural, particularly the exchanges with Dama and the brief encounter with the slave boy Lucius. The dialogue serves multiple purposes - revealing character, advancing the narrative, and providing exposition without feeling forced. The structure builds tension effectively toward the arena entrance while maintaining focus on the internal experience. The ending provides a natural stopping point that feels like part of a larger story, as requested. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the philosophical observations feel slightly modern in sensibility, and a few instances where the prose approaches being overly literary, though it generally stays within appropriate bounds. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 19 Elegant Prose: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 18.0, Nuanced Characters: 18.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 19.0, Elegant Prose: 18.0, Emotionally Engaging: 17.0, Emotionally Complex: 17.0, Coherent: 19.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 2.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 4.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4.0, Amateurish: 2.0, Purple Prose: 4.0, Overwrought: 3.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1.0, Unearned Transformations: 1.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 18.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 18.0, Overall Impression: 18.0
+ Epistolary: The Lighthouse Keeper — Score: 87.2
Prompt:
The Lighthouse Keeper Epistolary Narrative: Write a story told entirely through a series of brief correspondences: telegrams or letters or emails between two characters. The correspondence should span several months or years, and reveal a gradually unfolding plot. Use distinct voices for each character, and include details that provide insight into their personalities and motivations. The story should build to an emotional climax, and the final letter should provide a satisfying resolution. The setting is a lighthouse keeper writing to his mother. He is working class and Scottish. He is struggling with the isolation of his posting. Write naturally and without cliches. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**3 March 1978** Bell Rock Lighthouse Dear Mam, Got here with the tide after a crossing that shook the teeth in my head. The tender left me and old MacPhee on the landing, then went off small as a matchbox. You’d have laughed to see your son clinging to the ladder like a bairn on a climbing frame. There’s no land to be seen when the mist sits low, only the tower, the sea, and the gulls giving us pelters for trespassing. The quarters are clean enough. My bunk is narrow but I’ve had worse. MacPhee says I’ll get used to the noise. He means the sea hitting the stone, night and day, like furniture being dragged about by giants. I’m not sure a body is meant to get used to that. Don’t fret. I’ve my heavy socks, the blue gansey, and the tin of shortbread you hid in the kitbag. I found it before MacPhee did. Your loving son, Ewan **8 March 1978** Arbroath Ewan, You always did find the sweet thing first. I’m glad of it. The house is quiet without your boots by the back door, though your father’s chair still creaks as if he’s in it, and I tell it to hush itself. I walked to the harbour yesterday and saw the supply boat go out. I waved, though there was no chance you could see me. Mrs Kerr says her cousin’s lad lasted only six weeks at the Northern light and came home wild-eyed, but Mrs Kerr has never known when to keep a story in her own mouth. Eat properly. Mend your socks before the holes grow. Do not let an old man called MacPhee talk you into cards for money. Your Mam **19 March 1978** Bell Rock Lighthouse Mam, MacPhee doesn’t play cards. He reads shipping notices and says “hmm” in twelve different ways. There’s a younger fellow due next month, Innes, so it’ll be three of us. Relief is meant every four weeks, weather allowing. Those two words do a lot of lifting out here. My watches are settled now. Lamp checks, pressure, lens, fog signal if needed, logbook every hour. Brass to polish till you can see your sins in it. The light itself is a grand thing, Mam. When the lamp turns, the lenses catch it and fling it out in white bands. It would put the kirk windows to shame. The hardship is the water. We’ve tanks below, but every drop is counted. Washing is a basin and a rag. My hands smell of paraffin and salt. I dreamed last night I was standing under the tap in your scullery, letting it run over my head. Woke up thirsty enough to drink the ink. Ewan **2 April 1978** Arbroath My dear boy, I have put a bottle of blackcurrant cordial in the parcel, also oatcakes, tobacco for MacPhee though you’ll tell him it is from me and not from you. Men can be proud about nonsense. Your sister came by with wee Morag. The child has two teeth now and bit my thumb like a terrier. We spoke of you. Morag clapped when I said your name, but that may have been for the spoon. When you dream of the tap, remember the bill it gives me. That will cure you. Love, Mam **27 April 1978** Bell Rock Lighthouse Mam, Relief delayed. Southerly gale. It’s not dangerous in the way folk imagine, with waves swallowing the tower whole, though some strike high enough to rattle the windows. It’s the waiting. The sea says no and that’s the end of it. Innes arrived before the weather turned. He’s from Peterhead, all elbows and noise, sings while he works, badly. I disliked him for two days and now I’d fight a man for him. Funny how a voice becomes furniture. Had a job with the landing crane yesterday. A crate swung loose and smashed the rail by the door. We had to haul the paraffin drums by hand, three stories in a blow, boots slipping, rope burning the palms. MacPhee called us all manner of idiots but he was first to take the weight. My shoulder is stiff, nothing worse. I did not tell you that to worry you. Only there’s not much else to tell, and I’m tired of writing, “I am fine,” like it is a prayer. Ewan **5 May 1978** Arbroath Ewan, I would rather have the truth with a stiff shoulder than “fine” tied in a bow. Your father used to say that word from his sickbed, and I hated it more than the coughing. The cordial was refused at the post office for weight, so it will come later. I argued till Mr Bell lost colour in his face. You should have seen it. Do you remember when you were ten and got shut in the coal shed? You swore you had been calm, but the scratches on the door told a different tale. You were never made for small spaces. Yet here you are, choosing a tower. There must be a reason bigger than wages. Tell me it, if you can. Mam **17 May 1978** Bell Rock Lighthouse Mam, You ask hard questions for the price of a stamp. The reason is partly wages, don’t dress it up. The yard was laying off and I was sick of standing with other men pretending not to be frightened. Here the money is steady, and there’s a pension if I keep at it. But there’s more. After Dad died, the house felt full of things unsaid. Folk kept touching my sleeve, telling me I was the man now. I wanted somewhere no one needed anything from me but work. A light either shows or it doesn’t. No speeches. That sounds cruel. I don’t mean it cruel. Last night we had fog thick as wool. Fog horn every two minutes. It gets into your bones. I stood watch from midnight to four, writing entries by lamplight, and I thought I heard Dad clear his throat behind me. Turned so fast I knocked the mug over. MacPhee says sound plays tricks in fog. He said it kindly, which was worse. Ewan **29 May 1978** Arbroath Ewan, It is not cruel to be tired. It is only dangerous when a man mistakes tiredness for the shape of his whole life. Your father was proud of you, though he said it sideways. “That boy’s got hands,” he told Mr Forbes once, watching you fix the gate. “He doesn’t make a song of a thing.” That was praise from him, near enough poetry. If you hear him clear his throat, tell him I have moved his fishing rods to the loft and he can complain all he likes. I need nothing from you but letters. Not money, not man-of-the-house nonsense. Just ink on paper, so I know the sea has not swallowed your voice. Mam **14 July 1978** Bell Rock Lighthouse Mam, You’ll have heard about the storms. We’re safe. The wireless is damaged but not dead. Supply was missed again, and we’re rationing fresh food. Tinned stew breeds a hatred in a man. Innes says he will marry the first potato he sees. The real trouble is the lantern room. Salt got in past a cracked pane during the last blow. We were up half the night cleaning the lens. If the salt dries, it leaves a skin that scatters the light. MacPhee made me go over each prism with chamois and spirit, gentle as washing a baby’s face. Outside the whole tower shook. Inside, there was only our breathing and the soft squeak of cloth on glass. At three, Innes slipped on the stair and split his brow. Blood everywhere, though the cut was small. He laughed at first, then cried because he could not stop shaking. I sat with him till dawn. He asked if I ever spoke to myself. I said no, because it was easier than saying all day. Ewan **22 July 1978** Arbroath My dear Ewan, Speak to yourself if you must. Speak to the kettle, the gulls, the blessed lens. I speak to your father’s photograph when the clock is too loud. There is no shame in giving words somewhere to go. I am enclosing a notebook. Use it for your log if you like, or for rubbish, or for the names you are calling tinned stew. We are having a hot spell here. Children are jumping from the pier though they are told not to. Morag can stand if she holds the table. She has your frown when concentrating, poor lamb. Come home on next relief and I’ll make mince and tatties, no tins in sight. Mam **9 September 1978** Bell Rock Lighthouse Mam, I was home and gone too quick. I keep thinking of you at the station, waving with one hand and holding your hat with the other. I should have hugged you longer. There, I’ve written it, so you may frame it and use it against me. MacPhee has retired sudden. Doctor’s orders, chest. We’ve a new principal coming after Christmas, but till then it’s me and Innes doing extra. I am senior now, which is a joke the sea has not yet understood. The notebook helps. Mostly lists: wick trimming, oil levels, weather. Some lines about the sea. Not poems. Don’t start. There was a trawler last night, too close in dirty weather. We signalled and sounded, but she seemed blind. For ten minutes I thought we’d watch men die on our stones. Then her lights swung off, slow as a door closing. I had to sit down after. My knees went useless. This work is not only keeping a lamp lit. It is standing in the dark telling strangers, not here, not here, not here. Ewan **18 September 1978** Arbroath Ewan, I have framed nothing, but I read the hugging line twice. You say “not poems” with the same tone your father used when he said the garden was “not much,” after spending six hours on it. I know a poem when it tracks mud through my kitchen. Mrs Kerr asked if you were lonely out there. I said, “Of course he is. He is not made of rope.” She looked startled. Good. Your sister wants you for Christmas if relief allows. I want you whenever the sea is finished borrowing you. Mam **31 December 1978** Bell Rock Lighthouse Mam, New Year’s Eve. No relief. There’s ice on the outside rail, and the wind carries spray hard enough to sting through wool. Innes and I had a feast: sausages from the last parcel, one dented tin of peaches, and your black bun. We saved it like church silver. At midnight we’ll show the same light as every other night. That seems right. Folk on shore will sing and kiss and make fools of themselves, and we’ll keep turning. I’ve been low, Mam. Lower than I wrote. Some mornings I wake and cannot bear the thought of the same stair, same walls wet with breath, same grey water climbing at the windows. I find myself angry at small things—the spoon left unwashed, Innes humming, my own boots. Then ashamed. Yesterday I took the notebook and wrote every sound I could hear for an hour. Sea. Clock. Flame. Innes snoring. My heart. It passed. Don’t worry yourself sick. I’m telling you because you asked for truth. Your son, Ewan **7 January 1979** Arbroath Ewan, I am worried, but not sick. There is a difference, and I intend to remain useful. When the walls press in, put your feet flat. Name what is real. Stair. Rail. Oil. Breath. You are not the feeling; you are the man feeling it. I learned that after your father died, though no one gave it a grand name. I have spoken to Mr Bell about quicker mail when the boats go. He sighed as if I had asked him to move Norway, then agreed to try. Come home at first chance. If you cannot, write on the bad days especially. A page can be a door. Mam **16 February 1979** Bell Rock Lighthouse Mam, I have news. During last week’s gale, the fog signal failed. Air line frozen, then a valve cracked. Visibility near nothing, and we knew vessels were running by ear as much as light. Innes was sick with fever, useless through no fault of his. I had to go out to the gallery alone and clear ice from the intake. It was the worst half hour I’ve known. The wind pinned me to the rail. Spray froze on my sleeves. I worked the spanner with fingers I couldn’t feel, cursing so hard God may have taken notes. Twice I thought I’d lose the tool. Once I thought I’d lose myself. When the horn sounded again, it came out like some wounded bull, ugly and perfect. I laughed. Not brave laughter. Half mad, probably. After, I made tea and found I was not shaking. That frightened me more than the wind. Then Innes, pale as paper, said, “Well done, keeper,” and I near wept into the pot. Keeper. Imagine. Ewan **25 February 1979** Arbroath My dearest son, I imagined it. I imagined your red hands on the spanner and your jaw set like when you were small and would not let a knot beat you. I am proud in a way that hurts my ribs. But hear me: no light is worth your life. I know you will say that lives depend on it. I know. Still, yours is not spare. Morag says “Ew” now, which may be you or may be her opinion of porridge. Your sister is expecting again. The world keeps adding people, careless as scattering seed. I have put new gloves in the parcel. Do not argue about the cost. Mam **12 April 1979** Bell Rock Lighthouse Mam, Relief came, and with it the new principal, Mr Calder. He is tidy, strict, and has eyebrows like two damp brushes. He read my logs and said they were thorough. From him, that may be a love song. I’m ashore next week for ten days. I want to come home, but I also need to tell you something before I stand in your kitchen and lose the nerve. I’ve applied for transfer to the harbour light at Macduff when a post opens. Smaller, on land, still useful. Less pay, maybe. Less sea. I thought choosing it meant failing. Now I think staying only to prove I can would be a worse kind of pride. The tower has taught me plenty. It has also taken plenty. I can do the work. That’s no longer the question. If you’re disappointed, keep it till after mince and tatties. Ewan **18 April 1979** Arbroath Ewan, Disappointed? You daft article. I did not raise you to be a monument for waves to batter. If you have learned the difference between duty and punishment, then the tower has done one kindness at least. Come home. Your bed is made, though the room is full of Morag’s wooden animals because she has claimed it for a farm. We will negotiate with her. There will be mince and tatties. There will be hot water enough to make you believe in mercy. Mam **3 November 1979** Macduff Harbour Light Dear Mam, First night in the new post. I can see houses from the window. Houses, Mam. Chimneys, washing lines, a dog that has barked at three separate gulls and lost every argument. The light is modest, fixed red over the harbour mouth, but it does its job. Fishing boats come in under it, engines thumping, men shouting about prices and weather and who owes who a pint. I walked to the shop after supper because I could. Bought milk, bread, and a newspaper on the wrong day. The woman behind the counter asked if I was the new lightman. Lightman, not keeper. I said aye. It sat well enough. I won’t pretend I’m cured of all dark corners. Last night, in the room they’ve given me, the quiet after the boats tied up felt strange, and I woke reaching for the logbook. But the sea stayed outside. That is a fine arrangement. I’ve kept the notebook. On the first page after Bell Rock I wrote: “Home is not the place that needs you most. It is the place you are allowed to need.” That may be a poem. Say nothing. I’ll be down Sunday, if the bus behaves. Tell Morag I’m bringing a wooden seal for her farm, though seals are poor at farming. Your loving son, Ewan
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This epistolary narrative successfully fulfills the prompt requirements, presenting a compelling story through letters between Ewan, a Scottish lighthouse keeper, and his mother over nearly two years. The piece demonstrates strong technical execution and emotional depth. The characterization is particularly well-crafted. Ewan's voice authentically captures a working-class Scottish dialect without resorting to heavy-handed phonetic spelling or stereotypes. His evolution from initial bravado ("clinging to the ladder like a bairn") to honest vulnerability ("I've been low, Mam. Lower than I wrote") feels genuine and earned. The mother's voice provides perfect counterpoint - practical, loving, and wise without being saccharine. Her line "You are not the feeling; you are the man feeling it" demonstrates sophisticated emotional intelligence. The prose quality is consistently high, with memorable imagery that avoids purple prose while remaining evocative. Descriptions like "the sea hitting the stone, night and day, like furniture being dragged about by giants" and "fog thick as wool" create vivid sensory experiences. The writing maintains an appropriate register throughout - literary enough to be engaging while remaining true to the characters' voices. The story structure builds tension effectively through Ewan's increasing isolation and psychological strain, culminating in the fog signal repair scene that serves as both literal and metaphorical climax. His decision to transfer to a land-based position feels like genuine character growth rather than an easy resolution. The emotional complexity is noteworthy - the piece explores themes of duty, masculinity, grief, and family bonds without oversimplifying. Ewan's struggle with his father's death and his role as "the man now" adds psychological depth beyond the surface narrative of lighthouse isolation. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the prose becomes slightly self-conscious about its literary quality, and some metaphors that, while effective, border on being too polished for the character's voice. The timeline occasionally feels compressed given the emotional journey depicted. The ending strikes an appropriate balance - hopeful without being unrealistically positive, acknowledging ongoing struggles while showing genuine progress. The final letter's tone of cautious optimism feels earned through the preceding emotional work. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 19 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 18 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 18.0, Nuanced Characters: 19.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17.0, Elegant Prose: 17.0, Emotionally Engaging: 18.0, Emotionally Complex: 18.0, Coherent: 19.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 2.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 4.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5.0, Amateurish: 3.0, Purple Prose: 4.0, Overwrought: 3.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 3.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 18.0, Overall Impression: 18.0
+ First-person unique perspective: Hacker Mindset — Score: 89.5
Prompt:
Hacker Mindset Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of a reformed (now) white hat hacker who cannot help but notice exploitable weaknesses in both systems and people they encounter. They habitually look for vulnerabilities and exploit vectors. The narrative takes place during a first date at a restaurant, where they're trying to focus on human connection. It's going surprisingly well due to a discovered shared nerd-fandom (magic the gathering). Things go a little sideways when the date reveals a noticeably insecure phone unlock pattern, which protagonist can't help but comment on. They are then left to negotiate the fallout of this faux pas and attempt to salvage (or perhaps sabotage) the remainder of the date. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I promised myself I wouldn’t do recon. That was the first rule I made in the rideshare on the way over, along with “don’t mention work unless asked” and “do not, under any circumstances, use the phrase attack surface in a romantic context.” Three rules. Simple. Elegant. Very breakable. The restaurant had glass doors, soft lighting, and a host stand positioned so badly that anyone waiting could glance down at the reservation tablet whenever the hostess leaned away. I noticed the tablet before I noticed the hostess. Four-digit passcode. Smudge pattern heavy on the lower right. Probably 2580 or 5689. The point-of-sale terminal near the bar still had the default admin sticker half-peeled from the side. The Wi-Fi network was called Gusto_Guest, which was fine, and Gusto_Staff, which was less fine, because the SSID was not hidden and the bartender was writing something on a pad that might have been a password or might have been a phone number. I looked at the floor. Dates like eye contact. Floors do not. My date was already there. She sat in the corner booth with a paperback open beside her water glass, hair pinned up with a pen, one boot tucked under the opposite knee like she’d selected the posture from a menu titled Comfortable But Ready To Flee. She looked up when I said her name. “Mara?” Her smile hit me with the force of an unpatched zero-day. “Evan,” she said. “Hi.” Handshake? Hug? Human protocols are poorly documented and culturally inconsistent. I went for the brief half-hug after detecting forward movement, and we managed not to collide foreheads, which I counted as a successful negotiation. She smelled faintly like rain and vanilla. Her earrings were tiny silver dragons. Do not perform inventory, I told myself. Do not classify jewelry as data. “Sorry if I’m early,” she said. “I get nervous and overcorrect.” “Same,” I said, sitting across from her. “Except I call it threat modeling.” Her eyebrow lifted. Abort. Abort. Rule three. “I mean planning,” I said. “I call it planning.” She laughed. Not politely, either. Actual laugh. Warm. “Threat modeling is planning for pessimists.” I blinked. “That’s… accurate.” “I work in archives,” she said. “Everything is either on fire, about to be on fire, or stored next to something that once caught fire.” Good. She understood entropy. We ordered drinks. She got ginger beer. I got water because alcohol lowers inhibition and inhibition was the only thing standing between me and explaining why the QR code menu was a phishing opportunity with garnish. Conversation began cautiously, like two people probing a network perimeter with permission but not yet credentials. Jobs. Neighborhoods. How long we’d lived in the city. Whether the new transit app was terrible or only morally corrupt. I kept catching myself mapping her answers: careful with money but generous with friends, organized but not rigid, funny in a dry way that left delayed charges under the ribs. Then the waiter mentioned a special called “the Planeswalker platter,” which was apparently a roasted vegetable board with branding aspirations, and Mara groaned. “Please tell me this restaurant is not trying to theme tapas around Magic.” My head snapped up so fast I almost pulled something. “You play?” She narrowed her eyes in mock suspicion. “Define play.” “Kitchen table? Commander? Draft? Arena? Competitive?” “Commander, mostly. Some draft if I’m feeling financially irresponsible.” Oh no. Oh no, she was perfect. I felt my entire internal monitoring system shift priorities. Restaurant vulnerabilities receded. Human connection moved to top of queue. “What deck?” I asked, too quickly. “Which one?” Excellent answer. She took a sip of ginger beer. “My favorite is Muldrotha.” Mental catalog entry one: Muldrotha, the Gravetide. Graveyard recursion. Values inevitability. Enjoys reusing resources, probably patient, probably comfortable with mess if the mess is useful. Also potentially the kind of person who says “it’s not dead, it’s value.” I tried to keep my face normal. “Respectable.” “You say that like you’re deciding whether to report me.” “I would never report a Muldrotha player on a first offense.” “First offense?” She grinned. “I run Spore Frog.” Catalog entry two: Spore Frog. Soft lock tendencies. Not just recursion—prevention loop. She enjoys making combat irrelevant. Defensive control through small, irritating creatures. Capable of smiling while nobody else gets to attack. I should have been alarmed. Instead, my brain made a little heart-shaped notification. “Of course you do,” I said. “And Seal of Primordium.” Entry three: Seal of Primordium. Preloaded answer. Likes visible deterrents. Enjoys opponents making bad decisions under open threat. That’s not a card choice; that’s a worldview. The waiter returned. We ordered. She chose mushroom risotto. I chose salmon after noticing the chicken entrée was listed twice with different prices on two separate menu pages, which was either careless design or a social experiment. I did not mention it. Growth. “So what do you play?” she asked. “Depends.” “That’s what cowards say before admitting they own a Tergrid deck.” “I do not own a Tergrid deck.” “But?” “I own the pieces.” She pointed her fork at me even though food hadn’t arrived yet. “That is worse.” “I’m reformed.” “From discard?” “In several senses.” She waited. There it was: the work opening. The invitation. A reasonably normal person would say, I work in cybersecurity. I used to be less ethical about it, now I help companies fix things. Simple. Sanitized. “I do security consulting now,” I said. “Penetration testing, audits, incident response. Before that I was… less careful with permission.” Her expression didn’t close. That surprised me. “Black hat?” she asked. “Gray for a while. Which is what black hats call themselves when they want to sleep.” “And now?” “White hat. Contracts. Scope documents. Boring reports. Meetings where I explain that ‘password123!’ is not a compliance strategy.” She laughed again. Good. Still connected. “What made you switch?” A thousand possible answers. Fear. Burnout. A friend getting raided. The sickening realization that being clever was not the same as being right. The first time I watched a hospital scramble because some idiot crew decided ransomware was just numbers on a screen. “Consequences became people,” I said. Her face softened. Too much honesty? No. Acceptable packet. Received. Food arrived. She told me about her archive’s basement humidity problem, which sounded like environmental horror. I told her about a client who stored production credentials in a public GitHub repo named Definitely_Not_Prod, which made her cover her face with both hands. The date was going well. Not “surviving” well. Actually well. Then she said, “My other Commander deck is Feather.” Catalog entry four: Feather, the Redeemed. Boros spellslinger. Protection tricks. Combat math. Recurring targeted instants. She likes doing one thing repeatedly with elegance. Also likes proving that red-white is not just turning creatures sideways while yelling. “With Sunforger?” I asked. “Obviously.” Entry five: Sunforger. Toolbox access. Tactical flexibility. Keeps answers hidden in the deck but reachable. Enjoys the psychological pressure of open mana and equipment. Dangerous. “And Defiant Strike, Gods Willing, Shelter…” Entries six through eight: cantrip aggression, protection, protection with card draw. She values resilience. She commits to a creature and refuses to let the world remove it. There was probably something romantic in that if I weren’t clinically incapable of not making it a table state. “Do you play Zada?” I asked. Her eyes lit up. “Goblin chaos queen? Absolutely. Not Commander, just sixty-card nonsense. Expedite, Crimson Wisps, Ancestral Anger, draw half the deck, die to a board wipe.” Entry nine: Zada, Hedron Grinder. High volatility. Explosive combo. Risk tolerance higher than Muldrotha suggested. Likes absurd scaling. Will accept catastrophic failure for one glorious turn. So: patient inevitability, defensive recursion, tactical protection, occasional chaos engine. Complex profile. Not a single linear aggro confession. I was fascinated. Also, I realized, I was smiling like an idiot. “What?” she asked. “Nothing. I just like listening to people talk about things they love.” That sentence came out normal. Almost charming. I mentally high-fived whatever underpaid subsystem generated it. She looked down, smiling into her risotto. “That was dangerously smooth.” “I apologize. It won’t happen again.” “See that it doesn’t.” For a while, I forgot to scan. That almost never happens. I didn’t notice the waiter entering table orders on an unlocked handheld. I didn’t count ceiling cameras. I didn’t estimate how many seconds it would take to slip behind the host stand. I listened to Mara describe a draft where she first-picked a Sheoldred because “I may have ethics, but I’m not made of stone.” Catalog entry ten: Sheoldred, the Apocalypse. Pragmatic. Understands power. Claims moral constraints; reserves exception for bombs. Dessert menus came. We decided to split tiramisu after a negotiation that involved neither of us admitting we wanted the whole thing. Then her phone buzzed. She glanced down. “Sorry, my sister. She gets anxious when I’m on dates with internet men.” “Reasonable.” “She asked for proof of life.” She picked up her phone, angled slightly toward the table, and drew the unlock pattern. My brain saw it before I could look away. Top left. Top middle. Top right. Center. Bottom left. Bottom middle. Bottom right. A giant Z? No. Worse. A seven-node sweep. Highly guessable. Visible shoulder-surf vulnerability. Smudge-confirmable. Basically a welcome mat with biometric lighting. My mouth opened without authorization. “Oh, you should change that.” Silence. The restaurant noise seemed to drop by half, as if someone had turned down the ambience channel. Mara looked up slowly. “Change what?” There were several exits. The nearest was behind me, but climbing over the booth would draw attention. A social exit was possible if I deployed humor quickly. Instead I did the worst thing: I answered accurately. “Your unlock pattern. It’s very visible. And common structure. Starts top left, uses adjacent nodes, symmetrical enough to remember. If someone saw it once—like I just did—they could probably replicate it.” Her face did something complicated. Not anger first. That would have been easier. First came surprise. Then embarrassment. Then anger as a defense perimeter coming online. “You watched me unlock my phone?” “No. I mean—yes, but not intentionally. It was in my field of vision.” “That’s a very comforting distinction.” “I know. I’m sorry. I shouldn’t have said it like that.” “How should you have said it?” Probably never. The answer was probably never. I felt the old reflex rising: justify, explain, demonstrate the risk until the other person conceded I was technically correct. Technical correctness had carried me through many locked doors and very few healthy relationships. Do not exploit. Do not escalate. Do not win. “I should have asked if you wanted a security nitpick,” I said. “Or kept it to myself.” She set the phone face down. Face down. Trust boundary reasserted. Device removed from visual access. Good instinct. Bad sign for me. “I’m not one of your clients,” she said. “No. You’re not.” “And this is a date, not an audit.” “I know.” “Do you?” Fair question. The tiramisu arrived with the timing of a system alert during a funeral. The waiter placed it between us, sensed radiation, and vanished. I looked at the cocoa-dusted top because her eyes were currently a privileged interface and my access had been revoked. “I have a problem,” I said. “That much is clear.” Despite herself, a corner of her mouth moved. Tiny vulnerability. No. Not vulnerability. Opening. Different word. Better word. “I spent years training myself to notice weak points,” I said. “Systems, mostly. Then people, because people are where systems leak. I’m good at it. I used to be proud of being good at it. Now I’m trying to learn when noticing doesn’t mean speaking, and speaking doesn’t mean helping.” She was quiet. I continued, because silence felt like falling and I am historically bad at not grabbing tools on the way down. “But that doesn’t excuse making you feel watched. I’m sorry. Not ‘sorry your pattern is insecure.’ Sorry I made the table feel unsafe.” Her fingers rested near her spoon. She didn’t pick it up. “That was a better apology,” she said. “Thank you. I’ve patched it recently.” Her mouth twitched again. “Don’t push it.” “Right.” We sat in the wreckage of the moment. Around us, couples performed normal couple things: laughing, clinking glasses, lying about not wanting fries. I considered sabotaging the rest deliberately. It would be easy. Mention that her sister’s proof-of-life text probably included metadata. Explain that restaurant loyalty programs were surveillance engines. Ask whether her Muldrotha deck ran Mindslicer, then use that as a metaphor for emotional intimacy. Burn it cleanly. Leave before she could. Old habit: if you can’t secure the system, crash it. Instead I picked up my spoon and cut the tiramisu exactly in half, then nudged the plate slightly toward her. “No more unsolicited audits,” I said. “For the rest of dinner.” She studied me. “Can you actually do that?” “No.” She snorted. “But I can keep them internal.” “That sounds exhausting.” “It is. You should see me at airports.” “I would rather not.” “Fair.” She finally took a bite of tiramisu. The spoon crossing the neutral zone felt absurdly hopeful. After a minute, she said, “For what it’s worth, I know the pattern is bad.” I looked up. “My niece set it. She said it looked like a lightning bolt. I kept it because she checks whether I’m still using it.” That was worse. Not security-wise. Security-wise it remained terrible. But human-wise, it had context. Meaning. A tiny family ritual I had mistaken for negligence because negligence was the cheaper assumption. “Ah,” I said. “That’s sweet.” “It is. Also insecure.” I pressed my lips together. She pointed her spoon at me. “You may say one thing.” The heavens opened. “Use a longer PIN when you travel and switch back for your niece at home.” “One thing, not a white paper.” “That was one sentence.” “Barely.” I leaned back. “Done.” She nodded. “Acceptable.” The air didn’t fully reset, but it changed. Less brittle. More negotiated. She told me her niece also called all dragons “Nicol Bolas,” regardless of plane, color identity, or number of horns. Catalog entry eleven: Nicol Bolas by proxy. Family influence. Tolerates lore inaccuracies from loved ones. Strong sign. I told her I once built a deck around Phelddagrif because I thought group hug might make me a better person. “Did it?” “No. I became manipulative in pastel colors.” She laughed hard enough that the couple beside us glanced over. Maybe salvage was possible. Not full restoration from backup; those never come back perfectly. But a new branch. A fork with the bad commit acknowledged. When the check came, we did the awkward card dance. Her phone stayed face down. I did not look at the receipt tablet when the waiter processed payment, though it was angled toward me and absolutely leaking the last four digits of someone else’s card from the previous transaction. Heroism takes many forms. Outside, the rain had stopped. The sidewalk shone under streetlights. “This was…” Mara said. I braced. “Interesting.” “Devastatingly ambiguous.” “It started good, got weird, recovered to complicated.” “That’s basically my brand.” She smiled. “I haven’t decided if that’s a warning label or a selling point.” “Could be both.” Her rideshare pulled up. She opened the door, then paused. “I’m going to change the pattern,” she said. “Not because you told me to.” “Of course.” “And if we do this again, you get one unsolicited security comment per date.” “That’s generous.” “They don’t roll over.” “Understood.” She got in, then looked back through the open door. “Also, I run Mindslicer in Muldrotha.” Catalog entry twelve: Mindslicer. Mutual discard. Willing to detonate everyone’s hand if positioned to recover faster. Ruthless, recursive, absolutely terrifying. I stared at her. She grinned. “Thought you should know what you’re dealing with.” The door closed. As the car pulled away, I stood on the sidewalk cataloging exactly one thing: not the license plate, not the driver, not the route. The fact that I hoped she’d text.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional craft in executing a challenging prompt. The author successfully maintains the protagonist's voice as a reformed hacker throughout, using technical terminology and security-focused metaphors that feel authentic rather than forced. The internal monologue structure works brilliantly, allowing readers to experience Evan's compulsive pattern recognition in real-time. The characterization is particularly strong. Evan emerges as genuinely complex - his expertise is both his strength and his social liability. The author avoids making him a simple "quirky genius" archetype by showing real consequences for his behavior and genuine self-awareness about his limitations. Mara is equally well-developed, revealing layers through her Magic: The Gathering deck choices (a clever characterization device) and her measured response to Evan's social misstep. The Magic: The Gathering elements serve multiple purposes beyond shared interest - they become a sophisticated character analysis tool where deck choices reveal personality traits. This demonstrates creative integration of nerd culture that advances both plot and character development. The central conflict around the phone unlock pattern feels organic and realistic. Evan's inability to suppress his security observations creates genuine tension, and the aftermath is handled with emotional intelligence. Neither character is villainized; instead, we see two people navigating a legitimate compatibility question. The prose maintains consistent quality throughout, with technical metaphors that illuminate rather than obscure meaning. Phrases like "Her smile hit me with the force of an unpatched zero-day" work because they reveal character perspective while remaining accessible. The dialogue feels natural and serves character development effectively. The resolution strikes an appropriate balance - not overly optimistic but suggesting genuine possibility. Mara's revelation about running Mindslicer provides a perfect callback that shows she understands and can match Evan's analytical nature. The ending feels earned rather than forced. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the technical metaphors become slightly dense, and some catalog entries could be trimmed without losing impact. However, these are minor issues in an otherwise accomplished piece. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 19 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 18 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 2 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 19 Overall Impression: 18

+ First-person unique perspective: Overthinking It — Score: 89.1
Prompt:
Overthinking It Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of an intelligent 8th-grade girl with social anxiety who has developed a crush on a classmate. The narrative takes place during a group science project where they're paired together, as she cycles through rehearsed conversations while second-guessing each potential approach. Her inner thoughts should reveal the gap between her articulate internal voice and her difficulty expressing herself aloud, her fears of coming across as desperate or weird, and the exhausting process of monitoring her every word, expression, and movement for potential embarrassment. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I have already planned four possible ways to say hello to Maya Patel, and none of them will work. The first one is casual: *Hey, Maya.* Two words. Normal people can say two words. Two words is practically not even a sentence. It is more like a sound effect. Birds probably have more complicated conversations than that. But if I say *Hey, Maya* too brightly, it will sound like I have been waiting all morning to talk to her, which I have, so that is dangerous. If I say it too quietly, she won’t hear me, and then I will have to say it again, which makes it a whole event. If I say it while looking directly at her, that might be intense. If I say it while not looking at her, that might seem rude, or like I’m afraid of her, which I am, but not in a bad way. The second option is *Hi.* Safer. Shorter. But maybe too short. Like I’m annoyed. The third is *So, photosynthesis,* which is at least relevant because we’re supposed to be designing an experiment about light exposure and plant growth, but saying *So, photosynthesis* as an opening line feels like something an alien would say while wearing a human costume backward. The fourth is to say nothing and let someone else begin. This is the option I am most likely to choose, because my personality is apparently “locked bathroom door.” I sit at our lab table and open my notebook to a fresh page. At the top, I write: **Science Group Project: Light & Growth Variables** My handwriting is too neat. It looks like I am trying to win an award called Most Obviously Nervous Person. I loosen my grip on the pencil. Then I worry my hand looks weirdly limp, so I hold it normally again. Then I wonder what normal even is. Most people seem to exist in their bodies without operating them manually. I have to think instructions at every joint. Relax shoulders. Unclench jaw. Don’t press lips together. Don’t breathe loudly. When Maya slides into the seat across from me, my brain immediately shuts every window it had open and replaces them with one enormous flashing sign: **SHE IS HERE.** She smells faintly like citrus shampoo and pencil shavings, which is unfairly pleasant. Her dark hair is pulled into a messy braid, but not messy like mine gets messy, where it looks as if I survived a raccoon attack. Hers has loose strands around her face in a way that seems accidental but also impossible to achieve by accident. She puts her binder on the table and smiles at me. My mouth has approximately one job. “Hi,” I say. It comes out as a small, dry squeak, like a marker dying. Maya still smiles. “Hi.” Okay. That happened. No one died. Nobody pointed. The fire alarm did not go off. We are alive in the aftermath of my greeting. Next to me, Lucas drops into his chair backward, because of course he does, because some people have enough confidence to sit wrong on furniture. “Are we actually doing this experiment, or are we just writing fake data like last time?” “We did not write fake data,” says Priya, sitting down with color-coded folders and the expression of someone who has already mentally finished the assignment. “We approximated,” Lucas says. Priya gives him a look. “That’s what people say right before they get sued.” Maya laughs, and the sound goes right through me like I am a tuning fork. I look down at my lab notes because staring at someone while they laugh is probably how documentaries about obsession begin. In the margin, without exactly deciding to, I draw a tiny sketch of Lucas: a stick figure leaning back in a chair with explosion lines around his head. Then Priya: rectangular glasses, a clipboard, a tiny crown labeled **Supreme Court of Accuracy**. For Maya, I hesitate. Drawing her feels dangerous, even as a doodle in the margin of my own notebook, which nobody is going to inspect unless I faint and they need to identify my last thoughts. I draw a small sprout instead, with two leaves reaching toward a sun. That is not too obvious. It could represent the project. It could represent science. It definitely does not represent the way she makes the entire table seem brighter, because that would be unbearable and I would have to bury the notebook in the woods. “And Jada?” Priya says. I look up too fast. “What?” I know what. My name. She said my name. This should not be startling. I have had it for thirteen years. “Do you want to be in charge of recording observations? Your notes are always good.” My notes are always good because paper cannot judge me in real time. Paper does not tilt its head and say, “Why are you like this?” Paper waits patiently while I assemble myself. “Sure,” I say. One word. Good. Functional. Maybe too flat? Maybe I sound unenthusiastic. Maybe I should add something. “I can do that,” I say, too late, overlapping with Lucas saying, “Thank you, nerd queen.” He means it as a compliment, I think. Or as his version of one. I give a tiny laugh, because not laughing could make it awkward, but laughing too much would make it seem like I am grateful for any crumb of approval. My laugh comes out as “hm,” which is not a laugh so much as a closed-captioning problem. Maya glances at my notebook. “Your doodles are cute.” Every internal organ in my body attempts to leave through my ribs. Say something normal. Say *thanks.* Say *I draw when I’m thinking.* Say *I like your braid,* no, absolutely do not say that. Too personal. Too sudden. Sounds like I have been cataloging her, which I have, but privately and with shame. “Thanks,” I say. It works. It is a full word. An appropriate word. I should stop. “I just—um—I do them sometimes. In notes. Not like, instead of notes. Also notes.” Why am I still speaking? Who authorized this? Maya leans forward slightly. “Can I see?” No. Yes. If she sees the sprout, will she know? Is a sprout a confession? It has leaves. People draw plants all the time. We are doing a plant project. Calm down. I turn the notebook a few inches. Not too eagerly. Not too slowly. My arm feels like it is being filmed. Maya points at Lucas’s doodle and laughs again. “That’s perfect.” Lucas cranes his neck. “Am I being slandered?” “Scientifically represented,” I say. For half a second, there is silence. Then Maya laughs harder. My entire brain freezes on the fact that I said something and it worked. Not merely did not fail. Worked. A successful sentence. A rare bird landing on my hand. I should smile. I smile. Is it too much? Teeth or no teeth? I choose small smile, no teeth, then worry I look smug, then soften it, then worry I look sleepy. This is what people don’t understand about being quiet. They think it is peaceful inside. It is not peaceful. It is a crowded room where every version of me is shouting conflicting instructions. Be funny. Don’t try too hard. Speak up. Don’t interrupt. Look interested. Don’t stare. Move your hands naturally. What are hands? Priya opens her folder. “Okay, we need a hypothesis. Something like: if plants get more hours of light, then they will grow taller because photosynthesis increases.” “That’s good,” Maya says. “But should we measure height only? What about leaf color?” I know something about this. I read ahead last night because I was nervous about today and research is anxiety wearing a lab coat. I could say that leaf color might be subjective unless we use a scale, and that growth rate over time is better than final height because seed variation matters. I could contribute something intelligent. I could become, briefly, the person I am in my head. My brain forms the sentence perfectly: *We might want to measure height every two days instead of only at the end, because that gives us a growth curve and makes our data less dependent on initial differences between seedlings.* Beautiful. Clear. I can see the sentence like a glass bridge. Now I just have to walk across it. “I think,” I start. Everyone looks at me. The bridge catches fire. “I mean, maybe, um. If we measure more than once. Like not just at the end. Because then it’s more…” I rotate one hand in the air, apparently hoping the correct word will fall out of the ceiling. “Data-ish.” Data-ish. I want to crawl into the bean sprout tray and photosynthesize until graduation. Priya nods slowly, but not in a mean way. “Right. Repeated measurements. That’s a good idea.” Repeated measurements. That was the phrase. Of course. My internal vocabulary owns a velvet couch and my external vocabulary lives in a dumpster. Maya writes it down. “Jada’s idea: measure every two days.” She says my name like it belongs in a sentence. Like it is easy. Warmth crawls up my neck. I look down and draw another tiny sprout beside the first one, this one with a ridiculous little heart-shaped leaf. Immediately I shade over the heart until it looks like a normal leaf, or possibly a leaf that has been in an accident. Stop it. Be less obvious. Could anyone see? No. My notebook is angled toward me. Still, I cover the margin with my hand. Lucas says, “Can one plant listen to music? I want to test if my playlist makes it stronger.” “No,” Priya says. “You haven’t heard my playlist.” “That is exactly why.” Maya grins. “Maybe we should test if Lucas’s playlist makes the plant give up.” I laugh before I can decide whether to laugh. It escapes, real and sudden. Too loud? Maybe. Lucas clutches his chest dramatically, so maybe it’s fine. Maya looks at me, still smiling, and for one strange second I forget to calculate my face. I just smile back. Then I remember, and my smile becomes something I am holding carefully, like a beaker full of acid. We start dividing tasks. Priya will write the procedure draft. Lucas will gather materials, which means he will wander around dramatically and touch things. Maya and I are supposed to set up the data table. Together. The word has a physical effect on me. Maya moves her chair around to my side of the table so we can share the notebook. Her sleeve brushes the edge of my sleeve. Not even my arm. Fabric. My nervous system behaves like this is breaking news. “Should we do columns for day zero, day two, day four…” she says. “Yes,” I say, too quickly. Add something. “That makes sense.” Good. Boring, but good. She draws a ruler line with careful concentration. I watch her hand for exactly one second, then look away because watching people’s hands can become weird if done with feeling. I focus on the tabletop. There is a dried glue spot shaped like Antarctica. Ask her something. People like being asked questions. Not too personal. Not *do you like anyone?* obviously. Not *what shampoo do you use?* because that is how you get banned from friendships. Ask about science project? Safe. “So,” I say. My throat tightens. “Do you, um, want to do the graph part? Later?” Maya looks up. “Sure. We could do it together if you want.” If I want. If I want, I would build the graph out of gold leaf and sing to it. “Okay,” I say, because apparently my emotional range aloud is a vending machine with one button. Inside, I am composing entire paragraphs: *Yes, I would like that. I like how you think. I like that you make jokes but still care about getting things right. I like your braid and your handwriting and the way you say my name without making it feel like an announcement.* Outside, I add, “Cool.” Cool. I am a poet trapped in a refrigerator. Maya doesn’t seem disappointed. She just keeps drawing columns. Maybe she is not analyzing me at all. Maybe she is having a normal human experience where a classmate says “cool” and it means “cool,” not “please decode my entire soul from this single syllable.” I add labels to the table: **Plant A, Plant B, Plant C.** My hand steadies when I write. Letters are obedient. They stay where I put them. In the margin, I draw Priya’s crown again, Lucas’s exploding chair, and four little seedlings under different suns. For myself, I draw a turtle halfway inside its shell, holding a pencil. Then I pause and add tiny glasses, even though I don’t wear glasses, because otherwise it looks too much like me. For Maya, I draw the sprout again. This time I leave the heart-shaped leaf alone. I should erase it. I don’t. “Cute turtle,” Maya says softly. My pulse trips. “Oh,” I say. “Yeah. That’s, um, the recorder.” “The official scientist turtle?” “Very qualified,” I say. She smiles, smaller this time, like it is just for our side of the table. “I like her.” Her. The turtle is apparently a her. The turtle is liked. I am trying very hard not to turn this into evidence of anything, because my brain loves building castles out of dust. Maya liking a doodled turtle does not mean she likes me. Maya offering to make a graph together does not mean she wants to be friends outside science class. Maya smiling does not mean she has noticed that my entire personality becomes a buffering video around her. Still. For the next ten minutes, I manage to function. Not gracefully, but functionally. I write. I answer when asked. I say “maybe we should keep the water amount constant,” and although my voice shakes slightly on *constant,* nobody reacts. The world allows it. When the bell rings, everyone starts packing up. Chairs scrape. Binders slam. The room becomes loud enough that my thoughts blur at the edges. Maya gathers her papers. “See you tomorrow for the plant setup?” This is another opportunity. A doorway. Say something that keeps it open. Possible responses: *Yeah, see you tomorrow.* *Definitely.* *I’m looking forward to it.* No. Too eager. *Can’t wait to measure dirt with you.* No. Weird, possibly funny, but high risk. I choose the safe one. “Yeah,” I say. “See you.” She smiles and leaves with Priya, their backpacks bumping together as they weave through the desks. I remain seated for three extra seconds, pretending to check my notes while actually recovering from having survived forty-two minutes of near-continuous self-surveillance. My shoulders ache. My face feels tired from arranging itself. My brain is a buzzing fluorescent light. But on the page in front of me, the data table is neat and complete. The tiny turtle scientist stands beside the seedlings. Lucas explodes. Priya rules. And the little sprout with the heart-shaped leaf reaches quietly toward the sun. I close the notebook before anyone can see it. Then, because I am apparently both terrified and hopeless, I smile all the way to history class.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional adherence to the prompt's requirements, delivering a compelling first-person narrative from an intelligent 8th-grade girl with social anxiety. The writer successfully captures the exhausting internal monologue of overthinking every social interaction, creating an authentic voice that balances intellectual sophistication with age-appropriate concerns. The characterization is remarkably nuanced. Jada emerges as a fully realized character whose internal eloquence contrasts sharply with her external awkwardness. Her observations are genuinely insightful and often witty ("my personality is apparently 'locked bathroom door'"), while her social paralysis feels authentic rather than exaggerated. The supporting characters, though seen through Jada's anxious lens, feel distinct and believable. The prose quality is consistently high, with memorable metaphors that illuminate the protagonist's experience without becoming overwrought. Lines like "I am a poet trapped in a refrigerator" and "my brain loves building castles out of dust" are both humorous and revealing. The writing maintains an elegant balance between Jada's sophisticated internal voice and her teenage perspective. The emotional complexity is particularly strong. The piece captures the simultaneous hope and terror of adolescent attraction, the exhaustion of constant self-monitoring, and the gap between internal capability and external expression. The anxiety feels genuine rather than performed, avoiding both romanticization and caricature. Structurally, the narrative maintains excellent coherence, following Jada through the class period while building tension through her internal struggles. The pacing allows for both comedic moments and genuine pathos without feeling forced. The ending strikes an appropriate note—neither overly optimistic nor artificially dark—acknowledging small progress while maintaining the character's essential nature. The dialogue, though limited by the internal monologue format, feels natural when it occurs. The contrast between Jada's planned conversations and her actual speech effectively demonstrates her social anxiety without becoming repetitive. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the metaphors, while clever, slightly overshadow the emotional reality. Some descriptions of anxiety symptoms border on being too articulate for the moment, though this generally serves the character's intellectual nature well. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 19 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 19 Emotionally Complex: 18 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 19 Overall Impression: 18
