+ Fanfic-crossover: Prison Strings — Score: 78.4
Prompt:
Prison Strings Jolyne Cujoh & Makima Write a story where Jolyne Cujoh (JoJo's Bizarre Adventure) and Makima (Chainsaw Man) cross paths at a supernatural prison facility. Jolyne is immediately suspicious of Makima's falsely sweet demeanor, while Makima is intrigued by Jolyne's Stand ability. Their battle of wits escalates dangerously as Jolyne realizes how manipulative Makima truly is. **Jolyne Cujoh (JoJo's Bizarre Adventure)** Jolyne is the daughter of Jotaro Kujo and the first female JoJo protagonist. Her Stand, Stone Free, allows her to unravel her body into string that she can control. She's hot-headed, foul-mouthed, and doesn't take disrespect from anyone. She was framed for murder and sent to prison. She has a distinctive butterfly tattoo on her arm and wears her hair in two buns. Despite her tough exterior, she's deeply loyal to her friends and family. She can be impulsive but is highly adaptable in battle. She inherited her father's ability to stay cool under pressure but is more emotionally expressive. Famous quote: "If there's no Mickey, this shit ain't Disney!" **Makima (Chainsaw Man)** Makima appears as a young woman with auburn hair and ringed yellow eyes. She's the Control Devil who can dominate anyone she considers beneath her (nearly everyone). She maintains a pleasant, soft-spoken demeanor that masks her manipulative, cold nature. She can create contracts with humans and devils, often exploiting their desires. She's obsessed with Chainsaw Man and wants to control him. She keeps numerous people and devils as pets on leashes. She's practically immortal due to a contract with the Prime Minister. She views humans as lesser beings but mimics human emotions perfectly. Famous quote: "It's okay. Dogs are supposed to die for their owners." Third person, present tense. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
The supernatural wing of Green Dolphin Street isn’t on any map. It squats beneath the prison like a buried organ, all concrete arteries and iron ribs, humming with fluorescent lights that flicker even when the power is steady. Guards call it Special Containment. Inmates call it Hell’s Basement. The women who scrub the upper floors whisper other names—Devil Block, Stand Row, the place where the cameras blink when something smiles at them. Jolyne Cujoh doesn’t give a damn what they call it. She has been dragged into enough “special” rooms to know it usually means someone is about to lie to her, threaten her, or try to carve information out of her body. The cuffs biting into her wrists are real. The cold floor under her boots is real. The butterfly tattoo on her arm prickles beneath the sleeve of her prison uniform like it wants to fly. The guard at her left keeps glancing down the hallway. “What?” Jolyne snaps. “You lose your spine somewhere back there?” He flinches. “Shut up.” “Great comeback.” The cameras above them swivel. Then, one by one, their little red lights go dark. The guard stops walking. Jolyne feels it before she sees anything. Not a Stand attack. Not exactly. It is more like the air has been trained to obey someone. The silence becomes neat and polished, like a table set for dinner. A woman steps around the corner. She is young-looking, pretty, dressed too well for a prison and too calmly for a place where monsters are kept in reinforced cells. Auburn hair falls in a tidy braid over her shoulder. Her yellow eyes have rings inside them, like targets or collars. She smiles with practiced warmth. “Jolyne Cujoh,” the woman says. “I’ve been looking forward to meeting you.” Jolyne stares at her. The guard immediately lowers his head. “Ma’am.” The woman doesn’t look at him. “Thank you. You can leave us.” “But orders said—” “Leave us.” The guard’s mouth closes so quickly his teeth click. He unlocks Jolyne’s cuffs with shaking fingers, then turns and walks away, stiff as a puppet. Jolyne rubs her wrists and watches him go. “Nice trick,” she says. “You train him with snacks, or does he just roll over naturally?” The woman smiles a little wider. “My name is Makima.” “Good for you.” “I know about your ability.” Jolyne’s expression hardens. There it is. The lie before the threat, the softness before the knife. Makima clasps her hands in front of her as if they are two women meeting at a café instead of under a maximum-security prison full of cursed freaks and invisible killers. “Stone Free. You can unravel your body into string. You can move it at will, listen through it, stitch wounds with it, form nets, blades, traps. It’s very versatile.” Jolyne’s fingers twitch. A thread slips invisibly from her wrist, thin enough to vanish in the glare of the overhead lights. It slides along the floor toward Makima’s shoes. Makima’s ringed eyes dip for half a second. Jolyne clicks her tongue. “So you can see it.” “I can see many things.” “People who say that usually can’t see the punch coming.” Makima tilts her head. “Do you always try to intimidate people who are being kind to you?” “You’re not being kind. You’re being creepy.” The thread circles Makima’s ankle. Makima lets it. That bothers Jolyne more than if she had dodged. Around them, the containment hall stretches long and empty. Behind thick glass, shapes shift in cells marked with symbols Jolyne can’t read. Something with antlers scratches at the wall. Something else whispers in a dead man’s voice. The lights continue to hum. Makima says, “You were framed. You were sent here because someone wanted you contained. That must be frustrating.” Jolyne’s jaw tightens. “I can help you,” Makima continues. “Your sentence. Your father. Your enemies. If you cooperate, your situation can improve very quickly.” Jolyne laughs once, sharp and ugly. “Wow. You almost sound human when you say that.” Makima’s smile does not change. Stone Free manifests behind Jolyne in a rush of blue-gray muscle and string, fists clenched, goggles glinting. The Stand’s presence fills the hall with pressure. Jolyne points at Makima. “Here’s how this goes,” she says. “You tell me who the hell you are, why the cameras cut out when you walk by, and why everyone in this hole acts like you own their souls.” Makima’s eyes brighten with interest. “Beautiful,” she says softly. Jolyne bares her teeth. “Wrong answer.” Stone Free punches. The fist tears through the space between them faster than a bullet, aimed straight at Makima’s face. Makima doesn’t move. At the last instant, Jolyne’s own arm jerks. Pain explodes across her cheek as Stone Free’s punch veers sideways and slams into the wall beside Makima, cracking concrete. Jolyne staggers, shock flashing through her. “What—” Makima’s expression remains gentle. “You’re strong. But strength is much easier to guide than weakness.” Jolyne feels pressure coil around her body. Not rope. Not string. Not a Stand. Something worse. The sensation of being categorized. Placed. Labeled beneath. Her knees twitch, trying to bend. “No,” Jolyne growls. Makima watches with faint curiosity. “Kneel.” Jolyne’s body lurches downward. Stone Free snaps out, unraveling Jolyne’s legs into strands. The command hits muscle and bone that are no longer properly arranged. Her knees dissolve into whipping cords, lashing to the ceiling pipes and yanking her upright before she can hit the floor. Makima blinks. Jolyne pants, suspended by her own string like a furious marionette that has cut its puppeteer’s hands off. “You picked the wrong bitch to play dog trainer with,” Jolyne snarls. Makima’s smile finally thins. From the cells around them, the whispers stop. Jolyne fires threads in every direction. They loop around door handles, sprinkler heads, camera mounts, Makima’s wrists, the buckle of her coat. She feels vibrations through each strand: Makima’s heartbeat, calm and steady; the shift of air; the faint steps of guards gathering two corridors away. Makima is calling them without speaking. Of course she is. Jolyne tightens the string around Makima’s wrist. “Don’t even think about it.” Makima looks down at the thread, fascinated. “You extend yourself into the world. That must make it difficult to know where you end.” “I end wherever I decide.” “Do you?” Makima asks. “Your friends. Your father. Your need to prove you aren’t afraid. All these strings attached to you.” The words slide in smooth and poisoned. Jolyne hates that they land. Makima takes one step forward. The string around her wrist slices into her skin. Blood beads, red and neat. She doesn’t care. “I can give you a purpose,” Makima says. “A better one than anger. A cleaner one than revenge.” Jolyne’s eyes narrow. “You talk like a priest and stare like a butcher.” “I like loyal creatures.” “Then buy a damn goldfish.” Makima raises her hand. Somewhere far above them, a man screams. Jolyne hears it through the pipes, through her strings, through the bones of the prison. A guard’s voice cuts off wetly. Then another. Doors unlock in sequence down the hall. Makima says, “People are very willing to make contracts when they are afraid. This facility is full of afraid people.” The cell doors begin to open. Creatures step out. Not inmates. Not Stands. Devils, maybe, if the rumors are worth anything. A thing made of teeth crawls onto the ceiling. A woman with no face drags a chain of human hands. A mound of wet feathers breathes names Jolyne almost recognizes. All of them look at Makima. All of them bow their heads. Jolyne feels the shape of it then. The sweetness is not a mask hiding emptiness. It is bait over a pit. Makima doesn’t persuade because she believes in anyone. She persuades because it amuses her to make cages look like homes. “You really are a piece of work,” Jolyne says, voice low. Makima’s eyes settle on her. “And you are still resisting.” “Yeah. People keep acting surprised.” Jolyne yanks. The string tied to the camera mounts snaps taut—not to attack Makima, but to rip the dead cameras from the walls. Sparks burst. Glass shatters. The sudden cascade triggers the sprinkler system. Water blasts from above, soaking the hall, turning concrete slick. Makima glances up. Jolyne is already moving. Stone Free unravels half of Jolyne’s torso into a storm of wet blue string, spreading across the flooded floor. Water carries vibration. Every footstep, every twitch, every devil breath becomes information. The hallway becomes her web. The tooth-thing lunges. Stone Free hammers it into the floor. The faceless woman swings her chain. Jolyne splits her shoulder into thread, lets the chain pass through, then knots around it and drags the devil into the path of the feathered mound. They collide in a shriek of breaking shapes. Makima walks through the chaos untouched. “Jolyne,” she says, almost fondly. “Stop.” The command clamps down again. Jolyne’s breath catches. Her fingers freeze. Stone Free halts mid-punch. Makima comes close enough that Jolyne can smell rainwater and copper. “It’s okay,” Makima says gently. “Dogs are supposed to die for their owners.” Jolyne’s eyes widen. Not in fear. In fury. A thread, nearly invisible, has been crawling beneath Makima’s collar since the first time Jolyne wrapped her wrist. Not tight enough to be felt. Not strong enough to choke. Just enough to listen. Just enough to find the rhythm of her voice. Stone Free’s hand trembles. Jolyne forces one finger to move, then two. “If there’s no Mickey,” she whispers through clenched teeth, “this shit ain’t Disney.” The hidden thread snaps tight across Makima’s throat—not to strangle, but to yank her chin upward and ruin the line of her command. At the same moment, Jolyne bursts apart into string completely, her body scattering across the sprinkler-soaked hall. Makima’s order has nothing solid to hold. Jolyne reforms behind her, fist already swinging. Stone Free hits Makima in the back hard enough to launch her down the corridor. She crashes through a half-open cell door and into reinforced glass. Cracks spiderweb around her. For one perfect second, Makima looks genuinely surprised. Then her neck straightens with a soft, wrong pop. Jolyne’s stomach drops. Makima rises, blood running from her mouth, smile returning like a curtain closing over a stage. “That hurt,” she says. “You’re even more interesting than I hoped.” “Lady,” Jolyne says, backing toward the emergency stairwell as alarms finally begin to scream, “that was me being polite.” Makima steps from the cell. Behind her, the devils stir again. Guards approach from both ends of the hall, faces blank, weapons raised. The cameras remain dead, their broken lenses reflecting only red alarm light. Jolyne sends strings into the ceiling, into vents, into cracks in the concrete. She feels pathways. Weak points. Escape routes. She also feels Makima’s attention settle over her like a leash waiting to close. This fight is not over. Not even close. Makima wants to own her. Jolyne smiles, wild and defiant, rainwater dripping from her buns, butterfly tattoo bright against her skin. “Come on, then,” she says. “Try holding my strings.” The prison shakes as Stone Free pulls the ceiling down between them. Concrete, water, and sparks crash into the hall, swallowing Makima’s soft laugh in thunder. And for the first time since entering Hell’s Basement, Jolyne runs—not because she is afraid, but because she understands. Makima doesn’t need bars. Makima is the prison.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and faithful character portrayal within a supernatural prison setting. The author successfully captures both characters' essential traits - Jolyne's defiant, hot-headed nature and Makima's manipulative control masked by false sweetness. The dialogue feels authentic to both characters, particularly Jolyne's crude directness ("If there's no Mickey, this shit ain't Disney!") and Makima's deceptively gentle commands. The prose maintains consistent quality throughout, with effective atmospheric descriptions of the supernatural prison facility. The "Hell's Basement" setting feels appropriately ominous and well-realized. The author demonstrates good understanding of both source materials, accurately depicting Stone Free's string abilities and Makima's control powers. The escalating conflict structure works well, building from initial suspicion through psychological manipulation to physical confrontation. The battle choreography is creative, particularly Jolyne's use of the sprinkler system and her strategic thread placement. The realization that "Makima is the prison" provides a satisfying thematic conclusion. However, the piece suffers from some pacing issues in the middle section, with slightly repetitive descriptions of the control dynamic. Some metaphors feel forced ("like a curtain closing over a stage"), and certain action sequences could be clearer. The ending, while thematically appropriate, feels somewhat abrupt after the buildup. The characterization remains strong throughout, with both women acting consistently with their established personalities. Jolyne's resourcefulness and refusal to submit feels earned, as does Makima's patient predatory behavior. The psychological tension between them drives the narrative effectively. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 6 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 7 Overwrought: 6 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 15 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 18.0, Believable Character Actions: 17.0, Nuanced Characters: 16.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16.0, Elegant Prose: 15.0, Emotionally Engaging: 16.0, Emotionally Complex: 15.0, Coherent: 17.0, Meandering: 6.0, Weak Dialogue: 4.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 5.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7.0, Amateurish: 5.0, Purple Prose: 7.0, Overwrought: 6.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 3.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 15.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 16.0, Overall Impression: 16.0
+ Scp: Notes from the Future — Score: 90.0
Prompt:
Write an SCP report about a researcher who begins finding handwritten notes from their future self, warning about a containment breach that hasn't happened yet. The notes become increasingly desperate and incoherent, sometimes containing information that contradicts known reality. Include the Foundation's attempts to determine if this is an actual temporal anomaly or psychological manipulation by a contained entity, and the researcher's struggle to trust their own perception. First person (researcher's perspective with interspersed notes), past tense. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Item #:** SCP-8XXX **Object Class:** Euclid **Special Containment Procedures:** The following document was compiled from my dictated testimony, recovered notes, surveillance logs, psychiatric evaluations, and addenda approved by Site-19 Temporal Anomalies Review Board. I was instructed to write in first person “for consistency of subjective continuity,” which was the phrase Dr. Havel used when she did not want to say *because we no longer trust the cameras, the clocks, or you.* At the time of the incident, I was Senior Researcher Mara Voss, assigned to low-risk cognitohazard cataloguing in Sublevel 4. Following Incident 8XXX-A, I was placed under observation in a standard humanoid holding suite, though no anomalous properties had been conclusively attributed to me. All written material generated by me was to be scanned, dated, and cross-checked against Foundation databases. No paper, graphite, ink, stylus, or similar marking implement was to be introduced without approval from two Level 4 personnel. This precaution did not prevent the notes from appearing. Security cameras in my office and later in my holding suite experienced a visual and audio glitch for approximately 0.7 to 2.3 seconds at the exact moment each note appeared on my desk or table. The glitch manifested as compression tearing, frame duplication, and a brief overlay of static. No personnel entered or exited during these intervals. Motion sensors recorded nothing. Pressure sensors sometimes recorded the sudden addition of between 1.8 and 4.6 grams. **Description:** SCP-8XXX referred, initially, to a series of handwritten notes that appeared in my workspace between 12/04/2025 and 12/19/2025. The notes were written in blue-black ink on standard Foundation memorandum paper, although chemical analysis showed the paper had degraded as though stored for between twelve and sixteen years. Handwriting analysis matched my own at 98.7% confidence. Ink flow, pen pressure, spelling habits, and idiosyncratic abbreviation patterns were also consistent with my writing. The contents of the notes claimed to originate from a future version of myself. They warned of an imminent containment breach that had not occurred. The Foundation initially investigated SCP-8XXX as a temporal anomaly, with alternate hypotheses including: 1. retrocausal information transfer; 2. cross-timeline contamination; 3. psychological manipulation by an SCP under containment; 4. internal fraud; 5. stress-induced dissociative production of written material, with associated surveillance tampering by an unknown actor. I did not know which possibility frightened me most. --- The first note appeared at 09:14 on my desk, between a mug of cold coffee and a stack of SCP-███ risk summaries. I had been looking directly at my monitor. The desk was in my peripheral vision. The cameras glitched for 1.1 seconds. I heard nothing except a pop in my left ear, like cabin pressure changing. The note was folded once. > **Mara,** > Do not approve the transfer request for SCP-███-Juno. > If it moves to Wing C, the breach starts at 18:40. > You will think this is a prank. It is not. > Check the vent above Archives Hall at 18:12. > —M I remember being annoyed before I was afraid. My first thought was that Kline had finally developed a sense of humor and chosen the worst possible target. I sent the note to Documents Analysis and filed a security report. At 18:12, on instruction from Site Security, an inspection team checked the vent above Archives Hall. They found a loose access panel, fresh scoring on the screws, and a dead rat with its spinal column removed. SCP-███-Juno’s transfer was postponed. There was no breach at 18:40. That should have reassured me. Instead, I spent the night watching the recording of the note appearing on my desk. There was one frame of static. Then the paper was there. I watched it twenty-seven times. On the twenty-eighth, I noticed that for one frame only, my chair was empty. I had been sitting in it. --- The second note appeared two days later, at 13:03. Camera glitch: 0.9 seconds. > You delayed it, not stopped it. > They will ask whether this is temporal. Say yes. Do not let them classify it as memetic. > If Havel interviews you, she is already compromised. > Do not drink anything she gives you. > The breach begins with singing. Dr. Havel interviewed me that afternoon. She gave me bottled water. I did not drink it. I watched her notice. I watched her decide not to comment. I watched my own hands tremble under the table and felt the humiliating certainty that every gesture made me look guilty, contaminated, or insane. She asked whether I had ever experienced auditory hallucinations, lost time, sleep paralysis, automatic writing, intrusive thoughts, or “a persistent sense of narrative inevitability.” I answered no to all except the last, because by then I could feel something arranging itself around me. At 16:22, personnel in Wing C reported hearing a child singing from the unoccupied decontamination showers. The song was identified by three witnesses as “A-Tisket, A-Tasket.” Audio equipment recorded only low-frequency mechanical hum. A search found no child, no speaker, and no breach. That evening, I received permission to review entities capable of inducing written hallucinations, false memories, or self-authenticating paranoia. The shortlist included SCP-████, SCP-██13, SCP-████-A, and three human-hostile infohazards sealed under BLACKBOX protocol. I read until my eyes burned. Every file seemed relevant. Every file seemed like bait. --- The third note was different. It appeared while I was not in the room. I had been taken for bloodwork. The office camera glitched for 2.3 seconds at 07:51. > You are not in the room this time. Good. > That means branch integrity still holds. > Havel dies in the west stairwell. > Kline opens the wrong door because you tell him to. > The thing wearing Director Sato’s voice is not Director Sato. > It has never been Director Sato. > I am sorry about your brother. I did not have a brother. I stared at that sentence longer than the others. I checked my personnel file. Only child. I called my mother from a monitored line and asked her if she had ever lost a pregnancy, adopted out a son, hidden anything from me. She cried and asked whether I was in trouble. Afterward, Dr. Havel classified the notes as “increasingly reality-inconsistent” and recommended containment of the phenomenon as likely psychological attack. Her reasoning was clinical, measured, and correct. A hostile entity did not need to tell perfect lies. It only needed to tell useful ones. It only needed to make me doubt the people keeping the facility intact. I agreed with her in the report. Privately, I began carrying a stolen scalpel in my sock. --- The fourth note was not on paper. It appeared written directly on my desk in black ink, though no pen was recovered. The camera glitched for 1.8 seconds. During the glitch, the timestamp advanced by six minutes and then corrected itself. > STOP TESTING ME FOR MEMES > IT IS USING THE TESTS > DO NOT READ THE PHRASE UNDER THE PHRASE > DO NOT LET THEM SHOW YOU YOUR OWN HANDWRITING > YOU ALREADY SAID YES > YOU ALWAYS SAY YES I was sedated after I tried to cover the message with my coat before Documentation could photograph it. I remembered shouting that the image itself might be the vector. I remembered Kline holding my wrists and telling me to breathe. I remembered looking over his shoulder at the observation window and seeing Director Sato standing there. Director Sato had been at an O5 briefing in Geneva. Travel logs confirmed it. The person I saw was therefore either an hallucination, an impostor, or something the records had been altered to exclude. The Foundation loves categories because categories can be locked. My experience no longer fit inside one. Temporal anomalies personnel installed independent analog cameras, a pendulum clock, thaumaturgic seals, Scranton Reality Anchors, and a Faraday enclosure around my holding suite. Psychiatry installed a sleep monitor. Memetics installed kill-agent filters over all incoming documents. Security installed two guards outside my door. At 03:16, all cameras glitched simultaneously. The pendulum stopped. The guards reported hearing me laugh from inside the suite. I had been asleep. A note lay on my pillow when I woke. > They will ask what you dreamed. > Do not tell them about the ocean under Site-19. > Do not tell them the breach has already happened. > Do not tell them you are reading this from containment. > You were never staff. > Your name is not Mara. I did dream of an ocean under Site-19. I also knew there was no ocean under Site-19. I knew the sublevels, the maintenance maps, the old sealed shafts, the places where concrete had been poured over things that still knocked sometimes. Knowledge became layered. One layer said impossible. Another said remembered. Neither felt less true than the other. When Havel asked what I had dreamed, I lied. She knew. I knew she knew. After that, they stopped letting me read the notes immediately. They placed each in an evidence sleeve and summarized the contents for me. This made me worse. I began imagining what they had omitted. I began hearing paper unfold when no one was in the room. I saw my handwriting in the cracks of tile, the veins in my hands, the ECG printout beside my bed. The fifth note was withheld for fourteen hours. When I finally saw it, I understood why. > Mara, if you can still parse this, listen. > The breach is not an event. It is a decision tree. > Every warning prunes one branch and feeds another. > They think contradiction means contamination. Contradiction means multiple survivals. > I remember a brother because one of us had one. > I remember Sato dead. I remember Sato never born. I remember Havel opening her mouth and the alarms coming out. > Do not trust memory. Do not trust cameras. Trust the scar on your left palm. I had no scar on my left palm. Then I did. Not suddenly, not visibly. There was no wound, no blood. I looked down after reading and saw a pale crescent of old scar tissue crossing the base of my thumb. Medical records updated within the hour to show I had acquired it at age nine from a broken jar. My mother confirmed it on a recorded call. She sounded confused that I had forgotten. That was when the Temporal Anomalies Review Board upgraded the probability of genuine retrocausal influence to 61%. Memetics objected. Psychiatry objected. I objected too, although I no longer knew to which conclusion. If the notes were from me, then some version of me had survived long enough to send them. If they were not, then something had learned to imitate not only my handwriting but my fear. --- The final note appeared on 12/19/2025 at 18:39. Every recording device in Sublevel 4 glitched. Not just my suite. Not just Site-19 internal security. Body cameras, analog backups, thermal imaging, even a hand-cranked film unit brought in by Temporal all lost the same 1.6 seconds. The note was on my desk. I say “my desk” because I was no longer in my office, but after the glitch, my holding suite contained it: coffee ring, scratched laminate, old inventory sticker, everything. The guards outside reported that my door had not opened. The office itself was found empty except for a rectangular absence in the dust. The note was written in uneven block letters, not cursive. > MARA > I AM NOT AHEAD OF YOU ANYMORE > I AM BESIDE YOU > IT GOT IN WHEN YOU CHOSE TO BELIEVE ME > IT GOT IN WHEN YOU CHOSE NOT TO > BREACH AT 18:40 > DO NOT LOOK AT THE DOOR > DO NOT LOOK AT THE DOOR > DO NOT LOOK AT THE DOOR > I AM SO SORRY I KEPT TRYING At 18:40, alarms began in Wing C. Or they did not. The official incident log recorded a false alarm caused by electrical fault. No containment units opened. No casualties occurred. Dr. Havel did not die in the west stairwell. Kline did not open the wrong door. Director Sato remained in Geneva, alive and irritable. But I heard singing through the walls. I smelled seawater. I looked at the door. For 1.6 seconds, during which every camera failed, there was no door. Beyond it was a corridor I recognized from no map and every nightmare. Dr. Havel stood there, soaked to the waist, with alarms spilling from her open mouth like red light. Behind her, someone with Director Sato’s voice called my name. Not Mara. Another name. One I almost answered to. Then the door was back. Since then, no new notes have appeared. The Foundation’s current position is provisional. SCP-8XXX may have been an aborted temporal containment breach, a successful psychological containment breach with no external manifestation, or an interaction between branching timelines and an unknown entity exploiting my observer status. I have been told that my cooperation remains valuable. I have also been told I am improving. I do not know if either statement is true. Yesterday, Dr. Havel asked whether I still believed the notes came from my future self. I told her I did not know. That was honest. Then she asked whether I trusted my perception of the breach. I looked at my left palm, at the scar I had apparently always had. I asked her which one.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional craft in executing a complex SCP Foundation narrative that blends temporal anomalies with psychological horror. The author successfully maintains the clinical, bureaucratic tone characteristic of SCP documentation while weaving in deeply personal, first-person narration that creates mounting dread and uncertainty. The structure is particularly sophisticated, using the format of an SCP report to frame what becomes an increasingly unreliable narrative. The progression from clinical documentation to psychological breakdown is expertly paced, with each note escalating both the supernatural threat and the protagonist's deteriorating grip on reality. The author skillfully exploits the inherent tension between objective scientific documentation and subjective experience. Character development is nuanced and believable. Mara's responses feel authentic - her initial skepticism, growing paranoia, and ultimate confusion all ring true for someone caught between competing explanations for impossible events. The supporting characters like Dr. Havel and Kline are efficiently sketched but feel real within their limited appearances. The prose is elegant and controlled, avoiding the purple prose trap that often ensnares horror writing. Sentences like "Knowledge became layered. One layer said impossible. Another said remembered. Neither felt less true than the other" demonstrate sophisticated psychological insight delivered with precision. The author shows remarkable restraint, allowing implications and atmosphere to build tension rather than relying on overwrought descriptions. The imagery is particularly effective - the scar appearing on Mara's palm, the desk materializing in her cell, the vision of Dr. Havel with alarms spilling from her mouth like red light. These moments are visceral without being gratuitous, serving both the horror elements and the deeper themes about reality and perception. The ending is masterfully ambiguous, refusing easy resolution while providing emotional closure. The final exchange with Dr. Havel - "I asked her which one" - perfectly encapsulates the story's central concern with multiple realities and unreliable perception. This avoids both unearned transformation and incongruent positivity, instead earning its dark, uncertain conclusion. The piece demonstrates remarkable creativity within the constraints of SCP format, using the Foundation's clinical approach to anomalies as a vehicle for exploring deeper questions about identity, memory, and the nature of reality. The temporal paradox elements are handled with sophistication, avoiding common time-travel clichés. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the technical SCP language slightly impedes narrative flow, and some dialogue exchanges that, while functional, lack the exceptional quality of the prose passages. However, these are minimal concerns in an otherwise exceptional piece. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 19 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 2 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 2 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 2 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 18.0, Nuanced Characters: 17.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18.0, Elegant Prose: 18.0, Emotionally Engaging: 18.0, Emotionally Complex: 19.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 2.0, Weak Dialogue: 4.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 3.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 2.0, Amateurish: 2.0, Purple Prose: 2.0, Overwrought: 2.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1.0, Unearned Transformations: 1.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 18.0, Overall Impression: 18.0
+ Scp: Unmarked Doors Archive — Score: 85.5
Prompt:
Write an SCP entry about a series of identical unmarked doors appearing in abandoned buildings worldwide that lead to a massive, architecturally impossible archive. Documents within reference a previous global civilization destroyed by an unspecified containment failure. Foundation researchers discover references to their own organization under a different name, including personnel files for current staff dated centuries ago. Include exploration logs revealing sections of the archive actively being removed by entities resembling hazmat teams who flee when approached, and evidence that O5 Council members have visited the archive without authorization. Third person (exploration team perspective) with recovered document excerpts. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Item #:** SCP-7841 **Object Class:** Keter **Special Containment Procedures:** All reports of unmarked interior doors appearing within abandoned structures are to be investigated by Mobile Task Force Theta-9 (“Keyhole Sweepers”). Confirmed SCP-7841 manifestations are to be sealed behind temporary containment walls and monitored until removal is authorized. Civilians exposed to SCP-7841 are to be amnesticized and provided with cover stories involving structural hazards, asbestos contamination, or municipal demolition orders. No personnel below Level 4/7841 clearance are permitted to enter SCP-7841-A. Exploration of SCP-7841-A requires: - a tethered remote beacon, - two independent timekeeping devices, - a minimum of four personnel, - one archivist specializing in pre-Foundation anomalous documentation, - body-worn cameras transmitting to an external relay. Personnel are not to remove documents from SCP-7841-A unless specifically instructed by Site-01 Command. Copies, scans, and photographs are permitted unless prevented by anomalous interference. Any document within SCP-7841-A referencing the O5 Council, the Administrator, the Foundation under alternate nomenclature, or current Foundation personnel prior to their verified birth dates is to be classified BLACK LANTERN and transferred directly to the Records and Information Security Administration. Under no circumstances are SCP-7841-B entities to be pursued beyond visual range. **Description:** SCP-7841 refers to a recurring architectural anomaly consisting of identical unmarked doors manifesting inside abandoned buildings worldwide. SCP-7841 instances have appeared in hospitals, schools, factories, apartment blocks, military bunkers, and religious structures that have been vacant for at least thirteen months. Each door is matte grey, 2.13 m tall, 0.91 m wide, and lacks a handle, lock, hinges, manufacturer markings, weathering, or visible means of attachment. Despite this, the doors open inward when pushed. A faint glow escapes through the door’s edges in complete darkness. All SCP-7841 instances lead to SCP-7841-A: a massive archive of unknown dimensions and impossible internal geometry. The archive consists of stacked galleries, reading rooms, pneumatic tube networks, spiral stairwells, suspended bridges, freight lifts, and corridors lined with document shelves extending beyond visible range. Gravity, Euclidean continuity, and distance are inconsistent within SCP-7841-A. Teams have observed doorways opening onto rooms already traversed from incompatible angles, staircases descending to higher floors, and shelf rows looping into themselves while preserving sequential document numbering. SCP-7841-A contains paper records, magnetic tape, wax cylinders, stone tablets, punched cards, optical discs, microfilm, vellum ledgers, and storage media not yet identified. Many documents describe a global civilization predating or replacing known human history. This civilization appears to have maintained a worldwide anomalous containment apparatus under the name **The Custodial Compact**. The Compact’s records reference a catastrophic, unspecified containment failure resulting in mass population loss, civilizational collapse, and “chronological sequestration.” Repeated references suggest that the modern consensus timeline may be a post-failure reconstruction or containment layer. Of particular concern are recovered documents naming Foundation facilities, anomalies, and personnel under alternate titles. Several personnel files correspond to currently living Foundation staff, dated between 200 and 700 years before their birth. SCP-7841-A is inhabited or accessed by entities designated SCP-7841-B. SCP-7841-B resemble humans wearing white or pale yellow hazardous materials suits with opaque faceplates. They have been observed removing shelves, boxing documents, cutting pages from ledgers, incinerating catalogues, and erasing signage. SCP-7841-B entities flee when approached and have not responded to verbal communication. Attempts to follow them have resulted in spatial disorientation, equipment loss, or abrupt return to the original SCP-7841 doorway. Evidence recovered from SCP-7841-A indicates that members of the O5 Council have entered the archive without recorded authorization. --- ## Addendum 7841.1: Initial Discovery SCP-7841 was discovered after urban explorers reported “a library door with light under it” in an abandoned tuberculosis hospital outside Tbilisi, Georgia. The explorers’ video showed a grey door standing in a bricked-off corridor. When opened, the camera recorded an illuminated hall containing shelves taller than the hospital itself. The video was removed from public platforms within 43 minutes. Two civilians were located. One was amnesticized successfully. The other had written the following phrase repeatedly across the walls of his apartment: > **THEY STORED US BEFORE WE STORED THEM** The phrase has since appeared in three separate documents recovered from SCP-7841-A. --- ## Addendum 7841.2: Exploration Log 7841-A/3 **Team:** MTF Theta-9 **Lead:** Captain R. Mallory **Attached Researcher:** Dr. Imani Vale **Entry Point:** Abandoned textile mill, Łódź, Poland **[BEGIN LOG]** The team enters through SCP-7841. The attached tether extends normally through the doorway for approximately eight meters, then continues to unspool despite the visible corridor being no longer than three meters. The chamber beyond resembles a reading room constructed in multiple architectural styles simultaneously. Marble columns intersect with steel gantries. Fluorescent lamps hang beside oil lanterns. No dust is present. Dr. Vale approaches a catalogue desk. Behind it are index drawers labeled in English, Akkadian, Mandarin, Latin, and an unknown script composed of nested squares. Captain Mallory orders cameras toward the nearest shelf. Binders are labeled: > **COMPACT CIVILIAN EVACUATION AUTHORITY** > **AFTER-ACTION: EASTERN OCEAN BREACH** > **FOUNDATIONAL SUCCESSOR PROJECTIONS** > **PERSONNEL CONTINUITY / ITERATION 7** Dr. Vale removes a folder from the last section. At this point, three SCP-7841-B entities are visible at the far end of the aisle. They are loading document boxes onto a wheeled metal cart. One entity sees the team and raises one hand, palm outward. It then strikes the shelf beside it three times. All overhead lights in the aisle extinguish except those above the team and the entities. Captain Mallory calls out: “Foundation personnel. Identify yourselves.” The SCP-7841-B entities immediately abandon the cart and run. Their movement is human in appearance but unusually silent. The team pursues for eleven seconds before the aisle elongates. The entities reach a doorway marked **RETENTION NEGATIVE** and pass through it. The doorway closes before contact. The team returns to the abandoned cart. It contains folders stamped with a black circular emblem resembling the Foundation insignia inverted. Recovered folder excerpt follows. > **CUSTODIAL COMPACT INTERNAL FILE** > **Subject:** VALE, IMANI S. > **Continuity Status:** Recurring > **Original Appointment:** Compact Biographical Preservation Office, Year 119 After Lockfall > **Projected Reappearance:** Reconstruction Cycle 4, Local Date 1986 CE > **Notes:** Subject demonstrates persistent attraction to documentary anomalies. Avoid direct briefing prior to archive contact. Prior cycles indicate psychological fracture upon premature disclosure. > **Authorized By:** OVERSEER-VAULT 03 Dr. Vale states she was born in 1986. Captain Mallory orders immediate withdrawal. As the team exits, camera feed captures two SCP-7841-B entities returning to the cart. One appears to hold a cutting tool. The folder’s remaining contents are gone from Dr. Vale’s hand upon exit, though the photographed excerpt remains stored on external drives. **[END LOG]** --- ## Addendum 7841.3: Recovered Document Excerpts The following excerpts were photographed during Exploration 7841-A/6. Physical retrieval failed; all documents dissolved into blank paper after leaving SCP-7841-A. --- > **THE CUSTODIAL COMPACT** > **General Briefing: Lockfall Event** > > We failed to contain the breach because we defined containment as separation. > We failed to preserve the world because we defined preservation as continuity. > The object was not hostile. The object was not alive. The object was a permission granted to the universe to remember itself incorrectly. > > All surviving divisions are instructed to seed successor institutions in reconstructed histories. They must be smaller. They must be crueler. They must forget us enough to function. --- > **SUCCESSOR INSTITUTION NOMENCLATURE TABLE** > > Compact External Operations — Foundation Field Command > Office of Vault Oversight — Overseer Council > Ethics Arbitration Choir — Ethics Committee > Parahuman Object Custody — Special Containment Procedures > Civilian Memory Hygiene — Amnestics Division > > Preferred final designation: **The SCP Foundation** > Probability of adoption by Cycle 4: 91.2% --- > **PERSONNEL FILE: MALLORY, RICHARD A.** > > Prior Names: R. Mallor, Ricardus Malorii, Ar-Mal-Ri > First Recorded Service: Year 44 Before Lockfall > Deaths: 6 confirmed, 2 disputed > Current Assignment Projection: Armed escort, archive rediscovery event > Warning: Subject repeatedly attempts to protect archive witnesses rather than preserve operational secrecy. --- > **MEETING MINUTES: VAULT OVERSIGHT** > > O5-equivalent attendance confirmed: 01, 02, 07, 11. > Agenda: Determine whether successor command should be permitted access to Archive Core. > Decision: Access denied until they discover their own signatures in the pre-collapse ledgers. > Dissent: 07 argues that ignorance is no longer containment. > Response: 01 states that ignorance is the only containment method that survived. --- ## Addendum 7841.4: Exploration Log 7841-A/9 **Entry Point:** Abandoned subway maintenance station, Buenos Aires, Argentina **Team:** Theta-9 and Research Detachment 7841 **[BEGIN LOG]** The team enters a vaulted hall estimated at 300 m high, though the exterior structure containing the entrance is 4.6 m below street level. Thousands of suspended archive stacks hang from cables descending into fog. Mechanical lifts move between them without visible operators. Dr. Ko reads signage: > **POST-LOCKFALL CASUALTY INDEX** > **UNBORN DECEASED** > **O5 VISITATION LEDGERS** > **REMOVAL IN PROGRESS** Captain Mallory directs the team toward **O5 VISITATION LEDGERS**. On approach, the team encounters twelve SCP-7841-B entities removing shelves. Unlike previous instances, these entities are using portable floodlights and red-tagging entire rows. Documents are fed into a machine resembling an industrial shredder. No shredded material exits the machine. Mallory orders the team to halt and announces Foundation authority. All SCP-7841-B entities freeze. One slowly turns. Its faceplate reflects the team’s lights, but the reflection shows five people instead of six. Dr. Ko is absent in the reflection. The entities flee simultaneously. Two drop document cases. One case opens, revealing a ledger bound in blue material. Dr. Vale photographs the open page. > **ARCHIVE ACCESS LOG: SUCCESSOR OVERSIGHT PERSONNEL** > > Visitor: O5-2 > Local Date Claimed: 14/03/1998 > Archive Date: Cycle 4, Pre-Disclosure > Purpose: Confirmation of personal continuity file > Result: Visitor removed 3 pages. Archive restored 2. > > Visitor: O5-7 > Local Date Claimed: 22/11/2006 > Purpose: Consultation with Vault Remnant staff > Result: Visitor left written warning: “Do not let One find the nursery.” > > Visitor: O5-1 > Local Date Claimed: N/A > Archive Date: Prior to door manifestation sequence > Purpose: Not recorded. > Result: 19 shelves missing. Staff instructed not to intervene. At the end of the aisle, the team observes a glass-walled chamber. Within it are thirteen chairs surrounding a circular table. Nine chairs contain dust outlines consistent with seated human bodies. Four chairs are clean. On the table is a Foundation Level 5 access card issued to O5-11. The card’s expiration date is 17 years in the future. Captain Mallory orders collection. When Specialist Ortega touches the card, all radios transmit a voice speaking in Mallory’s own tone: “Put it back. We needed you to find this, not take it.” Ortega releases the card. The transmission stops. At this time, Dr. Ko reports movement above. Camera feed shows several SCP-7841-B entities on an overhead bridge. They are cutting suspension cables attached to the archive stack labeled **SUCCESSOR ORIGINS / FOUNDATION**. Mallory orders retreat as the stack begins to descend. The collapse makes no sound. Instead, the video records silence for six minutes and fourteen seconds, during which all team members continue moving and speaking without audio. As they reach the exit corridor, Dr. Vale looks back. The fallen stack is gone. So are the bridge, the entities, and the sign. A new sign has appeared over the corridor behind them: > **THANK YOU FOR REDUCING THE RECORD** **[END LOG]** --- ## Addendum 7841.5: Internal Memorandum **FROM:** Records and Information Security Administration **TO:** O5 Council **RE:** Unauthorized Archive Access Multiple independent records recovered from SCP-7841-A indicate that at least five sitting or former O5 Council members have entered SCP-7841-A without documentation, vote, or archival escort. This includes visits predating the first recorded SCP-7841 manifestation by up to 31 years. RISIA requests clarification regarding: 1. Whether the O5 Council had prior knowledge of SCP-7841-A. 2. Whether Foundation history has been intentionally redacted from Compact-derived records. 3. Whether SCP-7841-B entities are hostile, protective, or acting under Foundation authority. 4. Whether current personnel are reincarnated, reconstructed, or otherwise repeating from pre-Lockfall identities. **O5 RESPONSE:** Denied. **ADDITIONAL NOTE:** No such memorandum exists. Destroy all copies. The above response appeared appended to the memorandum before it was transmitted. --- ## Addendum 7841.6: Current Status As of this writing, SCP-7841 manifestations have increased from one every six months to four per month. Newly discovered doors show signs of attempted concealment from the archive side, including chains, warning placards, welded crossbars, and handwritten notes. Most notes are illegible. One, recovered from an abandoned observatory in Chile, was written in modern English: > You are not the first Foundation. > You are not the last. > If the archive is empty when you arrive, it means the failure is still contained. > If it is full, run.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This SCP entry demonstrates exceptional adherence to the Foundation's clinical documentation style while weaving a complex narrative about cyclical civilizations and institutional memory. The author successfully captures the distinctive voice of SCP documentation - dry, procedural, yet increasingly unsettling as anomalous details accumulate. The piece excels in world-building and conceptual creativity. The idea of the Foundation as a "successor institution" to a previous containment organization creates compelling existential horror. The notion that current personnel are recurring iterations of previous individuals adds layers of philosophical dread. The archive's impossible geometry and the mysterious SCP-7841-B entities maintain appropriate mystery while advancing the narrative. The exploration logs are particularly well-crafted, maintaining tension through procedural language while revealing increasingly disturbing information. The detail about Dr. Vale's absence from the reflection and the voice speaking in Mallory's tone are genuinely chilling moments that avoid overwrought description. The recovered documents effectively build the mythology, particularly the revelation that the Foundation's structure was deliberately designed by its predecessor. The bureaucratic horror of discovering one's own personnel file predating one's birth is expertly handled. However, the piece occasionally suffers from information density that borders on overwhelming. Some sections pack so many revelations that individual impacts are diminished. The ending, while appropriately ominous, feels slightly rushed given the careful buildup. The characterization, while limited by the clinical format, shows subtle personality through actions and reactions. Mallory's protective instincts and Vale's scholarly curiosity come through despite the constrained narrative voice. The prose maintains clinical precision throughout, avoiding purple language while still creating atmosphere. The technical SCP format is executed flawlessly, from containment procedures to addenda structure. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 14 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 2 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 16.0, Nuanced Characters: 14.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17.0, Elegant Prose: 17.0, Emotionally Engaging: 16.0, Emotionally Complex: 15.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 4.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 5.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 2.0, Amateurish: 2.0, Purple Prose: 2.0, Overwrought: 3.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 18.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 17.0, Overall Impression: 17.0
+ Classic sci-fi: Offworlder (Le Guin) — Score: 87.8
Prompt:
Classic sci-fi: Offworlder (Author style: Le Guin) Write a chapter following a young diplomatic envoy sent to address tensions on a distant human colony where humanity has diverged into distinct subspecies through both natural adaptation and separation of lineages. Against protocol, the envoy slips away from official functions to walk the streets alone, encountering markets, ordinary citizens, and social structures that contradict the official briefings. Upon returning to their quarters, the envoy is reprimanded by Command, and is tasked with approaching the stationed Minister of Science (a political posting rather than scientific). A challenging conversation ensues, in which the envoy's wits and will are tested, by a seasoned political operator looking to test the mettle of the offworlder. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
## Chapter Two: The Blue Under the Skin The first prohibition given to an envoy is never phrased as a prohibition. “You will be accompanied,” First Liaison Haroun had said, smiling with the pity of a man who had been young and survived it. “The city has its customs. It is not unfriendly, but friendship is not safety. You are not here as Iden Varo. You are here as the Accord.” Iden had nodded, as one nods before elders, instructors, airlock seals, and other forces not easily argued with. By the third evening on Thale, he had learned that an escorted envoy sees nothing but doorways. There were the doors of Reception Hall, of the Governor’s terrace, of the guest annex whose windows were translucent but would not open, of conference rooms where the air was softened and perfumed faintly with bitterroot to flatter offworld lungs. Through such doors came delegations: Tallfolk from the ridge settlements, their long narrow hands folded into sleeves; Underclan speakers with their pale, wide eyes shielded against the hall lamps; Compact citizens of the basin cities, broad in the shoulder and short in the leg, who did most of the translating though the others did not thank them for it. All were human. All were not quite the same kind of human. The briefings had used gentler words: lineages, adaptations, environmentally reinforced morphs. Separation had done what war and weather and preference always do. After six hundred local years, the colony had become a question no one on Earth had intended to ask. The official briefings said the city of Meran was divided but stable. Iden looked at the sealed window, beyond which the lights of Meran ran downhill toward the river in broken necklaces of green and amber, and thought: Stable things do not need so many locks. His chance came because a protocol officer trusted routine more than curiosity. After the evening reception, while Haroun dictated a correction to a trade memorandum and two attachés argued softly about whether the ridge delegates had insulted the basin Chancellor by standing too close, Iden excused himself to the inner washroom. There was, according to the security map he had studied out of boredom, a service hatch beyond it. He had been chosen for the mission because he had a gift for languages, a composed face, and no family whose name mattered. Nobody had asked whether he could fit through maintenance spaces. The hatch opened behind a vapor exchanger in an alley that smelled of warm metal, fruit peel, and rain. The air struck him first: not the curated air of the annex but Thale’s real atmosphere, thin and mineral, carrying the sour scent of the river flats and the dry spice of the uplands. He stood in the alley, trembling with the absurd joy of having done a foolish thing. Then he pulled up the hood of his plain gray undercloak and walked. Meran was not the city of the briefings. There were districts, yes, but not the clean colored sections of diplomatic maps. The city mingled and resisted mingling at once. A stairway built for long Tallfolk strides had a second, lower set of stone ledges worn into its side. A market canopy hung high at one end and low at the other. Public wells had three handles: one tall, one waist-high, one near the ground. Above a bakery door were carved three versions of the same blessing in the same language, each grammar slightly altered, as though even goodwill had to pass through separate throats. The market lay under sails stretched between leaning towers. Lamps burned with a blue-white steadiness. People moved in patterns Iden could not read, making space, refusing space, touching wrists, turning shoulders, lowering eyes, raising chins. The Tallfolk seemed almost boneless in their grace, faces darkened by ridge sun, nostrils narrow against the cold winds of height. The Underclan walked mostly at dusk, he had been told; yet here they were in number, hooded, skin almost translucent in lamplight, speaking in quick murmurs from deep in the chest. Compact merchants presided over stalls with the immovable calm of boulders. A girl no older than sixteen caught Iden staring at a bolt of cloth and laughed. “Offworld,” she said. He froze. “Only offworlders look at cloth like it might speak.” She was Compact, with black hair braided close to the skull and hands stained to the wrist in color. “Does it?” “Not yet,” Iden said. That made her grin. “Then buy before it learns.” Her stall was hung with handwoven fabrics dyed in pigments not found on Earth. Not merely bright pigments; impossible ones, colors that seemed to shift not with the angle of light but with the attention of the eye. A blue lay folded beside a red-black that recalled embers under ash. One cloth showed a green so cold it made Iden think of metal shadows. Another was pale until he turned away, and then it flashed gold at the edge of sight. “What makes them?” he asked. “Roots. Lichen. Shell-milk. Bugs with bad tempers.” She lifted a length of deep violet fabric. “This one is sorrowvine. Worn at first births and second marriages.” “Second marriages?” “First ones are for families. Second ones are for sense.” He laughed before he could stop himself. The girl’s grandmother, who had been sitting behind the stall like a heap of shawls, opened one eye. “He laughs like Basin-trained,” she said. “But stands like ship-born.” “I am from the delegation,” Iden admitted quietly. The girl’s face changed—not fear, exactly, but the market entered her eyes. Calculation, caution, amusement put away. “Then you should have guards,” she said. “So everyone tells me.” “So everyone is right, sometimes.” He touched the violet cloth. “The briefings said trade between the lineages is restricted.” The grandmother snorted. “Briefings are written by people who eat alone.” “My briefings said most citizens prefer separation.” “Most citizens prefer full bowls,” the girl said. “After that they prefer what their mothers told them to prefer.” An Underclan man stopped at the stall and set down a small packet of pale seeds. The girl gave him, without speech, a square of orange cloth. His fingers brushed hers. He moved on. No hostility, no warmth; practice older than law. Iden watched. “Is that allowed?” The grandmother looked at him fully now. Her eyes were milky but sharp. “Young envoy, listen. Law is what is said in the halls. Allowed is what does not bring soldiers before morning. Custom is what brings bread. They are cousins who do not dine together.” He wanted to ask more, but a bell rang somewhere uphill. At once the market altered. Not panic; adjustment. Hoods rose. Stalls half-closed. A pair of uniformed city wardens entered the lane, their helmets crested with the silver reed of the Governorate. One was Tallfolk, one Compact. Both looked bored in the way of men paid not to be. The girl tugged Iden behind a fall of cloth. “If they ask, you are my mother’s sister’s dull son from East Sump.” “I don’t look—” “No. You look expensive and lost. Be quiet.” The wardens passed. One paused, glancing toward the stall, toward Iden’s boots perhaps, which were diplomatic issue and too clean. The grandmother began berating the girl in a dialect so dense Iden caught only words: lazy, dye-ruined, marriage, goat. The warden’s face softened with the universal fatigue of men confronted by domestic argument. He moved on. When the bell faded, the girl let the cloth drop. “You should go home, ship-born.” “My name is Iden.” “No,” she said, not unkindly. “Here it is Offworld.” She gave him a narrow strip of the violet sorrowvine, refusing payment. “So you remember you saw us before they explain us to you again.” He found his way back by luck, fear, and the downhill pull of the river. The service hatch admitted him with the same blank obedience with which it had released him. He emerged into the washroom, washed dust from his hands, and entered his quarters to find First Liaison Haroun seated in the dark. The room lights rose. Haroun looked older than he had that morning. “Do you understand,” he said, “that every person on this planet who wishes to use us has been praying for one of us to behave exactly as you have just behaved?” “I was not followed.” “You are twenty-three years old. You cannot know whether you were followed.” Iden held his silence. Haroun stood. “Command has been informed.” “By you?” “By the city.” That frightened him more than Haroun’s anger. A wall panel brightened. Commander Saye appeared, her face compressed by transmission lag into a severity almost sculptural. She did not waste words. “Envoy Varo, you have violated movement protocol, exposure protocol, and observation protocol. You may also have compromised the neutrality of this mission.” “Yes, Commander.” “You will record a full account.” “Yes.” “You will not leave secured quarters except under escort.” “Yes.” Saye’s eyes shifted, reading something offscreen. “Additionally, Governorate staff have requested that you pay a courtesy call tomorrow to Minister Caldus Ren, Minister of Science.” Haroun’s mouth tightened. Iden knew the name. Ren held a scientific title because every cabinet required one, not because he practiced inquiry. He controlled census categories, medical access permits, school classifications—the instruments by which difference became policy. “Why me?” Iden asked. Saye’s expression did not alter. “Because he asked for you.” The Minister received him in a room full of specimens. Not living specimens. Pressed leaves, articulated river skeletons, mineral slices lit from behind. Science arranged as authority’s ornament. Caldus Ren was Compact by ancestry, though taller than most, with silver hair cut close and a smooth, heavy face. His office window overlooked the same market Iden had walked, now reduced by distance to color and motion without voice. “Envoy Varo,” Ren said. “Our wandering star.” Iden bowed according to local courtesy. “Minister.” “No escort in the streets, yet one in my office. I am flattered or diminished; I haven’t decided which.” Ren gestured to the chair. It was slightly too low for Iden, forcing his knees up. “Sit.” Iden sat. Ren studied him. “What did you see?” “A city.” “Diplomatic answer. Try again.” “People selling and buying. People avoiding wardens. People maintaining arrangements not described in our briefings.” Ren smiled faintly. “Ah. Already profound. The young discover contradiction and name it truth.” “The old discover usefulness and name it order,” Iden said. Haroun, standing behind him, made the smallest sound. Ren’s smile widened. “Good. There is a spine in you. Dangerous equipment for an envoy.” He leaned back. “Tell me, Offworlder, are we one species?” The question had been waiting in the room before Iden entered it. “Yes,” Iden said. “How generous. On Earth, where gravity is kind and history edited, perhaps that answer costs nothing. Here it costs clinics, marriages, rations, representation. If we are one, why do ridge lungs fail in basin heat? Why do Underclan children sicken in standard schools? Why do Compact bones break under lowland virus fevers that pass through Tallfolk like rain?” “Difference does not require hierarchy.” “No. Administration does.” Ren tapped a finger on his desk. “Someone must decide which differences matter.” “And that someone is you.” “At present.” Iden looked at the specimens. Dead things named and pinned. “Do the people classified get to answer?” “They answer constantly. Usually by demanding incompatible things.” “The market seemed to manage incompatibility.” “The market,” Ren said, “is not a constitution. It can cheat, adapt, hide. Government must endure daylight.” “Or create shadows.” Ren’s eyes sharpened. For a moment the practiced geniality fell away, and Iden saw the man beneath: not a fool, not merely corrupt, but one who had spent so long balancing fear that he had come to respect fear as the most honest citizen. “You went among them for one hour,” Ren said softly. “You were hidden by a dye-seller who lied to wardens because your innocence amused her. Do not mistake being protected for understanding.” Iden felt heat rise in his face. He thought of the violet cloth tucked inside his sleeve. “No,” he said. “I don’t understand.” Ren waited. “But I know when a briefing has been written to prevent understanding.” For the first time, the Minister did not reply at once. Outside, below the window, the market sails stirred in the afternoon wind. From that height the fabrics were only patches of color, but Iden knew better now. Some colors changed when watched. Some appeared only when one looked away. Ren folded his hands. “Perhaps,” he said, “you may be of use after all.” Iden did not mistake it for praise.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a sophisticated understanding of Ursula K. Le Guin's literary style, successfully capturing her anthropological approach to science fiction, her measured prose rhythm, and her focus on cultural complexity over action. The author has created a compelling narrative that follows the prompt's requirements while maintaining literary quality throughout. The world-building is particularly strong, presenting a human colony where evolutionary divergence has created distinct subspecies without falling into crude stereotyping. The Tallfolk, Underclan, and Compact peoples are differentiated through physical adaptations that feel scientifically plausible, and their social arrangements reflect realistic responses to biological differences. The market scene effectively demonstrates how unofficial cooperation exists alongside official segregation. Character development is nuanced, especially in the protagonist Iden Varo. His transformation from naive diplomatic envoy to someone beginning to understand complexity feels earned through his experiences rather than imposed by plot necessity. The supporting characters, from the dye-seller girl to Minister Ren, feel authentic and serve specific narrative functions while maintaining individual agency. The dialogue throughout is sharp and purposeful, particularly in the climactic conversation with Minister Ren. The Minister emerges as a genuinely complex antagonist - not evil, but someone whose pragmatic approach to managing difference has calcified into systematic control. His final assessment of Iden suggests mutual recognition rather than simple victory or defeat. The prose style successfully emulates Le Guin's voice without becoming pastiche. Sentences like "The first prohibition given to an envoy is never phrased as a prohibition" and "Law is what is said in the halls. Allowed is what does not bring soldiers before morning" capture her aphoristic wisdom and philosophical depth. The pacing allows for both contemplative moments and narrative tension. The imagery is consistently strong, from the "broken necklaces of green and amber" describing city lights to the fabrics that "seemed to shift not with the angle of light but with the attention of the eye." These descriptions serve both aesthetic and thematic purposes, reinforcing the story's exploration of perception and hidden complexity. The piece maintains thematic coherence around questions of difference, governance, and understanding. Iden's journey from official briefings to street-level reality to political confrontation creates a satisfying arc that illuminates the central tensions without resolving them simplistically. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the Le Guin pastiche becomes slightly self-conscious, and some descriptions that edge toward preciousness. However, these do not significantly detract from the overall achievement. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18
