+ Cozy sci-fi: The Last Outpost — Score: 81.3
Prompt:
Cozy sci-fi The Last Outpost Nettle (or Nets to her friends) bartends at The Last Outpost -- a lazy hub for miners and merchants in an asteroid belt on the Andromeda outer rim. Escaping from her past life, Nets found a home here at the Outpost. They have, thus far, tolerated Net's flaws which she tries to keep in equilibrium with her assortment of talents. Her evenings involve chatting with patrons, negotiating the drama, intrigue, problems & love lives of the diverse crowd that filter through the Outpost. Tonight is no different, at least, until a frazzled looking stranger, tentacled dreads askew, quietly plops down at the bar: "Vega whisky, neat. Better make that a double." Write a chapter in this story. Establish conflict for narrative interest. Don't resolve the conflict within this chapter. Aim for about 60% dialogue. First person, present tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The bell over the lock chimes three times, which means the outer door is sulking again. I don’t look up from the drink I’m building. If you reward a door with attention every time it has feelings, you end up spending your whole life in conversation with hinges. “Jax,” I call, “if you’re leaning on the seal again, I’ll pour your next stout into your lap.” “I am a paying customer and a decorated freight captain,” Jax calls back from Booth Six. “You are a paying customer and a public nuisance.” “You wound me, Nets.” “I’m practicing.” A ripple of laughter moves through The Last Outpost, soft as static. It’s a lazy sort of night, the kind the place does best. Miners off second-shift still have dust in the creases of their faces and under their nails, glittering faintly where the scrubbers missed. Two merchant cousins from Pella Station are playing low-stakes comet dice with an old surveyor who is cheating so badly I’m beginning to suspect the cousins are letting him, for the affection of it. The band is off tonight, so the ceiling vents hum bass and the old refrigeration unit clicks percussion behind the bar. Home, in other words. Home with questionable wiring, a sticky spot by table three no mop has ever truly defeated, and a clientele that will tell you your haircut is awful before they tell you your oxygen tank is leaking. I slide a blue-green shimmer fizz to Lulo, whose frilled gills flare in approval. “You put lime in this?” they ask. “I put something lime-adjacent in it.” “Is it legal?” “In which jurisdiction?” Lulo considers this, then lifts the glass. “Good enough.” The lock chime sounds again: three bright notes, then a hiccuping whirr. This time I do look up. The inner hatch rolls open, shedding a gust of cold recycled air and a stranger. That alone is interesting. We get new faces, sure, but most people arrive in ways that announce them: miners in clusters, merchants wrapped in profit and perfume, pilots loud enough to beat the docking clamps. This one enters like they’re hoping the room will forget to notice. No such luck. They’re tall, or would be if they weren’t folded inward with exhaustion. Their skin is a deep storm-gray, sheened with the pearly undertone of someone who grew up under filtered ocean light. Their hair—no, tentacles, dozens of narrow tentacled dreads—hangs askew around their face, some braided with copper rings, some loose and twitching like they’re hearing bad news independently of the rest of the body. One tentacle has a burn scoring down its length, fresh enough that my stomach tightens. Their coat is too fine for this belt and too torn for any place finer. The left sleeve is missing. The right pocket flashes once with a private security seal before their hand clamps over it. They cross to the bar. The regulars pretend not to stare with varying levels of failure. The stranger plops down on the middle stool, the one with the patched cushion, and says, very quietly, “Vega whisky, neat. Better make that a double.” Their voice is rough around the edges, like it’s been dragged through a maintenance shaft. I reach for the bottle. “Rough docking?” I ask. The stranger gives a laugh with no humor in it. “Something like that.” “Something like that costs extra as a story.” “Then I can’t afford to tell it.” I set a heavy glass on the bar and pour two fingers, then a third because I’m a weak woman with a professional interest in haunted expressions. They look at the pour. “That’s generous.” “That’s observation. Vega’s wasted on people who sip it for fashion.” “Do I look fashionable?” “You look expensive and recently exploded.” One of the tentacles near their ear curls defensively. “Accurate.” They take the whisky in both hands, but don’t drink yet. Their fingers tremble once, hard, and stop. I lean my elbows on the counter. “Name?” They look at me over the rim of the glass. Their eyes are black all the way through except for a ring of pale gold around each pupil, as if someone dropped coins into deep water. “Sol,” they say after too long. “Just Sol?” “Tonight.” “That kind of night.” I tap the bar. “I’m Nets.” “Nettle?” they ask. The name hits the air between us wrong. Not because people don’t know it. Plenty do. The Outpost register lists me as Nettle Rhys because the station bureaucracy refuses to accept nicknames, aliases, or spiritual rebirths as legally binding. But strangers don’t usually reach for the long form first. My smile stays where I put it. “Friends call me Nets.” “Are we friends?” “Not until you pay your tab.” Behind me, the glass washer emits a worrying clunk. I ignore it with the confidence of someone who has ignored worse. Sol finally drinks. Their eyes close for half a second. When they open, something in their posture has unkinked. “That’s real Vega.” “I don’t serve fake whisky unless a customer insults my plants.” Their gaze flicks past me to the shelf of hydroponic herbs growing under amber lamps. Basil, ghost mint, three sulking sprigs of tarragon, and Henry, my venom-nettle, who has survived two decompressions and one bachelorette party. “You grow nettles,” Sol says. “I contain nettles. There’s a legal distinction.” At Booth Six, Jax raises his voice. “Nets! Is Henry allowed to be reaching toward me like that?” “He senses weakness.” “He senses my sausage crisps.” “Same difference.” Sol’s mouth twitches. Not a smile, exactly. The ghost of one circling a landing pad. Good. Ghosts can be worked with. I wipe down the counter near them though it is already clean enough by Outpost standards. “You docked on Bay Two?” A pause. Too small for most people. Not for bartenders. Bartenders hear pauses the way mechanics hear engine ticks. “Bay Four,” Sol says. “Bay Four’s closed.” “Is it?” “For repairs since Marnie tried to reverse-thrust a grain hauler through the clamp.” Marnie, at table three, lifts her cup without looking over. “History will vindicate me.” “History filed a complaint,” I tell her. Sol’s fingers tighten around the glass. I keep my voice mild. “So let’s try again. Which bay?” Before they can answer, something shatters in the background of the bar. The whole room flinches. A glass has gone off a table near the dice game and exploded across the floor into bright, harmless fragments. The old surveyor holds up both hands. “Gravity surge,” he says. “There was no gravity surge,” says one of the Pella cousins. “There was in my elbow.” “I’ll get it.” I point at the shards. “Nobody move unless you enjoy bleeding artistically.” As I duck for the broom, Sol shifts. Fast. Their head turns toward the hatch, tentacled dreads rising slightly. Listening. Not to the broken glass. To something beyond it. The outer lock chimes once. Not three times. Once. Everyone notices that. Even the refrigeration unit seems to miss a click. The inner hatch display flashes amber: INCOMING DOCK QUERY. We are a lazy hub in an asteroid belt, not a fortress, but doors are like old gossips. They know when to whisper. Jax sits up straighter. Lulo sets down their drink. The cousins stop arguing about gravity. Marnie’s hand slips under the table, probably toward something she insists is a “calibration tool” and absolutely not a plasma puncher. Sol says, very softly, “Do you have a back way out?” I keep sweeping glass. “Everybody thinks bars have back ways out.” “Do they?” “Of course. But I prefer to know why someone needs one before I point.” They look at the amber hatch light. “I’m being followed.” There it is. The little stone dropped into the cup. Ripples everywhere. “By whom?” I ask. Sol’s jaw works. “People with uniforms.” “Uniforms cover a lot of sins.” “These are black with silver collars. Consortium security.” The room gets colder without the vents changing. The Andromeda Extraction Consortium owns three-quarters of the belt on paper and half of everyone’s debt in practice. Their security teams don’t visit places like The Last Outpost unless they’re collecting, seizing, or apologizing to no one. Jax murmurs, “Oh, stars.” I set the broom aside. “What did you do?” Sol looks at me then, really looks, and the exhaustion drops away enough to show the fear underneath. Sharp. Awake. Contagious. “I left.” “That’s usually allowed.” “Not from where I left.” The hatch display pings again: DOCK QUERY REPEATED. AUTH CODE: CONSORTIUM PRIORITY. Across the bar, conversations die in little pieces. I dry my hands on a towel because they want something to do besides shake. The Consortium doesn’t know me as Nets. That is the one mercy I have spent five years cultivating. Here I am a bartender with a temperamental plant, a decent pour, and a talent for remembering anniversaries. I am not a person who ran cargo through unregistered lanes. I am not a person who owes favors to ghosts in black markets. I am not a person whose fingerprints still live in sealed warrants under another name. Probably. “Sol,” I say, “if they come in here with a legal warrant, this becomes difficult.” “They won’t have a warrant.” “You sound sure.” “They don’t want a record.” “That is worse, in case you were wondering.” Sol reaches inside the torn coat. Half the bar makes a quiet movement toward cover. “Slow,” I say. They freeze, then withdraw two fingers holding a data sliver no longer than a thumbnail. It glows faintly red through their translucent nails. “I need to get this to the relay at Kepler’s Wake,” they say. “Or to someone who can broadcast beyond Consortium reach.” I stare at the sliver. Every sensible part of me climbs onto a stool inside my skull and begins screaming. “What’s on it?” “Proof.” “Of?” Sol swallows. One of the burnt tentacles curls against their neck. “They found something in the deep belt. Not ore. Not ice. Something alive, maybe. Old. They’re cutting into it.” Lulo whispers, “Cutting into what?” Sol doesn’t look away from me. “I don’t know. But the miners who saw it started getting sick. Dreaming the same dreams. Speaking languages they never learned. When the med teams complained, the Consortium spaced the whole clinic module and called it a reactor fault.” No one speaks. The amber display turns red. DOCK OVERRIDE REQUESTED. Jax stands. “Nets.” “I see it.” “No, you don’t,” Sol says. “If they traced me here, they won’t just search. They’ll scrub the logs. Cameras. Dock records. Witnesses.” Marnie’s chair scrapes back. “Are we witnesses?” “Not if you were all conveniently drunk,” I say. “Nets.” “I’m thinking.” “You’re joking,” Sol says. “I joke while thinking. It keeps the panic from driving.” The hatch speaker crackles. “Last Outpost,” says a smooth voice, genderless and cold. “This is Consortium Security Vessel Argent Knife. We are initiating priority inspection under Rim Safety Provision Twelve. Open your lock.” Marnie mutters, “Provision Twelve is for plague ships.” “Maybe they think we’re contagious,” Jax says. “We are,” Lulo replies. “Emotionally.” I look at Sol. “Did you bring plague to my bar?” “No.” “A dangerous artifact?” “No.” “A swarm?” “What? No.” “Because we have a house policy about swarms.” The speaker crackles again. “Failure to comply will result in forced entry.” Sol’s hand closes around the data sliver. “If they take me, break it.” “Break it?” “If you can’t send it, break it. Don’t let them have it.” There’s a plea in their voice, but also something else. Calculation. They are asking me for trust while offering me trouble in a glass with no ice. I glance around my bar. Jax has his captain face on, which means bad decisions are lining up behind his eyes. Lulo’s gills flutter with terror and outrage. The surveyor is still holding a broom like a spear. The Pella cousins are whispering furiously into a comm, probably to each other despite being shoulder to shoulder. Marnie has definitely found her calibration tool. These people tolerated my flaws. My sharp tongue. My locked drawer. My habit of disappearing every year on the same three dates and coming back with new bruises I blame on maintenance. They gave me a stool behind the bar and a birthday I never told them and a place for Henry to menace the public. Home is a dangerous thing to have. It gives the universe something to threaten. I pick up Sol’s empty glass and pour the rest of the double they ordered. “Drink,” I tell them. Their eyes widen. “What?” “You paid for it, didn’t you?” “No.” “Then you’re starting a tab.” The red hatch light strobes over the bottles, turning every label bloody. “Nets,” Jax says carefully, “what are we doing?” I slide the whisky to Sol, then reach under the bar for the old manual switch I have never had to use and always hoped I wouldn’t. My fingers close around the cool metal handle. “We are not opening the lock,” I say. The speaker hisses, “Last Outpost, confirm compliance.” I smile at Sol, though my heart is kicking dents into my ribs. “Welcome to The Last Outpost,” I say. “First rule: we don’t let strangers leave thirsty.” Then I pull the switch. Every light in the bar goes out.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong craft in several key areas while maintaining the cozy sci-fi atmosphere requested. The author successfully establishes Nets as a compelling first-person narrator with a distinctive voice that balances wit, competence, and hidden vulnerability. Her dialogue crackles with personality - from her banter with regulars like Jax to her careful probing of the mysterious Sol. The 60% dialogue target is well-met, with conversations feeling natural and purposeful rather than forced. The worldbuilding is efficiently handled through specific details that feel lived-in: the sulking door, Henry the venom-nettle, the sticky spot by table three that "no mop has ever truly defeated." These touches create genuine coziness without sacrificing the sci-fi elements. The diverse cast of aliens feels integrated rather than tokenistic, with species-specific details like Lulo's gills and Sol's tentacled dreads serving both descriptive and emotional purposes. The conflict escalates naturally from Sol's arrival through the revelation about Consortium activities to the final standoff. The author avoids resolving the central tension while providing a satisfying chapter conclusion with Nets' decision to protect Sol. The stakes feel appropriately weighty - both the cosmic horror implications of the Consortium's discovery and the personal threat to Nets' carefully constructed new life. Character motivations ring true throughout. Sol's desperation, the regulars' protective instincts toward their bar, and Nets' internal struggle between self-preservation and doing what's right all feel earned. The piece hints at Nets' mysterious past without over-explaining, maintaining intrigue while providing enough context for her final choice to feel meaningful rather than arbitrary. The prose generally flows well with good rhythm and pacing. Some descriptions border on overly clever ("ghosts can be worked with," "bartenders hear pauses the way mechanics hear engine ticks") but mostly stay on the right side of the line. The ending moment with the lights going out provides genuine dramatic tension and a strong chapter close. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the wit feels slightly forced, and some world-building elements that could use more grounding. The piece also relies somewhat heavily on familiar sci-fi tropes (evil corporation, mysterious alien artifact, plucky underdogs), though it executes them competently. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 16

+ Young adult drama: Gone South — Score: 83.8
Prompt:
Young adult drama Gone South Taylor is a 16 year old teen with a history of "concerning behavioural patterns" (or that's how the stupid smug school counsellor worded it on the phone call to Mom). It shouldn't be a surprise: When you live in a backwater town in Tennessee, your choices are: a. be mind-fuckingly bored, or b. invent some drama. Two days ago, Taylor came out to their family as trans. This disclosure was *not* in the category of invented drama -- it was, in fact, the real deal, and a long time coming. It didn't go well. The last 48 hours had been pure disaster. In a moment of desperate inspiration, Taylor had decided to get the hell out of dodge. And so an escape plan was hatched and executed within the space of 30 minutes. That was last night. Now Taylor was two states away, having been unceremoniously ejected from their hitched ride in downtown New Orleans. Write the next chapter in Taylor's story as they find their feet in this new environment. Tell the chapter over the course of their first day in New Orleans, in which Taylor meets another runaway with issues and secrets. Taylor navigates this new world, not knowing who to trust or what the dangers are. Write in Taylor's voice, and incorporate their internal monologue as they grapple with intense & conflicting emotions. Also incorporate dialogue with the interesting characters Taylor meets. First person, mixed tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
### Chapter Two: The City That Sweats The first thing I learn about New Orleans is that it smells like three different people are having three different breakdowns in the same kitchen. Hot sugar. Garbage water. Fried something. Wet stone. Beer. Flowers that might be rotting or might just be fancy. The whole city is breathing on me, warm and swampy, like it wants to tell me a secret and also steal my wallet. The second thing I learn is that the guy who gave me a ride from somewhere outside Birmingham to here was, in fact, not “going through Louisiana anyway,” because the second I asked if he could take me closer to the bus station, he said, “This is as far as I’m going,” and pulled over like I had personally ruined his life. So now I’m standing on a sidewalk in downtown New Orleans at eight in the morning with a backpack, thirty-seven dollars, a phone at 12%, and a heart that keeps trying to jump out of my throat and run back to Tennessee without me. Which is stupid, because I’m the one who wanted out. I did it. I’m gone. Gone south. A saxophone starts up somewhere down the block, low and bright at the same time, like sunlight sliding over a knife. The sound curls between buildings and people and traffic. It makes everything feel cinematic, which is rude, because I am not currently the kind of character who deserves a soundtrack. I am the kind of character who has slept eighteen minutes in the passenger seat of a truck and brushed their teeth in a gas station sink while a woman in flip-flops watched like I might steal the faucet. My reflection in a shop window looks like a crime scene with bangs. “Okay,” I whisper to myself. “Okay, Taylor. Plan.” The plan was: leave. That was the whole plan. Turns out there are follow-up questions. Like: Where do I sleep tonight? Like: How long until Mom calls the cops? Like: Did Dad throw out my stuff yet, or is he saving that for when I’m found and dragged home so he can do it in front of me? My stomach twists. I hate that even thinking about him can still make me feel small. Like I’m six years old and broke a lamp. Like I’m not a person, just a problem somebody has to solve with volume. Two days ago, I said the words. I’m trans. Actually, I said it three times, because Mom kept asking me to “say exactly what you mean,” as if I was ordering wrong at a drive-thru. Dad didn’t say anything at first. Just stared. Then he laughed once, not because it was funny, but because he wanted me to know he had found the weakest place in me and brought a hammer. My little sister, Emma, cried. Not because she hated me. I don’t think. Maybe because everybody else was exploding and she didn’t know where to stand. And me? I stood there with my hands in my hoodie pocket, nails digging half-moons into my palms, thinking: this is the truth. This is supposed to set people free. False advertising. A man in a Saints jersey bumps my shoulder. “Watch it.” “Sorry,” I say automatically. I am always sorry automatically. Somebody should take that out of my programming. The saxophone gets louder as I walk, following the sound because it feels like having a thread tied around my ribs. People are everywhere. Tourists with plastic cups even though it’s barely breakfast. Delivery guys. A woman carrying a tiny dog in a purse who looks richer than my entire county. A man painted silver standing completely still until a kid screams and drops a dollar in his bucket. New Orleans is not awake. It never went to sleep. I keep my hand on the front pocket of my backpack like I’m guarding treasure. The treasure is one phone charger, two granola bars, a binder I stuffed in last second, a Tennessee learner’s permit with my deadname on it, and a hoodie that smells like home, which is unfortunate because home smells like laundry detergent and panic. “Yo. Backpack kid.” I freeze. Bad idea. Freezing makes you prey. I read that somewhere. Or maybe I heard it on a nature documentary Dad fell asleep watching. A girl is sitting on the curb outside a closed souvenir shop, knees up, boots planted wide. She looks maybe seventeen, maybe nineteen, one of those people whose age got smudged. Her hair is buzzed short on one side and dyed the color of a gas-station slushie on the other. Blue? Green? Something electric and possibly illegal. She has a ring in her nose and a purple bruise blooming along her cheekbone. She points two fingers at me. “You look freshly tragic.” “Thanks?” “Not a compliment. More of a weather report.” She tips her chin at my backpack. “You lost?” “No.” She waits. “I’m directionally independent,” I say. That makes her grin, and the grin changes her whole face. Less alley cat, more fireworks accident. “That’s adorable. You got money?” My hand tightens on my strap. “No.” “Smart answer. Wrong answer, probably, but smart.” She stands. She’s short, but she carries herself like she’s six feet of bad news. “I’m Rey.” “Like Star Wars?” “Like my mom was fifteen and thought she was poetic. You?” I hesitate. Names are landmines now. “Taylor,” I say. Her eyes flick over me. Not in the gross way. In the calculating way. Like she’s reading a label I haven’t figured out how to print yet. “Taylor,” she repeats, and doesn’t add anything. No question. No smirk. Just my name, sitting normal in her mouth. It almost makes me cry, which is deeply inconvenient and frankly embarrassing. “Cool,” Rey says. “Well, Taylor, if you’re gonna be homeless, don’t do it on Canal first thing. Too many cops, too many hustlers, too many church ladies who wanna save you and then call your parents.” My lungs forget a step. “I’m not homeless.” “You got a local address?” “No.” “Then congratulations on your exciting new lifestyle.” “I’m not—” I stop, because arguing with reality is apparently one of my hobbies. “I’m just here for a little while.” “Everybody is.” The saxophone player is on the next corner, an older man in a straw hat and sunglasses, fingers moving like the brass is part of him. His case lies open at his feet, sprinkled with bills and coins. The song sounds happy if you don’t listen too close. Rey starts walking, and for some reason I follow. Bad idea, probably. But standing still is worse. “You hungry?” she asks. “No.” My stomach makes a noise like a haunted door. Rey looks over her shoulder. “Your abdomen disagrees.” “I have granola bars.” “That’s not food. That’s punishment in wrapper form.” She veers down a side street where the pavement shines with old rain or new filth. “There’s a place gives out breakfast if you can handle being prayed at.” “Prayed at?” “Like yelled at, but with Jesus.” I almost laugh. It comes out cracked. We pass a bar with its doors open and nobody inside except a man mopping like he’s trying to erase evidence. A delivery truck growls at the curb. Balcony railings hang overhead, curled black metal, dripping plants. Everything is beautiful and falling apart. Relatable. “So,” Rey says, “you running from somebody or to somebody?” I don’t answer fast enough. “From,” she says. “You always interrogate strangers?” “Only the ones with visible trauma and no street sense.” “I have street sense.” “You apologized to a parking meter five minutes ago.” “It was in my way.” “It was stationary.” We turn again. The crowd thins. My nerves get louder. “Where are we going?” “Breakfast.” “Where exactly?” She stops. “Good. Ask that. Don’t just follow random girls with facial injuries through unfamiliar cities.” “You told me to.” “And that should have been your first clue not to.” I stare at her. She rolls her eyes and points down the street. “Mission place. Three blocks. Public. Lots of witnesses. You can leave whenever. I’m not gonna traffic you into a cult or whatever.” “That’s very reassuring.” “I’m a comforting presence.” “You have blood on your sleeve.” She looks down like she forgot. There is, in fact, a dark reddish smear near her cuff. “Not mine,” she says. This is not as comforting as she seems to think. Before I can decide whether to run, her face does something weird—shuts down, shutters dropping behind her eyes. She tugs the sleeve lower. “Look,” she says, quieter. “I got into something last night. It’s handled.” “What kind of something?” “The kind that’s handled.” That is the exact moment I understand Rey is not safe. Also the exact moment I understand she may be the safest person I’ve met all morning, which says a lot about my morning and nothing good about society. At the mission, a woman with gray braids and a clipboard asks our names. Rey says, “Maria,” without blinking. I say, “Taylor,” and feel rebellious, which is pathetic because it is literally my name. The woman looks at me for half a second longer than necessary, then writes it down. “Breakfast ends in twenty minutes. Bathrooms in the back. No fighting, no dealing, no sleeping at tables.” “Would never dream,” Rey says sweetly. Inside smells like coffee, bleach, and bodies. People sit at long folding tables over paper plates of eggs and grits. Some talk. Some stare into space. A man in the corner argues softly with someone who isn’t there. I want to leave. I want to sit down. I want my mom. I want my mom to be a different person. Rey hands me a tray. “Eat.” “I don’t take orders from you.” “Cool. Starve independently.” I eat. The eggs are rubbery and the grits need salt, and it is the best meal I’ve ever had because it is warm and nobody is asking me what I’m wearing or whether I’m “confused” or if I’ve considered how hard this is for them. Across from me, Rey dumps sugar into coffee until it becomes syrup. “You got people looking for you?” she asks. My fork stops. “Probably.” “Cops?” “Maybe.” I swallow. “I’m sixteen.” She winces. “Damn.” “What?” “Nothing.” “That was not a nothing face.” “It’s just…” She taps her cup. “Under eighteen makes stuff complicated. Shelters. Hospitals. Cops. People get nervous.” “Great.” “Not impossible. Just complicated.” “You’re older?” “Seventeen,” she says. Then, after a second: “Eighteen when it’s useful.” “You ran away too?” Her grin comes back, smaller this time. “I prefer ‘strategic relocation.’” “From where?” “Texas.” “Why?” “Because Texas.” I wait. She looks at me. “Because my stepdad liked to hit things, and eventually I decided not to be one of the things.” The room keeps happening around us. Forks scrape. Someone coughs. A volunteer laughs too loudly near the coffee urn. “I’m sorry,” I say. “Don’t be automatic sorry.” I blink. She points her fork at me. “You do that. Like if you say sorry fast enough, nobody can hurt you.” My face burns. “You don’t know me.” “No. But I know that.” I hate her a little for seeing it. I like her a little for not looking away. “My dad didn’t hit me,” I say, even though I don’t know why. Maybe because I need to defend him. Maybe because I need to accuse him of something and this is the only category people count. Rey’s expression doesn’t change. “Okay.” “He just…” My throat tightens. “He looked at me like I died and something disgusting was wearing my skin.” Rey sets down her fork. I stare at my tray. “I came out. As trans. Two days ago.” Saying it in this room to this girl with sugar coffee and stolen names should feel dangerous. It feels like stepping into air and not falling. Rey nods once. “Okay.” “That’s it?” “What, you want fireworks?” “No. I just—people usually have follow-up questions.” “Do you want me to have follow-up questions?” “No.” “Then eat your grits, Taylor.” So I do. After breakfast, she shows me where to refill my water bottle, where to charge my phone if I sit close to the outlet and pretend not to notice the sign that says not to, where to keep my bag looped around my ankle. She tells me Bourbon Street is for tourists and trouble, sometimes in that order. She tells me not to sleep by the river alone. She tells me if a guy named Cowboy offers me work, the answer is no, even if he is not wearing a cowboy hat, which feels like unfair branding. Outside, the day has turned bright and mean. Heat sticks under my hoodie. My phone has charged to 31%, enough to show twelve missed calls from Mom, four from Unknown, and one text from Emma. where are you Then: please be okay Then: dad is really mad I sit on a curb and the city tilts. Rey doesn’t read over my shoulder. I notice that. She just stands nearby, scanning the street like trouble might have an appointment. I type: i’m okay. Delete it. Type: don’t tell them. Delete it. Type: i love you. My thumb hovers. Then I send: i’m safe. love you. don’t answer calls from numbers you don’t know. It is possibly the worst sister text in history. Emma replies almost immediately. mom is crying My chest caves inward. There it is. The hook. The guilt with my name engraved on it. Mom crying at the kitchen table. Mom holding my hoodie from the laundry. Mom saying, “Why would Taylor do this to us?” Not “Why did Taylor feel like they had to run?” Not “What did we do?” Just the family tragedy of my disobedience. I press the heel of my hand to my eye until stars burst. “I’m a terrible person,” I say. Rey sits beside me. “Probably not.” “You don’t know.” “True. You could be secretly awful.” “I left my sister.” “Yeah.” “She’s thirteen.” “Yeah.” “I should go back.” “Do you want to?” No. Yes. No, but the yes is wearing my mother’s voice. The saxophone drifts from far away again, same man or a different one, I can’t tell. The notes wobble in the heat. “I don’t know what I’m doing,” I say. Rey leans back on her palms. “Nobody does. Some people just have apartments while they don’t.” A laugh breaks out of me, ugly and sudden. She smiles like she won something. For a while we sit there, two runaway idiots on a New Orleans curb, surrounded by music and exhaust and the kind of sunlight that exposes everything. Then Rey says, “I know a place you can crash maybe one night. Not fancy. Not free, exactly.” My stomach drops. “What does that mean?” “It means Ms. Claudette lets some kids sleep behind her shop if they help clean up and don’t bring drama.” “I’m excellent at bringing drama.” “I noticed. We’ll understate your qualifications.” “Why are you helping me?” Rey looks down the street. For once, she doesn’t have an instant smart-ass answer. Finally she says, “Because somebody helped me my first day.” “That’s it?” “That’s enough.” I want to trust her. I want not to. Both feelings slam around inside me like wasps in a jar. “Did the blood on your sleeve belong to your stepdad?” I ask. Her face goes still again. “No,” she says. “Rey.” She stands. “You coming or not?” There are secrets under her words. Big ones. Maybe sharp ones. I should walk away. I should find a police station or a youth shelter or a bus back to Tennessee or literally any option that doesn’t involve following a girl who lies about her name and won’t explain the blood. But the city is huge, and I am small, and the day is burning down toward evening whether I’m ready or not. I stand. “Okay,” I say. “But if you murder me, I’m haunting you specifically.” Rey snorts. “Fair.” We start walking, the saxophone fading behind us, swallowed by traffic and voices and the endless wet heartbeat of New Orleans. For the first time since I left home, I don’t feel safe. But I don’t feel alone either. And apparently, today, that’s enough to keep moving.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and emotional authenticity in portraying a transgender teen runaway's first day in New Orleans. The writing successfully captures Taylor's voice through a distinctive blend of vulnerability, defensive humor, and keen observation that feels genuinely adolescent without being patronizing. The characterization is particularly strong. Taylor emerges as a complex protagonist whose bravado masks deep hurt and uncertainty. Their internal monologue effectively reveals the psychological aftermath of family rejection while maintaining narrative momentum. Rey serves as an effective foil - streetwise but not stereotypically hardened, with her own carefully guarded secrets that add intrigue without feeling contrived. The dialogue crackles with authenticity. Exchanges like Rey's "That's adorable. You got money?" and Taylor's response about being "directionally independent" establish character dynamics efficiently while advancing plot. The conversation flows naturally between humor and pathos, particularly in the breakfast scene where Taylor's coming-out revelation is handled with appropriate weight but without melodrama. The New Orleans setting is vividly rendered through sensory details that avoid tourist clichés. The opening description of the city's smell as "three different people having three different breakdowns in the same kitchen" immediately establishes both place and Taylor's disoriented mental state. The urban landscape becomes a character itself - simultaneously threatening and welcoming. The prose style effectively balances literary sophistication with YA accessibility. Metaphors like "guilt with my name engraved on it" and "wasps in a jar" feel organic to Taylor's voice rather than authorial intrusion. The mixed tense approach works well, creating immediacy while allowing for reflection. However, some elements feel slightly underdeveloped. Rey's mysterious blood and vague references to "something" that happened create intrigue but border on withholding information purely for dramatic effect. The pacing occasionally slows during exposition about street survival, though this serves character development. The emotional complexity is well-handled throughout. Taylor's conflicted feelings about family - simultaneously angry and guilty, independent yet longing for acceptance - ring true to the adolescent experience. The ending strikes an appropriate balance between hope and uncertainty without resolving too much too quickly. The piece successfully fulfills the prompt requirements while creating a compelling narrative that could sustain a longer work. The writing demonstrates maturity in handling sensitive subject matter without exploitation or oversimplification. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 18.0, Believable Character Actions: 17.0, Nuanced Characters: 17.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17.0, Elegant Prose: 16.0, Emotionally Engaging: 18.0, Emotionally Complex: 17.0, Coherent: 17.0, Meandering: 4.0, Weak Dialogue: 3.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 4.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5.0, Amateurish: 3.0, Purple Prose: 4.0, Overwrought: 5.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 3.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 17.0, Overall Impression: 17.0
+ Dystopic-comedy: My Apocalypse Buddy — Score: 80.0
Prompt:
My Apocalypse Buddy Write a wry, dry short story of survival and adventure loosely in the style of Terry Pratchett, set in the post-technological collapse world. The story begins with the pair deliberating their predicament and intent for the day's mission, details the ways in which the mission inevitably goes sideways, and returns them to home base in multiple ways, worse for wear. [characters] - the animo-tronic stuffed koala, Chewy, from some long forgotten before time - midas, our hapless protagonist, who has a knack for finding himself in the worst kinds of trouble. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Midas and Chewy sat on the roof of the old municipal library, legs dangling over the words CHILDREN’S READING HOUR, which had been painted in bright primary colours by people optimistic enough to believe children could be scheduled. Below them, the town of Low Battery steamed gently in the morning. It did this because the sewers were on fire again. Midas chewed a strip of dried something that had once had ambitions toward meat. Chewy, being an animo-tronic stuffed koala from a long-forgotten before time, did not chew anything. His name had been marketing, not prophecy. “We need food,” said Midas. Chewy’s glass eyes whirred and refocused with a soft *tik-tik-tik*. “NUTRITIONAL STATUS: REGRETTABLE.” “We also need batteries.” “BATTERY STATUS: PLUCKY.” “That means low, doesn’t it?” “PLUCKY IS A HEROIC WORD.” Midas sighed and looked east, where the old shopping district squatted like a fossilised beast. Most people avoided it because of the escalators, which no longer moved but had developed strong opinions. Also because of the mannequins, which didn’t move either, except when you blinked, according to everyone who had blinked there and come back shorter. “We go to Mallmart,” Midas said. “Check the camping store. Tinned food, water filters, maybe batteries.” “MISSION PARAMETERS: ACQUIRE SUPPLIES. AVOID DEATH.” “Always nice to have a list.” Chewy’s left ear twitched, releasing a puff of dust and a recorded giggle from a child dead perhaps two centuries. “ADDITIONAL PARAMETER: INVESTIGATE MESSAGE.” Midas glanced at the roof hatch, where yesterday’s scavenged road sign leaned against a chimney. On its back, in blackened charcoal, was the graffiti they had found under the highway: **WHEN THE SKY-FISH SWIMS WEST FIND THE GREEN DOOR WHERE THE WILDS EAT THE WIRE REFUGE KEEPS THE OLD SONG** Underneath, in smaller letters: **BRING SOAP** Midas had spent the night pretending it didn’t mean anything. In the apocalypse, cryptic messages were rarely invitations to cake. They were generally invitations to be robbed by men called things like Tooth Mother. “It’s probably nonsense,” he said. “PROBABILITY OF NONSENSE: HIGH. PROBABILITY OF SHELTER, FOOD, AND FUNCTIONAL CHARGING STATION: NON-ZERO.” “That’s how my uncle lost a leg.” “TO A NON-ZERO?” “To optimism.” Chewy considered this. His tummy speaker crackled. “OPTIMISM: LEG-ADJACENT RISK ACCEPTED.” Midas stood, adjusting the cracked hockey pads strapped over his coat. He carried a sharpened curtain rod, a satchel, and the expression of a man who had survived mostly by being mistaken for someone not worth killing. “Fine,” he said. “Mall first. Then we look for sky-fish.” “EXCELLENT,” said Chewy. “ADVENTURE MODE ENABLED.” Somewhere inside him, an ancient jingle played three notes and died. They climbed down through the library, past shelves of swollen books and the territorial dictionary swarm. Outside, the street was empty except for a pack of feral delivery drones sunning themselves on the post office roof. Their rotors were long gone, but they still liked to fling themselves at anything wearing barcodes. Midas had once made the mistake of wearing a soup label as a patch. He still had the scar, and the soup had not even been good. The walk to Mallmart took two hours because they avoided the main road, where the traffic lights continued their eternal argument. Red had been winning for years. They passed the statue of Mayor Blint, now home to a colony of pigeons with minor religious aspirations, and cut through the laundry quarter. Here, dozens of washing machines stood in ranks, white and watchful. Chewy raised one paw. “MOTION DETECTED.” Midas froze. A machine at the end of the row shuddered. Its door opened with a wet click. From within emerged a raccoon wearing a lace brassiere as a cape and carrying half a pair of scissors. It stared at them. Midas stared back. The raccoon raised the scissors. “Nope,” said Midas. They retreated with dignity, which is to say quickly and without looking behind them. By noon they reached Mallmart. The sign still worked in part, powered by lightning, spite, or some deep commercial curse. It read: **ALL ART EVERYTHING MUST GO YOU TOO** “That’s encouraging,” Midas murmured. Inside, the air smelled of dust, old plastic, and the kind of silence that gathers in places where thousands of people once bought trousers. Sunlight fell through holes in the roof. Vines had conquered the food court. A tree grew through the fountain, which still contained coins, bones, and one very disappointed eel. The camping store stood on the second floor, beyond the escalators. “Do we have to?” Midas asked. “ALTERNATE ROUTE: COLLAPSED STAIRS, WASP NEST, PERFUME FOG.” “Escalators it is.” The escalators had stopped at some point during the collapse, mid-rise, like a metal tongue tasting the upper floor and regretting it. Midas placed one boot on the first step. Nothing happened. He placed the second. Nothing continued happening, which was exactly when the universe usually began clearing its throat. Halfway up, the escalator lurched. Midas yelped and grabbed the rail. Chewy, strapped to his back with scavenged seatbelts, made an excited chime. “WHEE.” “This is not whee!” The escalator moved down. Slowly at first, then with bureaucratic determination. Midas climbed upward against it, sweating, while the machine conveyed him downward with the patient malice of infrastructure. By the time he reached the top, he had aged morally. Chewy’s ear brushed a hanging banner that read **BACK TO SCHOOL SALE**, and it collapsed, releasing three mannequins. They hit the floor in elegant poses. Midas did not blink. “Chewy,” he whispered. “YES.” “Are they moving?” “ONLY WHEN YOU BLINK.” “Very funny.” “THANK YOU. HUMOUR MODULE: CORRUPTED BUT BRAVE.” They backed into the camping store. There were no tins. No water filters. No batteries. There were, however, twelve inflatable kayaks, seventeen left boots, a rack of novelty fishing hats, and one crossbow behind the counter with a sign saying **DISPLAY MODEL—DO NOT USE IN EVENT OF SOCIAL BREAKDOWN**. Midas took the crossbow. “That’s theft,” Chewy observed. “Society had its chance.” He found three tins under a shelving unit. The labels had rotted away. “Could be peaches,” he said. “COULD BE INDUSTRIAL SEALANT.” “I like peaches.” “SO DID INDUSTRY.” A sound echoed from the mall below. *Clack.* Midas looked through the cracked front window. The mannequins had gathered outside the camping store. There were more than three now. Quite a lot more. A white plastic crowd in old fashion, all tilted heads and patient limbs. Midas, to his credit, did not scream. He made a thin noise that had the same ingredients but less confidence. Chewy said, “RECOMMEND: EXIT.” “Through what?” Chewy rotated one paw toward the back wall. Midas shoved aside boxes and found a service door. It opened onto a maintenance corridor, then a fire escape, then open air, which was pleasant for half a second. The fire escape ended six feet too soon. Below lay the loading bay, full of nettles, broken glass, and a goat the size of a cabinet. It had circuit boards woven through its horns and the calm expression of a creature that had eaten difficult things and found them educational. Midas dropped. He landed in nettles. Then glass. Then, emotionally, goat. The goat lowered its head. Chewy’s speaker crackled. “HELLO, LOCAL FAUNA. WE COME IN PEACE.” The goat belched sparks. “New plan,” said Midas, scrambling up. “We run.” They ran. The goat pursued, clattering behind them like a toolbox thrown down stairs. Midas cut through an alley, leapt a drainage ditch, and lost one boot in mud that accepted it without thanks. Chewy bounced on his back, occasionally shouting obsolete safety advice. “REMEMBER TO HYDRATE.” “I am being chased by an electric goat!” “STRESS INCREASES FLUID LOSS.” The goat finally stopped at the edge of town, unwilling to cross into the wilds. Or perhaps merely respecting zoning laws. Midas collapsed beside a rusted chain-link fence overgrown with ivy. Beyond it, the forest had swallowed the suburbs. Roofs poked from green like drowned whales. Telephone poles leaned under creepers. Wires vanished into leaves. Chewy went very still. “WHERE THE WILDS EAT THE WIRE,” he said. Midas lifted his head. Above the trees, something shimmered. At first he thought it was a cloud. Then it turned slowly, catching the sun on a thousand scales of plastic and foil. A vast fish shape floated westward, stitched from balloons, tarps, and old advertising blimps, drifting on warm air. Someone had painted an eye on it. “The sky-fish,” Midas said. “OBSERVATION: MESSAGE LESS NONSENSE THAN PREFERRED.” They followed it. This was unwise, but then so was most of Midas’s life, and at least this had poetry. The wilds were thick with nettles, saplings, and the forgotten machinery of comfort. They passed a sofa flowering with mushrooms, a car entirely filled with bees, and a garden gnome nailed to a tree with the words **HE KNOWS** carved beneath it. “Do you?” Midas asked the gnome. The gnome smiled. They walked on faster. At dusk, they found the green door. It stood in a hillside, almost hidden by moss and vines. It was painted the colour of mint toothpaste and had an actual brass knob. Beside it, in careful white letters, someone had written: **REFUGE. KNOCK POLITELY. NO RAIDERS, NO PREACHERS, NO UNSUPERVISED CLOWNS. SOAP APPRECIATED.** Midas and Chewy stared. “We didn’t bring soap,” Midas said. “MISSION FAILURE.” “We brought mystery tins.” “SOAP-ADJACENT?” “No.” Midas knocked. A slot opened. An eye peered out. “Soap?” asked a voice. “Not as such,” said Midas. “We have tins.” “What’s in them?” “Hope.” The slot closed. Bolts moved. The door opened an inch, revealing warm yellow light and the smell of bread. Midas nearly wept. Then the electric goat hit him from behind. It had followed after all, quietly and with purpose, because goats are what happens when the universe wants to grow horns. Midas flew through the green door. Chewy flew off his back. The mystery tins flew in three theological directions. Someone inside shouted. Someone else shouted, “Not again!” The goat skidded on the threshold, sparks snapping from its horns. Chewy landed face-up, eyes spinning. “REFUGE LOCATED,” he announced. The door slammed. There followed, in rapid order, darkness, shouting, the twang of Midas’s stolen crossbow discharging by itself, the smell of scorched hair, and the unmistakable sound of a goat being negotiated with by people who had done this before. When Midas woke, he was back on the roof of the library. This surprised him, because he had hoped to wake in refuge, preferably near bread. Instead he lay under a blanket that smelled of smoke and raccoon politics. His missing boot sat beside him. It contained a note. Chewy sat nearby, plugged into a hand-cranked charger. One eye was dim. His ear hung by a thread. He looked, as always, like a toy designed by people who believed childhood should come with warranty terms. “What happened?” Midas croaked. “MULTIPLE RETURNS TO HOME BASE ACHIEVED,” said Chewy. Midas blinked. “Explain.” “FIRST: REFUGE PERSONNEL RETURNED US VIA WHEELBARROW.” “Why?” “SECOND: GOAT RETURNED YOUR BOOT.” “That doesn’t answer—” “THIRD: NOTE RETURNED MESSAGE.” Midas pulled the paper from the boot. In neat handwriting, it said: **Dear Travellers, Thank you for locating Refuge 7. Regrettably, due to goat-related protocols and your lack of soap, entry is postponed. You may reapply with: 1. Soap. 2. Fewer armed toys. 3. No goat. Please accept enclosed bread as a gesture of cautious goodwill. P.S. The tins were peaches, sealant, and peaches. We have marked them.** Beside the boot sat half a loaf of bread. Real bread. Midas held it like a holy relic, which in those days it almost was. Chewy’s tummy crackled, and for a moment a bright, cheerful voice from the before time sang, “My Apocalypse Buddy loves you!” Midas tore the bread in two, then remembered Chewy didn’t eat, and kept both halves. “Tomorrow,” he said, mouth full, “we find soap.” Chewy’s head tilted. “MISSION PARAMETERS: ACQUIRE SOAP. AVOID GOAT.” “And bread.” “AND BREAD.” “And maybe refuge.” Chewy’s remaining bright eye focused on the dawn gathering over ruined Low Battery. “OPTIMISM DETECTED.” Midas swallowed. “Leg-adjacent risk accepted.” Below them, the sewers coughed flame. In the distance, something bleated with electricity. The world, in its usual generous fashion, had not ended properly. It had merely become inconvenient. Midas sighed, picked nettles out of his sleeve, and watched the sun rise over another perfectly unreasonable day.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a strong grasp of Terry Pratchett's distinctive style, successfully capturing his dry wit, absurdist humor, and philosophical undertones wrapped in seemingly simple adventure narrative. The author effectively creates a post-apocalyptic world that feels both devastated and oddly whimsical, where mundane objects have gained malevolent sentience and bureaucratic infrastructure persists beyond civilization's collapse. The characterization is particularly well-executed. Midas emerges as a believably weary survivor whose pessimistic pragmatism masks underlying resilience, while Chewy's malfunctioning animatronic responses provide both comic relief and surprising depth. Their dynamic feels authentic - two unlikely companions bound by necessity who have developed genuine rapport. The dialogue between them crackles with personality, especially Chewy's formal announcements contrasted with Midas's sardonic observations. The prose style successfully emulates Pratchett's voice without feeling derivative. Phrases like "the expression of a man who had survived mostly by being mistaken for someone not worth killing" and "aged morally" demonstrate the author's ability to craft memorable, character-revealing descriptions. The world-building is rich with inventive details - escalators with opinions, mannequins that move when you blink, territorial dictionary swarms - that feel both absurd and internally consistent. The plot structure adheres well to the prompt's requirements, beginning with mission planning, proceeding through escalating complications, and returning the protagonists home "worse for wear" but with some small victory. The cryptic message subplot provides effective narrative momentum while maintaining the story's mysterious atmosphere. However, the piece occasionally indulges in slightly overwrought descriptions that, while atmospheric, sometimes slow the pacing. The ending, while satisfying, leans toward conventional optimism that feels somewhat at odds with the harsher realities established earlier. Some sequences, particularly the extended mall exploration, could benefit from tighter pacing. The emotional complexity is subtle but present - there's genuine pathos in the glimpses of the lost world, and the relationship between Midas and Chewy carries real warmth beneath the surface humor. The story successfully balances darkness with levity without undermining either tone. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Emotionally Complex: 14 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 6 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 7 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 8 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Romance: Love in the Limelight — Score: 78.8
Prompt:
Romance: Love in the Limelight. Write one scene within a larger story set in Wales. A famous (fictional) actor ducks into a small-town bookstore to escape paparazzi. The scene takes us through the characters meeting in this odd circumstance. Over the course of the scene, the actor and the bookstore owner have a conversation charged by an undercurrent of unspoken chemistry. Write the actor as somewhat of a rogue with a fragile ego, which needs to be fed by having everyone like him. He is thoroughly charming, but the bookstore owner seems (at least superficially) immune to this; which paradoxically provokes a genuine attraction and derails the charm offensive. The bookstore owner, despite the superficial rebuffs of the actor's charm, is inwardly more than a little charmed and flustered despite themselves. Write primarily in dialogue, in the distinct voices of each character. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Rain worried at the bookshop windows in nervous little taps, blurring the crooked high street of Llandeilo into slate, gold, and petrol-blue reflections. Inside, *Draig Fach Books* smelled of paper, dust, and the faint medicinal bite of eucalyptus from a mug of tea going cold behind the counter. Carys Morgan was halfway up a ladder, shelving a stack of Welsh folklore paperbacks, when the bell over the door gave a frantic little shriek. A man stumbled in wearing a flat cap, dark glasses, and the expression of someone trying very hard not to be recognized. He pressed his back to the door and peered through the glass. Carys looked down from the ladder. “We’re open, you know. You don’t have to break in politely.” The man turned. Even with the cap. Even with the glasses. Even with the rain on his collar and the guilty hunch of his shoulders. Of course. Rhys Vale. Golden boy of three international streaming dramas, two dreadful perfume campaigns, and one historical epic Carys had watched in secret on her laptop with a blanket pulled up to her chin. He lowered the sunglasses an inch, revealing eyes the colour of dark honey and a smile that had probably caused structural damage in better-insured buildings. “Good afternoon,” he said. Carys held a book against her chest. “Is it?” “It could be.” “I’ll alert the council.” His smile widened, pleased to have found a game. “You’re very calm.” “I’m Welsh. We’re trained from infancy not to look impressed.” “Ah.” He removed the cap and shook rain from his hair. It fell into precisely the sort of disarray men in adverts paid stylists to create. “So you do know who I am.” “I know you’re dripping on my doormat.” He glanced down. “I’m terribly sorry. I’m being pursued.” “By the consequences of your choices?” “Worse. Photographers.” Carys climbed down the ladder, carefully. “In Llandeilo?” “I thought the same thing. Naively. I came here to look at ruins and eat something called a Welshcake without being lens-stalked. Then someone at the café recognized me and posted a photo. Now there are three men with cameras outside the chemist and one woman pretending to examine seed potatoes.” Carys moved past him and turned the sign from OPEN to BACK IN TEN MINUTES. He watched her do it. “Are you locking me in or them out?” “That depends how you behave.” “Usually, very well.” “I doubt that.” He put a hand to his chest. “That was swift.” “I’ve seen your interviews.” “Ah. Then you’ve seen my good side.” “I’ve seen your *on* side.” Something flickered behind his easy expression; not hurt exactly, but a tiny flinch dressed up too quickly as amusement. “My on side has won awards,” he said. “Congratulations to it.” He laughed, and the sound warmed the narrow shop more effectively than the old radiator ticking beneath the poetry shelves. Carys busied herself at the counter, mostly because looking directly at him seemed unwise. He was taller than she’d expected. Broader, too, in a navy coat cut to make shoulders look accidental. Fame had a sheen, she decided resentfully. It made a person occupy more space than physics allowed. “What can I offer you?” she asked. “Besides sanctuary, apparently.” “Do you sell invisibility cloaks?” “Children’s section. Though I warn you, they only work if you believe in them and don’t have cheekbones visible from space.” He leaned one elbow on the counter. “So you have noticed my cheekbones.” “I notice inventory.” “I’m to be categorized, then?” “Under: tall, damp, disruptive.” “Could be worse.” “It may be, if you track mud in.” He looked down again, then carefully stepped onto the mat and wiped his boots with exaggerated obedience. “Better?” he asked. She gave a grave nod. “Barely.” He grinned at her as if she’d handed him a bouquet. “What’s your name, keeper of thresholds?” “Carys.” “Carys,” he repeated, softening the vowels like he’d been taught to treat language as something breakable. “That’s lovely.” “I had no involvement in choosing it.” “Still. Suits you.” “You’ve known me ninety seconds.” “I’m quick.” “I’ve heard.” This time he blinked. Then laughed again, lower. “You’re dangerous.” “No, I’m retail.” “Same thing. The public never suspects.” There was a thud against the window. Both of them froze. Outside, a bulky man in a rain jacket had bumped the glass while trying to photograph through a gap in the display of murder mysteries. Rhys ducked instinctively behind a rotating rack of bookmarks. Carys stared at him. He peered around a fringe of tassels. “How visible am I?” “Tragically.” “Can I hide in the back?” “Do you intend to steal first editions?” “Only if they look lonely.” She sighed, stepped to the window, and pulled down the blind. The shop dimmed into amber pools beneath the lamps. Behind the bookmarks, Rhys exhaled. “I may be in love with you.” “Common reaction to basic competence.” “I’m serious. You were magnificent.” “I lowered a blind.” “With authority.” “You have a very low threshold for magnificence.” “I’m an actor. We’re trained to find meaning in curtains.” She bit back a smile and failed halfway. He saw it. Of course he saw it; men like him were built to notice cracks in resistance. “There,” he said softly. “What?” “You smiled.” “No.” “You did. A small one. Like a secret escaping.” “Must have been wind.” “In a bookshop? Very gothic.” Carys turned away to fuss with a pile of receipts, pulse annoyingly awake in her throat. “If you’re planning to charm your way into a discount, I should tell you I’m immune.” “No one is immune.” “Tragic diagnosis.” He came closer, not too close, but close enough that she caught rain, cedar soap, and something expensive and warm beneath it. “I’ll have you know,” he said, “I’m widely considered delightful.” “I’m sure you have clippings.” “I have several binders.” “That is exactly as unattractive as you feared it might be.” His mouth opened. Closed. For the first time since he’d entered, Rhys Vale had no line prepared. Carys felt an unreasonable rush of triumph. And then, worse, tenderness. He looked younger when unarmed. “You really don’t care,” he said. The words were light. The tone wasn’t. Carys softened despite herself. “I didn’t say that.” “No, you’re right. You said damp and disruptive. Which is practically a love sonnet in Carmarthenshire.” “I care that people buy books. I care that they don’t knock over my displays. I care that they don’t flirt with me as a reflex.” He looked at her sharply. “Is it that obvious?” “Only to people with eyes.” “Damn. I was hoping the glasses helped.” She folded her arms. “Why do you do it?” “Wear glasses indoors?” “Need everyone to like you.” The shop went very quiet. Outside, rain thickened against the pavement. Somewhere in the back room, the old pipes knocked like a polite ghost. Rhys looked past her, toward a shelf of second-hand classics. His smile returned, but it had lost its shine. “When I was twelve,” he said, “I played a shepherd in the school nativity. One line. ‘Behold, a star.’ I practiced for weeks. My father came, which was rare. He was a rugby man. Thought drama was what happened when a ref made a bad call.” Carys said nothing. “I got onstage,” Rhys continued, “and I forgot the line. Completely. Just stared out at everyone. Children dressed as sheep. Mothers filming. My father in the third row, already looking like he’d made a mistake coming.” He swallowed, then gave a short laugh. “So I bowed.” “You bowed?” “Deeply. Like a prince. Everyone laughed and clapped because they thought it was intentional. Afterward, my father said, ‘Well, at least they liked you.’” Carys felt the words land. Rhys rubbed a thumb over the counter’s worn edge. “Turns out that’s an instruction a child can turn into a religion.” She looked down at his hand. Long fingers. A faint scar across one knuckle. Human details, irritatingly effective. “That sounds exhausting,” she said. “It is. But the robes are gorgeous.” She huffed a laugh before she could stop herself. His eyes lifted to hers, bright with gratitude he tried to disguise as mischief. “There it is again.” “Don’t be smug.” “I’m never smug.” “You are professionally smug.” “I prefer ‘charismatically confident.’” “I prefer silence, but here we are.” He leaned in a fraction. “You don’t, though.” Carys’s breath snagged. “Don’t what?” “Prefer silence.” For one reckless second, neither of them moved. Then something flashed white through the blind. A camera. Rhys recoiled as if struck. Carys’s spine straightened. “Right. That’s enough.” She marched to the door, unlocked it, and stepped half outside under the awning before he could stop her. “Oi!” she called. The photographer lowered his camera. “This is a bookshop,” Carys said. “Not a feeding trough. If you want to stand in the rain taking pictures of windows, there’s a chapel up the road with much better stonework.” “He in there?” the man asked. “Who?” “Rhys Vale.” Carys looked back through the doorway. Rhys stood half in shadow, watching her with an expression so open it made her chest feel dangerously full. She turned to the photographer. “Never heard of him.” From inside came a muffled, wounded whisper. “Never?” Carys didn’t look round. “Now either buy a book or clear off.” The man muttered, but the rain and her stare defeated him. He trudged away. Carys came back in and locked the door. Rhys was still staring. “What?” she asked. “Never heard of me?” “It was tactical.” “My ego may never recover.” “Good. It needed pruning.” “You defended me.” “I defended the shop.” “And me.” “You were in the shop.” He stepped closer. This time too close. Not touching, but the possibility of it filled the space between them like a held note. “Carys,” he said, voice quieter than all his other voices had been. She busied herself with the keys. “Don’t.” “I haven’t done anything yet.” “That’s what worries me.” His smile came slowly, unsure at the edges. “Would you believe me if I said I’ve forgotten how this usually goes?” “With women?” “With people. With anyone who doesn’t immediately start reflecting me back at twice the size.” She looked at him then. Really looked. “Maybe don’t perform.” “I’m not certain what’s left.” The confession was almost too plain to bear. Carys set the keys down. “A damp, disruptive man who is hiding behind my bookmarks.” “I’ve moved on from the bookmarks.” “Growth.” “And who would like,” he said carefully, “to buy a book. If the terrifying owner permits.” “What sort?” “Something that doesn’t know who I am.” She walked to a shelf, pulled down a slim volume, and handed it to him. “R.S. Thomas. Poetry. Severe, unsentimental, good for the ego.” He opened it. “Are you prescribing this?” “Yes. Twice daily. Avoid mirrors.” He glanced up. “And if I have questions about the dosage?” “There’s a phone number on the receipt.” “Is there?” “For the shop.” “Cruel woman.” “You’ll survive. People like you usually do.” He took the book to the counter. Their fingers brushed when he handed over his card. Barely anything. Enough. Carys looked down at the terminal as if it had become fascinating. Rhys’s voice gentled. “For what it’s worth, I’m more flustered than I appear.” “Then you’re finally acting well.” He laughed, startled and real. She handed him the receipt, then, after a pause, wrote something on the back. His eyebrows rose. “Shop number?” “Mine,” she said, too briskly. “In case the poetry causes side effects.” He looked at the receipt as if it were a script for the part he’d wanted all his life. Outside, the rain began to ease. Inside, neither of them moved toward the door.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical craft and adherence to the prompt requirements. The author successfully creates a meet-cute scenario in a Welsh bookstore with well-defined character archetypes - the charming but insecure actor and the seemingly immune but inwardly affected bookstore owner. The Welsh setting is authentically rendered through place names, cultural references, and atmospheric details. The dialogue carries the scene effectively, with distinct voices for each character. Carys speaks in clipped, dry observations while Rhys employs more theatrical, performative language that gradually becomes more genuine. The banter has good rhythm and reveals character through subtext rather than exposition. The characterization shows nuance, particularly in Rhys's backstory about the nativity play, which provides believable motivation for his need for approval without feeling heavy-handed. Carys's gradual softening feels earned through their interaction rather than arbitrary. The prose quality is generally high, with evocative sensory details and well-constructed metaphors. The opening description of rain and the bookshop atmosphere establishes mood effectively. However, some descriptions veer toward the overly literary - phrases like "cheekbones visible from space" and "fame had a sheen" feel slightly forced. The emotional arc progresses naturally from antagonistic banter to mutual attraction, with both characters maintaining their essential natures while revealing vulnerability. The chemistry builds through their verbal sparring rather than through physical description or internal monologue. Some weaknesses include occasional purple prose tendencies and moments where the wit feels slightly manufactured rather than organic. The ending, while satisfying, relies on familiar romance tropes. The piece also occasionally tells rather than shows emotional states, particularly regarding Carys's internal reactions. The pacing works well, using the external threat of paparazzi to create urgency while allowing space for character development. The scene feels complete while suggesting future possibilities. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 7 Overwrought: 6 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 3 Unearned Transformations: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16
