+ Historical fiction: Gladiator — Score: 86.3
Prompt:
Historical Fiction: Write a scene from a story set during the height of the Roman Empire, focusing on a slice of a day in the life of a gladiator. Do not write a combat scene. Use sensory details to capture the sights, sounds, and smells of ancient Rome. Explore the gladiator's thoughts and emotions. The story should also touch on the larger political and social issues of the time period. The piece should feel like a slice of a larger story. First person, past tense, 1000 words.
Model Output:
By the time the first horn sounded from the baths of Titus, I had already been awake for an hour, lying on my back in the narrow dark of the cell and counting the drips from the cracked cistern outside the wall. One, two, three—then a pause long enough for a man to remember his mother. The straw under me smelled of sweat, oil, and the sour breath of men who slept too close together. Beyond the barred opening, dawn pressed itself pale against the corridor. Someone coughed. Someone muttered a prayer in Thracian. Farther away, in the yard, the mule that turned the mill began its slow, miserable circle, and the stone groaned like an old slave rising from his knees. I rubbed the sleep from my eyes and felt the familiar roughness of my palms. The calluses had become a map of my life. There was the thick pad where the wooden sword struck day after day, the cracked line across my thumb from a shield rim, the old welt on my wrist where a trainer’s cane had taught me not to lower my guard. A citizen might read the lines of his hand and imagine fortune. Mine told me only what I had survived. Across from me, Brennus was still asleep with his mouth open, a scar on his cheek silver in the early light. He was a Gaul, huge as a temple column and twice as stubborn, though he cried in his sleep sometimes and called for a girl named Aeda. Beside him, little Syrus sat upright, awake as always, twisting a thread around his finger. He had been born a slave in Antioch and knew three languages well enough to insult a man’s ancestors in all of them. “You were talking again,” Syrus whispered. “What did I say?” “Names.” He looked at me carefully. “A woman’s name.” I sat up. The straw crackled beneath me. “You heard wrong.” He gave the smallest shrug. In the ludus, mercy often came disguised as indifference. The keeper came then, banging his staff along the bars. “Up, dogs. Up, heroes. The people of Rome are hungry.” That was how he greeted us on days when there would be a spectacle. Not every day brought blood to the sand, despite what boys in the street imagined. Much of our life was porridge, drills, liniment, inspection, waiting. Waiting wore a man down more steadily than the sword. Waiting gave him room to think. We filed out with the others into the yard. The air still held the night’s coolness, but Rome was already opening its many mouths. From beyond the high walls came the creak of cart wheels over paving stones, the curses of drivers forced by law to finish deliveries before the city grew too crowded, the bray of donkeys, the slap of sandals, a woman selling hot chickpeas calling out as if she were announcing an oracle. Smoke rose from cookshops and floated over the wall, bringing with it the smell of frying garlic, cheap wine, damp wool, and human waste not yet washed from the gutters. Rome never smelled of one thing. It smelled of everything at once—bread and piss, incense and rot, crushed flowers, sweating bodies, fish sauce sharp enough to wake the dead. The sky over the rooftops was streaked with rose. Above it all, the Colosseum shouldered itself into the morning, tier upon tier of arches pale as bone. Even from the yard, where the walls tried to hide the city from us, I could see the topmost stones catching light. Men called it the Flavian Amphitheatre when they wanted to flatter emperors. The rest of us called it the place that ate voices. We were given breakfast in wooden bowls: barley mash with beans, thick enough to paste a wall. Gladiators were fed like oxen and buried like kings, if the crowd loved them. I ate slowly, though hunger clawed at me. The lanista paid for our food and complained of the cost at every opportunity, but he knew well enough that muscle brought coin. A lean, starving man died too quickly to please anyone. Brennus sniffed his bowl. “They fatten us before slaughter.” Syrus smiled without humor. “You say that every morning.” “One morning it will be true.” “It is true every morning,” I said, and lifted another spoonful. The words settled among us like dust. After we ate, we went to the practice ground. The sand had been raked clean, though it still held yesterday’s footprints under its surface, all those erased struggles waiting beneath the neat lines. Wooden posts stood in rows. Training swords hung on pegs. Shields leaned against the wall, their leather covers dark from years of oil and hands. Our doctor, a Greek named Menekrates, examined my shoulder before drills. His fingers were cool and smelled of vinegar. “You slept on it badly,” he said. “I slept on straw.” “Then blame the straw. It cannot answer.” He pressed at the joint until I hissed. “You pray to Hercules, yes?” he asked. “When I remember.” “Remember today. He knew labors.” Menekrates tied a strip of linen around my arm. “Though even Hercules would have cursed the management of this place.” That made me laugh, despite myself. Hercules had been my favorite since I was a boy in Hispania, before Rome took me first as taxpayer, then soldier, then prisoner of debt, though each step had been dressed in lawful words. My father kept a little bronze of him near the hearth—club in hand, lion skin across his shoulders, face bent not in triumph but endurance. I had liked that. Mars belonged to generals and marble men on horseback. Jupiter belonged to emperors. Hercules belonged to those who carried what they were given because no god appeared to lift it from their backs. At the shrine near the yard gate, a niche blackened by lamp smoke held a crude figure of him. Some past gladiator had carved it from bone. The head was too large, the club a crooked stump, but men touched it as they passed. I did so that morning, pressing two fingers to the worn surface. Not for victory, I thought. Not even for life. Only for strength enough to remain myself. The drill master shouted us into pairs. We moved through forms beneath his eye: step, turn, guard, withdraw. Wood struck wood. Sand rasped underfoot. Sweat warmed my tunic and ran down my spine. The sun climbed, gilding the walls, and the coolness fled. Soon the yard smelled of men, dust, leather, and the olive oil rubbed into arms and shoulders to keep the skin supple. No blades were drawn. No crowd roared. Yet the rhythm of training held its own violence, not in wounds but in repetition. Again. Again. Again until the body obeyed faster than fear could speak. At midmorning, the lanista arrived with a magistrate and two attendants. Our owner wore a white tunic with a purple stripe too broad for his rank and rings that flashed whenever he lifted his hand. He had once been a butcher’s son, men said, which perhaps explained why he inspected us like fine cattle and spoke of honor as if it were a cut of meat. The magistrate, soft-faced and perfumed, pinched a fold of his toga away from the sand. He looked younger than I was, though he carried himself with the boredom of age. Behind him, an attendant held wax tablets. “These are the men?” the magistrate asked. “The finest in my school,” said the lanista. “Disciplined. Popular. The editor will be satisfied.” The editor—the sponsor of the games—was some senator seeking public favor, or perhaps trying to hide a scandal beneath the roar of the mob. Grain had been short that month. The Tiber floods had fouled warehouses. Bread lines stretched longer each morning, and in the Subura people shouted that the emperor’s ministers sold Egyptian wheat before it reached Roman mouths. A clever noble knew when to give the people spectacle. Hunger could become anger, but anger could be trained to cheer at the right time. The magistrate’s gaze slid over us. “The crowd wants novelty. Germans, Nubians, beasts if possible.” “Beasts are costly.” “So are riots.” The lanista smiled tightly. “My Thracian there has a following. And the Spaniard.” His hand indicated me. I lowered my eyes, as a slave should, though I had been told often enough that I was not a slave in the old sense. I was bound by contract, by debt, by a signature made after wine and despair, by laws written by men whose sandals had never touched the mud of a conscription camp. Rome had many words for chains. Some were iron. Some were ink. “The Spaniard,” the magistrate said. “Look up.” I did. His eyes held no cruelty. That was almost worse. Cruel men acknowledged you existed enough to hate you. This one looked as if he were choosing fruit. “He has a stern face,” he said. “The people like that. What is his name?” “Marcellus Felix,” said the lanista. Felix. Lucky. The name still tasted bitter. It had been given to me after my first victory in the arena, when the crowd spared me with lifted thumbs and laughter. Before that, I had been Lucius Aelius Marcellus, son of a potter, husband to Livia, father to a child I had held only three days before the fever took him. Names, too, could be conquered. The magistrate’s attendant scratched marks into wax. “Have him visible in the procession.” The lanista bowed as if granted a province. When they passed on, Brennus muttered, “Visible. Like a painted whore.” “At least whores are paid directly,” Syrus said. The drill master struck his cane against a post. “Silence.” We trained until noon. Afterward, we were allowed to wash. In the changing room, the air was thick with steam from the small furnace and the metallic tang of blood from old bandages soaking in a basin. I scraped oil and dirt from my arms with a strigil, watching the gray sludge curl away. Men joked as they cleaned themselves, louder than necessary. Laughter in the ludus often meant fear had walked in and taken a seat. Through the open window I could see a slice of street. Children had gathered near the outer gate, hoping to glimpse us. One boy held a wooden sword and wore a pot lid tied to his arm. He thrust at another boy, who fell dramatically and kicked his legs. The others roared approval. I looked away. After washing, I sat in the shade beside the shrine with a heel of bread and a cup of watered wine. The bread was coarse, full of grit from the millstones. Still, I chewed carefully, grateful for the work of my teeth, the simple fact of swallowing. A fly worried at the rim of my cup. From somewhere nearby came the scent of roses. Perhaps a garland seller had set up outside for those on their way to temples or tombs. In Rome, the same flowers crowned brides, gods, corpses, and victors. Syrus sat beside me. “A letter came.” My hand tightened around the cup. “For you,” he said. “From the woman in Hispania.” He drew a folded scrap from inside his tunic. How he had gotten it, I did not ask. Syrus had ways of passing through the world like smoke through shutters. The seal was cracked. Of course it had been read. The lanista read everything, searching for plots, debts, useful grief. The writing was not Livia’s; she had never learned letters. Some village scribe had shaped her words. Marcellus, it began, not Felix. I stopped there and closed my eyes. Around me, Rome continued: hammer on stone, vendor cries, mule bells, the distant thunder of applause from some theater performance or public announcement in the Forum. The empire breathed and fed and counted its taxes. Legions stood on the Rhine and in Dacia. Ships crossed from Alexandria heavy with grain. Marble rose over neighborhoods where families slept six to a room. The emperor’s face shone on coins in every market stall, promising peace. Pax Romana, they called it. Peace, enforced by men with swords, celebrated by men who paid to watch others use them. I read the letter twice. Livia wrote that the kiln had cracked in winter. That my brother had taken ill but recovered. That a tax collector had come with two soldiers and taken three amphorae, though there had been no sale. That she had heard from a merchant that I was famous in Rome. She did not say she was proud. She did not say she forgave me. At the end, she wrote: If Hercules could be freed from his labors, perhaps men may also be. I held the words until they blurred. Syrus pretended not to notice. He picked at his bread and watched a line of ants find a crumb near his sandal. “Will you answer?” he asked. “Yes.” “What will you say?” I looked toward the amphitheatre, bright now under the afternoon sun, its arches crowded with tiny moving figures preparing for tomorrow’s show. Somewhere inside, workers would be spreading fresh sand, testing lifts, checking cages, painting scenery to turn death into myth. Rome loved myths because in them suffering had shape. A monster, a labor, a hero, a prize. Real suffering sprawled without poetry. It filled tax ledgers and slave markets, soldiers’ graves and women’s letters. “I will say I am alive,” I told him. “That is not much.” “It is today.” The second horn sounded, calling us back to the yard. I folded the letter and tucked it beneath the linen at my shoulder, close enough to feel when I moved. As I stood, I touched the little bone Hercules again. His carved face had no expression, or perhaps it had been worn away by too many desperate fingers. I imagined him after the labors, not triumphant, not crowned, only tired, standing in the dust with the lion skin heavy on his back and wondering what a man did with himself when the task was finished. I did not know whether the gods listened. Rome had so many of them, and all were busy. But the smell of incense from the street drifted over the wall just then, mingling with sweat, barley, smoke, and roses, and for a breath I felt less alone. Then the drill master shouted my name—Felix, always Felix—and I went back into the sun.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This is a sophisticated piece of historical fiction that demonstrates strong craft and literary merit. The author successfully creates an immersive Roman setting through carefully chosen sensory details - the dripping cistern, the smell of sweat and straw, the sounds of the awakening city. The first-person narration from Marcellus Felix is consistently maintained with an authentic voice that feels both ancient and accessible. The characterization is particularly strong. Marcellus emerges as a complex figure - not a stereotypical gladiator but a former potter, husband, and father bound by debt rather than slavery. His internal life is rich with memory, loss, and quiet dignity. Supporting characters like Brennus and Syrus are efficiently sketched but feel real, each with distinct voices and backgrounds. The prose quality is excellent throughout. Sentences like "Rome never smelled of one thing. It smelled of everything at once" and "Waiting gave him room to think" demonstrate economy and precision. The writing avoids purple prose while maintaining literary elegance. The pacing is deliberate but never drags, building atmosphere through accumulated detail rather than dramatic incident. The piece excels at showing rather than telling. Political and social commentary emerges naturally through observed details - the magistrate choosing gladiators like fruit, the bread lines, the tax collectors. The author weaves in larger themes about Roman society, class, and power without heavy-handed exposition. The emotional core is genuinely affecting. The letter from Livia provides a poignant anchor to Marcellus's lost life, and his response - "I will say I am alive" - carries real weight. The ending strikes an appropriate note of quiet resilience without false optimism. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the historical voice slips slightly modern, and some descriptions, while beautiful, occasionally border on being slightly overwrought. The piece also takes some time to fully engage, though this serves the contemplative mood. The work successfully fulfills all prompt requirements: it's set in Roman times, focuses on a gladiator's daily life outside combat, incorporates sensory details and political context, uses first person past tense, and feels like part of a larger narrative. The writing demonstrates genuine literary skill and emotional intelligence. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 18 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Epistolary: The Lighthouse Keeper — Score: 89.8
Prompt:
The Lighthouse Keeper Epistolary Narrative: Write a story told entirely through a series of brief correspondences: telegrams or letters or emails between two characters. The correspondence should span several months or years, and reveal a gradually unfolding plot. Use distinct voices for each character, and include details that provide insight into their personalities and motivations. The story should build to an emotional climax, and the final letter should provide a satisfying resolution. The setting is a lighthouse keeper writing to his mother. He is working class and Scottish. He is struggling with the isolation of his posting. Write naturally and without cliches. 1000 words.
Model Output:
12 January 1978 Ardnamurchan Lighthouse Acharacle, Argyll Dear Mam, I got here in the dark, which is maybe the worst way to arrive anywhere new. The ferry was late and the relief man, Mr. Beaton, had been waiting in the van with the engine running and no chat in him. We drove out past Kilchoan with the road going thin and black between the ditches. I could hear the sea before I saw it. The light is on a point they call Ardnamurchan, the most westerly bit of the mainland, though I’ve not yet had time to admire myself for being so far flung. There’s a big shoulder of rock called Sanna nearby, pale sand beyond it, and the Small Isles out to the west when the weather permits. Beaton says on a clear day you can see Rum like a sleeping beast. The tower is sound. White stone, red railings, stairs enough to punish a man for every cigarette he ever smoked. The quarters are clean but bare. I’ve put your picture on the mantel and the wee brass horse from Dad’s shelf on the windowsill. Don’t worry about me. It’s decent work and regular. I’m to keep the log, tend the generator, watch the lamp, and mind myself. Your loving son, Iain 26 January 1978 Govan Iain, Your letter was a long time coming. I had near worn a track from the sink to the front door. You say not to worry, which is what folk write when they know fine it does no good. I am glad the place is clean. Put a mat under the brass horse or it will mark the sill. Do not smoke on the stairs. Do not go climbing about rocks in the dark. Eat proper meals. If there is no butcher, buy tins. Your Auntie Morag says tinned peaches are as good as fresh if you don’t look at them too closely. I looked up Ardnamurchan in the big atlas at the library. The page is mostly water. I did not like that. I am sending wool socks and a fruit cake. Mrs. Donnelly at number 12 asked if you are “romantic-looking now, alone at a lighthouse.” I told her you are a daft long drink of water and she can keep her romances. Write every week. Mam 9 February 1978 Ardnamurchan Lighthouse Dear Mam, The cake arrived in one piece, which is more than can be said for the postie’s mood. He says the road after Salen is a disgrace and ought to be taken up and laid again by men with a conscience. The socks are grand. Thank you. My feet have not been dry since Tuesday but they’ve been warm, which is a separate blessing. There are three of us in the rotation but I’m here alone this fortnight. Beaton has gone home to Oban for his daughter’s wedding and young MacRae is away with flu. The Board says I can manage. I can. The lamp is a Dalen affair with acetylene backup, and the fog signal could wake the dead in Tiree. The quiet is not really quiet. It is gulls, wind at the seals, rain like handfuls of grit, the generator clattering, the sea breathing in the dark. At night I hear things in the walls, though it’s only the pipes. Tell me what’s doing in Govan. Tell me the ordinary stuff. Iain 18 February 1978 Govan Son, Ordinary stuff then. Mr. Haldane has painted his door blue and everyone is acting as if he put a dancing girl in the close. The butcher has grown a moustache that does not suit him. Your cousin Paul was in court for fighting outside the Clachan Bar and your Auntie Morag says it was not his fault, which is how we know it was. I went to the bingo and won four pounds. I spent one on bacon and one on coal, and the other two are in the sugar tin because I am wild with money. I am pleased you are managing, but I do not like “I hear things in the walls.” Pipes or not, you sleep with a lamp by you. And mind you, your father used to say a man by himself will argue with his own boots if left long enough. If you start losing arguments to footwear, you come home. Mam 7 March 1978 Ardnamurchan Lighthouse Dear Mam, There was a storm last night that made the glass shake in the frames. I was on the gallery at half two, harnessed on, checking the panes. The beam kept sweeping over the water and showing it all torn white. Every time it came round I saw the same rock ledge below, then darkness, then the ledge again, like a thought you can’t get rid of. I’m all right. I put that in before you start. A fishing boat came close in the afternoon, too close for my liking. I signalled with the lamp and they changed course. I could see a man on deck raise his hand. It fair lifted me, that little wave, which is a foolish thing to admit. Another human body, no more than that, and me near greeting over it. I’ve taken to speaking aloud when I’m doing jobs. “Spanner, Iain.” “Kettle on, Iain.” “Do not trip, Iain, you useless article.” It helps. Your son, Iain 15 March 1978 Govan Iain, It is not foolish. A wave is a wave. Your father stood at the shipyard gate for thirty years and would still come home pleased if somebody had nodded to him like he mattered. Speak aloud if you want. Sing too. You have a voice like a hinge but no one is there to suffer it. I spoke to Father Campbell after mass. He asked after you and said solitude can be a trial or a gift. I told him he could try it himself and let us know which. I have enclosed the clipping about the strike, since you asked for ordinary things. Men are still men, bosses are still bosses, rain is still rain. Mam 2 May 1978 Ardnamurchan Lighthouse Dear Mam, Spring has come, or something wearing its coat. The machair near Sanna is greening, and there are flowers tucked low out of the wind. I walked there on my day off when MacRae was here, and the sand was so white it looked foreign. Eigg was clear, and Rum beyond it, blue and sharp. I stood looking till I forgot to be cold. MacRae is twenty-three and thinks himself wasted because he can play three chords on a guitar. He says the lights will all be automated one day and men like us will be put out to pasture. Beaton says that’s been said since Queen Victoria and he’ll believe it when a machine can thaw a frozen valve with its own breath. I am sleeping better. Your letters help more than I say. Love, Iain 10 May 1978 Govan Son, I am glad you walked. See, there is a world even at the edge of the world. I am sending more tea, the strong kind, and a packet of shortbread that was meant for the Women’s Guild but I hid it. If asked, I will deny everything. Do not listen too much to boys with guitars. They think sadness makes them interesting. Your last line pleased me. I will not make a song and dance, but I read it twice. Mam 1 November 1978 Ardnamurchan Lighthouse Mam, I should have written sooner. There was an accident last week. Not mine. MacRae slipped on the lower path carrying oil cans. Broke his leg and cut his head. It was bad weather and no boat could come in. I got him up to the kitchen on my back, though he’s taller than me and cursed the whole way like a man paying rent on the words. The radio was patchy. Beaton was away. For five hours it was me, him, the blood on the floor, and the foghorn going every thirty seconds. He’s in Fort William hospital now. He’ll be fine, they say. He wrote to say he remembers none of it, which I envy. I can’t get the sound of him falling out of my head. A small sound, Mam. Not like in films. Just a crack and then the wind. I have not slept much. Iain 7 November 1978 Govan Iain, I am coming. Do not write back to argue. I have spoken to your Uncle Tam and he will take me as far as the ferry. Mrs. Donnelly will feed the cat. I have packed the brown case. You carried that boy and saved him. Now you are sitting with the fright after the doing is done. That is when fright gets its teeth in. I know. When your father died, I made the tea, spoke to the doctor, found his good shirt, answered the door, and only the next week did I drop a cup and shake like a fool. You are not a fool. You are tired and heartsore. If the Board has rules about mothers, they can write them on paper and burn them. Mam 14 November 1978 Ardnamurchan Lighthouse Dear Mam, By the time this reaches you, you’ll maybe already be on the way, so I’ll leave this with the post in Kilchoan and hope it crosses you. Don’t come. Not because I don’t want you. God knows I do. But the road is poor, the weather is turning, and I will not have you rattling across half Scotland because your grown son can’t bear the night. I spoke to Beaton. Properly, not just about valves and paraffin. He sat with me in the kitchen and said he once spent a winter at Flannan after a death in his family and thought the sea was calling his name. Then he poured whisky into two chipped mugs and told me if I ever repeated that he’d deny it. The Board is sending MacRae’s replacement early. I’ll not be alone this month. Stay home, Mam. Keep warm. I’ll come for Christmas if they’ll grant leave. Iain 20 November 1978 Govan Iain, Your letter met me at the door with my hat on. I sat down in my coat and cried, partly from relief and partly because I had spent two days practising what I would say to the Board man when he tried to stop me. It was a very good speech. A waste. I will stay, since you ask it like a man and not a bairn hiding a cut knee. But you will write twice a week until Christmas. Not long letters. A line will do. “Alive. Ate soup.” That sort of poetry is acceptable. I am proud of you for speaking to Beaton. Men of your father’s generation would rather drown than say they were wet. Mam 24 December 1978 Govan Iain, The house is ready. I have made soup, mince pies, and too much trifle because I misjudged my own enthusiasm. Your room is aired. The cat has taken against the clean sheets, which means he approves. If the weather keeps you, send word. If not, I will be at Queen Street in the blue coat. Bring nothing but yourself. Mam 3 January 1979 Ardnamurchan Lighthouse Dear Mam, I’m back on the point. The train away from Glasgow felt worse than the first time, which I did not expect. Christmas was grand. I should have said it better while I was there, but being in the house again put a lump in me. The kettle, the damp towels over the pulley, you telling the cat he was a Protestant for refusing fish on a Friday. It near undid me. I found the packet you put in my bag. I’ll wear Dad’s scarf, though it smells of the wardrobe and your lavender soap more than him now. I am not cured of loneliness, if that is a thing men get cured of. But I know its shape better. Some nights it stands right in the room. Some nights it stays outside with the weather. Love, Iain 11 January 1979 Govan Son, Nobody is cured of loneliness. We only learn where to set it down while we get on with the potatoes. I am glad you took the scarf. Your father wore it on the bus to work every winter. He pretended not to like the colour, but he would look for it if I’d moved it. Mrs. Donnelly says you looked thin. I told her it was the cut of your coat. Eat more. I have been thinking: I might come in summer, when the road is civil and the sea is showing off. Not to rescue you. To see where you are. Mam 30 June 1979 Ardnamurchan Lighthouse Mam, You’ll have the travel times by now. I’ll meet you at Kilchoan on Thursday, weather willing. Bring boots, not your church shoes. The path down to the fog signal is steep, and I want you to see it without breaking your neck. The place is all light just now. It hardly gets dark. At midnight there’s still a band of silver over the Minch, and the islands sit out there like they’ve been put in a row for inspection. There are seals on the skerries below the tower. They make noises like old men disagreeing. I’ve made up the spare bed. Beaton says mothers inspect corners, so I’ve dusted even behind the wireless. I am looking forward to seeing you here. That surprises me, though I don’t know why. Iain 9 July 1979 Govan My dear Iain, Home again, with sand still in the turn-ups of my trousers and a face burnt on one side like a badly fried egg. I will say this: the lighthouse is not the end of the world. I thought it was. I thought of you perched out there in punishment, with nothing but black water and the wind gnawing at you. But I saw the land rolling behind it, the road, the crofts, the children at Kilchoan waving at the van. I saw Sanna Bay shining as if someone had scrubbed the earth clean. I saw your hands on the rail of the gallery, steady as anything, and the lamp turning behind you. You are lonely there, yes. I could feel it in the kitchen when the wind dropped and there was suddenly too much silence. But you are not lost. Thank you for taking me up the tower, even though I made a disgraceful noise on the stairs. Thank you for showing me the logbook and the seals and the place where MacRae fell, though that was hard. Thank you for letting me see the life you are making. I have put the shell you gave me on the mantel beside your father’s photograph. It looks well there. With love, Mam 2 December 1980 Ardnamurchan Lighthouse Dear Mam, News came official today. Ardnamurchan is to be automated within eighteen months. MacRae will be pleased to have been right and insufferable with it. Beaton only grunted, but he polished the brass after supper like he was dressing a body. They’ll offer postings elsewhere. Some keepers are taking redundancy. I don’t know yet. I thought I would be glad. Then I stood outside and looked up at the tower and felt as if I’d been told an old workmate had taken ill. Daft, maybe. It has been walls and weather and wages. It has also kept me alive in more ways than one. Do you think I should come back to Glasgow? Iain 9 December 1980 Govan Iain, I cannot answer that for you, though I badly want to, which is a mother’s chronic disease. Ask yourself this: are you staying because the place needs you, or because leaving frightens you? And if you come home, are you coming toward something, or only away from the sea? There is work in Glasgow, though not enough and not easy. There is your room, though you are too old to live under my feet unless needed. There is company, and there is also loneliness in a crowd. Do not imagine the city is a cure. It is only noisier. Whatever you choose, choose like a free man. Mam 18 May 1982 Ardnamurchan Lighthouse Dear Mam, Tonight is my last watch. The engineers have been here all week with their boxes and cables, speaking in cheerful voices as if no one was dying. The new gear works. The light turns without me. The logbook is to be closed at midnight. Beaton left this morning. He shook my hand hard and said, “You were all right, Govan,” which from him is a love poem. MacRae sent a postcard from Aberdeen: “Told you. Machines.” I have pinned it above the stove to hate properly. I’ve taken the brass horse from the sill. No mark, because you warned me. I walked to Sanna after tea. The tide was out and the sand went on forever. Rum was hidden, Eigg half there, Muck just a dark smudge. I thought about the first night and the road in the rain, and how I believed the place would swallow me. It did not. It took a bite now and then, but it gave things back too: patience, mostly. The knowledge that a man can be by himself and still not vanish. I am coming home for a month. After that I’ve accepted a post with the harbour authority at Oban. Shore work. Lights and buoys, maintenance, a small room over an office to start with. Close enough to the sea to hear it, far enough to buy a paper without planning a campaign. So that is my decision. Not Glasgow, not exile. Something between. At midnight I’ll put my name in the book one last time. Then I’ll make tea and sit till morning. I thought I would be ashamed to cry, but I am not. The lamp will go on turning. Ships will still see it. That will have to be enough. I’ll be at Queen Street on Friday, half three if the trains behave. Don’t make trifle. Make soup. Your loving son, Iain 23 May 1982 Govan My dear boy, Soup is made. Lentil, with a ham bone, the way you like it. There is bread from McAllister’s and butter in the dish. I have not made trifle, though there are two tins of peaches in the press in case of emergency or happiness. I read your letter at the table and then again by the window. I am sorry for the leaving, and glad for it too. That is allowed. A heart can hold both if it must. Your father would have liked “something between.” He was always telling me a boat was safest tied up, and I was always telling him boats were not built for that. We were both right, depending on the weather. Come home Friday. Bring Dad’s scarf if you still have it. Bring the brass horse. Bring whatever of the lighthouse fits in your bag, and leave the rest where it belongs, shining for strangers. I will be at the station in the blue coat. With all my love, Mam
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This epistolary narrative demonstrates exceptional craft in multiple dimensions. The author successfully creates two distinct, authentic voices - Iain's introspective, poetic sensibility contrasts beautifully with his mother's practical, warm directness. The Scottish working-class dialect feels natural and unforced, avoiding stereotypical representations while maintaining authenticity. The story structure is masterfully paced across nearly five years, allowing the emotional arc to develop organically. Iain's journey from initial isolation through crisis to eventual acceptance and growth feels entirely earned. The lighthouse automation serves as both plot device and metaphor for inevitable change, handled with subtlety rather than heavy-handedness. The prose quality is consistently high, with particularly strong imagery that captures the harsh beauty of the Scottish coast without becoming overwrought. Phrases like "the sea breathing in the dark" and "loneliness... some nights it stands right in the room" demonstrate sophisticated metaphorical thinking. The mother's voice provides perfect counterpoint - grounded, humorous, and deeply loving without sentimentality. The emotional complexity is perhaps the story's greatest strength. The relationship between mother and son evolves through genuine crisis (MacRae's accident) and recovery, with both characters growing. The mother's decision to visit, then Iain's request that she not come, followed by her eventual summer visit, creates a realistic push-and-pull of independence and connection. The ending avoids false optimism while providing genuine resolution. Iain's choice of Oban - "something between" - perfectly captures the story's theme of finding balance between isolation and connection, tradition and progress. His tears at the lighthouse's decommissioning feel completely authentic, as does his mother's bittersweet response. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the prose edges toward preciousness, and some letters feel slightly more literary than strictly realistic correspondence might warrant. However, these are minor quibbles with an otherwise exceptional piece that fully realizes its ambitious scope and emotional goals. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 19 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 19 Emotionally Complex: 19 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 2 Weak Dialogue: 1 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18

+ First-person unique perspective: Hacker Mindset — Score: 88.0
Prompt:
Hacker Mindset Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of a reformed (now) white hat hacker who cannot help but notice exploitable weaknesses in both systems and people they encounter. They habitually look for vulnerabilities and exploit vectors. The narrative takes place during a first date at a restaurant, where they're trying to focus on human connection. It's going surprisingly well due to a discovered shared nerd-fandom (magic the gathering). Things go a little sideways when the date reveals a noticeably insecure phone unlock pattern, which protagonist can't help but comment on. They are then left to negotiate the fallout of this faux pas and attempt to salvage (or perhaps sabotage) the remainder of the date. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I notice the fire exits first. That’s not a brag. It’s a defect. Other people walk into a restaurant and notice the lighting, the smell of garlic, whether the hostess smiled in a way that felt genuine or compulsory. I notice the fire exits, the POS terminal on the bar counter angled toward the dining room, the manager’s office door propped open with a case of tonic water, and the QR code taped to each table in a little acrylic stand like an altar to convenience and bad hygiene. The QR code has been reprinted three times. The newest one is slightly misaligned over an older sticker. I do not peel it up. This is called growth. My date is already there, sitting in the booth under a painting of lemons that has never seen a lemon tree in its life. Her name is Mara. She has curly hair, a green cardigan, and the kind of expression people get when they’re trying not to check whether the person approaching them matches their profile pictures. Okay. Human connection. Eye contact. Smile. Do not assess attack surface. “Hi,” I say. “Hi,” she says, and smiles for real, or real enough that my brain doesn’t immediately file it under social engineering. We do the awkward half-stand, half-wave, should-we-hug negotiation. I opt for the wave because I have spent years learning that the safest exploit is often no exploit at all. I sit. She orders sparkling water. I order iced tea. The waiter leaves us with menus and a little silence, and I can feel my mind start to crawl along the edges of it, looking for a seam. “So,” she says, “your profile said cybersecurity, but, like, the ethical kind?” There it is. The door with a sign on it. “The ethical kind now,” I say, because apparently I believe first dates are a good place for ominous qualifiers. Her eyebrows lift. “I mean, I do security assessments. Pen testing. Mostly corporate stuff. I help people find holes before someone worse does.” “Someone worse than you?” “Historically? Occasionally.” She laughs. Good. Laughter is a successful handshake, a 200 OK from the social web server. Don’t ruin it. “I work in archives,” she says. “University special collections. Mostly manuscripts, old maps, some private papers.” My brain perks up like a raccoon hearing a trash can lid clatter. Archives. Old maps. Private papers. Access controls probably based on trust, underfunded IT, students reusing passwords. No. Stop. Not the university. Not her job. Not tonight. “That sounds amazing,” I say, because it does. “It is. Except when someone comes in convinced we have a secret medieval spellbook that will prove their YouTube channel right.” “Do you?” “If I did, I wouldn’t tell you on a first date.” Excellent. Boundary-aware. Possibly dangerous. The waiter returns. We order. She gets mushroom risotto. I get salmon because it is the first thing I see that doesn’t require a decision tree. We drift through the standard packets: where did you grow up, siblings, pets, the weirdest job you’ve ever had. Each answer comes with metadata. Mara grew up in Dayton. Her first pet was a fat orange cat named Sir Pounce. Her mother’s maiden name appears in a story about a family reunion where all the Kellehers fought over potato salad. Her elementary school was St. Brigid’s, which she mentions because a nun once confiscated her Pokémon cards and never gave them back. My brain quietly opens a spreadsheet. Common security questions: - Childhood pet - Mother’s maiden name - First school - Childhood city I mentally close the spreadsheet. Then I imagine setting the spreadsheet on fire. Then I imagine securely wiping the ashes. Ask her about herself, not as an enumeration exercise. Be normal. “So you were a Pokémon kid,” I say. “Oh, absolutely. But I fell harder into Magic later.” My fork pauses halfway to the bread plate. “Magic as in…?” “The Gathering,” she says. The restaurant recedes. It’s embarrassing how fast the firewall drops. There are things you can fake interest in and things you can’t. I have listened politely to people explain ultramarathon pacing, artisanal soap, small-batch bourbon, and the emotional lives of houseplants. But Magic? Magic is a native protocol. “What format?” I ask, too quickly. She grins. “Commander, mostly. Don’t judge me.” “I would never.” “You say that like someone who absolutely would.” “I play Yuriko.” “Oh, so you’re a bad person.” “I said white hat, not saint.” She laughs again, and this time I don’t analyze it. I just enjoy it. For fifteen minutes, maybe twenty, I forget to be a surveillance apparatus wearing a button-down shirt. We talk about opening packs as kids, the smell of old cards, the tragedy of mana screw, whether Universes Beyond is brilliant or an omen of the end times. She has a Muldrotha deck she calls “compost crimes.” I tell her about my friend’s Krenko deck, which once made enough goblins to require dice, coins, and eventually “a conceptual understanding of numbers.” She says, “Okay, but what’s your pet card? Not best card. Pet card.” “Baleful Strix,” I say instantly. “Oh, that’s very on-brand.” “It replaces itself and discourages attacks. That’s basically my entire personality.” “Mine is Sun Titan,” she says. “I like getting things back.” There is a moment after she says it. A small one. Maybe she doesn’t mean anything by it. Maybe everyone is a graveyard recursion metaphor if you stare long enough. But she looks down at her water, and I feel something in me soften in a way I do not have a CVE number for. This is going well. This is somehow going well. The food comes. The risotto smells like butter and thyme. My salmon is good enough that I notice it, which means my threat model is relaxing. Then her phone lights up. It’s face down beside her plate, but it buzzes, and she flips it over. The screen glows with a notification from someone named Jules: DID YOU SURVIVE NERD DATE? Mara makes a tiny embarrassed noise and angles it away. “Sorry,” she says. “My roommate is invested.” “Understandable,” I say. “I have also informed a third party of my location and approximate life signs.” “Good. Sensible.” Then she unlocks her phone. Pattern unlock. Not PIN. Not biometrics. A pattern. A large, clean, visible L-shape with a flourish: top left, top middle, top right, center, bottom center. Five nodes. Drawn with the casual confidence of someone entering the same motel room code for five years. My mouth knows before my judgment does. “Oh no,” I say. Her thumb freezes. “What?” I hear the sentence forming. I see the bridge out. I accelerate. “That pattern is really easy to shoulder-surf.” Silence. Not total silence. Restaurants are never silent. Forks scrape. Someone at the bar laughs too loudly. A cappuccino machine screams in the distance like it has seen the source code of the universe. But at our table, something locks. Mara lowers the phone. I should stop. I do not stop. “Sorry, it’s just—patterns are generally weaker than people think, especially if they’re short or start in a corner, and that one has a really common shape. Plus there are smudge attacks, and—” “Smudge attacks?” “Where someone can infer the pattern from finger oils on the screen.” Her expression changes from amused to guarded. Not angry exactly. Worse: disappointed in a way that suggests she had just begun to believe I was not a walking cautionary tale. I put down my fork. Idiot. No, not idiot. Idiot is too kind. Idiots trip over things. I installed the rake, mapped its coordinates, labeled it RAKE_DO_NOT_STEP_ON, then performed a running jump onto it. “I’m sorry,” I say. She looks at me for a beat. “Do you always do that?” “Notice? Yes. Comment? I am trying to do less of that.” “But not tonight.” “Apparently not tonight.” Her phone is still in her hand. The screen has gone dark. I can see our reflection in it: her, guarded; me, performing a live post-incident review on my own personality. “I didn’t mean to make you feel watched,” I say. “But you were watching.” “Yes.” Honesty. Dangerous. Necessary. “I mean—not like that. Not intentionally. I notice security stuff automatically. Exits, cameras, locks, passwords on sticky notes. Phone patterns. It’s annoying. I know it’s annoying.” “Is this the part where you tell me you could hack me?” Absolutely not. Never say yes. Even if yes. Especially if yes. “No,” I say. “This is the part where I tell you that was rude and I’m sorry.” She studies me. My brain, traitorous little goblin, supplies options. Option one: Salvage. Be humble. Change subject. Offer repair. Option two: Explain at length. Convince her she should be grateful. Die alone surrounded by mechanical keyboards. Option three: Make a joke. High variance. Option four: Flee through previously identified fire exit. “I spend a lot of time,” I say carefully, “getting paid to be professionally suspicious. It’s useful in the right context. It’s corrosive in the wrong one.” “And a first date is the wrong one?” “Almost certainly.” “Almost?” “I mean, if the waiter asks for your Social Security number, I reserve the right to intervene.” A corner of her mouth twitches. Not a full recovery, but a signal. “Noted.” I breathe, but only internally. Externally breathing too visibly seems like it might be weird. She sets her phone down, face down again. “For the record, I know it’s not great security.” I blink. “You do?” “I work in archives, not a cave. We have trainings. Terrible ones, mostly. With stock photos of hackers in hoodies.” “We don’t all wear hoodies.” “You are currently wearing a hoodie under a blazer.” “That is a tactical cardigan.” “It has a zipper.” “Advanced tactical cardigan.” The twitch becomes a small laugh. Connection reestablished, maybe with packet loss. She takes a sip of water. “I use the pattern because my hands are usually full, and Face ID hates my winter scarves, and I got lazy. There. My shame.” “I respect the threat model of mittens.” “You should.” “I should also have kept my mouth shut.” “Probably.” Fair. The risotto steams between us. My salmon cools in the manner of all consequences. She tilts her head. “Can you turn it off?” “The noticing?” “Yeah.” “No,” I say. “But I can decide what to do with it. Sometimes. With practice. And occasional failure.” “Tonight being…?” “A useful case study in failure.” “I don’t want to be a case study.” “Right. Sorry. Bad phrasing.” She leans back. “You’re really bad at this.” “Dating?” “Not sounding like a corporate breach report.” “I contain multitudes, most of them formatted as PDFs.” That gets a real laugh. Relief hits me so hard I almost confess to three more things, which is apparently how my nervous system celebrates. Don’t. Do not mention her cat. Do not mention St. Brigid’s. Do not mention Kelleher. Do not explain that her roommate’s name is now in your mental graph alongside Dayton and Sun Titan. Do not, under any circumstances, say “attack surface” again. She picks up her fork. “Okay. Since you ruined the vibe—” “I did.” “—you have to answer a question.” “Anything except my first pet’s name.” She pauses. Damn it. I wince. “Sorry. Security question joke.” “Oh my God.” “I know.” “No, actually, that one was funny. Annoying, but funny.” “I’ll take it.” She points her fork at me. “Worst deck you’ve ever built. Not power level. Morally.” Ah. Mercy, then. A pathway out. “I built a group hug deck that wasn’t group hug.” Her eyes narrow. “Go on.” “It pretended to help everyone draw cards and ramp.” “Uh-huh.” “And then it punished them for having cards and lands.” “That’s not group hug. That’s group mugging.” “Correct.” “You monster.” “I dismantled it.” “Reformed white hat, indeed.” The table warms again. Carefully. Not the easy warmth from before, but something more deliberate, which might be better or might just be more fragile. We talk about decks we regret, cards we hate on principle, whether anyone who says “it’s just casual” before playing a turn-one Mana Crypt should be legally required to apologize. But under it all, I am aware of the line I crossed. Not because phone security doesn’t matter. It does. That’s the trap: being right about the wrong thing at the wrong time in the wrong way. I used to think truth was a skeleton key. Apply enough of it and every lock opens. It turns out people are not locks. Or they are, but not the kind you pick. The kind you are invited through, if you can stop jiggling the handle. When dessert menus arrive, she looks at hers for three seconds and says, “The cheesecake here is good. My dad used to bring me here after spelling bees.” Spelling bees. Restaurant history. Father. Childhood reward rituals. More breadcrumbs, glowing in the dark. I let them lie there. “Then we should get cheesecake,” I say. “We?” “If you’re amenable to a shared dessert with a man who has already criticized your phone hygiene.” “I’m considering it.” “That’s more than I deserve.” “Yes.” She smiles when she says it. We order cheesecake and two coffees. Her phone buzzes again. She flips it over, reads, rolls her eyes, and this time does not unlock it. Instead, she turns it toward me. The notification preview says: WELL??? IS HE WEIRD HOT OR WEIRD WEIRD? I look at it. Then at her. “This feels like a trap,” I say. “It is.” “I refuse to assist in my own classification.” “Coward.” “Security professional.” She laughs. A minute later, she picks up the phone and, under the table, unlocks it. I look away so hard I practically study the lemon painting. Look at me, respecting privacy. Look at me not being a disaster for three consecutive seconds. When I glance back, she’s typing. Maybe to Jules. Maybe: weird weird, send extraction team. Maybe: jury still out. Maybe: he knows what Baleful Strix is. I can live with any of those. The cheesecake arrives with two forks and a raspberry sauce drizzle attempting cursive. We eat from opposite sides, negotiating the crust without discussing it. This, too, is a protocol. Shared resources. Implicit rules. Trust established through small, observable restraint. After a few bites, she says, “If I wanted to make my phone less embarrassing, hypothetically…” I keep my face neutral. Do not pounce. Do not become a training module. “Hypothetically,” I say, “a longer PIN is good. Six digits minimum, more if you can tolerate it. Password manager. Updates. Don’t reuse passwords. Multifactor on important accounts.” She watches me. “And,” I add, “I can send you a couple resources if you want. Or not. I will not bring a slide deck to date two.” “Date two?” My heartbeat does something noncompliant. “I mean. If. Hypothetically.” She takes another bite of cheesecake, considering. “No slide deck.” “Agreed.” “No auditing my life.” “Absolutely.” “No using anything I told you tonight to guess my passwords.” “I would never.” She looks at me over her coffee. I hold up both hands. “I mean that. The old version of me liked proving doors were unlocked. The current version is trying to be the kind of person people don’t regret inviting in.” That lands somewhere. I can’t tell where, exactly, but her expression softens again, and this time it is not because of Magic. “Okay,” she says. Just okay. But okay is underrated. Okay is a green light after a warning. Okay is a patched vulnerability. Okay is not trust, but it is the possibility of trust. Outside, later, the air is cold. She wraps her scarf twice around her face, and I do not say a word about Face ID. We stand on the sidewalk in the blue-white glow of the restaurant sign. The date is ending, which means a new negotiation begins: hug, handshake, escape, sequel. “I had a good time,” she says. “Despite the incident?” “Despite the incident.” “I also had a good time.” “You’re very strange.” “I’ve received that feedback.” “But not boring.” “Also received that feedback, usually in less flattering language.” She smiles. “Text me when you get home?” “Yes.” “And I’ll change my unlock thing.” “Only if you want to.” “I want to. But if you say ‘good threat modeling,’ I’m leaving.” I close my mouth. She laughs, steps forward, and hugs me. For once, I do not catalog exits, cameras, reflections, unlocked devices, overheard names, or the waiter entering a four-digit manager code into the terminal behind the window. I notice her hair smells faintly like citrus. I notice her cardigan is soft. I notice she holds on for exactly long enough that I don’t have to wonder if it was only politeness. Then she lets go. “Goodnight, tactical cardigan,” she says. “Goodnight, compost crimes.” She walks away, and I do not watch which direction for long enough to learn anything useful. This is called growth. Probably.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional craft in executing a challenging narrative premise. The author successfully maintains the protagonist's distinctive voice throughout—a reformed hacker whose analytical mindset creates both humor and genuine interpersonal tension. The internal monologue feels authentic and consistent, balancing technical knowledge with self-awareness about social dysfunction. The characterization is particularly strong. The protagonist emerges as genuinely three-dimensional: competent in their field yet struggling with basic human interaction, self-aware enough to recognize their flaws but still prone to stepping on conversational landmines. Mara is equally well-developed, neither a mere romantic interest nor an idealized figure, but someone with her own agency who responds realistically to the protagonist's social missteps. The technical elements are seamlessly integrated. The hacker mindset manifests naturally through observations about fire exits, POS terminals, and security vulnerabilities, never feeling forced or expository. The Magic: The Gathering shared interest serves as an effective bridge between characters while reinforcing their personalities through card preferences and play styles. The central conflict—the protagonist's inability to suppress their security-focused observations—creates genuine dramatic tension. When they comment on Mara's phone pattern, the fallout feels earned and realistic. The recovery process is handled with particular skill, avoiding easy resolution while maintaining hope for connection. The prose style effectively mirrors the protagonist's analytical nature without becoming dry or overly technical. Metaphors like "social web server" and "200 OK" feel organic to the character's worldview. The pacing builds naturally from initial observations through shared connection to crisis and tentative resolution. The dialogue, though limited, rings true to both characters. Mara's responses feel genuine rather than serving merely as setup for the protagonist's observations. The banter about Magic cards reveals character while advancing their relationship. The ending strikes an appropriate balance—neither overly optimistic nor pessimistically dark. The protagonist shows genuine growth in restraining their analytical impulses during the final hug, while the relationship remains realistically uncertain. The "This is called growth. Probably." conclusion perfectly captures the character's ongoing struggle with self-improvement. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the technical metaphors feel slightly strained, and some repetitive elements in the protagonist's self-recrimination. However, these don't significantly detract from the overall effectiveness. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18

+ First-person unique perspective: Overthinking It — Score: 88.8
Prompt:
Overthinking It Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of an intelligent 8th-grade girl with social anxiety who has developed a crush on a classmate. The narrative takes place during a group science project where they're paired together, as she cycles through rehearsed conversations while second-guessing each potential approach. Her inner thoughts should reveal the gap between her articulate internal voice and her difficulty expressing herself aloud, her fears of coming across as desperate or weird, and the exhausting process of monitoring her every word, expression, and movement for potential embarrassment. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The projector squeaks every time Ms. Alvarez changes slides, like a tiny mouse is being stepped on somewhere inside the ceiling. Squeak. “Today you’ll be working with your partners to design a model ecosystem,” she says, tapping the remote against her palm. “You’ll need to include producers, consumers, decomposers, energy transfer, and at least three limiting factors.” Squeak. A new slide appears with a picture of a pond and a cartoon frog that looks too cheerful considering it is probably about to be eaten by a heron. I am trying not to look at Noah Ellis. Trying not to look is different from not looking, obviously. Not looking is just a thing normal people do, like breathing or choosing a pencil. Trying not to look involves a full-body awareness of exactly where your eyes are pointed at every second. It involves pretending to read the slide even though the words have become meaningless. It involves knowing that Noah is two tables away, slightly behind me on the left, because when Ms. Alvarez said “partners” he shifted in his chair and his sneaker squeaked against the floor, which is not the same as the projector squeak but apparently my brain has decided to catalogue every noise he makes. This is ridiculous. I am a person with a functioning brain. I get A’s on lab reports. I can explain symbiosis, trophic levels, and why invasive species are basically ecological jump-scares. I can write a five-paragraph essay in forty minutes and still have time to underline my thesis. I should be able to sit in a classroom near one human boy without feeling like my heart is trying to submit a late assignment. Squeak. “Your partners are assigned,” Ms. Alvarez says. My stomach performs a complicated gymnastics routine. Please don’t let it be Noah. Please let it be Noah. Please don’t let it be Noah, because if it is Noah I will have to speak to him with my real mouth instead of with the imaginary version of myself who is charming and calm and says things like, “Good point, Noah,” without sounding like she is accepting an award. Please let it be Noah, because if it is not Noah, then I will spend the whole period watching him work with someone else, probably someone like Ava, who can laugh without covering her mouth and who always manages to make messy buns look intentional instead of like she fought with a pillow and lost. Ms. Alvarez starts reading names. “Amaya and Jordan.” I fold my hands on my desk. Not too tightly. Tight hands look nervous. Loose hands look normal. But if they’re too loose, they look limp, like dead fish. Why am I thinking about hand posture? No one is looking at my hands. Unless Noah is looking. He is not looking. Why would he be looking at my hands? “Lena and Sophie.” I am Lena. There are three Lenas in the grade, but in this class I am the only one, so that is me. Wait. She said Sophie. Of course she said Sophie. That is fine. Sophie is nice. Sophie lets people borrow highlighters and has handwriting like a font. This is good. Safe. No emotional danger. I can breathe. Then Ms. Alvarez frowns at her clipboard. “Actually, Sophie, you’re with Marcus. I made a mistake. Lena, you’re with Noah.” There is a pause that probably lasts one second but expands into a full historical era. The projector squeaks. Noah turns around. My bones become made of cooked spaghetti. Okay. This is fine. This is scientifically not life-threatening. I am being paired with a classmate for an academic activity. People survive this every day. Civilizations have risen and fallen. One time I got a 92 on a math quiz even though I forgot there was a quiz. I can do this. Noah picks up his notebook and comes over to my table. Do I smile? A smile says friendly. Too much smile says I have been waiting for this exact moment since September and have maybe written my first name with your last name in the margins of my agenda, which I absolutely have not done except once as a joke that was not funny even to me. Small smile. Closed-mouth? Closed-mouth can look annoyed. Teeth? Teeth can look intense. Maybe half-smile. Is half-smile creepy? Stop planning your face. “Hey,” he says. Say hey. This is the simplest possible human exchange. It has one syllable. Cavemen could manage this. “Hi,” I say, which comes out smaller than intended, like the word has been folded into quarters. He sits across from me. His hair is doing that thing where one piece falls forward near his eyebrow and he doesn’t notice. Or maybe he does notice and chooses to let it be there because he is secretly very aware of his hair and has arranged it to look accidentally good. That is none of my business. “So,” he says, opening his notebook, “ecosystem.” I should say something. I have many somethings. An entire conference of somethings is taking place in my head. Option one: “We could do a pond ecosystem because it has clear producer-consumer relationships, like algae, insects, frogs, fish, herons.” Good. Intelligent. Relevant. Shows I understood the assignment. But maybe too intense. Maybe it sounds like I am trying to become Ms. Alvarez Jr. Also if I say “herons” weirdly, then he might think of that forever. Herons. Hair-ons. No. Option two: “Do you have any ideas?” Polite. Collaborative. But what if he says “not really” and then we sit there in a silence so large it becomes a third partner? Option three: “I like your shirt.” Absolutely not. The shirt is just a blue T-shirt with a faded mountain on it. Complimenting it would reveal I have noticed his clothing, which is basically a confession. Also what if he says, “Thanks, my mom got it at Target,” and then we both have to think about his mom and Target. Option four: something funny. “We could make the decomposers the stars of the show. Very underrated. Worms deserve awards.” No. Too weird. Unless it lands. If it lands, he laughs, and then I seem quirky in a good way. But if it doesn’t, I become Worm Girl until graduation. Noah taps his pencil. Not impatiently, maybe just pencil-tapping. People tap pencils. It doesn’t mean they are judging you. Unless they are. “Pond?” I say. Just the one word. Excellent. Very eloquent. Truly, someone alert the debate team. “Yeah,” he says. “Pond works.” He writes POND ECOSYSTEM at the top of the page in all caps. He accepted the idea. That means the idea was fine. Maybe he thinks I’m smart. Maybe he thinks I only know one-word ecosystems. No, stop. I pull my notebook closer and write the same heading. My handwriting is usually neat, but now my P looks like it is trying to escape. “We need producers,” he says. “Algae,” I say too fast. “Yeah. And duckweed?” Duckweed. He knows duckweed. Why is that attractive? It is literally a tiny floating plant. My standards are now aquatic vegetation knowledge. “Duckweed is good,” I say. Good? I sound like a teacher putting a sticker on a worksheet. Duckweed is good, Noah. Excellent duckweed. Please proceed to amphibians for extra credit. He looks up at me. “Are frogs primary consumers or secondary?” I know this. I know this so much that my brain immediately opens a whole textbook page. Tadpoles can eat algae, adult frogs eat insects, so it depends on life stage and diet. In an ecosystem model, adult frogs are usually secondary consumers if they eat primary consumers like insects. I can explain this clearly. I can be helpful. “They’re…” I begin. My throat tightens. He is waiting. Not rudely. Just waiting. “They eat bugs,” I say. Incredible. Groundbreaking. Nobel Prize for Bugs. “Right,” he says, nodding. “So secondary?” “Yeah.” I want to crawl into the recycling bin and be processed into loose-leaf paper. Why can’t I say the things I know? Inside my head, I am fluent. Inside my head, I am composed and sometimes even funny. I have paragraphs with transitions. I have evidence. I have tone. But when words have to travel from brain to mouth, they go through some kind of emotional airport security and arrive missing half their luggage. Noah draws a little frog. It is not very good. Its eyes are uneven and one leg is too long. I could say, “Nice frog.” That might be cute. But what if it sounds sarcastic? What if he knows the frog is bad and thinks I’m making fun of him? What if he doesn’t know the frog is bad and then I accidentally encourage false confidence in scientific illustration? He glances at the drawing and laughs quietly. “That frog has seen things.” My chest does something dangerous. Say something back. Say: “It’s a war veteran.” No, too much. Say: “He’s contemplating his place in the food web.” That is actually funny. Maybe. Say it. “He’s…” I start. Noah looks up. “He’s weird,” I say. There is a beat. Then he laughs. Not a huge laugh. Not a fake cafeteria laugh. A small real one. Warmth spreads up my neck and probably into my face, which means I am blushing, which means he can see that I am blushing, which means I need to act normal immediately, which means I have forgotten how arms work. I move my pencil from the right side of my notebook to the left side for no reason. Okay. The laugh happened. That is data. Positive data. But do not overinterpret. One laugh does not equal affection. People laugh at weird frogs. This is not a romantic subplot. This is science. Squeak. Ms. Alvarez flips to a slide about energy pyramids. The projector squeaks again, and I almost thank it for interrupting my spiral. Noah says, “We need limiting factors too. Like pollution?” “Pollution, drought, predators,” I say, and then immediately wonder if “predators” counts as a limiting factor or just part of the ecosystem balance. It can limit population size. Probably fine. Unless Ms. Alvarez expects abiotic factors and I just made us look careless. “Predators definitely,” he says. “Like herons.” Herons. We have returned to herons. Full circle. “We could make a poster,” he says. “Or a shoebox model?” I hate shoebox models because glue gets everywhere and someone always brings cotton balls as clouds even when clouds are not relevant. But if I say that, I might sound negative. If I agree to shoebox model, we might have to meet outside of class. Meeting outside of class with Noah would be both the best and worst possible outcome. Best because more time. Worst because more time. Say what you prefer. People are allowed to have preferences. “Poster,” I say. “Maybe easier to show arrows.” That was a complete sentence. A short one, but complete. Celebrate privately. Do not smile too much. “Good idea,” he says. Good idea. He said good idea. My brain immediately tries to save the audio file. Do not stare at him. Look at the notebook. Write “poster.” Underline it once. Not twice. Twice is aggressive. We divide tasks. He will draw organisms because he says, “Even if they look traumatized, at least they’ll be visible,” and I will label the energy transfers and limiting factors. This is actually a good arrangement because labeling is safe. Words on paper do not require eye contact. But then he asks, “Do you want to present, or should I?” Presenting in front of class makes my stomach cold. Presenting with Noah makes it worse, because if I mess up, he will be standing beside me as a witness to my public unraveling. But if I say “you should,” will I seem lazy? If I say “I can,” will I sound brave and then have to actually be brave, which feels like fraud? “I can do some,” I say. He smiles. “We can split it.” We. I am not going to think about “we.” It is a pronoun used for convenience. It does not mean anything. People say “we” about lab partners and sports teams and accidentally matching socks. My foot starts bouncing under the table. Stop. He might see. I press my heel into the floor. Now my shoulders are tense. Relax shoulders. But consciously relaxing shoulders makes them feel artificial, like I am a puppet being operated by someone who read one article about body language. Hands visible? Hidden hands seem suspicious. Pencil in hand. Pencil gives hands a job. Noah leans over to see my notes. His sleeve brushes the edge of my notebook, not me, but apparently my nervous system cannot tell the difference between fabric near paper and an electrical emergency. “You have really organized notes,” he says. “Oh,” I say. Say thank you. Say something. “I like notes,” I say. I like notes. I like notes? Of all the possible sentences in English, I chose the one that makes me sound like I have a secret shrine to office supplies. But Noah just says, “That’s lucky for me.” Lucky for me. Again, probably nothing. Probably a normal thing. But my heart is building a conspiracy board with red string. The bell rings, sudden and violent. Chairs scrape. People stand. The room fills with backpack zippers and voices. The project is not finished, so we have to work next class. That means this will happen again. I am relieved and terrified, which is apparently my default setting now. Noah tears a corner off his notebook page and writes something. “We should maybe share a doc,” he says. “For the labels and stuff.” A doc. Yes. Practical. Academic. Not a marriage proposal. Calm down. He slides the paper toward me. His email address is on it. I take it carefully, as if it might explode. “Okay,” I say. “I’ll make it.” “Cool. See you tomorrow, Lena.” He says my name like it is normal, because to him it is. To me, it becomes a tiny glowing object I have to carry around for the rest of the day without dropping it. “See you,” I say, and it almost sounds like a person talking. He leaves. I sit there for three extra seconds pretending to put my pencil away, because standing up immediately might look like I am fleeing, even though I am absolutely fleeing internally. The projector squeaks one last time as Ms. Alvarez turns it off. I put Noah’s email in the front pocket of my binder, then move it to the inside pocket, then worry that inside pocket is too hidden, like I am treasuring it, which I am but should not appear to be, so I move it back. My brain starts drafting the email before I even reach the hallway. Hi Noah, here’s the doc. Too plain. Hey Noah! I made the doc for our ecosystem project. Exclamation point: friendly or desperate? No exclamation point: cold or normal? Maybe: Hey, I made the doc. We can add stuff whenever. We. Again. I step into the crowded hallway, holding my binder against my chest like armor, rehearsing every version of a message that is only supposed to contain a link.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional adherence to the prompt's requirements, delivering a compelling first-person narrative from an 8th-grade girl with social anxiety. The author masterfully captures the exhausting mental gymnastics of overthinking every social interaction, creating an authentic voice that feels genuinely adolescent without being condescending. The internal monologue structure works brilliantly, revealing the stark contrast between the protagonist's articulate inner voice and her stilted external communication. Lines like "I like notes" followed by her mortified internal reaction perfectly illustrate this gap. The character's voice remains consistently authentic throughout - intelligent but age-appropriate, anxious but not pathologically so. The prose demonstrates remarkable restraint and precision. The recurring projector squeak serves as both atmospheric detail and nervous system barometer. Small observational details like Noah's uneven frog drawing and the protagonist's analysis of hand positioning feel genuine rather than forced. The writing avoids purple prose while maintaining vivid imagery through the character's hyperaware perspective. The emotional complexity is sophisticated - the protagonist simultaneously hopes for and dreads being paired with Noah, creating genuine tension. Her overthinking feels exhausting in exactly the right way, making readers both empathize with and occasionally feel frustrated by her spiraling thoughts. The author captures how social anxiety transforms simple interactions into elaborate strategic calculations. The pacing builds naturally from setup through the pairing announcement to their actual interaction, maintaining engagement without manufactured drama. The ending feels earned - a small victory (making him laugh) followed by immediate return to overthinking the follow-up email, which perfectly captures the cyclical nature of anxiety. Minor weaknesses include occasional repetitive sentence structures in the internal monologue sections, though this arguably serves the character's obsessive thought patterns. The piece could have pushed slightly deeper into the specific triggers of her anxiety, but the length constraints likely prevented this exploration. The dialogue, while minimal, feels authentic to awkward teenage interaction. The contrast between what she wants to say and what actually emerges creates both humor and pathos without mocking the character's struggles. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18
