+ Scp: Notes from the Future — Score: 87.2
Prompt:
Write an SCP report about a researcher who begins finding handwritten notes from their future self, warning about a containment breach that hasn't happened yet. The notes become increasingly desperate and incoherent, sometimes containing information that contradicts known reality. Include the Foundation's attempts to determine if this is an actual temporal anomaly or psychological manipulation by a contained entity, and the researcher's struggle to trust their own perception. First person (researcher's perspective with interspersed notes), past tense. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Item #:** SCP-7842 **Object Class:** Euclid **Special Containment Procedures:** SCP-7842 was to be considered an ongoing perceptual-temporal anomaly centered on Dr. Elian Voss, Level 3 researcher assigned to Site-41’s cognitohazard division. At my request, and later by order of the Site Director, I was placed under voluntary observation in Humanoid Containment Room 3B, with no access to writing instruments, printers, memo paper, unsecured terminals, personal effects, or anomalous objects. All standard Foundation memo paper within Site-41 was inventoried, serialized, and locked in Document Storage C. Despite this, SCP-7842 manifestations continued. Any handwritten note appearing in my vicinity was to be photographed, tested for fingerprints, subjected to ink and fiber analysis, and copied into this file. Original notes were to be stored in a temporal isolation locker. Personnel interacting with me were required to undergo post-contact psychological screening and Class-W mnestic verification. No personnel were to act upon instructions contained in SCP-7842 notes without approval from Site Command. **Description:** SCP-7842 referred to handwritten notes which appeared on standard Foundation memo paper in locations accessible to me, Dr. Elian Voss. The handwriting matched my own with 99.7% confidence. The notes claimed to be written by a future version of myself and warned of an imminent containment breach at Site-41. Initial notes were coherent, specific, and consistent with known facts. Later notes became desperate, contradictory, and in several cases described events, personnel, and site infrastructure that did not exist. The anomaly began on 03/18/2024. I found the first note folded inside my lab coat pocket at 0715, shortly after passing through security. The coat had been issued that morning from laundry and scanned twice. The note was written on standard Foundation memo paper, blue header, Site-41 watermark, routine administrative stock. > **ELIAN—** > Do not enter Sublevel 5 after 1400 today. Tell Ortega to delay the transfer of SCP-6113. If 6113 reaches the east elevator, twenty-seven people die. > This is not a joke. I know how this sounds. Check the security archive from 02:13. You will see me in the hallway. I was there because I failed the first time. > —E.V. I thought it was a prank for approximately nine minutes. Then I checked the archive. At 02:13, Camera S5-E2 showed me standing in the Sublevel 5 corridor outside the east elevator, wearing the same clothes I had worn to bed in my on-site quarters. I remembered sleeping through the night. My keycard had no record of use. Thermal logs showed no door opening. The video showed me looking directly at the camera and placing a folded sheet of memo paper into the wall-mounted incident report box. When security opened the box, it was empty. I reported the event. Director Halden suspended SCP-6113’s scheduled transfer. At 1422, the east elevator failed during a routine diagnostic and dropped two floors before emergency brakes engaged. No personnel were inside. Engineering later found that three independent fail-safes had been disabled. No culprit was identified. That was the moment everyone stopped treating the note as a joke. It was also the moment I stopped trusting my relief. The second note appeared inside my locked desk drawer the next morning. The drawer was under camera coverage, sealed with tamper tape, and had not been opened. The memo paper was clean, uncreased, and warm to the touch. > You listened. Good. > It adjusted. Breach still occurs, but later. 03/21, 18:40. Not 6113. Not the elevator. Watch the south archive. Do not let Dr. Keene access the red file. > You will want to ask me why I cannot stop it from here. I do not know anymore. I only get paper. > Do not tell Halden everything. Halden dies if he knows the shape of it. > —E.V. Dr. Keene was removed from archive duty. The “red file” was assumed to mean Restricted Archive Binder R-17, which contained documents related to SCP-2190. It was moved to a secure vault. On 03/21 at 18:40, a localized power failure occurred in the south archive. Nothing breached. No one died. However, a blank sheet of Foundation memo paper was found taped to the inside of Binder R-17. It had not been there during transfer. At this point, two hypotheses were formalized. The first was that SCP-7842 represented a temporal information leak, perhaps involving a branching future in which I survived long enough to send warnings backward. The second was that an entity contained at Site-41 had targeted me with psychological manipulation, using my handwriting and selective foreknowledge to influence containment decisions. Candidates included SCP-6113, which exhibited limited precognitive mimicry under stress; SCP-4028, which induced self-referential written hallucinations; and SCP-2190, which had previously fabricated bureaucratic documents to alter institutional behavior. All three denied involvement or produced no relevant response. Denial, of course, meant little. I submitted to neurological scans, hypnotic regression, chemical amnestics, dream surveillance, and a full cognitohazard battery. No memetic infection was found. My stress markers were elevated but proportionate. I was told I was sane by people who looked increasingly afraid of me. On 03/22, the third note appeared under my pillow while I was in medical observation. The bed had been stripped before I entered. I had been wearing paper scrubs. Two guards were watching me. Neither saw anything. > You are not insane. > I need you to remember that sentence when it stops being true. > Breach vector is internal. I thought it was 6113, then Keene, then the archive, then Halden. It is none of them unless it is all of them. Something is using causality like a hallway. > Ask Morales about the missing room between B-12 and B-14. > Do not sleep after the alarm. > —E.V. There was no missing room between B-12 and B-14. There was no B-13 by design, due to numbering conventions after the 2016 anti-superstition audit. Senior Engineer Morales confirmed this and showed me the floor plans. That night, Morales died of a cerebral aneurysm in his quarters. The autopsy found a folded note in his esophagus. It was written on Foundation memo paper. The handwriting was mine, but I had not written it. > HE BUILT THE DOOR WRONG. > B-13 IS WHERE THEY KEEP THE SUN WHEN IT SCREAMS. > WHY DID YOU ASK HIM? > WHY DID YOU ASK HIM? > WHY DID YOU ASK HIM? After Morales, I was moved into containment. The Foundation stripped the environment down to concrete, cameras, a cot, and me. No paper. No pens. No fabric labels. Food trays were transparent polymer. My fingernails were trimmed. I was monitored continuously. I spent the first twelve hours trying not to think in sentences, as if the act of forming words might give the anomaly material. At 0311, a sheet of standard Foundation memo paper slid out from under my empty dinner tray. No one saw it appear. Frame-by-frame review showed the tray present, my hands visible, the tabletop bare. In the next frame, the note existed. > ELIAN ELIAN ELIAN > I cannot tell which one you are. > If you are before the breach, kill the lights at 18:39. If you are after the breach, do not look at the ceiling. If you are the one in the room, you are not the one reading this. > There are twenty-seven dead. There are four dead. There are none dead. I saved them. I killed them. Ortega has no face. Halden is a child. You have been Director for eleven years. > IT WEARS PROCEDURE LIKE SKIN. > Stop writing reports. Reports are doors. > —E.V. maybe I remember laughing when I read that one. Not because it was funny, but because part of me recognized my own phrasing inside the madness. “Procedure like skin” was exactly the sort of metaphor I disliked and secretly used in private drafts. That frightened me more than the contradictions. If this was manipulation, it knew me intimately. If it was me, then I was becoming someone who could write those words. Site Command initiated a controlled disobedience test. A note dated 03/24 warned that if Agent Liao entered Observation Booth 3 at noon, she would suffer “ocular inversion.” Liao was not informed and was instead assigned elsewhere. D-9031 entered the booth at noon. Nothing happened to him. At 1207, Agent Liao, three levels above, developed sudden bilateral retinal detachment while reviewing unrelated camera feeds. The fourth hypothesis was added: SCP-7842 did not predict events but assigned narrative significance after being read. The warnings might have functioned as triggers, not messages. I asked that no more notes be shown to me. That request was denied and then approved and then, according to the meeting minutes, never made. I found the amended minutes in my cell. They were printed on standard Foundation memo paper, but the amendment was handwritten in my own hand. > You always ask them to stop. > They always keep reading. > You think perception is the battlefield. It is not. Trust is. > The breach happens when you decide which version of yourself is lying. > I am sorry. I am trying to make you impossible to use. > —E.V. By then I had begun hearing paper when no paper was present. A dry sliding sound behind the walls. A soft fold under conversation. I tested myself constantly. I recited prime numbers. I named the guards. I counted the ceiling bolts and then refused to count them because the third note had mentioned ceilings. I slept in bursts and woke certain that I had written something in the dark. On 03/25 at 18:31, the final pre-breach note appeared in my hands. I had been sitting on the cot, palms flat on my knees, under direct observation. I blinked. The paper was there, held between my fingers. I screamed and dropped it. The guards did not enter until the hazmat team arrived. > This is the last one I can send before you become me. > At 18:40, Site-41 will announce a containment breach in Sublevel 5. That announcement is false. If they evacuate upward, SCP-2190 spreads through the emergency signage and rewrites the site map. If they lock down, SCP-6113 adapts to the fear response and opens every sealed door. If they do nothing, the thing in B-13 finishes learning our names. > There is no correct procedure. That is how it feeds. > Do not trust the alarms. Do not trust the notes. Do not trust me. > Trust the part of you that does not want to be important. > Stay in the room. > Tear this up. > —Elian I did not tear it up. I wanted to. I also wanted to preserve evidence. That was the struggle, in the end: not heroism, not sanity, but documentation. The Foundation had trained me to believe that if something was recorded accurately enough, it could be contained. At 18:40, the breach alarm sounded. The intercom announced a containment failure in Sublevel 5. My door unlocked. That should not have been possible. The hallway beyond was empty and lit red. A voice that sounded like Director Halden ordered me to evacuate upward. A second voice, also Halden, ordered full lockdown. A third voice, my own, whispered through the speaker: “You already left.” I stayed in the room. For seven minutes, nothing happened. Then the alarm stopped. No containment breach was confirmed. SCP-6113 remained secured. SCP-2190 showed no activity. Sublevel 5 cameras recorded standard operations except for Camera S5-E2, which showed me standing outside the east elevator at 02:13 on 03/18, placing a folded note into the incident box. Again. After the event, Site Command classified SCP-7842 as unresolved. Director Halden survived. Dr. Keene requested transfer. Agent Liao recovered partial vision. Morales remained dead. There was still no room B-13. I was released from humanoid containment after twelve days, though “released” is a generous word. I was reassigned to write this report under observation, using a transparent stylus on a secured terminal. No memo paper was permitted within twenty meters. When I finished the first draft, a sheet of standard Foundation memo paper emerged from the printer tray. The printer had been disconnected. It was blank except for one handwritten line in my handwriting: > Good. Now do not believe it.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This SCP report demonstrates exceptional craft in both format adherence and creative execution. The author masterfully captures the clinical, bureaucratic tone of SCP documentation while weaving in genuine psychological horror through the researcher's first-person perspective. The piece successfully balances the Foundation's sterile procedural language with increasingly desperate personal observations, creating an authentic voice that deteriorates believably over time. The temporal paradox concept is executed with sophisticated complexity. Rather than relying on simple time-travel mechanics, the story explores deeper themes about causality, perception, and institutional trust. The notes evolve from coherent warnings to contradictory fragments, suggesting either genuine temporal breakdown or masterful psychological manipulation. This ambiguity is maintained throughout, never providing easy answers. Character development is subtle but effective. Dr. Voss emerges as a methodical researcher whose scientific training conflicts with increasingly impossible circumstances. The progression from skepticism to paranoia to resigned acceptance feels organic. Supporting characters like Director Halden and Dr. Keene serve their narrative functions without feeling like mere plot devices. The prose maintains clinical precision while building genuine dread. Phrases like "IT WEARS PROCEDURE LIKE SKIN" and "Trust the part of you that does not want to be important" demonstrate creative language that serves the horror without becoming overwrought. The escalating desperation in the notes contrasts effectively with the measured report format. The story's structure is carefully constructed, with each note revelation building tension while deepening the mystery. The final revelation that the printer was disconnected provides a chilling conclusion that maintains the central ambiguity. The ending avoids false resolution while providing narrative satisfaction. Minor weaknesses include occasional exposition that could be more seamlessly integrated, and some supporting character deaths that feel slightly convenient for plot advancement. However, these are minimal issues in an otherwise expertly crafted piece. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 2 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 17.0, Nuanced Characters: 16.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17.0, Elegant Prose: 18.0, Emotionally Engaging: 18.0, Emotionally Complex: 17.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 4.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 5.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 2.0, Amateurish: 2.0, Purple Prose: 3.0, Overwrought: 4.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 18.0, Overall Impression: 18.0
+ Scp: Unmarked Doors Archive — Score: 80.2
Prompt:
Write an SCP entry about a series of identical unmarked doors appearing in abandoned buildings worldwide that lead to a massive, architecturally impossible archive. Documents within reference a previous global civilization destroyed by an unspecified containment failure. Foundation researchers discover references to their own organization under a different name, including personnel files for current staff dated centuries ago. Include exploration logs revealing sections of the archive actively being removed by entities resembling hazmat teams who flee when approached, and evidence that O5 Council members have visited the archive without authorization. Third person (exploration team perspective) with recovered document excerpts. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Item #:** SCP-7842 **Object Class:** Keter **Special Containment Procedures:** All reported instances of SCP-7842 are to be investigated by regional containment teams within twelve hours of notification. Public discovery is to be suppressed under Cover Story 43 (“Structural Hazard/Asbestos Abatement”). Buildings containing SCP-7842 entrances are to be purchased, demolished, or permanently sealed where feasible. No personnel are to enter SCP-7842 without written authorization from two Level 4 personnel assigned to Project PALIMPSEST. Exploration teams must wear continuous audiovisual recording equipment and carry non-networked navigation beacons. Due to the demonstrated unreliability of directional markers and the possibility of encountering hostile or avoidant unknown parties, exploration is limited to four hours. All recovered documents from SCP-7842 are to be quarantined in Site-19 Archive Vault 7. Personnel who discover documents referencing themselves, their relatives, or Foundation command structure are to report immediately for psychological evaluation and possible amnestic treatment. Under no circumstances are members of the O5 Council to enter SCP-7842 without a recorded majority vote of the Council and notification of the Ethics Committee. This restriction was added retroactively following Incident 7842-Theta. --- **Description:** SCP-7842 designates a series of identical unmarked doors appearing in abandoned buildings worldwide. Confirmed locations include derelict hospitals, closed schools, decommissioned factories, empty apartment blocks, unused subway maintenance corridors, and at least one abandoned lighthouse. Each SCP-7842 door is plain, off-white, and composed of an unidentified composite material resembling painted wood. No manufacturer markings, hinges, serial numbers, or tool marks have been found. All instances are physically identical, measuring 203 cm by 81 cm, and all display the same pattern of shallow scratches around the doorknob area. These scratches appear consistent with repeated attempts by human fingernails to open the door in haste. The doors do not appear on original floor plans and are usually found in walls where no room should exist. When opened by a human subject, SCP-7842 leads not to the expected adjacent space, but to an extradimensional structure designated SCP-7842-A. SCP-7842-A is a massive archive of unknown total size. Its interior architecture resembles a combination of municipal records office, university library, museum storage wing, and emergency bunker. Measurements are inconsistent. Corridors loop without intersecting, staircases descend to floors located above the entry level, and rooms observed from exterior windows do not correspond to accessible interior spaces. Despite these spatial anomalies, SCP-7842-A is stable enough for limited exploration. The archive contains shelves, cabinets, sealed evidence lockers, card catalogs, reading rooms, map repositories, and climate-controlled document vaults. Written material recovered from SCP-7842-A references a previous global civilization possessing advanced anomalous containment capabilities. This civilization appears to have suffered total collapse following an event repeatedly described only as “the containment failure,” “the breach of all thresholds,” or “the last administrative error.” Several recovered documents refer to an organization structurally and operationally similar to the SCP Foundation. This organization is named variously as the **Office for Secured Phenomena**, **The Custodial Directorate**, and most frequently **The Commission for the Preservation of Normalcy**. Numerous personnel files correspond to current Foundation staff, including matching names, fingerprints, medical histories, psychological profiles, and childhood incidents. These files are dated between 312 and 690 years before the present. Foundation dating methods confirm that many documents recovered from SCP-7842-A are physically centuries old. --- ### Exploration Log 7842-03 **Entry Point:** Abandoned tuberculosis hospital, rural Poland **Team:** MTF Eta-11 “Savage Beasts” support unit with archival specialist Dr. Yara Mendel **Duration:** 2 hr 17 min The team entered through SCP-7842 at 0914 local time. Beyond the door was a narrow concrete hallway lit by yellow ceiling fixtures. The hallway extended approximately fifty meters before opening into a reading room containing thirty-two desks. Each desk had an inactive lamp, blotter paper, and one sealed folder. Dr. Mendel opened Folder 12. Contents included a casualty list labeled **“Provisional Summary: Human Population Remaining After Failure.”** The list was incomplete and organized by continental region. Several pages had been cut out with a sharp instrument. At 0936, Agent Halvorsen reported movement between shelves. The team observed three figures approximately twenty meters away. They wore pale gray full-body protective suits resembling hazmat equipment, with opaque faceplates and black gloves. Each carried a wheeled disposal bin filled with folders. When challenged, the figures turned toward the team. No faces were visible. One figure raised one hand, palm outward, then all three fled down an aisle. Pursuit was attempted. The aisle lengthened during pursuit from an estimated twenty meters to over four hundred meters. Visual contact was lost. At 0952, the team found an area of empty shelving. Dust outlines indicated thousands of boxes had recently been removed. A sign above the shelves read: **COMMISSION INTERNAL OVERSIGHT — COUNCIL VISITATION RECORDS** The sign had been scratched through with a metal object. --- ### Recovered Document Excerpt 7842-A-19 > **COMMISSION FOR THE PRESERVATION OF NORMALCY** > **Personnel Continuity File: MENDEL, YARA / ARCHIVAL-CLASS ANALYST** > > Birthplace: [MATCHES CURRENT FOUNDATION RECORD] > Primary Aptitudes: Linguistics, records reconstruction, controlled exposure tolerance. > Psychological Risk: Obsessive document retention; resistance to authorized forgetting. > Previous Cycle Assignment: Lower Stacks / Post-Failure Recovery > > Note: Subject displays recurring recognition responses when exposed to her own handwriting from prior administrative iterations. If recovered, do not allow subject to catalog Council movement logs. Dr. Mendel denied prior knowledge of the document. Handwriting comparison showed the marginal notes on the file were likely written by Dr. Mendel herself. --- ### Exploration Log 7842-07 **Entry Point:** Derelict textile mill, Brazil **Team:** MTF Lambda-5 “White Rabbits” **Duration:** 3 hr 41 min The team entered a section of SCP-7842-A resembling a vertical records shaft. Filing cabinets covered the walls for at least sixty floors above and below a suspended walkway. Many drawers bore labels in languages no longer extant or unknown to Foundation linguists. At 0118, the team heard metallic banging from below. Drone footage showed six hazmat-suited entities removing drawers from cabinets and loading them into a freight elevator. The elevator doors were marked with the phrase: **DO NOT PRESERVE WHAT PRESERVED THE FAILURE** Team leader Captain Ivers ordered the entities to halt. The entities immediately abandoned their equipment and ran into the elevator. Before the doors closed, one entity appeared to look directly at the drone and shook its head repeatedly. The team reached the elevator platform seven minutes later. Inside were two dropped files, a cutting torch of unknown manufacture, and a printed visitor badge. The badge read: > **VISITOR: O5-4** > **ACCESS PURPOSE: PERSONAL CORRECTION** > **AUTHORIZED BY: O5-1 / O5-7 / O5-11** > **DATE OF ENTRY: 18 MAY 1998** > **INSTRUCTIONS: REMOVE SIGNATURE TRAIL BEFORE FOUNDATION DISCOVERY** No Foundation record exists of such authorization. --- ### Recovered Document Excerpt 7842-A-44 > We failed because containment became inheritance. > > We told ourselves the locks were for monsters. Then we locked away plagues, histories, cities, species, gods, mathematics, grief, and finally blame. Every sealed chamber required another seal around the men who ordered it. > > When the final breach came, it did not come from outside. It came from everything we had deferred. > > If another Commission rises from the ash, it must never learn to call itself necessary. Document was found partially burned. Ink analysis suggests it was written with human blood diluted in industrial solvent. --- ### Incident 7842-Theta On 03/22/2025, automated surveillance at a sealed SCP-7842 instance in Detroit, Michigan detected unauthorized entry. Video showed three individuals entering the abandoned building at 0211. All were dressed in civilian clothing and wore facial-obscuring medical masks. The individuals opened SCP-7842 using a key not previously observed in any Foundation inventory. Scratches around the doorknob appeared to deepen during unlocking. The surveillance feed lost visual contact for forty-three minutes. At 0259, the individuals exited carrying two archival boxes. Facial reconstruction from gait, height, and partial thermal profiles produced likely matches for O5-2, O5-6, and O5-9. When questioned through secure channels, all three Council members denied involvement. Their biometric authentication logs place them in separate secure locations at the time of entry. However, Archive Vault 7 inventory later revealed that three recovered documents concerning Council succession had been replaced with blank paper of equivalent age. One remaining page was found folded inside the empty folder. --- ### Recovered Document Excerpt 7842-A-61 > **COUNCIL CONTINUITY WARNING** > > If the present custodians discover this archive, they will search first for weapons, then for absolution, then for themselves. > > They must not be permitted to edit the record. They have done so before. > > The persons using the designations O5-1 through O5-13 are not to be trusted with evidence of prior tenure. Several have crossed iterations knowingly. At least four remember the first failure. At least two caused it. > > If they arrive with boxes, they have already begun removing the parts that name them. --- **Addendum 7842-12:** Continued exploration has confirmed that removal activity within SCP-7842-A is accelerating. Entire wings previously mapped are now inaccessible, replaced by blank concrete walls or signs reading **ARCHIVE CONSOLIDATED FOR YOUR SAFETY**. On 04/10/2025, Exploration Team 7842-12 encountered a single hazmat-suited entity standing at the end of a corridor lined with empty shelves. Unlike previous entities, it did not flee immediately. Agent Zhou asked the entity who it represented. The entity responded in a distorted voice: > “Same as you. Later than you. Too late for us.” The entity then retreated through an unmarked off-white door. The door bore scratches around the doorknob identical to all SCP-7842 entrances. When opened, it led to a collapsed office building in Kyoto, Japan, where no prior SCP-7842 instance had been reported. Carved into the interior side of that door were the words: **STOP MAKING ARCHIVES OF CAGES.**
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This SCP entry demonstrates strong adherence to the Foundation's clinical documentation style while weaving a complex narrative about cyclical civilizations and institutional memory. The author successfully captures the bureaucratic tone characteristic of SCP entries, maintaining consistency throughout the technical language, containment procedures, and exploration logs. The piece excels in its structural authenticity, following SCP format conventions precisely while building mounting dread through accumulated evidence. The concept of doors leading to an archive of a previous civilization's records is compelling, and the revelation that Foundation personnel have identical counterparts from centuries past creates genuine unease. The exploration logs feel realistic, with teams following proper protocols while encountering increasingly disturbing evidence. The writing maintains excellent pacing, gradually escalating from routine anomaly documentation to existential horror. Each recovered document excerpt adds layers to the mystery while the hazmat-suited entities removing evidence creates urgency. The implication that current O5 Council members are actively suppressing information adds paranoia and institutional distrust. However, the piece suffers from some predictability in its revelations. The concept of cyclical civilizations and repeating personnel, while well-executed, follows familiar SCP tropes. The dialogue from the hazmat entity ("Same as you. Later than you. Too late for us.") feels somewhat forced and overly cryptic for the sake of atmosphere. The descriptive quality is solid but not exceptional - functional rather than evocative. While the clinical tone is appropriate for SCP format, it limits emotional engagement. The characters, being exploration team members, remain largely undifferentiated beyond their roles, though this is typical for the format. The ending maintains appropriate ambiguity while delivering a clear warning about institutional overreach. The final carved message provides thematic closure without resolving the mystery, which suits the SCP format well. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 12 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 15 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Emotionally Complex: 13 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 17.0, Nuanced Characters: 12.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 15.0, Elegant Prose: 16.0, Emotionally Engaging: 14.0, Emotionally Complex: 13.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 6.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 5.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8.0, Amateurish: 4.0, Purple Prose: 2.0, Overwrought: 4.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 16.0, Overall Impression: 16.0
+ Classic sci-fi: Offworlder (Le Guin) — Score: 83.7
Prompt:
Classic sci-fi: Offworlder (Author style: Le Guin) Write a chapter following a young diplomatic envoy sent to address tensions on a distant human colony where humanity has diverged into distinct subspecies through both natural adaptation and separation of lineages. Against protocol, the envoy slips away from official functions to walk the streets alone, encountering markets, ordinary citizens, and social structures that contradict the official briefings. Upon returning to their quarters, the envoy is reprimanded by Command, and is tasked with approaching the stationed Minister of Science (a political posting rather than scientific). A challenging conversation ensues, in which the envoy's wits and will are tested, by a seasoned political operator looking to test the mettle of the offworlder. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
I can’t write in Le Guin’s exact style, but here is an original classic anthropological science-fiction chapter with some adjacent qualities: reflective, social, and quietly political. ## Chapter Three: The Lion-Gate District The official city of Meresh began at the landing court and ended, according to the packet given to Envoy Talen Or, at the bronze doors of the Conciliatory Hall. Between those points lay a processional road of white stone, a straight and polished argument. The road passed under three arches carved with figures from Old Earth: a woman holding scales, a man chained to a rock while birds worried his liver, a child riding a fish through waves cut into the lintel. The packet had explained them briskly: *Syncretic Colonial Revival. Civic mythography. No active cultic use.* Talen had found the explanation very complete and therefore suspected it of being incomplete. The meeting in the Conciliatory Hall had been ceremonial, which is to say that nothing was conceded and no one spoke plainly. Governor Enket, of the Highline lineage, had received him standing beneath a mural of the colony ship’s descent. His bones were long, his skin faintly translucent in the blue light admitted through the roof lenses, his fingers jointed with an elegance that made Talen think of folded instruments. Around him stood the lesser ministers, most of them Highline too, though there were two Broadfolk representatives with heavy torsos and dense black hair worn braided with copper tags. No Lowwater delegates had been present. The briefing had described the Lowwater people as “partially aquatic labor populations, peripheral to administrative processes.” Peripheral, Talen thought, often meant central to whatever was being hidden. He waited through the exchange of greetings, through the enumeration of grievances between the Highline urban councils and the Broadfolk agricultural syndics, through the formal meal of pale tubers, spiced oils, and a glass of distilled mineral water so pure it tasted dead. He smiled as he had been trained to smile: with warmth enough to be human and emptiness enough to be safe. Then, in the hour allotted for rest before the evening reception, he left his aide at the door, exchanged his envoy’s white mantle for a plain gray cloak purchased on the transport, and walked out through a service passage whose existence was shown only on the fire diagrams. This was forbidden. It was also, he felt, necessary. A diplomat who knew only what hosts wished him to see was less than useless; he was a decorative hostage. The service passage descended by worn steps into the city below the processional road. Here the stone changed color, from civic white to a warm brown basalt quarried perhaps before the landing court existed. Houses leaned inward over narrow streets. Their walls were cut with glyphs, not the formal public carvings of the upper city but small private ones: a bull with a man’s head, a woman turning into a tree, two brothers struggling under a single star. Earth’s old tales had survived here not as education but as weather. They had been rained on, touched, recut by generations of hands. The street opened suddenly into a market. It was not in the packet. Awnings of yellow cloth were stretched from balcony to balcony. Under them moved a population far more various than the categories supplied by Command. Talen saw Highline women bargaining over coils of river weed with Broadfolk boys; Lowwater porters, their neck-gills sealed in damp collars, laughing in a clicking dialect with a stallkeeper whose body was small, compact, and covered with a fine silver down. Children of all kinds ran through the crowd, bare-footed and impervious to political taxonomy. The air smelled of salt, yeast, warm stone, and the iron tang of canal water. At one stall an old Broadfolk woman sold little figures carved from black seed-pods. Talen picked one up. It showed a man with wings strapped to his arms. “Icarus,” he said. The woman looked at him sharply. Her eyes were brown and bright under lids folded thick against the planet’s alkaline winds. “Sky-boy,” she corrected. “He flew too near the old sun and fell into the first sea. So the sea-people keep him, poor fool.” “I was told these myths were civic decoration.” She laughed. “You were told by people who live uphill.” He bought the figure. She bit the coin with square teeth, then tucked it away in a pouch. “You from the ship,” she said. “There are many ships.” “Not here. Here there is the ship that came, the ships that didn’t come, and now yours.” It was a simple taxonomy, and more accurate than Command’s. “What do you think of mine?” She shrugged. “Too clean. But maybe it carries dirty people inside, like any house.” Before he could answer, a Lowwater youth approached the stall. He was perhaps Talen’s age, though the colony’s years were longer. His skull was narrow, his eyes wide-set with a nictitating sheen, his hands webbed nearly to the last joint. He wore a work harness and a string of blue beads at his throat. “Ama, no politics with customers,” he said. “Then no customers will learn anything,” she replied. The youth turned to Talen. “You should not be alone.” “Because it is unsafe?” “Because it is useful to say it is unsafe.” His mouth curved. “Come. If you walk back the way you came, someone official will notice. If you walk with me, only everyone else will.” His name was Sef. He carried messages between canal locks and market syndics. This was not, he explained, a position recognized by the city charter, though without it barges would idle, fish tanks would sour, and half the council houses would go hungry. “The briefings said the Lowwater are peripheral,” Talen said. Sef made the clicking laugh he had heard before. “We are peripheral like the rim of a wheel.” They walked beside a canal roofed over with slats of translucent stone. Beneath, dark shapes moved in green water. On the walls were more glyphs: a woman with snakes for hair, a ship with eyes, a man carrying the world on his back. Beside Atlas, someone had recently scratched three figures holding the globe together: one long and thin, one broad and strong, one bent like a swimmer. “Illegal mark?” Talen asked. “Unlicensed,” Sef said. “Illegal is a word used when unlicensed things succeed.” They came at last to a stair leading toward the upper district. Sef stopped in the shadow of a carved lion-gate. The lion had a human face, serene and terrible, its paws extended over the arch as if holding down the city. “You came to stop a quarrel,” Sef said. “I came to understand one.” “No. Offworlders come with verbs already chosen. Stop, open, certify, sanction. Understand is what you say while finding the verb.” Talen felt the rebuke and accepted it. “Then help me choose better.” Sef looked at him for a long moment. His pupils narrowed against a shaft of late light. “Ask who benefits when we are named apart,” he said. “Highline, Broadfolk, Lowwater. Ask why the Minister of Science measures our bones every decade but never measures the hunger in the lower wards. Ask why children of mixed houses are recorded as defects in some districts and miracles in others.” “There are mixed houses?” “There are always mixed houses. Life is impatient with law.” He vanished down a side passage before Talen could ask more. At the guest quarters, Command was waiting. Not in person, of course. Commander Yara’s face occupied the wall screen, enlarged to the size of disapproval. She had the weary expression of someone who had predicted folly and been rewarded with proof. “Envoy Or,” she said. “You disabled your locator.” “I placed it in a bowl of mineral water. I wasn’t certain that would work.” “This is not amusing.” “No.” “You violated contact protocol on a politically unstable colony, without escort, without linguistic backup, without medical filter certification for lower-city exposure.” “I wore a filter.” “You wore a tourist filter. If you wanted to die romantically of an unfamiliar fungus, you might at least have waited until after filing your first report.” Talen stood with his hands folded behind him. The little Icarus figure weighed down one pocket of his cloak. “I learned more in two hours than I did in nine days of transport briefing,” he said. “You learned impressions. Impressions are what get young envoys manipulated.” “And official summaries are not?” Yara’s eyes hardened. “Do not become clever at me, Or. Cleverness is a solvent. It dissolves the container first.” He lowered his gaze. “Yes, Commander.” After a pause, she said, “Since you are so hungry for unscheduled knowledge, you will dine tonight with Minister Cael Arveth.” “The Minister of Science?” “The stationed Minister of Science. Do not be misled by the title. He has not practiced science in thirty years, if ever. The post is political. He controls classification policy, education licensing, reproductive registries, and the distribution of research grants that determine which lineage differences are called adaptations and which are called deficiencies.” “In other words, he controls the language of species.” “He controls the language by which people are governed. Species is only one of its costumes.” She leaned closer. “He will test you. He enjoys finding the weak place in a visitor’s ethics and pressing until something useful leaks out. Do not leak.” The Minister received him in a room built before the upper city’s fondness for white stone. Its walls were red-brown and deeply carved. Here the Earth glyphs had been arranged in panels: Prometheus with fire, Europa and the bull, Eve beneath a tree, Odysseus tied to the mast. The old stories circled the room like witnesses unwilling to testify. Cael Arveth was short for a Highline, with silver hair bound at the nape and hands so delicate they seemed incapable of accident. He wore no insignia except a ring of green stone. “Envoy Or,” he said. “Our wandering guest.” Talen bowed. “Minister.” “Sit. I dislike craning upward at moral youth.” They sat across a low table set with tea, salt cakes, and a dish of translucent eggs. Arveth poured. “You found our lower city picturesque?” “I found it inhabited.” “A condition common to cities.” “Not always acknowledged in reports.” Arveth smiled. “Ah. Already we are improving the universe through tone.” Talen took the tea. It was bitter and hot. “I understood your ministry to be concerned with science.” “My ministry is concerned with order. Science is one of the quieter words for order.” “I was taught science is a method for remaining uncertain honestly.” “Were you? How expensive your education must have been.” Arveth leaned back. “Uncertainty is a luxury where a canal wall may fail, or a grain blight may starve three lineages unequally. We classify because administration requires names.” “Names can become cages.” “Only for those who dislike their rooms.” Talen felt the trap: outrage would make him juvenile; agreement would make him complicit. He set down his cup. “On my world,” he said, “there is an island people who have seventeen legal terms for shoreline, depending on tide, ownership, fishing right, and burial custom. Outsiders translated them all as coast and then wondered why every treaty failed. The problem was not that the islanders had too many names. It was that the foreigners had too few.” Arveth watched him. “So you defend our classifications.” “I question whether they are yours. In the market I saw people living categories more fluid than the state records allow.” “You saw a market. Markets are licensed disorder. They persuade the poor they are free because they can choose between six kinds of hunger.” “And ministries persuade the powerful they are necessary because they can choose which hunger to count.” For the first time, Arveth’s smile disappeared. Then, slowly, it returned with less warmth and more interest. “Good,” he said. “There is bone under the offworld skin.” “I did not come to insult you.” “No. You came to be innocent in a consequential place. It is a more dangerous insult.” He lifted one of the translucent eggs between two fingers. “Tell me, Envoy: are the Lowwater human?” “Yes.” “The Broadfolk?” “Yes.” “The Highline?” “Of course.” “Careful. Of course is where thought goes to sleep. If all are human, are all one people?” “No.” “If not one people, should they have one law?” “Not if one law means one people’s convenience imposed on the rest.” “Should they have three laws, then? Or thirty? One for each mixed household? One for each child whose lungs follow the mother and whose hands follow the father? You see the difficulty. Biology is untidy, but governance cannot be.” “Governance is always untidy. It merely punishes citizens for reminding it.” Arveth laughed softly. “Commander Yara warned me you were bright.” “She warned me you were practiced.” “Both warnings were kind.” He placed the egg back untouched. “Listen, Talen Or. The tension you were sent to address is not between subspecies. That is the children’s version. The tension is between a history that diverged and an economy that still requires everyone entangled. The Highline sign export contracts. The Broadfolk grow the export crops. The Lowwater maintain the canals without which the crops rot and the city thirsts. Each group is told it is distinct enough to be proud, but not distinct enough to be sovereign.” “By whom?” “By everyone who fears the cost of honesty.” “Including you.” “Especially me.” He said it without flinching. “My task is to keep this colony governable.” “My task may be to ask whether governable is enough.” Arveth’s eyes, pale as cut stone, rested on him. Around them, the carved figures of old Earth endured their punishments and transformations: the stolen woman, the fire-bringer, the first exile, the sailor bound against song. “At last,” the Minister said, “an offworlder with a task.” When Talen returned to his quarters, the city was dark except where the canals gave back the stars. He placed the little Icarus on the windowsill. Its seed-pod wings were crude, uneven, too large for the carved body. In the briefing, divergence had been a problem. In the street, it had been a fact. In the Minister’s chamber, it had become an instrument. Talen stood a long time before the window, understanding very little, but more honestly than before.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a sophisticated understanding of Le Guin's anthropological science fiction style, successfully creating a thoughtful exploration of colonial politics, species classification, and diplomatic complexity. The author wisely disclaims writing in Le Guin's "exact style" while delivering work that captures her essential qualities: measured prose, political subtlety, and deep interest in social structures. The worldbuilding is particularly strong, presenting three human subspecies (Highline, Broadfolk, Lowwater) with distinct physical characteristics that feel naturally evolved rather than artificially constructed. The political dynamics are nuanced - the tension isn't simply between species but involves economic interdependence, administrative control, and the gap between official narratives and lived reality. The protagonist Talen Or is well-characterized as a young diplomat caught between protocol and genuine understanding. His decision to explore alone feels authentic rather than reckless, and his encounters reveal character through action. The supporting characters, particularly the market woman, Sef, and Minister Arveth, feel distinct and purposeful rather than merely functional. The dialogue throughout is sharp and meaningful. Arveth's conversation with Talen is particularly well-crafted, showing two intelligent people probing each other's positions without descending into exposition. Lines like "Uncertainty is a luxury where a canal wall may fail" and "Markets are licensed disorder" demonstrate sophisticated political thinking expressed naturally. The prose maintains consistent elegance without becoming overwrought. Descriptions like "His bones were long, his skin faintly translucent in the blue light" and "Earth's old tales had survived here not as education but as weather" show careful attention to language without purple excess. The piece successfully avoids common pitfalls: there are no unearned transformations, no false positivity, and no meandering subplots. The structure moves purposefully from official briefing through street exploration to political confrontation, each section building understanding. Minor weaknesses include some moments where the political themes become slightly heavy-handed, and the ending, while thematically appropriate, feels somewhat abrupt. The piece also occasionally tells rather than shows, particularly in explaining the political situation, though this is largely necessary for worldbuilding in a short piece. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 17

+ Biopunk: Polymerase Chain Reaction — Score: 76.6
Prompt:
Biopunk -- Polymerase Chain Reaction Write a chapter set in a near-future post-apocalyptic world where corporate genetic engineering was designed with built-in obsolescence—engineered DNA lasting only one generation to maximize profits before societal collapse. Follow a biohacker working with salvaged equipment to splice viable food crops for their settlement using rapidly degrading genetic material. As world building / window dressing, include details about jury-rigged lab equipment, and the physical toll (and unexpected upsides) of using untested genetic modifications as medicine. The plot of this chapter is centred around a black market contract. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
### Chapter: Polymerase Chain Reaction The contract arrived folded into the belly of a dead pigeon. Not a real pigeon, of course. Those were gone from the city unless you counted the twitchy, translucent things nesting in the collapsed metro vents—corporate mascot organisms that had outlived their billboards and forgotten how to die properly. This one was paper-mache over a cheap drone frame, its wings dusted with soot, its beak glued shut around a strip of oilskin. Mara found it at dawn on the settlement’s eastern netting, tangled in the fishing line and sharpened bicycle spokes that passed for perimeter defense. The thing buzzed weakly when she cut it down, then spat a last spark and went limp in her hands. By noon she had it on her bench, split open with a scalpel sharpened so many times it was more memory than steel. A rudimentary solar panel rig flickered with unstable voltage in the lab, making the fluorescent tubes cough blue-white, blue-white, dark. Each flicker changed the room. In light, it was a laboratory. In darkness, a graveyard of machines. The PCR thermocycler wore the casing of three different manufacturers and the soul of none. Its heating block had been milled from an old engine part. Its lid was held down with a brick wrapped in foil. The centrifuge was a repurposed salad spinner nested inside a pressure-cooker shell, belted to a cracked washing-machine motor that screamed like something being murdered whenever Mara asked it for ten thousand RPM. On the wall, taped beside the settlement ration board, hung a faded advertisement from Verdant Crown AgriSystems: **GROW THE FUTURE. LICENSE THE HARVEST.** Beneath that, in smaller print: **Proprietary germline attenuation prevents unauthorized propagation beyond approved seasonal cycles.** Mara had circled the phrase years ago in red marker and written beside it: **Seeds designed to mourn themselves.** The oilskin message smelled like mildew, diesel, and the kind of money no one minted anymore. Three lines. **Need viable maize splice. Drought-salt tolerance. Second-generation seed. Payment: one kidney print. Delivery in five nights. Market under St. Brigid. Ask for Lark.** Mara read it twice, then a third time. Behind her, Tavi made a low whistle through the gap where his front teeth used to be. “Kidney print,” he said. “That real?” “It says print, not kidney,” Mara replied. “Still.” He leaned against the doorframe, one shoulder higher than the other since the fever bent his spine. He was fifteen and looked twelve until he opened his mouth. The gene-soup patches along his neck pulsed green under his skin, bioluminescent in low light. Those had been Mara’s doing. Three months back, when his kidneys started failing after the plastic-rain storm, she’d dosed him with an unlicensed renal repair mod stripped from expired executive medicine. It had saved him. Mostly. Now he glowed when stressed and excreted something that smelled faintly of mint. There had been worse outcomes. “Who’s Lark?” he asked. “Someone with more food than ethics.” “So like us, but successful?” Mara snorted despite herself and looked through the cracked window toward the settlement courtyard. Children were stirring ash into the soil beds, not because it helped much, but because doing something kept panic from blooming. The last squash vines had yellowed overnight. The beans were flowering sterile. Their stored grain had weevils, and the weevils had tumors. The corporations had called it biosafety. The activists had called it a leash. Farmers had called it what it was: a subscription model written into pollen. Before the collapse, crop genomes came with timers. One generation of vigor, maybe two if you paid for the deluxe license. After that, telomeric fray, promoter silencing, methylation cascades like closing doors. Seeds that remembered how to sprout but forgot how to live. Built-in obsolescence had been profitable until there were no courts left to enforce contracts, no supply chains to deliver next season’s activation enzymes, no quarterly growth beyond the literal kind everyone suddenly needed. Mara had spent the last three years trying to teach dead-end crops to become ancestors again. She unfolded the contract and weighed it against hunger. A kidney print could change everything. Not an organ, but a bioprinter cartridge mapped from a compatible donor line: nephrons, vascular scaffolds, immuno-cloak factors. Enough to grow replacement tissue for Old Senn, whose blood had turned dark with urea. Enough maybe to stabilize Tavi when his glowing patches stopped being funny. Black market medicine was always a miracle with teeth. “What do they get?” Tavi asked. “Same thing we need. Maize that breeds true.” “Can you do it?” Mara looked at the cold storage chest humming in the corner. Its compressor had once belonged to an ice-cream truck. Its insulation was scavenged from funeral home walls. Inside were tubes of genetic material wrapped in foil, each labeled by hand: **CROWN-MZ-88 SALT**, **DRYLAND PROMOTER?**, **FUNGAL RESIST — DEGRADED**, **DO NOT USE UNLESS DESPERATE**, and, because Tavi had a sense of humor, **PROBABLY NOT HAUNTED**. The viable DNA was going bad. Every thaw bit chunks from it. Radiation had nicked it, time had chewed it, and some samples came preloaded with corporate self-destruct sequences like little legal bombs. “Maybe,” she said. “That means yes with bleeding.” “It means prep the gels.” By evening, the lab smelled of agarose, vinegar, hot dust, and old fear. Mara worked with gloves patched in two fingers and goggles fogged from a bad seal. She extracted what she could from desiccated maize kernels traded last winter from a caravan that no longer existed. The kernels were pale and shriveled, but when ground in buffer, they released enough DNA to make her hands tremble. The first amplification failed. The thermocycler browned out halfway through denaturation when the solar rig hiccuped under passing cloud. The machine clicked, paused, and restarted from the beginning, cooking her sample into molecular soup. Tavi swore at the ceiling and ran outside to adjust the panel angle by hand. The second amplification produced primer-dimers and a smear. The third gave bands—faint, uneven, but there. Mara stared at the gel under the blue LED transilluminator, its casing made from an old aquarium lamp. The bands glowed like ghost ladders. “There,” she whispered. Tavi leaned close. “That’s good?” “That’s a promoter sequence. Maybe the salt-tolerance regulator.” “Maybe?” “Science is mostly maybe wearing a clean coat.” “You don’t own a clean coat.” “Exactly.” The settlement medic, Juno, came in near midnight carrying a tray of tea and bad news. Her left arm had grown a fine layer of white fuzz since she’d taken a myco-immune mod to survive lung rot. On damp days, mushrooms the size of fingernails appeared along her elbow. She harvested them carefully. They tasted like pepper and kept infection down. “Rook’s fever broke,” Juno said. “Also his hair is cartilage now.” “Flexible?” “Enough.” “Then count it as a win.” Juno set down the tea and looked at the gel. “Is that our salvation or another way to starve creatively?” “Ask me tomorrow.” “Tomorrow I’ll ask why you’re taking a contract from St. Brigid.” Mara didn’t answer. Juno sighed. “Black market doesn’t pay that well unless someone else pays more to take it from you.” “They’re offering a kidney print.” “I know what they’re offering.” Juno’s voice softened. “That’s why I’m worried you’ll say yes to everything after.” Mara rubbed her thumb along a scar at her wrist, where an anti-radiation mod had overgrown into a ridge of black keratin. It ached before storms. It also let her work under UV longer than anyone else without blistering. Upsides, always unexpected. Costs, always deferred. “I’m not selling our only strain,” Mara said. “If I get it stable, I split it. One for us, one for Lark.” “And if Lark disagrees?” “Then we learn what kind of bird they are.” The actual splice took fourteen hours. She used CRISPR reagents older than Tavi, refreshed with enzymes expressed in yeast that had acquired a taste for antibiotics and occasionally tried to crawl out of the incubator. The guide RNAs were ugly, patched together from sequences in a pre-collapse textbook and corporate leak files. She targeted the failure points first: the sterility cassette, the methylation trigger, the suicide promoter activated after embryogenesis. Corporate engineers had been brilliant in the way landmines were brilliant. Every solution had a trap behind it. Remove one attenuation sequence and a second activated downstream. Stabilize germline expression and growth regulators surged until seedlings became tumors. Verdant Crown had not merely made plants dependent; they had made independence pathological. At dawn, Mara injected edited embryos into salvaged growth pods: glass jars with silicone seals, nutrient medium cloudy as broth, oxygen lines fed by aquarium pumps. The incubator was a converted bread warmer wrapped in emergency blankets. A thermostat stolen from a reptile tank clicked uncertainly. Then she waited. Waiting was the cruelest protocol. By the second day, contamination bloomed in six jars. Pink bacterial lace. Black fungal beads. One jar developed something like roots, then dissolved into green jelly that smelled of almonds. By the third day, two embryos sprouted. They were small, almost translucent, curled tight as commas. But they had roots. Real roots with branching hairs. Mara stained a clipped sample, checked expression markers, ran another amplification on leaf tissue no larger than a fingernail. The sterility cassette was silent. The drought-salt regulator was active. The germline markers held. She sat back so fast her stool skidded. Tavi, half asleep on the floor, jerked awake glowing bright enough to read by. “Fire?” “Lineage,” Mara said. “What?” “They might breed.” For a moment, neither of them spoke. Outside, someone laughed in the courtyard, a short surprised sound like a match struck in the dark. On the fifth night, Mara went to St. Brigid with twelve seeds sealed in a glass vial under her tongue and twelve more stitched into the lining of her boot. The black market lived in the crypt beneath the old church, where saints with smashed faces watched traders bargain over antibiotics, ammunition, clean water filters, memory drives, womb licenses, and jars of bees priced like jewels. The air was incense, sweat, and ozone from jury-rigged security scanners. Lark waited beside a pillar wrapped in copper wire. They wore a crow-feather coat and a respirator painted gold. Their eyes had horizontal pupils, goat-spliced or cosmetic; Mara couldn’t tell. Two guards stood behind them with bone-stock rifles and vat-grown muscle bulking their necks unnaturally. “Mara Vale,” Lark said. “You brought spring in your mouth?” Mara spat the vial into her palm and held it up. “Twelve,” she said. “Edited maize embryos. Salt-drought tolerant. Sterility suppression removed. Second-generation potential confirmed in markers, not field-tested.” Lark’s pupils widened. “Only twelve?” “Twelve is a farm if you know patience.” “And if I don’t?” “Then it’s dinner.” Lark laughed. “Payment on verification.” “Payment now.” A guard shifted. Mara felt the old fear move through her body and find all the places gene-medicine had changed. Her adrenal mod, taken during the siege winter, kicked hot into her bloodstream. Time sharpened. Sounds separated. She could hear water dripping three aisles away, hear Lark’s respirator valve stick on the exhale. The downside would come later: tremors, migraine, maybe temporary blindness. The upside was now. Lark produced a silver cartridge from inside their coat. Frost smoked from its seams. “Kidney print,” they said. “Viability seal intact. Compatible with broad-spectrum immuno-cloak.” Mara took it, checked the seal, checked the embedded timestamp, checked the donor hash. Real. Too real. “Who are you growing this for?” she asked. Lark tilted their head. “Does it matter?” “Yes.” “To you, perhaps. To hunger, no.” Mara looked past them at the market: at a mother trading wedding rings for fever tabs, at a man with scales coughing blood into a cloth, at jars of seeds locked behind glass like contraband gods. She had told herself the contract was simple. Seeds for tissue. Survival for survival. But corporate law had collapsed before corporate instinct. Somewhere, someone would try to own spring again. “The line breeds open,” Mara said. “No activation enzyme. No license lock. No kill switch.” Lark went very still. “That wasn’t requested.” “It is what exists.” “Dangerous choice.” “For whom?” For a long second, the crypt seemed to hold its breath. Then Lark smiled behind gold paint. “You biohackers are all priests. You pretend it’s about hunger, but really you want a world that doesn’t ask permission.” Mara closed her fist around the cartridge. “No,” she said. “I want breakfast.” She handed over the vial. Lark examined the seeds, then gestured to a guard. No bullets came. No betrayal, not yet. Mara walked out under the broken nave and into the thin gray morning. Halfway home, the adrenal crash hit. Her hands shook so badly she had to sit in the rubble of a bus stop. Her vision tunneled. Blood dripped from her nose onto her shirt in dark coins. She laughed anyway. In her boot, twelve stolen futures pressed against her ankle. By sunrise, she was back in the lab. The solar rig flickered awake, unstable voltage trembling through the wires, and the room became again a laboratory by flashes. Tavi held up the kidney cartridge like a holy relic. Juno began crying without making any sound. Mara cut the stitches from her boot and poured the hidden seeds into a cracked porcelain dish. They were ugly little things. Brown, uneven, too light. Alive. Outside, the settlement gathered around the soil beds as Mara pushed the first seed into ash-black earth with her thumb. No anthem played. No corporation claimed the moment. No license activated. For once, the future did not ask to be purchased. It simply waited to grow.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This biopunk chapter demonstrates strong worldbuilding and atmospheric writing, successfully creating a post-apocalyptic setting where corporate genetic engineering has collapsed. The author effectively establishes the premise of built-in obsolescence in crops and the protagonist's role as a biohacker working to restore viable seeds. The technical details are well-integrated, showing clear research into molecular biology while making the science accessible. The jury-rigged equipment descriptions are particularly effective - the PCR thermocycler held down with a brick, the centrifuge made from a salad spinner, and the incubator converted from a bread warmer all feel authentic and grounded. Character development is solid, with Mara emerging as a competent, morally complex protagonist. Her relationship with Tavi provides emotional grounding, and the physical consequences of genetic modifications (his glowing patches, Juno's mushroom growths) add texture to the world. The dialogue feels natural and serves character development well. The black market sequence builds appropriate tension, and Lark makes for an intriguing antagonist with their goat-spliced eyes and philosophical observations about biohackers being "priests." The moral complexity of the transaction - trading potentially dangerous freedom for medical necessity - adds depth. However, the piece suffers from some pacing issues. The middle section drags during the technical procedures, and some descriptions become overly elaborate. The ending, while thematically appropriate, feels somewhat rushed and overly optimistic given the established stakes. The transformation from desperate scarcity to hopeful planting happens too quickly to feel fully earned. The prose occasionally veers into purple territory with phrases like "seeds designed to mourn themselves" and "stolen futures pressed against her ankle," though these moments are generally well-controlled. The voice remains consistent throughout, maintaining the gritty, technical tone appropriate to the setting. The piece successfully addresses all prompt requirements: post-apocalyptic biopunk setting, jury-rigged equipment, genetic modifications with costs and benefits, and a black market contract driving the plot. The 1000-word length is well-utilized, though some sections could be tightened.
