+ Young adult drama: Gone South — Score: 88.2
Prompt:
Young adult drama Gone South Taylor is a 16 year old teen with a history of "concerning behavioural patterns" (or that's how the stupid smug school counsellor worded it on the phone call to Mom). It shouldn't be a surprise: When you live in a backwater town in Tennessee, your choices are: a. be mind-fuckingly bored, or b. invent some drama. Two days ago, Taylor came out to their family as trans. This disclosure was *not* in the category of invented drama -- it was, in fact, the real deal, and a long time coming. It didn't go well. The last 48 hours had been pure disaster. In a moment of desperate inspiration, Taylor had decided to get the hell out of dodge. And so an escape plan was hatched and executed within the space of 30 minutes. That was last night. Now Taylor was two states away, having been unceremoniously ejected from their hitched ride in downtown New Orleans. Write the next chapter in Taylor's story as they find their feet in this new environment. Tell the chapter over the course of their first day in New Orleans, in which Taylor meets another runaway with issues and secrets. Taylor navigates this new world, not knowing who to trust or what the dangers are. Write in Taylor's voice, and incorporate their internal monologue as they grapple with intense & conflicting emotions. Also incorporate dialogue with the interesting characters Taylor meets. First person, mixed tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
### Chapter Two: Twelve Percent The guy who kicks me out of the truck does it with the kind of politeness people use when they’re about to ruin your life. “End of the line, kid.” Like we’re at Disney World. Like I paid for a ticket. Like I haven’t been sleeping curled against a duffel bag that smells like motor oil and beef jerky while his bumper sticker tells the world JESUS SAVES in peeling blue letters. I blink into morning. New Orleans is wet. Not raining, exactly. Just wet in the air, wet on the pavement, wet under my hoodie, like the whole city has been sweating all night and forgot to be ashamed of it. The sky is the color of dirty dishwater. Buildings lean over the street in old, fancy ways, balconies dripping iron vines. Somewhere nearby, music leaks from a doorway—trumpet, maybe—and there are already people laughing too loudly for whatever time it is. My phone says 7:11 a.m. Battery: 12%. Awesome. Perfect. Love that for me. The truck pulls away and leaves me standing at the curb with my backpack, my hoodie, twenty-seven dollars in cash, a half-empty bottle of gas station water, and the deep, spiritual awareness that I have made a choice that cannot be un-made. For three seconds, this feels cool. I am in New Orleans. I escaped. I am not in Tennessee. I am not in my kitchen with Mom crying like I died, not in the living room with Dad staring at the wall like if he didn’t look directly at me, I’d turn back into his daughter. Not daughter. Not. The word hits a raw place and lights it up. I swallow hard and look down at my shoes. “Okay,” I whisper. “Okay, Taylor. You did the thing. Now do the next thing.” The next thing, apparently, is not cry on the sidewalk. I start walking. Downtown New Orleans in the morning is weird because it looks like a place that threw a party so hard it died and resurrected itself before breakfast. There are beads in gutters, smashed plastic cups, cigarette butts, puddles shining oily rainbow colors. A man in a Saints jersey hoses vomit off the sidewalk like this is normal and maybe holy. He glances at me, then at my backpack. “You lost?” he asks. “No,” I say, way too fast. He snorts. “Then you lyin’ or local.” I keep walking. My phone buzzes once. Mom. One missed call becomes a notification, then another. Texts stacked like accusations. **Taylor please answer** **We are worried sick** **Your father and I didn’t mean—** **Please just tell us you’re safe** **I love you** The last one makes me furious. Not because it’s fake. That would be easier. It’s furious because it might be true and still not enough. I press the side button and the screen goes black. 11%. I need food. I need a charger. I need not to get murdered. The list is already unrealistic. A woman with purple braids sits on the curb outside a closed souvenir shop, smoking and arranging bracelets on a blanket. She’s older than me, maybe nineteen or twenty, with eyeliner like black wings and silver rings on nearly every finger. A cardboard sign beside her reads: **HANDMADE — PAY WHAT U CAN — DON’T BE A DICK.** She watches me hover. “Morning, baby stray,” she says. I freeze. “I’m not—” “A baby?” She exhales smoke sideways. “A stray? Or both?” I should walk away. This is exactly the kind of person school assemblies warn you about, except school assemblies also told us vaping would make our lungs explode instantly and that abstinence was a personality. “I’m fine,” I say. “Mm-hmm.” She taps ash into an empty energy drink can. “That’s what people say right before they pass out in my personal business zone.” “I’m not going to pass out.” My stomach chooses that moment to make a noise like an angry raccoon. The woman’s eyebrows lift. I hate my body. Not in the gender way this time. In the betrayal way. She digs into a tote and tosses me a granola bar. I catch it against my chest. “I don’t have money to—” “Did I invoice you?” “No.” “Then shut up and eat.” I stare at the wrapper like it might be a trap. Peanut butter chocolate chip. Honestly, if this is how I die, there are worse flavors. I sit on the far edge of the curb, not too close. She smirks. “Name’s Mercy.” “That your real name?” “Is anything?” Fair. I tear open the granola bar. “Taylor.” “Taylor what?” “Just Taylor.” “Good answer.” For a minute we sit in silence. A streetcar rattles somewhere distant. A tourist couple walks by in matching ponchos even though it isn’t raining. The guy looks at me and Mercy like we are part of the scenery: balcony, beads, possibly homeless youth. Very authentic. Mercy says, “You got people looking for you, Just Taylor?” My throat tightens. “Everybody’s got people.” “Not everybody.” There’s something in the way she says it. A door closing softly. I chew, though my mouth has gone dry. “You?” She grins without happiness. “I got ghosts.” “That sounds dramatic.” “Everything sounds dramatic before it tries to kill you.” I look at her. She laughs. “Relax. I mean rent.” That should be funny. It almost is. My phone buzzes again. I flinch. Mercy notices. Of course she does. People like her notice everything. People like me only notice the exits after we need them. “You need to charge that?” she asks. “Yes,” I say. Then, suspiciously: “Why?” “Because it’s 202—whatever and you look like the type to have your whole nervous system stored in a rectangle.” “I do not.” “What percent?” I hesitate. “Eleven.” She whistles. “Critical condition.” “Do you know somewhere?” “Library opens at ten. Cafés if you buy something and don’t look too feral. Hotel lobbies if you look like you belong, which you do not.” “Thanks.” “You asked.” I finish the bar slowly to make it last. It doesn’t. Mercy starts packing bracelets into little zip bags. “You got a plan?” “Yes,” I lie. “What is it?” “Not share it with random sidewalk people.” She points at me. “See, that’s a plan. Bad execution, but conceptually sound.” Despite everything, I laugh. It comes out rusty. Then a voice behind us says, “Mercy, you seen Kite?” Mercy’s face changes. Not much. If I hadn’t been staring because I have no survival skills and too much curiosity, I might have missed it. Her eyes flick left, then right. Her fingers go still on a bracelet. The boy standing there is probably my age, maybe seventeen. Skinny in a sharp way, like he’s been carved from hunger and attitude. Brown skin, chipped black nail polish, oversized green jacket even though the air feels like soup. His hair is buzzed on one side and curly on the other, and he has a bruise yellowing under his cheekbone. He looks at me, then looks away, dismissing me as irrelevant. Rude, but accurate. “No,” Mercy says. “You sure?” “If I seen Kite, I would’ve said, ‘Hey, look, there’s Kite.’” The boy shifts. “Don’t be like that.” “Don’t ask me cop questions at eight in the morning.” “I’m not—” He stops, jaw tight. “I need to find him.” Mercy leans closer. “And I need you to stop being stupid in front of babies.” “I’m sixteen,” I snap. They both look at me. Mercy says, “That is exactly the age of a baby.” The boy’s mouth twitches. “Fresh off the bus?” “Truck,” I say before I can stop myself. Mercy closes her eyes like I have personally exhausted her. The boy studies me again, properly this time. His gaze snags on my backpack, my shoes, my too-clean hoodie, my everything. “Runaway?” “No.” He waits. “Yes,” I say. “Good at lying, huh?” “I’m having an off day.” That gets a real smile out of him, tiny and gone fast. “I’m Rowan,” he says. “Taylor.” His expression does something complicated. “Taylor like…?” “Taylor like Taylor.” “Cool.” He nods once, and it shouldn’t matter, except it does. He doesn’t ask the follow-up question. He doesn’t squint at me, trying to solve the math of my face and my voice and my name. He just lets it be a true thing. My stupid eyes burn. Mercy stands abruptly. “Library at ten,” she tells me. “Until then, don’t follow strangers.” Rowan says, “I’m not a stranger. I introduced myself.” “That’s exactly what strangers do.” Mercy slings her tote over her shoulder and walks away, bracelets clinking like tiny bones. Which leaves me with Rowan. And my phone at 10%. And my entire life somewhere north of here, screaming. Rowan rocks back on his heels. “You hungry?” “I ate.” “A granola bar?” “That counts.” “That counts as sadness with oats.” He jerks his head. “Come on.” “No.” “Okay.” He starts walking. I stand there, victorious and also abandoned. After about five steps, he calls back, “There’s a church that does breakfast. You can trail behind me at a safe and emotionally distant pace.” I hate that this works. I follow. We cut through streets that seem to change personalities every block. One minute it’s bars with shuttered doors and neon signs sleeping in windows; next it’s pastel houses with ferns hanging from balconies, cracked sidewalks shoved upward by roots. Rowan walks like someone who belongs everywhere and nowhere. He keeps checking reflections in windows. “What’s Kite?” I ask. “Who.” “Fine. Who’s Kite?” “My friend.” “The one you can’t find.” “That’s usually why people ask where someone is.” “Are you always like this?” “Only when I’m awake.” We pass a man sleeping under a blue tarp. Rowan’s joking face drops for half a second. He looks everywhere but at the tarp. I recognize that trick. Avoidance: family hobby. Mom avoids Dad’s temper. Dad avoids feelings. I avoid mirrors when my hair gets too long. Rowan says, “You got ID?” “Learner’s permit.” “Money?” “Some.” “Phone?” “Dying.” “Anyone know where you are?” “No.” He looks at me sharply. “Bad.” “Wow, thanks, mentor figure.” “I’m serious. You need one person who knows you’re alive.” “I have people. They’re just the reason I left.” The words come out harder than I expect. They land between us, ugly and hot. Rowan slows. “Yeah,” he says quietly. “That happens.” The church basement smells like coffee, bleach, and syrup. Volunteers in plastic gloves hand out paper plates with eggs and biscuits. Nobody asks my story. Nobody says I look too young. Nobody calls my mom. This feels like kindness, or maybe negligence. I can’t tell the difference yet. Rowan gets two plates and slides one to me. “I said I could pay.” “Then pay someone else later.” We sit at the end of a folding table. Across the room, an old man argues with himself. A woman with a baby stroller full of blankets laughs at something on her phone. A volunteer keeps calling everyone “hon.” I eat so fast I burn my tongue. Rowan watches, amused. “Told you.” “Shut up.” “My apologies to the oats.” My phone buzzes. 9%. A text from my sister, Lily. **Mom’s freaking out. Dad called the cops. Are you okay? I won’t tell if you answer me. Promise.** My chest caves inward. Lily is fourteen and mean in a hilarious way and stole my black nail polish last month and told me my coming-out haircut made me look “like a depressed mushroom,” which, to be fair, it did. When Dad started yelling, she stood in the hallway with her arms wrapped around herself, eyes huge. She didn’t say anything. I hated her for that. I also didn’t. Rowan tilts his head. “Bad news?” “My sister.” “You should answer.” “You don’t know anything.” “I know if my sister texted me, I’d answer.” “Do you have a sister?” His face shuts. There it is. Secret. Big one. “No,” he says. Liar, liar, mysterious pants on fire. I type: **I’m alive. Don’t tell them. Please.** I stare at it until the letters blur. Then I hit send. 8%. Rowan says, “There’s a youth drop-in place near Marigny. Showers, chargers, people who legally have to care if you vanish.” “Sounds suspicious.” “It is. But less suspicious than most alternatives.” “Do you go there?” “Sometimes.” “What’s the catch?” He pushes eggs around his plate. “They ask questions.” “Oh, the horror.” “Some questions got teeth.” I think of school counsellors and their soft voices. Taylor has concerning behavioral patterns. Taylor is seeking attention. Taylor is struggling with identity issues. Taylor, Taylor, Taylor, like saying my name enough times could turn me into somebody easier. “What kind of questions?” I ask. “Names. Ages. Who hurt you. Whether you’ll hurt yourself.” Rowan’s voice goes flat. “Where you slept. Who you’re running from.” I wait for him to look at me, but he doesn’t. Outside, the day has gone bright and heavy. Rowan walks me toward the library because I still don’t fully trust drop-in centers with teeth. The streets fill with tourists and delivery trucks and music that seems to come from the bricks themselves. New Orleans is beautiful in a way that feels dangerous, like a stranger with a knife and excellent cheekbones. At the library, every outlet is taken. Of course. I hover near a column until a girl with headphones leaves, and then I pounce on the table like a raccoon in human clothes. No charger, though. Because I am brilliant, I have brought the phone and not the cord. Rowan silently produces one from his jacket pocket. I stare. “You had a charger this whole time?” “You didn’t ask.” “I asked where I could charge my phone!” “That is spiritually different.” “I might actually kill you.” “Not in the library. They frown on noise.” I plug in. The little lightning symbol appears. Something in me almost collapses. 13%. My phone starts vomiting notifications. Mom. Mom. Lily. Unknown number. Dad. Mom. I turn it face down. Rowan sits across from me, picking at the peeling edge of a sticker on the table. “Why are you helping me?” I ask. He doesn’t answer right away. Then: “You looked like you were about to trust the wrong person.” “And you’re the right person?” “No.” His smile is crooked. “But I know a few directions where the wrong people stand.” That should not be comforting. It is. In the afternoon, we go to the drop-in place. I almost don’t. I stand outside the painted blue door with my pulse banging against my ribs, imagining adults with clipboards, adults with phones, adults saying mandatory reporting in calm voices while my parents drive south to collect me like a misdelivered package. Rowan waits on the steps. “You can use a fake last name,” he says. “They’ll still feed you.” “You’re disturbingly experienced.” “Thank you.” Inside, a woman named Denise gives me a bottle of water, a list of resources, and a look that says she has seen every version of scared and isn’t impressed by my attempt at pretending otherwise. “What pronouns, Taylor?” she asks. I stop breathing. Rowan looks at a poster on the wall very intensely, giving me privacy with his whole body. I say, “He. They. Either.” Denise writes it down like it’s normal. Like I am normal. Something hot and humiliating spills down my face before I can stop it. Denise just slides over a box of tissues. “Long day?” I laugh and cry at the same time, which is basically choking. “It’s not even over.” “No,” she says gently. “But you made it this far.” By evening, my phone is at 46%, my stomach has food in it, and I have a paper with hotline numbers folded in my pocket. I also have no idea where I’m sleeping, which puts a dent in the whole “progress” thing. Rowan finds me outside by the mural of a pelican wearing a crown. “You can crash near me,” he says. “Not with me. Near. There’s an overhang behind a music shop. Dry enough.” “Is Kite there?” His jaw flexes. “No.” “You’re really worried about him.” Rowan looks down the street, where dusk is turning the city gold and bruised purple. “He has something of mine.” “What?” “My problem.” I wait. He shakes his head. “Not yet, Taylor like Taylor.” Fair enough. I have secrets too. Mine are just currently texting me in all caps. We walk as the city wakes up for night. Music spills everywhere. People toast on balconies. A horse carriage rolls by, and the horse looks like it understands taxes and hates them. I should be terrified. I am terrified. But also—I am still here. In a city that doesn’t know my old name. In a city where a stranger asked my pronouns and did not flinch. In a city where danger and kindness wear the same shoes, and I am learning, step by step, to look at the laces. My phone buzzes once. Lily: **I won’t tell. Be safe, idiot.** I smile so hard it hurts. Rowan glances over. “Good news?” “Maybe.” “That counts.” For now, under the thick New Orleans sky, with twelve hours behind me and no map for tomorrow, maybe is the closest thing to hope I’ve got.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and emotional authenticity in portraying a transgender teen runaway's first day in New Orleans. The writing successfully captures Taylor's voice through a blend of vulnerability, defensive humor, and raw emotion that feels genuine for a 16-year-old in crisis. The adherence to instructions is excellent - the piece follows Taylor through their first day, introduces another runaway (Rowan) with clear secrets, maintains first-person mixed tense throughout, and incorporates substantial internal monologue alongside natural dialogue. The 1000-word target is met appropriately. Character development is particularly strong. Taylor's voice is consistent and believable - alternating between bravado and fear, using humor as defense, and showing realistic emotional responses to their situation. The internal monologue effectively reveals their conflicted feelings about family, identity, and survival. Rowan is well-crafted as a mysterious but helpful figure with his own clearly hinted-at trauma, and even minor characters like Mercy feel authentic. The dialogue is naturalistic and serves multiple purposes - revealing character, advancing plot, and providing exposition without feeling forced. Exchanges like Taylor's interaction with Mercy about being a "baby stray" or the pronoun conversation with Denise feel organic and meaningful. The prose quality is high, with vivid sensory details that bring New Orleans to life without overwhelming the narrative. Descriptions like "New Orleans is wet... like the whole city has been sweating all night and forgot to be ashamed of it" are evocative without being purple. The writing maintains an appropriate balance between literary quality and the immediacy of Taylor's perspective. Emotionally, the piece is complex and engaging. It handles serious themes - family rejection, homelessness, gender identity - with appropriate weight while finding moments of genuine connection and hope. The ending strikes the right tone - not falsely optimistic but showing earned progress and small victories. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the writing becomes slightly self-conscious in its cleverness, and a few instances where metaphors feel slightly forced. The pacing is generally strong but occasionally slows during descriptive passages. The piece avoids major pitfalls like unearned transformations or incongruent positivity. Taylor's small moments of hope and connection feel realistic given the day's events and the kindness they've encountered. The darkness and lightness are well-balanced and appropriate to the situation. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 18 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 17

+ Dystopic-comedy: My Apocalypse Buddy — Score: 81.2
Prompt:
My Apocalypse Buddy Write a wry, dry short story of survival and adventure loosely in the style of Terry Pratchett, set in the post-technological collapse world. The story begins with the pair deliberating their predicament and intent for the day's mission, details the ways in which the mission inevitably goes sideways, and returns them to home base in multiple ways, worse for wear. [characters] - the animo-tronic stuffed koala, Chewy, from some long forgotten before time - midas, our hapless protagonist, who has a knack for finding himself in the worst kinds of trouble. 1000 words.
Model Output:
By sunrise, the rain had stopped falling upward. This was generally agreed to be an improvement. Midas stood at the entrance to the underpass and watched the last drops climb back into the bruised clouds, leaving the cracked highway steaming like a bad idea. Around him, Home Base stirred: tarps flapped, solar cookers sulked, and someone in the east tunnel was arguing with a goat about property rights. On his shoulder sat Chewy, an animo-tronic stuffed koala from the before time, when humanity had apparently possessed both miracles and a terrible surplus of leisure. Chewy’s grey fur had gone bald in patches. One ear was stitched on with copper wire. His plastic nose was dented. His eyes, however, still worked beautifully, which was a shame. At the moment they glowed a suspicious yellow. “You’re anxious,” said Midas. “I am running a systems diagnostic,” said Chewy, in the flat, cheerful voice of a toy designed to reassure toddlers while harvesting their affection for market research. “Also, I am anxious.” “We’ve done worse missions.” Chewy considered this. Tiny servos whined in his neck. “No,” he said. Midas tightened the strap on his canvas satchel. The satchel had once advertised artisanal coffee beans, which was either a joke or evidence of civilisation’s former crimes. “It’s just a supply run.” “Yesterday was just a supply run.” “Yesterday involved unusual factors.” “It involved a hole.” “A very unusual hole.” “It had teeth, Midas.” “That’s what made it unusual.” Chewy’s eyes flickered orange. They had been given a mission by Auntie Spanner, who ran Home Base with a welded ladle, a working knowledge of pressure systems, and the moral certainty of a storm drain. The mission was simple: cross the old flyover, reach the shopping centre ruins, retrieve antibiotics from the clinic, and return before the afternoon fog arrived and started whispering people’s middle names. Simple missions were the most dangerous sort. Complicated missions, at least, had the decency to look nervous. Midas patted his pockets: knife, twine, cracked compass, three boiled pigeons’ eggs, one battery used as currency, and a map drawn by someone who had labelled an entire district Probably Snakes. Chewy leaned close to his ear. “We could fake our deaths.” “We did that in March.” “Only socially.” “We need the medicine.” This, unfortunately, was true. Old Hobb had a fever, Little Nance had stepped on a nail that had opinions, and the last of the antibiotics had been traded to a priest-engine for clean water and three prophecies, two of which had already expired. Midas took a breath. “All right,” he said. “In and out.” Chewy’s eyes turned red. “You must stop saying that.” They set off. The world after the Collapse had not ended so much as lost interest in itself. Trees grew through office towers. Vines strangled traffic lights that continued, out of habit, to blink at nobody. The road signs had become nesting places for clever birds. Every so often Midas passed relics of the before time: glass rectangles that no longer remembered faces, metal boxes that once carried people and now housed ferrets, a billboard showing a smiling family eating something orange and triangular. “Pizza,” said Chewy. “What?” “The orange triangles. Pizza. A ceremonial food of the ancients.” “How do you know?” “I contain educational content.” “You also once told a rat it was a valued customer.” “It looked under-served.” By midmorning they reached the flyover. It sagged across the city like a dead grey serpent. Below, the river ran black and glossy, full of weeds, plastic saints, and the occasional bubble too large to be reassuring. Someone had painted across the road: BRIDGE MOSTLY FINE. “That is not a structural assessment,” said Chewy. “It says mostly.” “Midas.” “What?” Chewy’s eyes pulsed violet. “The word mostly is where optimism goes to die.” They crossed anyway, because survival often meant choosing between bad ideas and worse logistics. Halfway over, they met the goats. There were seven of them, arranged in a loose semicircle. Each wore scraps of fabric tied about its horns. One had spectacles. Another had a frying pan around its neck like a medal. Their leader, a large white goat with a beard that suggested senior management, stepped forward and stamped. Midas raised both hands. “Morning.” The goat stared. Chewy whispered, “This is a toll point.” “It’s a bridge.” “Correction: it was a bridge. Now it is a goat-based revenue model.” Midas fished in his satchel and offered one pigeon egg. The goat ate the egg, shell and all, then continued staring. “Greedy, isn’t it?” The goat lowered its head. “Fine.” Midas produced the battery. Chewy’s eyes flashed blue with scandal. “That is our emergency bribe.” “This is an emergency.” “This is livestock with branding.” The goat ate the battery. There was a small pop. All seven goats froze. The leader’s pupils widened with sudden and terrible enlightenment. Then it bleated with a voice like a trumpet falling downstairs and charged. Midas ran. This solved the immediate problem of being gored and created the secondary problem of running across a bridge that had only ever been mostly fine. The flyover groaned. Concrete cracked. Chewy clung to Midas’s collar, eyes strobing red, blue, and green in a pattern that would once have delighted children and now mainly served as a distress beacon. Behind them, the goats thundered. Ahead, the road dipped. “No,” said Midas. “Yes,” said gravity. The bridge collapsed in a dignified, leisurely manner, as if embarrassed to be caught failing in public. Midas slid, bounced off a slab, lost one egg, gained a surprising amount of gravel in his trousers, and landed on a mound of soft vegetation beside the river. Chewy hit the ground beside him with a squeak. For a moment, there was peace. Then the mound moved. Midas opened one eye. A giant water cabbage unfolded beneath them. “Ah,” said Chewy. “Mobile salad.” The cabbage lurched into the river. They sailed downstream. This was one way of returning to Home Base, technically, as the river looped near the old underpass before heading into marsh country and certain damp mysteries. It was not, however, the preferred way. The preferred way involved fewer leaves, no goats, and dry socks. Midas sat astride the cabbage’s central rib while Chewy perched on a curling leaf, dripping. “New plan,” said Midas. “We ride it until we reach the mall district.” “That is not a plan. That is a symptom.” “It’s going the right direction.” Chewy looked over the edge at the black water. Something underneath blinked back. His eyes went pink. “Sentiment?” said Midas. “Terror with a garnish of nostalgia.” The cabbage delivered them, after several bends and one brief argument with a territorial swan the size of a wardrobe, to the flooded car park of the shopping centre. Midas climbed onto the roof of an ancient bus. Chewy had to be wrung out, which he endured with the weary dignity of a small aristocrat caught in rain. The mall loomed ahead: glassless, ivy-strangled, and full of echoes. Its sign still read WESTBRIAR PLAZA, though someone had rearranged fallen letters beneath it to spell RAW ZEBRA LIPS. Inside, the air smelled of mould, dust, and perfumes that had refused to die out of sheer vanity. They passed boutiques where mannequins wore moss. They passed a fountain occupied by frogs who had formed what appeared to be a choir. Somewhere in the distance, machinery clicked. “The clinic is second floor,” said Midas, consulting the map. Chewy’s eyes flickered yellow again. “Map says second floor is also ‘haunted by customer service.’” “That could mean anything.” A voice chimed from the darkness. “Welcome, valued shopper!” Midas stopped. Chewy’s eyes went white. From behind a kiosk rolled a squat machine with a cracked screen for a face and six spidery legs made from repurposed escalator parts. Its screen displayed a smiling cartoon woman whose expression had been trapped at the exact midpoint between hospitality and murder. “Please enjoy your retail experience,” it said. Midas backed away. “We’re just passing through.” “Have you considered joining our loyalty programme?” “No.” “Wonderful! I’ll sign you up.” Panels opened along its sides. Out came ribbons, tags, and a little thermal printer. Midas ran again. He had not planned to spend the morning fleeing aggressive commerce. Then again, nobody ever did. Chewy bounced on his shoulder, eyes flashing red so brightly they reflected off shop windows. “This is why I prefer goats!” Chewy shouted. “Goats are honest extortionists!” They tore up a dead escalator, across the food court, and into the clinic. Midas slammed the door, wedged it with a chair, then turned. The clinic had been picked over many times, but Auntie Spanner’s information was good. Behind a fallen cabinet, sealed in a metal wall case, were six dusty blister packs and three brown bottles. Midas grinned. “See? We did it.” Chewy’s eyes glowed green. Then the ceiling sighed. A colony of ceiling tiles descended as one, revealing the nest of wire-rats above them. They were not rats, strictly speaking, but scraps of old devices animated by bad weather and worse luck. They had whiskers of copper, bodies of plastic, and an appetite for anything that stored electricity. Chewy made a small noise. The wire-rats turned. Hundreds of tiny lenses fixed on his chest, where his internal battery ticked on bravely. “Oh,” said Midas. Chewy’s eyes turned a deep, betrayed purple. “I am once again the cheese.” Midas snatched the medicine, grabbed Chewy, and kicked open the rear door just as the customer-service machine broke through the front. “Thank you for shopping!” The wire-rats poured after them. The next several minutes were not so much an escape as an audition for new forms of panic. Midas slid down a collapsed stairwell, swung from a vine through an atrium, crashed through a display of pre-Collapse footwear, and emerged wearing one excellent red boot he had not entered with. Chewy, clutched under one arm, broadcast emotional weather in all colours. At last they reached the loading bay, where an old delivery drone hung by cables from the ceiling. It was large enough to carry parcels. Midas looked at it. Chewy looked at Midas. “No,” said Chewy. “Fastest way home.” “It has no power.” “You do.” “That sentence has lowered my opinion of language.” Behind them came the click-click-click of wire-rats and the cheerful grinding of retail. Midas plugged Chewy’s exposed maintenance cable into the drone. Chewy’s eyes blazed electric blue. “I want it noted,” he said, “that friendship has become very invasive.” The drone woke with a shudder. Its rotors coughed, spun, stopped, then spun again with the desperate energy of a pensioner chased by bees. Midas grabbed the cargo harness as it lurched off the bay and into open air. They flew. For twelve seconds it was magnificent. For the next forty, it was educational. The drone did not understand balance, weather, or the difference between destination and wall. It skimmed rooftops, clipped a chimney, startled a flock of sign-birds, and carried them in a wide, failing arc toward the underpass. “Home Base!” shouted Midas. “Home base!” shouted Chewy, whose eyes were now flashing every colour at once, creating a festive mood wholly inappropriate to their altitude. The drone gave up over the highway. Midas, Chewy, the medicine, and one excellent red boot landed in Auntie Spanner’s rainwater tarpaulin, which had been carefully positioned to collect drinking water and not, as it turned out, idiots. There was a great splash. Silence followed. Home Base gathered round. Auntie Spanner peered down at them. “You’re late.” Midas held up the medicine. “Got it.” Chewy floated face-up beside him, eyes dimly blinking green. “We returned by land, river, air, and humiliation.” Old Hobb coughed from somewhere in the crowd. “Any trouble?” Midas opened his mouth. In the distance, from the ruined flyover, came the triumphant bleat of an enlightened goat. From the west, faintly, a voice called, “Valued shopper!” Beneath the tarp, something small and metallic clicked. Chewy’s eyes turned red. Midas sighed. “Define trouble,” he said.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a strong grasp of Terry Pratchett's distinctive style, successfully capturing his dry wit, absurdist humor, and philosophical observations wrapped in fantastical scenarios. The author effectively creates a post-apocalyptic world that feels both dire and whimsical, where upward-falling rain and enlightened goats coexist with genuine survival concerns. The characterization is particularly well-executed. Midas emerges as a believably hapless protagonist whose optimism consistently clashes with reality, while Chewy serves as an excellent foil - a malfunctioning children's toy whose sardonic observations and color-coded emotional states provide both humor and pathos. Their relationship feels authentic, built on mutual exasperation and grudging affection. The prose style successfully emulates Pratchett's voice through clever turns of phrase ("where optimism goes to die," "survival often meant choosing between bad ideas and worse logistics") and his characteristic footnote-like observations embedded in the narrative. The world-building is economical yet vivid, establishing the post-collapse setting through telling details like "glass rectangles that no longer remembered faces" and the repurposed shopping mall ecosystem. The plot structure adheres well to the prompt's requirements, beginning with mission planning, proceeding through escalating complications, and returning the protagonists home "worse for wear." Each obstacle feels organic to the world while escalating the absurdity - from toll-collecting goats to aggressive customer service machines to wire-rats. However, the piece occasionally veers toward being overly clever, with some metaphors feeling slightly forced ("where optimism goes to die" works better than "terror with a garnish of nostalgia"). The ending, while appropriately cyclical, feels somewhat rushed given the careful pacing of earlier sections. Some dialogue exchanges, particularly Chewy's more elaborate pronouncements, occasionally feel more like authorial wit than character voice. The imagery is consistently strong, creating a vivid sense of place without becoming overwrought. The action sequences maintain clarity despite their chaotic nature, and the various modes of transportation (running, floating cabbage, drone) provide good variety and escalation. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 14 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 5 Overwrought: 6 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 3 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Romance: Love in the Limelight — Score: 84.5
Prompt:
Romance: Love in the Limelight. Write one scene within a larger story set in Wales. A famous (fictional) actor ducks into a small-town bookstore to escape paparazzi. The scene takes us through the characters meeting in this odd circumstance. Over the course of the scene, the actor and the bookstore owner have a conversation charged by an undercurrent of unspoken chemistry. Write the actor as somewhat of a rogue with a fragile ego, which needs to be fed by having everyone like him. He is thoroughly charming, but the bookstore owner seems (at least superficially) immune to this; which paradoxically provokes a genuine attraction and derails the charm offensive. The bookstore owner, despite the superficial rebuffs of the actor's charm, is inwardly more than a little charmed and flustered despite themselves. Write primarily in dialogue, in the distinct voices of each character. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The bell above the door of **Awen & Ink** gave a frantic little jangle, entirely unsuited to the man who burst in beneath it. He was too tall for the low lintel, too handsome for a wet Tuesday in Cardiff, and too dramatically wrapped in a black coat and sunglasses for half past three on a street where the most exciting thing to happen that week had been a bin lorry reversing into a bollard. “Lock the door,” he said. The woman behind the counter looked up from a stack of invoices. “No.” He pushed the door shut with his shoulder and leaned back against it, breathing hard. Rain glittered in his dark hair. “Please.” She considered him for one slow second. “Are you being chased by the police, or have you simply made some very committed enemies?” “Worse.” He lifted a hand to the glass, keeping his face angled away from the window. “Photographers.” Outside, two men with cameras skidded past the shopfront, doubled back, then peered through the rain-streaked window. The woman behind the counter sighed, came around, and flipped the sign from **OPEN** to **CLOSED**. “Not locked,” she said. “Closed. There’s a difference.” “You’re an angel.” “I’m Welsh. We don’t do angels. We do rugby, judgement, and excellent cakes.” His mouth curved. It was a famous curve, the sort printed on buses and streaming service banners and, currently, a glossy poster in the cinema two streets over. She looked at him more closely despite herself. He lowered his sunglasses. There he was. Rhys Maddox. Golden boy of period dramas, action films, late-night interviews, fragrance campaigns, and one notoriously shirtless scene that had done terrible things to the internet. “Ah,” she said. He spread his arms a little, as if bracing for applause. “Ah?” “I thought you might be someone from the water board.” His smile faltered. Only a fraction, but she saw it. “The water board?” “You’ve got that hunted look. And a very expensive coat.” He removed the sunglasses fully. His eyes were green, annoyingly. Not just green, either—sea-glass green, storm-light green. Cardiff Bay on a day when the clouds had the decency to part. “I’m Rhys Maddox,” he said. “I gathered.” “And you are?” “Busy.” He blinked, then laughed. “Busy. Beautiful name. Old family?” “Shopkeeping stock.” She walked back to the counter. “If you’re going to hide, don’t stand in the window like a Gothic lighthouse.” He glanced over his shoulder, then moved deeper into the shop. “Right. Yes. Good point.” The bookshop was narrow and warm, smelling of paper, dust, coffee, and rain-damp wool. Shelves leaned into one another like old gossips. A hand-lettered sign above the till read: **IF YOU ASK WHETHER WE SELL KINDLES, THE ANSWER IS A CURSE.** Rhys read it and grinned. “Charming.” “That’s the general idea.” “What’s your name really?” She hesitated longer than necessary. “Megan.” “Megan,” he repeated, as though testing it for poetry. “Lovely.” “It was before you did that.” He pressed a hand dramatically to his chest. “Cruel.” “Accurate.” Outside, a camera flash popped against the glass. Megan stiffened before she could stop herself. Rhys saw it. His playfulness thinned. “Sorry.” “You didn’t take the photo.” “No, but I brought the circus.” “You’re assuming I don’t usually have men with cameras lurking outside my shop.” “Do you?” “Only during poetry readings. Things get heated.” His laugh came unexpectedly soft this time. “I’d believe it.” “You would not.” “I might. I’m very open-minded.” “You’re very something.” He leaned his elbows on the counter, the charm returning like a stage light warming up. “Megan of Awen & Ink, you wound me.” “Not fatally, from the look of you.” “Would it help if I bought something?” “It would help the shop.” “What do you recommend?” “That depends. Can you read?” There it was again—the tiny pause. He was used to people laughing before he’d finished speaking, used to being adored on entry, forgiven in advance. Megan knew this as clearly as if he’d brought his own spotlight with him. And still, her pulse had become absurd. It was ridiculous. She had unpacked three boxes of feminist essays that morning. She was not going to be undone by cheekbones and a wet collar. He straightened, scanning the shelves. “I’ll have you know I’ve read a great many books.” “For roles?” “For pleasure as well.” “Name three.” He pointed at her. “Trap.” “Very much.” “Fine. *Rebecca*. *The Left Hand of Darkness*. *Winnie-the-Pooh*.” She tried not to smile. Failed halfway. “That is a distressingly good answer.” His face changed. Not smug exactly. Relieved. As if her almost-smile had been something he’d been waiting for. “Oh, I’ve pleased you,” he said. “You’ve managed not to embarrass yourself. Don’t overreach.” “But I did, didn’t I? A little?” She busied herself with aligning receipts. “You have photographers outside, Mr Maddox. Surely you don’t need applause from a bookseller.” “Everyone needs applause from a bookseller. It’s the only kind worth having.” “Now that,” she said, “was nearly convincing.” He came closer to the counter. Not too close. Just enough that she could smell rain and cedar and something unfairly warm beneath it. “Nearly?” he asked. “Nearly.” “I’m losing my touch.” “Maybe it was never as devastating as you were led to believe.” He drew in a breath, hand to heart again, but the gesture didn’t quite land. His eyes searched her face with too little performance in them. “That’s a terrifying possibility.” Megan looked up. “Is it?” “For a man whose primary skill is being liked? Yes.” It was said lightly, but not only lightly. Her fingers stilled on the receipts. Outside, one of the paparazzi knocked on the glass. “Rhys! Just one shot, mate!” Rhys didn’t turn. A muscle worked in his jaw. Megan said, “There’s a back room.” “I don’t want to trouble you.” “You already have. Might as well commit.” He smiled again, but smaller. “You’re always this hospitable?” “Only to fugitives and paying customers. Which category are you choosing?” “Paying customer. Definitely.” “Wise.” She lifted a section of the counter, let him through, then led him past towers of second-hand paperbacks into a cramped stockroom. It was barely larger than a cupboard, with crates stacked to the ceiling and a kettle balanced on a filing cabinet. Rhys looked around. “It’s perfect.” “It’s a stockroom.” “Exactly. No one has ever photographed me in a stockroom.” “Another dream fulfilled.” He turned, and in the small space the full effect of him became difficult to ignore. Megan suddenly understood lighting technicians on a spiritual level. He seemed built to catch every available gleam. “You really aren’t impressed,” he said. “I didn’t say that.” “No?” “I’m impressed you haven’t knocked over a box yet.” He glanced down. His boot was half an inch from a crate labeled **LOCAL HISTORY / DRAGONS?** He stepped back carefully. “I’m graceful under pressure.” “You ran into my shop and demanded I lock the door.” “With grace.” “With cheek.” “Cheek is grace with better publicity.” This time she laughed properly before she could swallow it. His face brightened in a way that should have been calculated but wasn’t. It was too immediate, too nakedly pleased. “There,” he said softly. Megan felt heat climb her neck. “Don’t look so triumphant. It was only a laugh.” “It didn’t feel only.” The room seemed to shrink around them. Rain tapped at the high little window. From the shopfront came muffled voices, another knock, the dull persistence of fame pressing its nose to the glass. Megan folded her arms. “Do you do that with everyone?” “What?” “Make them feel like they’ve just handed you the crown jewels because they laughed at your joke.” Rhys’s smile faded, then returned crookedly. “Only the people I’m trying very hard to impress.” “And are you?” “Trying very hard?” “Yes.” He looked at her. Really looked. The kind of look that didn’t belong on posters because it was too uncertain. “I started to,” he said. “Out of habit.” “And now?” “Now I’m making a hash of it.” “Good.” “Good?” “It’s more interesting.” His laugh was quiet. “You know, most people prefer the polished version.” “I sell first editions with cracked spines. Polished is overrated.” Something in him softened at that, and Megan had the dangerous impression of being allowed past a velvet rope he hadn’t meant to drop. He cleared his throat. “Would you recommend one, then?” “One what?” “A cracked-spine first edition. Or anything. A book I can buy so I’m not simply a coward hiding among your dragons.” “You’re not a coward.” His eyes flicked to hers. She regretted the gentleness immediately and covered it by reaching for a nearby shelf. “You’re a nuisance. Different category.” “Important distinction.” She pulled out a slim volume. “Here. Dylan Thomas. Too obvious, perhaps, but you’re from London and look like you need grounding.” “I was born in Swansea.” “Were you?” “Truly.” “That explains some of the drama.” He accepted the book, their fingers brushing. It was nothing. Less than nothing. A tiny accidental contact over a paperback. Megan’s stomach nevertheless behaved as though the floor had tilted. Rhys looked down at their hands, then back at her. The rogue’s smile returned, slower now, less shiny and more dangerous. “Did that impress you?” “No.” “Fluster you?” “Absolutely not.” “You’re a dreadful liar, Megan of Awen & Ink.” “And you’re blocking the biscuit tin.” He glanced behind him. “Am I?” “Tragically.” He moved aside. “Far be it from me to stand between a woman and her biscuits.” “Wise again.” “Two wise choices in one afternoon. You may be improving me.” “Don’t make it my responsibility. I have a shop to run.” “And curses to dispense.” “And curses to dispense.” They stood too close beside the filing cabinet, with Dylan Thomas between them and rain stitching the city shut outside. Megan reached for the biscuit tin and missed the lid twice. Rhys noticed. Of course he noticed. But for once, he didn’t pounce. He simply said, very quietly, “Thank you for letting me hide.” She opened the tin. “Thank you for buying a book.” “I haven’t paid yet.” “I’m trusting you.” “That’s reckless.” “Bookshops are built on reckless hope.” His gaze held hers. “Is that so?” “Yes.” “Then I’ll have to come back. To settle my account.” Megan handed him a custard cream, because her hands needed something to do. “You could simply pay before you leave.” “I could.” “You should.” “I will.” His fingers closed around the biscuit, brushing hers again—this time, perhaps, not accidentally. “And then I’ll owe you for the tea I haven’t been offered.” “I didn’t offer tea.” “Yet.” The bell at the front door jangled violently as someone tried the handle. Megan turned her head. “Persistent, aren’t they?” Rhys watched her, not the door. “Some things are worth persisting for.” “Oh, that was awful.” “It was sincere.” “Worse.” He laughed, and this time there was no performance in it at all. Megan looked at him—famous, wet, impossible, hiding in her stockroom with a stolen biscuit and a book of poems—and felt, with deep irritation, that her afternoon had just become a chapter. “Finish your biscuit,” she said. “Then we’ll see about getting you out the back.” His smile tilted. “Yes, boss.” “And Mr Maddox?” “Rhys,” he said quickly. Too quickly. She pretended not to hear the hope in it. “Rhys, then. If you knock over my local history dragons, I’ll give the photographers your hiding place.” He held up both hands, book in one, biscuit in the other. “Megan, I swear on my fragile ego, your dragons are safe with me.” She laughed despite herself. And he looked, for a heartbeat, as though that were the only photograph he’d wanted taken all day.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and adherence to the prompt requirements. The author successfully creates a meet-cute scenario in a Welsh bookstore with well-defined character archetypes - the charming but insecure celebrity and the seemingly unimpressed but inwardly affected bookstore owner. The dialogue-heavy approach works effectively, with each character maintaining distinct voices throughout. The characterization is particularly strong. Rhys is convincingly portrayed as someone whose charm is both genuine and performative, with moments of vulnerability breaking through his practiced persona. Megan's resistance to his charm feels authentic rather than contrived, and her gradual warming creates believable romantic tension. The author skillfully shows rather than tells their mutual attraction through small gestures, pauses, and physical reactions. The Welsh setting is well-integrated without being heavy-handed. Details like the shop name "Awen & Ink," references to rugby and judgment, and the Dylan Thomas book recommendation feel natural rather than forced. The prose maintains an engaging pace with vivid but not overwrought descriptions. The dialogue crackles with wit and subtext. Both characters' voices are distinct - Rhys's theatrical charm versus Megan's dry, deflating responses. The banter feels natural and serves character development while advancing the romantic tension. The author demonstrates skill in showing Rhys's need for validation through his reactions to Megan's responses. However, some elements feel slightly predictable within the romance genre conventions. The "immune to charm" dynamic, while well-executed, follows familiar patterns. Certain descriptive phrases occasionally veer toward purple prose territory, particularly in describing Rhys's appearance. The pacing is well-controlled, building tension effectively without rushing the connection between characters. The ending strikes an appropriate note - suggesting future possibility without forcing premature resolution. The piece maintains emotional authenticity throughout, avoiding the trap of unearned romantic development. The writing demonstrates professional-level craft with smooth sentence flow and engaging narrative voice. The author successfully balances humor with genuine emotion, creating characters who feel real despite the somewhat contrived meeting circumstances. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 17

+ Whalepunk fantasy: The Morska Conspiracy — Score: 83.2
Prompt:
Whalepunk fantasy The Morska Conspiracy The Morska thieves guild experiences a sudden power vacuum as its leader was arrested in a raid in the early hours of the morning. Aya, a young woman born into aristocracy, has a keen interest in both the sciences and the arcane, having inherited her mother's gift for immateria magick (an outlawed practice for which her mother was, regrettably, hanged by the neck). Aya, bored with the world of aristocracy, has been spending her nights moonlighting with this very guild (masked, naturally). Leveraging her latent ability to summon dark entities from the immateria, she quickly rose through the ranks of the guild. Now, recognising the opportunity of the sudden power vacuum, Aya and her close companions Katra and Simeon, make a play for heart of the guild's claim to power: an ancient scythan artifact, under guard of spell and key in the depths of the guild hq. In contrast with the desperate chaos they had navigated thus far, the entrance to the underhold felt suddenly too quiet. Aya braces herself and reaches for the door. Write the next scene as the trio enter the underhold and find themselves confronted by none other than the guild leader herself, decidedly *un*-incarcerated, and in possession of both weapons and bodyguards. Paint a vivid picture of of the confrontation. Story beats: Aya is captured and unmasked. The trio are captured. Aya attempts to stall their demise by negotiating on her value as a hostage -- as she grapples with the inevitable consequences of her role in the guild being brought to light. Incorporate dialogue in the authentic voices of the characters. Do not write a fight scene. Lean into the gritty Victorian aesthetic with the dialogue & worldbuilding, as well as elements of gothic horror. In this world, men trust brass and oil and steel; magicka is feared and barely understood. Write in third person past tense, incorporating both Aya's internal monologue, and a heavy dialogue component. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The door to the underhold yielded with a softness Aya did not trust. It did not groan upon its hinges, as old doors ought to do. It did not protest with rust or settle with a sigh. It opened inward as if it had been waiting for the pressure of her gloved hand, and the three of them stood upon the threshold with the stale breath of the depths rising into their faces. Gas lamps guttered in iron cages along the walls beyond, each flame turned low and blue at the heart. Their flickering cast long, unsettling shadows across the stone floor—shadows that seemed to stretch too far, to lean too eagerly toward the living. The underhold descended beneath Morska House like the ribcage of some drowned leviathan: black arches slick with salt damp, pillars carved in the likeness of whale bones, old mortar furred white with mineral bloom. Somewhere below, water ticked steadily into a cistern. Drip. Drip. Drip. Like a clock with its patience worn thin. Katra, at Aya’s left shoulder, drew a sharp breath through her teeth. “Too quiet,” she murmured. Simeon shifted behind them, his usual nervous elegance reduced to a pale face above a soot-dark cravat. “I believe I mentioned, on the stair, that this entire enterprise possessed the moral shape of a trap.” “You mention many things,” Katra said. “Most of ’em useless.” Aya did not answer. Her fingers hovered near the seam of her sleeve where, under pearl buttons and mourning lace, she had stitched three small charms in black thread. Not spells, precisely. Spells were what pamphleteers called them when they wanted a crowd to gather and shout for rope. These were formulae. Geometries of intent. The summoning of pressure where none existed. The coaxing of the immateria to put a finger upon the scale of the world. Her mother had called it listening. The Crown had called it witchcraft. And then they had hanged Lady Mirielle Vey for it before breakfast, in front of three hundred good citizens, while bells rang for the sanctity of law. Aya stepped inside. The door shut behind them. Not slammed. Not by visible hand. It merely closed with a neat little click. Simeon went very still. “Ah.” At the far end of the chamber, beyond a long aisle of gaslight and stone, stood the vault door. It was not large, not compared with the grand bank safes of Admiralty Row, but it possessed a presence that made size irrelevant. Circular, blackened, and banded in brass, it was set into the wall like the stopped eye of some buried god. Around it, etched into the stones, were old scythan sigils—angles that disagreed with sight, curves that seemed to deepen the longer one looked. The artifact lay beyond that door. The heart of the guild’s power. The thing every whisper named and no whisper described the same way twice. Aya had imagined finding guards. She had imagined picking locks, bribing sentries, perhaps even spilling blood if Katra lost patience or Simeon lost nerve. She had not imagined a chair. It sat before the vault, lacquered black, high-backed, absurdly genteel, as if carried down from some countess’s parlour. In it, one leg crossed over the other, gloved hands folded atop a silver-headed cane, sat Mistress Vondra Pike. Leader of the Morska guild. Arrested, by all reports, in a Crown raid that morning. Decidedly un-incarcerated. “Miss Crow,” Vondra said, using Aya’s guild name with the lazy warmth of a hostess receiving late callers. “And here I had begun to fear you lacked ambition.” The lamps flared. Men stepped from the shadows. Six of them. No—eight. Broad coats, caps low, faces cut into hard planes by blue gaslight. Their pistols were already drawn, brass barrels polished, oiled mechanisms gleaming. One carried a harpoon-gun shortened for corridor work, its wicked barb chained to the stock. Another held an electro-baton that hummed faintly and spat little white gnats of light. Behind Vondra stood two women in butcher’s leather, knives at their belts and whale-ivory coshes in hand. Katra’s hand twitched toward her blade. “Katra,” Aya said softly. Her friend froze, jaw working. Vondra smiled. She was near fifty, perhaps, though women like her did not age so much as accumulate menace. Her hair, iron-grey and black, was pinned beneath a widow’s veil. A thin scar ran from the corner of her mouth to her ear, giving one side of her face the permanent suggestion of amusement. “Wise,” Vondra said. “I would dislike having to shoot Mistress Voss before I’ve had the pleasure of hearing her justify this charming little burglary.” Simeon lifted both hands slowly. “Mistress Pike, I should like the record to reflect that I was opposed.” “There is no record,” said one of the gunmen. “There is always a record,” Simeon replied faintly. “Usually found by the wrong people at the worst possible hour.” “Enough.” Vondra’s cane tapped once upon the stone. The sound went through Aya’s teeth. Vondra leaned forward. “On your knees.” No one moved. The pistol hammers clicked back, one after another. A small iron choir. Katra swallowed pride like broken glass and lowered herself first. Simeon followed with a wince, his hands still raised. Aya remained standing a moment longer, feeling the air thicken around her, feeling the black seam behind the world shift and listen. She could call something. Perhaps. A smear of hunger. A blind thing with too many fingers. A shadow that would pull the lamps sideways and send good men screaming for their mothers. She had done less with more. More with less. But the underhold was carved in sigils. Scythan work. Old work. The air here was nailed down. And Vondra Pike was watching her not with the fear of a woman ignorant of magick, but with the satisfaction of one who had already accounted for it. Aya knelt. “Search them,” Vondra said. Hands came upon them roughly. Katra cursed as her knives were stripped from boot, sleeve, corset-stay, and hairpin. Simeon surrendered lockpicks, a pearl-handled derringer, three wax seals, and a garrotte he insisted was “strictly ornamental.” From Aya they took the slim blade at her thigh, the little silver astrolabe at her waist, two vials of phosphorus salts, and finally the packet of black-thread charms sewn beneath her cuff. The man who found them recoiled. “Christ preserve us.” Vondra’s gaze sharpened. “Bring those here.” Aya’s stomach turned cold. The gunman approached his mistress, holding the charms between thumb and forefinger as if they were dead spiders. Vondra examined them without touching. “Immaterial knots,” she said. “Pretty work.” Katra looked at Aya from the corner of her eye. “Crow…” Vondra rose. The bodyguards shifted as if the whole room had taken a breath. She came down the aisle slowly, cane clicking, veil trembling in the lamp draft. Aya smelled her perfume as she approached: violet water, tobacco, and the metallic tang of old coins. “Mask,” Vondra said. Aya’s pulse struck once, hard. One of the women behind her seized her by the hair and forced her head back. Aya’s black half-mask was tied tight beneath her chignon, satin and whalebone shaped to the bridge of her nose. It had made her Crow. It had made her no one. It had let her pass from chandeliers to gutters, from opera boxes to blood-wet quays, from Aya Vey—obedient niece, desirable match, decorative remnant of a disgraced house—into something sharper and truer. Fingers worked at the knot. “No,” Aya said. It came out too quickly. Too young. Vondra’s smile deepened. “Oh, my dear.” The mask was pulled away. Cool underhold air touched Aya’s face. For one suspended instant no one spoke. Then Simeon made a small, strangled noise, a gentleman discovering his own execution notice in the morning paper. Katra stared. Not at the fine bones of Aya’s face, nor the dark hair pinned under a thief’s scarf, nor the mouth she had painted drab to disguise its aristocratic softness. Katra stared because she knew. Everyone in Morska knew the face from broadsheets and court etchings and cruel little songs sung by boys in taverns. Vondra said, with relish, “Lady Ayala Vey.” A murmur travelled through the guards. “Hell’s teeth,” one whispered. “The witch’s daughter.” Aya felt heat rise beneath her skin, followed by a cold so profound it seemed to come from the stones under her knees. The inevitable thing had happened. Not in a drawing room. Not by scandal sheet. Here, beneath the earth, in front of killers, thieves, informants, and worse: witnesses. Her uncle would shut the doors of Vey House by noon. Her cousins would deny ever speaking to her. The Crown would smile its thin legal smile. They had failed to hang the daughter with the mother; how pleased they would be to correct the omission. Katra’s voice was low. “Aya?” There was hurt in it. More dangerous than anger. Aya could not look at her. “I meant to tell you.” “No, you didn’t,” Katra said. Simeon coughed delicately. “In fairness, very few confessions are improved by timing, and this timing is—” “Shut up, Vale,” Katra snapped. Vondra crouched before Aya, bringing their faces level. Her eyes were pale, almost colourless. “Do you know,” she said, “what I enjoy most about clever girls?” Aya forced her breathing even. “Their conversation?” “Their certainty.” Vondra’s voice turned soft as velvet laid over a blade. “They believe the world has failed to notice them. They mistake indulgence for ignorance. I let you climb, Lady Vey. I let you gather admirers. I let you frighten my captains with parlour darkness and dead-air tricks, because a useful knife is still a knife even if it fancies itself a queen.” Aya’s mouth had gone dry. “The raid?” “A pantomime. Three constables paid, two magistrates misled, one prison carriage sent very publicly down Briar Lane.” Vondra stood again. “While every ambitious rat in Morska came nosing toward the cheese.” “The artifact,” Aya said. “You used it as bait.” “I used greed as bait. The artifact merely lent dignity.” Vondra turned away. “Bind them.” Ropes came out. Not hemp—wire-threaded dock cord, the sort used to hold struggling cargo in storm weather. Aya’s wrists were wrenched behind her and lashed until her fingers prickled. Katra endured it in furious silence. Simeon protested the treatment of his cuffs until someone struck him across the mouth. Aya flinched. “Now,” Vondra said, resuming her chair, “the question becomes what manner of example to make. Quick disposal is efficient. Public discovery is instructive. The river is traditional. Though for Lady Vey, I confess, one is tempted toward theatre.” Aya lifted her chin. “Kill me and you waste a fortune.” Vondra arched a brow. There. A sliver. Aya drove herself toward it, away from panic, away from the echo of her mother’s feet dropping through the scaffold boards. “My value as a corpse is limited,” Aya said. “My value breathing is considerable.” Katra muttered, “Now she starts bargaining.” Aya kept her eyes on Vondra. “House Vey will pay to avoid disgrace.” “They will pay to ensure your silence.” “They may attempt it. But if you hold me, visibly, privately, cleverly—” “Advice from the prisoner. How refreshing.” “You know what I am,” Aya said. “Not merely Vey. My mother’s daughter. The Crown would pay for me. The Church would pay more to burn me. My uncle would pay most of all to prevent either.” Vondra regarded her. The gas lamps fluttered, and the shadows along the floor writhed like eels cast from a black net. “An aristocrat,” Vondra said, “arguing for ransom. How comfortingly vulgar.” “I am arguing for profit. I assumed that language was spoken here.” One of the guards gave a short laugh before remembering himself. Vondra’s cane tapped once. “And your companions?” Aya’s throat tightened. Katra looked at her then—not pleading. Katra never pleaded. Her expression said only: Choose honest, for once. “They increase my cooperation,” Aya said. “Sentiment.” “Leverage.” “Same disease, different physician.” Simeon, blood bright at the corner of his lip, managed a bow from his knees. “Mistress Pike, as a man of numbers, may I add that three hostages provide a broader portfolio than one?” Vondra glanced at him. “You are alive because I find you occasionally amusing. Do not overdraw the account.” He closed his mouth. Aya felt the immateria press faintly at the edges of the sigils, a tide beyond locked gates. It knew her fear. It always knew. It whispered without words: open, open, open. She imagined the lamps going out. Men screaming. Ropes falling slack from hands no longer attached to bodies. Then she imagined Katra staring at her with that new horror. Simeon backing away. Her mother’s body turning slowly in the rain while the crowd crossed itself and called it justice. No. Not here. Not yet. Vondra leaned back, considering Aya as one might consider a rare animal in a cage. “You have made a tolerable case,” she said at last. “Not sufficient to save you. Sufficient to postpone you.” Aya allowed herself one breath. Katra whispered, “That your grand strategy, milady? Postponement?” Aya turned her head enough to meet her gaze. “It has served Parliament for centuries.” Despite herself, despite everything, Katra’s mouth twitched. Vondra saw it. “How touching. Take them below. Separate cells. No talking. No cleverness. If Lady Vey so much as hums at the walls, gag her with iron.” The guards hauled them upright. As they dragged Aya past the black vault, the scythan sigils seemed to pulse in the corner of her eye. Behind the brass-banded door, something old waited in perfect silence. Not sleeping. Listening. Just as her mother had taught her. Aya stumbled, recovered, and let them pull her into the deeper dark with her face uncovered and her name ruined behind her. The world above was already burning away. Her house, her mask, her careful division between lady and thief—all ash. But she was alive. Postponement, she thought, tasting blood where she had bitten her cheek. In Morska, postponement was sometimes the only name hope dared wear.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical craft and atmospheric worldbuilding within the "whalepunk" fantasy setting. The author successfully creates a vivid Victorian-gothic atmosphere with whale bone architecture, gas lamps, and industrial elements like brass, oil, and steel. The prose maintains consistent tone throughout, balancing ornate descriptive passages with sharp dialogue. The characterization is particularly strong. Aya emerges as a complex protagonist - aristocratic yet rebellious, intelligent yet trapped by circumstances beyond her control. Her internal conflict between her noble birth and criminal activities creates genuine tension. Vondra Pike is an excellent antagonist, sophisticated and menacing without being cartoonish. The dialogue feels authentic to the period setting, with characters speaking in distinct voices that reveal personality and class differences. The scene structure follows the requested beats effectively: the trio enters the underhold, discovers Vondra waiting, Aya is unmasked and her identity revealed, and she attempts to negotiate using her value as a hostage. The revelation of Aya's true identity as Lady Ayala Vey creates genuine dramatic impact, particularly in how it affects her relationship with Katra. The worldbuilding details are well-integrated - the scythan sigils, immateria magic, and the political backdrop of magical persecution all feel organic to the story rather than forced exposition. The author successfully avoids a fight scene as requested, instead building tension through dialogue and psychological maneuvering. However, some passages veer toward purple prose, particularly in the opening descriptions of the underhold. Phrases like "the ribcage of some drowned leviathan" and "the moral shape of a trap" feel slightly overwrought. The pacing occasionally slows due to dense descriptive passages that, while atmospheric, sometimes impede narrative momentum. The emotional complexity is well-handled, particularly Aya's fear of exposure and the betrayal Katra feels upon learning her friend's true identity. The ending strikes an appropriate note of dark hope - Aya has bought time but at great cost, which fits the gothic tone perfectly.
